Michael Taube: May Doug Ford's third majority win herald an era of fiscal conservatism

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-taube-may-doug-fords-third-majority-win/docview/3172324024/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Michael+Taube%3A+May+Doug+Ford%27s+third+majority+win+herald+an+era+of+fiscal+conservatism&amp;title=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives appear to be well on their way to victory in this Thursday's election. They're leading in opinion polls by an average of 40 to 44 per cent - and are ahead of Bonnie Crombie and the Liberals by 10 points or more. Barring an unforeseen turn of events, the PCs will form a third successive majority government and may achieve a small increase in their total number of legislative seats.

Document 2 of 281

Candidates talk health, Northern advocacy, effectiveness of opposition

Author: Leeson, Ben

Publication info: Leeson, Ben. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candidates-talk-health-northern-advocacy/docview/3171101073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candidates+talk+health%2C+Northern+advocacy%2C+effectiveness+of+opposition&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Leeson%2C+Ben&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Their representatives were nowhere to be seen, but Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives and their record in provincial government were very much in the crosshairs during a meet-the-candidates event hosted by CARP Sudbury at Royal Canadian Legion Branch 76 in Sudbury on Saturday afternoon.
Candidates in Sudbury and Nickel Belt for the four major Ontario parties were invited to the only all-candidates event to be organized in the city ahead of the snap Feb. 27 election.
NDP incumbents France Gelinas, for Nickel Belt, and Jamie West, for Sudbury, and Green Party mainstay David Robinson, also for Sudbury, attended in person and discussed issues including healthcare, climate change, regional decision-making and arts funding, as well as the inclusion of Indigenous voices in provincial government.
Some candidates, including Nickel Belt Liberal Natalie Labbee, were absent due to prior commitments. Labbee was away on her honeymoon, but provided opening and closing statements via video.
PC candidates Max Massimiliano, for Sudbury, and Randy Hazlett, for Nickel Belt, declined to attend.
Much of the discussion focused on healthcare funding at the lack of primary caregivers for Northern Ontario patients, as well as the privatization of health services under the PCs and how the attendees would attempt to fight those efforts if elected as members of the opposition. "I have been an MPP for 17 years and I have been in opposition for all 17 years," Gelinas said. "You need people in power and you need people in opposition. How do we move things forward in opposition? By speaking with one voice. By bringing more and more people under the tent, so that we can be heard. We do live in a democracy and when Ford hears us, because there is enough of us speaking with one voice, aiming for the same goal, he backs down.
"He backed down when he was trying to take the rights of early childhood educators away, he backed down when he was trying to sell the Greenbelt to his friends and well-connected. When we get together and we speak with one voice, we are loud, we are heard by the Conservative government and we make them back down."
Faced with rivals who have suggested a Tory breakthrough in Sudbury or Nickel Belt would mean greater power and prosperity for the ridings, West pointed to a binder full or past funding announcements that were made in the city during his two terms as MPP, including provincial money for a HART Hub, for water infrastructure and for rural affairs funding.
"I hear my Conservative colleagues say if we don't vote Conservative, then the city won't get stuff," West said. "We are not in a tinpot dictatorship. Nickel Belt has been NDP for 57 years and if it was only based on who is the government in power, Nickel Belt would be crumbling and falling apart and it would have nothing, so don't vote for a government that will lie to you and try to bribe you with your own tax dollars. You pay taxes, you work hard and you get stuff, because our city needs it. That's what's important. It is not about a voice at the table. It's about a voice that will speak up against authority.
"The voice at the table said nothing in Sault Ste. Marie when they had a Conservative MPP and they lost their healthcare facility, with 10,000 people losing their doctors. The voice in Timmins, when their opioids treatment centre was closed down, said nothing. It doesn't matter if you're at the table if all you do is stand up and clap and cheer for the premier, then you are not bringing representation. You are not elevating or amplifying the people who voted for you. You are not listening to them or meeting with them. You are a cheerleader for Doug Ford and we all know that Doug Ford only cares about Doug Ford."
While acknowledging that Ford may well carry the day on the 27th, as provincial polling suggests, Robinson agreed with West and Gelinas on many of the issues facing voters in Sudbury and Northern Ontario, and complimented Gelinas in particular on her efforts as a health champion. But Robinson also touted his own credentials as an economist and track record of organizing community initiatives and suggested he would be better qualified as an advocate for the city and the North on Ford's "failed portfolios," such as healthcare, education and housing.
"If I were an MPP, you'd be giving me permission to call the mayor and the current Liberal member of parliament and the head of the university and the union leaders in this province and the chamber of commerce to get them to a meeting to work out a plan," Robinson said. "And in each of those failed portfolios, we want one big item we're going to speak about together."
He pointed to the Sudbury 2000 initiative in the 1980s, which saw several community leaders come together in the wake of a strike and downsizing by mining companies and help to secure funding for Laurentian University and the local taxation centre, among other projects.
"That's the way we make some progress on this thing," Robinson said.
"We have got to have a focus on what the key building target is for Sudbury and that is attracting talent. If we don't attract talent, this economy is dead. We have one leading sector, a mining supply sector, and we have to attract researchers and talented people around mining, implementing new mining technologies, funding mines, the electronics needed around mines, electrifying - all of those things. We need those researchers here and that's my first item for this community political coalition, to make progress."
In her video statements, Labbee not only levelled criticism at Ford and his government, but suggested Bonnie Crombie and the Liberals were better positioned to defeat the PCs than Marit Stiles and the NDP and urged longtime New Democrats to change their votes.
"France Gelinas has been health critic for the last 17 years and despite her best intentions, she didn't deliver for Nickel Belt, so why would it change now?" Labbee said. "With Bonnie as premier, she's going to do more for you and as your MPP, I will finally get us our fair share."
Greater Sudburians and voters across Ontario will go to the polls this coming Thursday.
bleeson@postmedia.com

Subject: Mines; Community; Mining; Voters; Funding; Union leadership; Voter behavior

Document 3 of 281

New Blue Party would cut taxes, control spending - Legault; Sudbury's potential not being realized, candidate says

Author: Legault, Brady

Publication info: Legault, Brady. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-blue-party-would-cut-taxes-control-spending/docview/3171100485/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Blue+Party+would+cut+taxes%2C+control+spending+-+Legault%3B+Sudbury%27s+potential+not+being+realized%2C+candidate+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Legault%2C+Brady&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Note: The Sudbury Star has given candidates running in the Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings the chance to submit a column about why they are running and their priorities if elected on Feb. 27. Here, Brady Legault of the New Blue Party lays out his reasons for running.
I am a married father of four who strongly believes in hard work and perseverance. As a licensed heavy-duty equipment technician in the construction industry, I am committed to doing whatever it takes to complete a job correctly and efficiently.
As someone who has lived in the North my whole life, I truly value the skill and perseverance needed to succeed and thrive in our climate. I have always had a keen interest in politics and the principles of the New Blue Party resonate deeply with my views and convictions.
Sudbury is a city with so much to offer. With our strong mining heritage and the industries that founded the city, our post-secondary institutions, and our healthcare facilities, Sudbury can emerge as a leader in technological innovation and progress.
Yet, there are cracks in the city's foundation. Despite being Northern Ontario's hub for health care, Sudbury has a health-care crisis. Despite being the largest geographical city in Ontario, Sudbury has a housing crisis.
Despite provincial and federal funding for mental health and addictions programs, Sudbury is still grappling with a mental health and opioid crisis.
How can we position ourselves as a model for other Northern Ontario cities when we are struggling with the fundamental elements of prosperity and personal freedom? We need to prioritize the well-being and security of our community by addressing these pressing issues head-on.
It is essential that we advocate for better health-care services to ensure prompt access for all residents; I believe that rehiring nurses and healthcare workers that were fired because of COVID -19 mandates would be the first place to start.
We must work towards creating safer neighbourhoods through increased community policing and support for local initiatives.
I am grateful for the provincial funding towards the HART hub in Sudbury and I am looking forward to seeing the impact it has in our communities; however, I am concerned it may not be enough.
Additionally, I would like to evaluate the OPP and Greater Sudbury Police in relation to their Mobile Crisis Rapid Response teams. I am interested in identifying potential ways they could enhance their effectiveness in our community through increased support or assistance from the provincial government.
Furthermore, we need to push for fair treatment in provincial funding and decision-making processes, so that Sudbury's voice is heard and valued as much as any other region in Ontario. By tackling these challenges, we can pave the way for a safer, cleaner and healthier future for all Sudburians.
The New Blue Party of Ontario has a mission to offer solutions to ensure our province's future is one of hope, opportunity, and prosperity. The party supports tax relief by cutting Ontario's HST from 13 to 10 per cent with a goal to scrap the HST entirely as part of its long-term plan. Sudburian's financial power has dwindled. Our dollar has less purchasing power and cannot go as far due to the outrageous inflation and rising costs of necessities. Not only that, but we are suffocating in taxes. We deserve a break from the heavy-handed taxes and out-of-control provincial government spending.
The New Blue Party has great financial policies that would help our dollar go further and afford us much-needed breathing room.
Sudburians need an MPP who will advocate for them in Queen's Park. Someone who will stand for their interests, concerns and work hard at obtaining and delivering solutions to their issues. The New Blue Party and I will work together to get things done so that Sudbury can become a city full of hope, endless opportunity and lasting prosperity.
Also running in Sudbury are Jamie West (Sudbury), who currently holds the seat for the NDP, Max Massimiliano (PCs), Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry (Liberals). David Robinson (Greens) and David Popescu (Independent.)
sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 4 of 281

Bringing costs down top priority for west

Author: West, Jamie

Publication info: West, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bringing-costs-down-top-priority-west/docview/3171100479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bringing+costs+down+top+priority+for+west&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=West%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Note: Note: The Sudbury Star has given candidates running in the Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings the chance to submit a column about why they are running and their priorities if elected. Here, Jamie West of the NDP lays out his reasons for running.
My name is Jamie West and I'm the Ontario NDP candidate for Sudbury. My family and I have always lived in Sudbury. I grew up in geared-to-income housing in the Donovan and I've learned that nothing beats hard work. I worked in construction to help pay for post-secondary and I'm a proud graduate of both Cambrian College and Laurentian University.
I have an amazing wife, Pam, who teaches Kindergarten. I also have three children, two boys and a girl.
Before being elected in 2018, I worked in construction, mining workplace safety and mental health. However, I'm most proud of my volunteer work. I was a volunteer for 17 years at United Way. I learned the importance of volunteer work through my mom who volunteered tirelessly and often encouraged me to get involved when I was growing up, too. There's no better feeling than being of service to your community.
I've always been drawn to teamwork, and I love helping others. I'm proud to be part of Sudbury, a community that cares, and I continue to work tirelessly for the people of Sudbury.
I've been the MPP for Sudbury for the last seven years. During that time, I've made it my mission to elevate and amplify your voices. Nothing has changed during this election. Since day one, I've continued to talk with you, I've always attended debates, and I've always been available for interviews.
But where are the Conservatives? They're denying interviews and they won't come to debates. I have a question: If Doug Ford won't let Conservative candidates speak for themselves during the election, why would anyone expect them to speak for you in the future?
In the freezing cold and through the snowstorms, I'm out every day talking with you. What I'm hearing loud and clear is that people are struggling. They're telling me that daily life has become unaffordable. They're also concerned about healthcare, education and childcare.
But, overwhelmingly, people just want a decent life. They work hard but basic costs, like rent and groceries, are too high. Bringing costs down is my priority for Sudbury. This includes implementing a grocery rebate. This means fewer people at food banks and more food on your table.
It includes building more affordable housing and implementing real rent control. I'm also focused on getting more family doctors in Sudbury.
Over the past seven years, my staff and I have helped thousands of you through my office and I've met thousands more in the community. I'm running in this election because I have the experience, I know Sudbury, and I believe in our community.
Together we made some great gains, but there's still a lot left to do. Like you, I know that hard work pays off and I'm not gonna give up this fight. An unnecessary winter election that is costing taxpayers $180 million, and Doug Ford's bullying tactics, won't stop me.
I'm proud to be blue-collar and working-class. I'm fighting for families. For workers. For seniors. And for youth. I'm fighting for you on the issues that matter most to Sudbury.
Thank you for all the support over the years, I look forward to being your MPP again. Merci pour tout le soutien au fli des années, j'ai hâte d'être Ã nouveau votre député provincial.
I'm here. I'm listening, and I'm ready to amplify your voices at Queen's Park - just like I've always done.
Also running in Sudbury are Max Massimiliano (PCs), Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry (Liberals), Bradley Legault (New Blue Party), David Robinson (Greens) and David Popescu (Independent.) sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 5 of 281

PCs say they are making 'historic investments' in LTC

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-say-they-are-making-historic-investments-ltc/docview/3171100346/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+say+they+are+making+%27historic+investments%27+in+LTC&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The PCs are defending their record when it comes to providing longterm care in Sudbury and across Ontario.
In an email, Grace Lee of the Ontario PC Party took issue with criticisms levelled by the Canadian Union of Public Employees during a press conference in Sudbury on Feb. 27.
For example, CUPE said the PCs promised previously to "increase beds by 30,000 by 2028 and 15,000 by 2023/24. But in their own 20242025 budget they reported that only 2,246 new beds have opened ... that amounts to a three per cent increase in the total number of LTC beds."
Lee, however, said that "is false. Since 2018, our PC team has gotten shovels in the ground for over 21,000 new long-term care beds.
"From protecting our economy to delivering better care, the Ontario PCs have a plan to stand up for communities across the province," she said in the email. "The NDP propped up the Liberal government for over a decade, ignoring long-term care and building just 611 beds in 7 years.
"In contrast, our PC team is making historic investments to expand long-term care capacity and help seniors live with the dignity they deserve.
In addition, Lee said the PCs passed the Fixing Long Term-Care Act "to ensure long-term care residents receive an average of 4 hours of daily direct care per day and, we (strengthened) the residents' Bill of Rights, implemented an annual survey, doubled the number of inspectors, and doubled the fines for offences.
"This is all on top of the record investments we have made to hire 100,000 new nurses, 15,000 new doctors, and over 25,000 PSWs since 2018." During their press conference in Sudbury, CUPE said long-term care homes are experiencing chronic under-staffing and funding.
The union said Ontario lacks long-term care beds - a situation it said will only get worse as the province's population ages.
In addition, the union was critical of private-sector operators of long-term care homes in Ontario, arguing they are bad for residents and workers.
ELECTION DETAILS Voting day Feb. 27, from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m.
Who's running? Sudbury: Jamie West (Sudbury), who currently holds the seat for the NDP, Max Massimiliano (PCs), Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry (Liberals). David Robinson (Greens), Bradley Legault and David Popescu (Independent) Nickel Belt: France Gelinas, who currently holds the seat for the NDP, Steve Hazlett (PC), Natalie Labbee (Liberals), James Chretien (Libertarian), Paul Divincenzo (New Blue) and Connie Hill (Green.)

Document 6 of 281

Make Laurentian University Ontario's mining champion; Ontario election It's one of David Robinson's four pillars as the Green Party candidate for the Sudbury riding

Author: Robinson, David

Publication info: Robinson, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/make-laurentian-university-ontarios-mining/docview/3171099604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Make+Laurentian+University+Ontario%27s+mining+champion%3B+Ontario+election+It%27s+one+of+David+Robinson%27s+four+pillars+as+the+Green+Party+candidate+for+the+Sudbury+riding&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Robinson%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Note: The Sudbury Star has given candidates running in the Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings the chance to submit a column about why they are running and their priorities if elected. Here, David Robinson of the Green Party lays out his reasons for running.
My job as Sudbury's Green candidate is to get climate change on your agenda and convince you it is part of a real economic development strategy for our city.
My job as your MPP would be to bring together political factions and leading institutions in the city in a unified campaign to get what Sudbury needs. No other candidate has the knowledge, experience or the proven record of working for Sudbury. No other candidate has the contacts and the respect in every corner of the city.
Star readers can look up "Dr. David Robinson, Sudbury" with Google. They will discover that I am the father of Sudbury's architecture school, the person who identified and first promoted our mighty and growing mining supply sector, who has done briefings for the Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce, consulted for First Nations communities and other northern communities, helped found Gallery 6500 in the Steelworkers hall, played a key role in bringing Sudbury's PlaySmeter Festival to the city.
They will find that I am the father of three, grandfather of two, married for 44 years, and a longtime climate activist. They find my columns in The Star, in Northern Ontario Business Magazine, or the Republic of Mining website and my two books.
They will see someone with a wide knowledge and a wider set of connections that I can use on Sudbury's behalf if I am MPP.
I won't talk here about our solid Green platform, although I helped write parts of it.
I won't explain how Doug Ford has failed on every major provincial portfolio, although, as an economist, I can make that case pretty easily.
I'll just lay out my four provincial-level priorities for Sudbury.
First, make Laurentian University Ontario's mining champion. The world needs the critical minerals we have in Northern Ontario. The mines won't be in Sudbury. How do we capture a big share of the money these minerals generate? I would work with city council, the university and others to bring more mining-related research and teaching to Sudbury's university and colleges. This is Sudbury's biggest economic development opportunity.
Success would support the growth of the mining supply and services sector, the main economic driver for Greater Sudbury. It is the only way we can capture a share of the coming critical minerals boom for Sudbury.
The key is to build sustained and unified local political pressure.
Second, attract talent by being a climate champion and a safe climate refuge. Sudbury is in a kind of climate sweet spot. If we make the right moves now, Sudbury will look like heaven to people in places facing rapid and devastating climate change. Talent drives the economy. We can target talented climate refugees from around the world and the USA.
Third, save everyone money. Sudburians spend close to half a billion dollars a year on fossil fuels. We can cut that cost by two-thirds by helping people convert to heat pumps and electric vehicles. Make the choice easy and let the wallets talk.
Fourth, fix the city's finances. Cities are creatures of the province. The province has been downloading costs and responsibilities onto the property tax bill. It is time to download some of the province's revenues. Cities should get most of the fuel tax and part of the HST. We need property tax reform to make the system fairer and more efficient.
These are all provincial issues. Other candidates are not talking about them because they don't understand them. They don't remember how ambitious and powerful the people of Sudbury are when they work together. We can be powerful again.
Also running in Sudbury are Jamie West (Sudbury), who currently holds the seat for the NDP, Max Massimiliano (PCs), Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry (Liberals), Brady Legault (New Blue Party) and David Popescu (Independent.) sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Economic development; Mining; Critical minerals; Climate change; Property taxes; Elections; Northern communities

Document 7 of 281

Ford promises action on Highway 69, Ring of Fire during Sudbury stop; Premier welcomes endorsement of electrical workers rep while visiting union hall

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-promises-action-on-highway-69-ring-fire/docview/3171099277/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+promises+action+on+Highway+69%2C+Ring+of+Fire+during+Sudbury+stop%3B+Premier+welcomes+endorsement+of+electrical+workers+rep+while+visiting+union+hall&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford said he will finish Highway 69 and extend a road to the Ring of Fire during a campaign stop in Sudbury on Sunday, although a timetable was not provided for either project.
"The good news is, guys, we're widening Highway 69," the Ontario premier said during a brief media scrum. "Last time I was here, which wasn't that long ago, I drove that highway - that's treacherous, you're putting your life in your hands."
Much of the route is now fourlaned but there is a remaining gap of 68 kilometres, which passes through the Henvey Inlet, Magnetawan and Shawanaga.
"We're working with the three First Nation communities," said Ford. "I think we have a deal, so now we'll be able to widen it. It's a priority."
Ford said his government, if re-elected, is also committed to expanding a 220-km stretch of Highway 11/17 from Temiskaming Shores to Cochrane. "If we can save one life, it's worth it," he said.
Asked when work on Highway 69 might resume, the premier did not provide a schedule, but made it sound urgent. "We're going to go as quickly as possible," he said. "That's with everything now, we have to start moving - get the bureaucracy and everything out the way." Ford blamed the federal government for encumbering mining projects with red tape and said he is eager to fast-track development, including in the Ring of Fire.
"We have 33 of the most critical minerals in the world and we have the largest deposit of minerals in the Ring of Fire," he said. "God willing we get re-elected, I've told the feds, things are going to change. We aren't going to screw around for 10-15 years to get a permit."
He said the First Nation communities in the Ring of Fire area - Webequie, Marten Falls and Aroland - are also impatient to get through the consultations phase and see actual progress made.
"We were going to build that road up there, and they're frustrated that the feds just keep holding them up," he said. "So we're moving forward. Take us to court, or do whatever you want - we're moving. Enough's enough; it's been 20 years."
The premier's remarks came after a presentation at the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers hall in New Sudbury, during which Local 1687 business manager Kevin Gaudaur threw his support behind the Progressive Conservative agenda.
"With the backing of the PC government, we've been able to modernize our training facility and offer more in-person and online training, to not only our members but also by opening our doors to high school students," he said.
Local 1687 represents more than 700 skilled workers in northeastern Ontario and many are anxious about the prospect of tariffs, the union rep noted.
"We need a leader who understands these challenges and is committed to fighting for fair trade and our members," said Gaudaur. "These reasons, coupled with (the government's) renewed commitment to enforce compulsory trade certifications in the electrical industry, are why I proudly endorse Doug Ford in this upcoming election and call on all IBEW members to do the same."
The premier said electrical workers are key to making countless projects a reality in Ontario. "There's nothing that can be built in this province without their hands on it somewhere," he said.
As for the threat of tariffs from the U.S., Ford said he is not going to shy away from a fight.
"It would devastate our economy, but I can assure you one thing, I'll make sure it devastates their economy as well," he said. "And we're going to continue to invest and have the backs of the skilled trades, because they're the backbone of our economy."
The premier also paid a visit to the Carpenters Union Local 2486 centre in Azilda, where he drove a nail into a board while surrounded by apprentices. The union received $3.2 million from the province in November to enlarge its training facility.
jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 8 of 281

Ontario election: Will Doug Ford's blue wave envelop Sudbury?; The PCs last held the riding almost 40 years ago but analysts are predicting a close race with the NDP. Meanwhile, housing, health care and boosting mining are top city concerns

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-election-will-doug-fords-blue-wave/docview/3171099169/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Will+Doug+Ford%27s+blue+wave+envelop+Sudbury%3F%3B+The+PCs+last+held+the+riding+almost+40+years+ago+but+analysts+are+predicting+a+close+race+with+the+NDP.+Meanwhile%2C+housing%2C+health+care+and+boosting+mining+are+top+city+concerns&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is a lot going on in the world right now, but as the provincial election nears, access to publicly-funded health care remains top of mind for many voters in the Sudbury riding.
Raymond Hammond, president of USW Local 6500, said his union wants to see a representative at Queen's Park who listens to community concerns, "someone that has lived in the community, worked in the community and knows the needs, as well as anyone else."
Highways, including the last 70 km of Highway 69, remain a priority for the Steelworkers. Of course, access to an efficient and vibrant health-care system is also a top concern.
"Sudbury needs safe highways that lead to the rest of the province. Sudbury needs faster access to public health care, including mental health (care), that is properly staffed and supplied. Sudbury needs an end to hallway medicine," Hammond said. "Sudbury needs properly funded and supported education to ensure their children are ready for the workforce, especially the local workforce. Sudbury needs transitional housing to offer proper support for its unhoused population. Sudbury needs to be more affordable."
The United Steelworkers have long supported the New Democrats, and Hammond said voters should keep one eye on the past, while the other focuses on the horizon.
"The priorities of the Local are simple. We need a government that takes care of the people; a government that addresses health care, including mental health care; education; highway safety; housing; and affordability," he offered. "These issues help everyone, and they enable the Local and any other union to concentrate bargaining power on wages, and other protections and benefits."
It is easy to make promises, but more challenging to actually bring results to fruition. Sudbury voters should consider who would make the strongest advocate.
"Voters need to think about the MPPs that have been there for them over the years and put in the time to talk to their constituents, attend community events and represent them at Queen's Park," Hammond commented. "Voters need to think about the differences between federal and provincial politics, and what our MPPs can actually do for us."
Mayor Paul Lefebvre seconded Hammond's thoughts on health care. He said on his mind this election is Health Sciences North and the state of the local system.
"We're seeing our hospital overwhelmed. We don't have the capacity to deal with the influx of folks, not only from Sudbury but from the huge catchment area it serves now," Lefebvre said last week. "For me, it's helping advocate and getting funding for our hospital to grow - to add more beds." As Lefebvre noted, Greater Sudbury has a current population hovering at about 180,000; however, the hospital and its mental health care programs are serving a population of about 400,000.
"The funding we receive is not equal to the needs and the demand on our system. That's why our city, as a whole, can't keep up," he said. "We are the hub of the northeast and when there are acute mental health care needs, often people are told they can access services here, causing us to be way over capacity. That is a challenge for us, to be able to maintain."
The state of the city's infrastructure is also a priority for Lefebvre.
"We want more housing. The challenge is we don't have all the infrastructure we need - water, wastewater, and certainly the roads," he said.
There is provincial funding available for northern municipalities, but there is a cap of $10 million. Lefebvre said he has been advocating for the province to raise that maximum. He said because Greater Sudbury is actually an amalgamated city of seven municipalities, it should qualify for more funding.
"We love that program, but we're capped out at $10 million. We would qualify for about $18 million, but if we were seven municipalities, we would qualify for $10 million each, therefore we would qualify for $70 million," Lefebvre argued.
Lefebvre said his third priority will be advocating for increased local post-extraction processing of critical minerals. Greater Sudbury is the national powerhouse, he said, with two smelters, two mills and two refineries.
"I believe folks from Sudbury want to see more of that supply chain stay here, so we can have more of the advanced processing," he said. "As we're seeing the USA wanting to onshore more of their manufacturing and using their technology, we should be looking at our own natural resources. In Sudbury, that means our own critical minerals, including nickel, and what more we can do here on the advanced processing side to become that hub, not just for Sudbury, but for Canada and around the world."
Lefebvre said Greater Sudbury has the expertise, land and natural resources to become a global hub, especially with the advent of battery-electric vehicles.
PCS LAST HELD SUDBURY IN THE 1980S The Sudbury riding has not been held by a Conservative since the 1980s, when Jim Gordon served (1981-87) under Premiers Bill Davis and Frank Miller, as well as Larry Grossman, who served as leader of the opposition from 1985-87.
For nearly 40 years, the Sudbury riding has bounced between the New Democrats and the Liberals.
MPP Jamie West has held the Sudbury seat for the NDP since 2018. He is running again, but if predictions are accurate, the blue tide is fast approaching.
As of Feb. 23, the Conservatives were leading election forecasts by a significant margin. The website 338canada.com is projecting the PCs will win 45 per cent of seats (give or take four per cent), while the Liberals will take 27 per cent of ridings. The New Democrats are only projected to win 19 per cent of seats and the Green Party of Ontario, six per cent.
Locally, 338canada.com says the Sudbury riding is a toss-up between the NDP and Doug Ford's PCs. It also has Nickel Belt leaning NDP.
Parties need to win at least 63 seats for a majority; 338canada is projecting the Conservatives will win 91 seats; while the New Democrats will win 19 seats and the Liberals, just 12 seats. The Greens are projected to win two seats.
In Sudbury, the Conservatives are represented by entrepreneur Max Massimiliano.
Born and raised in Greater Sudbury, Massimiliano is a business owner and community leader. His success "has been driven by key qualities such as integrity, a strong work ethic and a genuine commitment to his community," the PCs note on the party website. "Widely respected for his leadership, Max is focused on advocating for policies and initiatives that support local economic growth, job creation and health care access, all while ensuring Sudbury remains a great place to live, work and raise a family." Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry, a local businessman, is running for the Liberals.
Born and raised in Pakistan to a family that was involved in politics, Choudhry moved to Canada in 2012. He has owned or managed fast food businesses since his arrival. He is currently proprietor of Smoke's Poutinerie at 40 Elm St.
Choudhry said he was compelled to get involved with the Liberals after waiting last summer in the ER at Health Sciences North for more than 10 hours. His young family still has no family doctor and he wants the health care system fixed.
The Ontario Health Coalition says more than two million residents of Ontario are without a family physician.
"No matter how anyone tries to spin the numbers, the actual data from sources not tainted by politics show that more than a million Ontarians have lost their family doctor since before the Ford government was elected," the Coalition reported on Jan. 27. "In 2016, before the Ford government was elected, 1.3 million Ontarians did not have a family doctor, according to data from Statistics Canada. By July 2024, there were 2.5 million Ontarians without a family doctor, according to the Ontario College of Family Physicians. The numbers are projected to get worse."
A lack of family doctors is one aspect plaguing the health-care system, but the crisis runs deeper and cuts across various sectors. Attention must be paid to the social determinants of health, the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario argues. The RNAO is calling on the next government to strengthen the social determinants of health by improving access to "housing, social programs and liveable incomes to foster healthy, equitable, diverse and inclusive communities."
The association, which has a local chapter, wants health care to be prioritized across the province. The RNAO is calling for action on the nursing crisis by retaining registered nurses and nurse practitioners "with competitive compensation, full-time jobs, education funding and safe work environments."
It also wants the next government to consider the closure of supervised consumption sites. Keeping sites open and accessible saves lives, the association argues.
"With 10 Ontarians dying every day from the illicit drug supply, our leaders must also confront the toxic drug crisis. An essential step: reversing the current plan to close supervised consumption sites is essential," the RNAO stated on its website (rnao.ca). It is calling on Queen's Park to "commit to expanding mental health care - including more harm reduction and treatment services across the province - to save lives and support communities."
The Go-Give Project, which works with Greater Sudbury's more vulnerable population, echoes that sentiment. The organization wants voters to think about which party is best poised to provide more robust options for mental health care and the treatment of addictions.
Evie Ali, executive director of the Go-Give Project, said voters should keep in mind a commitment to funding - "are candidates prior - itizing better access to treatment and support?" she asked - as well as access to harm reduction services.
She also believes better co-ordination of care - with more collaboration amongst hospitals, social services and community organizations - is important.
Long wait times are not a barrier only for those suffering from physical ailments. Ali said there is an "urgent need" for more healthcare options throughout northeastern Ontario.
"The region has some services in place - like HSN's withdrawal management programs and mental health support - but long wait times and limited accessibility remain major roadblocks for many who need help," she commented. "One of the biggest gaps in care right now is harm reduction services. Earlier this year, Sudbury's supervised consumption site, The Spot, shut down due to funding issues. That site provided a safe place for people to use substances under supervision, reducing overdose risks and connecting users with support services. Without it, there are growing concerns about increased overdoses and the spread of infectious diseases."
Ali said finding solutions to these crises requires a community approach.
"At the end of the day, addiction and mental health challenges don't go away on their own," she said. "The election is a chance for Sudbury residents to push for real solutions that make a difference.
The RNAO wants a health care system that is "accessible, equita- ble, person-centred, integrated, not-for-profit and publicly funded." It is calling for taxation frameworks that reduce extreme income and wealth inequality.
"It's important to look beyond health care to address the fundamental factors that shape well-being," the RNAO commented online. "This includes building government-owned or non-profit housing; ensuring living wages and strong income supports; investing in quality education and social programs; enhancing sustainable transportation and public spaces; supporting community initiatives; and advancing equity, anti-racism and inclusion. It also requires ensuring health and safety in social media and other digital spaces. How do political candidates plan to address this?" CLIMATE CHANGE A CONCERN The RNAO is calling on the next government to look beyond the health care portfolio to truly address the crisis currently facing Ontario. As part of its approach, the nursing association wants action on the climate emergency.
"Build a low-carbon, just and equitable economy; phase out fossil fuels; and address pollution to improve health outcomes," the RNAO posted on its website.
David Robinson, running for the Green Party of Ontario, believes his party is best equipped to tackle the climate crisis.
Robinson believes climate change "is the elephant in the room no other party can address," the Green Party of Ontario stated on its website.
The economist and retired Laurentian University professor built a career studying economic development in northern Ontario. He initiated and helped establish the McEwen School of Architecture; and his research played an important role in developing the mining supply sector throughout the northeast.
A dedicated environmentalist, Robinson still teaches one course per term at LU on the environment.
"When we deal with transient top-of-mind issues, even Trump's tariffs, we have to make sure our solutions bend toward climate adaptation," he cautioned on the Green Party website. "Short-run panic will waste time and undermine our children's future."
Public Health Sudbury and Districts is calling on voters to consider the impact of the public health portfolio when they go to the polls. Investing in public health benefits all residents, the local health unit argues.
"Elections are critical times for us to take stock of where we are as a society and what we should prioritize and invest in for the next few years," Dr. Mustafa Hirji, acting medical officer of health, said in a press release published in early February. "Having just emerged from the worst public health emergency of a generation and facing population health challenges around substance use; resurgent vaccine-preventable infections around the world; and the spectre of an avian influenza pandemic, now is the time to invest in a public health sector that is sustainably funded and positioned to combat these threats."
But public health is reaching a breaking point, the result of a chronic lack of funding combined with increasingly complex concerns.
"Like Public Health, countless other community agencies and individuals across Sudbury and Manitoulin districts rally their efforts every day to respond to community needs, advance community health, promote equity and prevent disease; however, over the past decade, public health funding has not kept pace with inflation, while the population has grown and new mandates have arisen," PHSD stated. "With a continuously shrinking budget, Public Health struggles to deliver on its commitments to the community."
The provincial election takes place on Feb. 27.
Besides West, Massimiliano, Robinson and Choudhry, Brady Legault (New Blue Party) and David Popescu (Independent) are running in Sudbury.
CHAMBER SURVEY The Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce has posted a Q&A with candidates from the Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings. It covers several topics of interest, including health care, taxes, tariffs and Highway 69. To learn more about what the candidates have to say on local issues, please go to sudburychamber.ca/meet-your-candidates.
mkkeown@postmedia.com BlueSky: @mkkeown.bsky.social Facebook: @mkkeown

Full text: In the final week, Doug Ford's PC Party seems assured of victory in Thursday's election. Not that he or his team are taking anything for granted.

Document 11 of 281

Sudbury candidates talk health, Northern advocacy, effectiveness of opposition

Author: Ben Leeson Sudbury Star

Publication info: Ben Leeson Sudbury Star. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sudbury-candidates-talk-health-northern-advocacy/docview/3169870234/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: Their representatives were nowhere to be seen, but Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives and their record in provincial government were very much in the crosshairs during a meet-the-candidates event hosted by CARP Sudbury at Royal Canadian Legion Branch 76 in Sudbury on Saturday afternoon.

Document 12 of 281

Blue Party pro-life, would cut taxes, DiVincenzo says; Ontario election

Author: Divincenzo, Paul

Publication info: Divincenzo, Paul. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/blue-party-pro-life-would-cut-taxes-divincenzo/docview/3169666854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blue+Party+pro-life%2C+would+cut+taxes%2C+DiVincenzo+says%3B+Ontario+election&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Divincenzo%2C+Paul&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I am pleased to represent the New Blue Party for the Nickel Belt riding. My wife and I have lived in the province of Ontario our entire lives. Northern Ontario is near and dear to our hearts, and it has been the perfect setting to raise our four daughters. While I reside in the riding of Algoma-Manitoulin, I conduct business in the Nickel Belt region and have an interest in its development and well-being.
The New Blue Party was formed to give Ontarians a choice once again for a conservative government as all other parties continue to shift to the left. I chose to run for this party because I align very closely with their platform. There remain people in Ontario that want to vote according to conscience and support a pro-life candidate. The New Blue Party supports the protection and dignity of all human life, from conception to natural death and I stand with that declaration.
Economically, the only growth we are seeing in Ontario is the public sector workforce and the massive provincial debt. This just adds to the burden already carried by Ontarians. The New Blue has an interest in lessening the load by cutting the HST down to 10 per cent with a longer-term goal of scrapping the HST entirely.
They also support removing the federal government's carbon tax and the Ontario PC government's industrial carbon tax and carbon pricing system. Both would allow citizens of Ontario and Nickel Belt to feel some relief from the financial pressure we are all facing.
Lastly, I appeal to Nickel Belt constituents to vote for change that will reshape Ontario and make it a place where the young generation will see growing opportunities and hope for prosperity. We need more than nominal change for our young people. We need leaders who will focus on the future and put the best interests of the people first, every time.
Paul DiVincenzo is the New Blue Candidate for Nickel Belt.
NICKEL BELT CANDIDATES - France Gelinas (NDP) has held Nickel Belt since 2007. Challenging her besides Paul DiVincenzo are Natalie Labbee (Liberal), Randy Hazlett (PC) and James Chretien (Libertarian.)
Ontario goes to the polls on Feb. 27.
PROVIN CIAL ALLCANDIDATES MEETING TO TAKE PLACE FEB. 22 Voters in Sudbury and Nickel Belt will have the opportunity to see and hear most of the candidates for both provincial ridings at a special meeting on Feb. 22 at the Canadian Legion Branch 76, 1553 Weller St. in Minnow Lake. Liberal, Progressive Conservative, NDP and Green candidates have been invited to the event, which will begin at 1:30 p.m.
Candidates representing the major parties will have the opportunity for opening and closing remarks, and to answer questions from attendees. Other parties will have a table for material and can meet with those interested.
This event is sponsored by the local chapter of the Canadian Association of Retired Persons (CARP).
All are welcome to attend this free event.

Document 13 of 281

Ontario election: Nickel Belt voters feel ignored, abandoned; 'Some of the tidbits they give us are exactly that - tidbits,' says French River Mayor Gisele Pageau

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-election-nickel-belt-voters-feel-ignored/docview/3169666843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Nickel+Belt+voters+feel+ignored%2C+abandoned%3B+%27Some+of+the+tidbits+they+give+us+are+exactly+that+-+tidbits%2C%27+says+French+River+Mayor+Gisele+Pageau&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Jack Lahnalampi sliced his finger on a fillet knife this past fall it led to an appreciation of how other cuts, of the funding variety, are hurting many folks in Nickel Belt.
First, the Onaping resident had to drive down a stretch of Highway 144 that is notorious for its narrow shoulders and dangerous curves, amid Labour Day weekend traffic. "You can't make any time between here and Chelmsford," he said. "It's not worth passing, even when the conditions are good."
Eventually, he got to a walk-in clin icon Lasalle Boulevard-there were no medical options closer to his home -only to discover it was maxed out. "It was open but the parking lot was full and they had a sign saying they weren't taking anyone else in."
Next stop was emerg at Health Sciences North, where the bloodied angler waited a full 10 hours to get his wound addressed (and dressed). "It was about 3 a.m. by the time I got out of there," he said. "Just to get five stitches."
In the interim, he was able to "observe a lot." He saw some people struggling with substance and mental-health issues, and thinly stretched staff scrambling to deal with the crush of patients.
"It was pretty sad," he said. "And I know for some people in health care, it's dangerous for them."
Lahnalampi works as a miner, which of course has its own safety risks. His wife is a high school teacher - a theoretically less hazardous occupation. Except that many teachers these days are also dealing with violent behaviour, along with threats that generally turn out to be baseless but still empty schools and leave everyone on edge.
Education and health care are two of the province's biggest responsibilities, said Lahnalampi, if not the biggest. And both areas, in his view, require more resources, human and otherwise. "Where they should be spending money, they're not."
NICKEL BELT A MASSIVE RIDING Nickel Belt is a massive riding, stretching from French River to Foleyet, on a vertical axis, and from Wahnapitae to Beaver Lake, going from east to west. It straddles the watershed, so many of its rivers flow into James Bay. Its land mass tops that of many states, including Vermont and New Hampshire, although its population is not as dense as those U.S. counterparts, with bears and moose outnumbering humans throughout much of its thickly wooded sprawl.
The exception is the part closest to Greate Sudbury - actually part of the city - that includes Lively, Garson, Hanmer and Azilda. This immediate "belt" is more urban and also counts most of the mining and mining-supply operations that drive the local economy, along with some agriculture and manufacturing.
Threats of tariffs from the U.S. have created a lot of angst around the future of such enterprises, although Mike Parent, who represents the Val Caron/Blezard Valley area as councillor, said he was somewhat comforted by a recent analysis that put Greater Sudbury ahead of 40 other cities in its ability to withstand a trade dispute.
"We're going through uncertain times, but we have a lot of critical minerals," said Parent, who worked as a miner in the past and currently heads up Workplace Safety North. "And I think the province needs to continue to help mining companies exploit those minerals. A lot of the ore bodies are challenging to get at, so ongoing work to get to the Ring of Fire, for instance, should be a priority. We have an asset a lot of the world wants, but it's only an asset if we can access it, and I think there is a role the province can play in that."
Premier Doug Ford paid a visit to the councillor's ward - most of which is part of Nickel Belt - on a chilly day in late 2023 to announce more than $4 million for projects related to mining and EVs in the North, including $2 million for Frontier Lithium in Val Caron. Parent said he'd like to see more investments of that nature, as well more money pumped into transportation infrastructure.
"Highway 69 - finish the damn thing," he said. "I drive the road often enough, as most people do when they travel south, and we should be afforded the same quality of highways that southern Ontario enjoys."
It was more than three years ago now, he noted, that the latest section of four-laning - 14 kilometres in the French River area - was opened to traffic, and there has been no movement since (other than engineering work and behind-the-scenes negotiations with First Nations) on the remaining gap of 68 km.
Closer to home, the councillor said he's working hard to enhance access and amenities for Kalmo Beach, ideally with some provincial support. "It's kind of the jewel of the Valley, and it's not really getting any attention," he said of the Whitson Lake attraction. "So I'll be advocating for funding from all levels of government to improve the beachfront and set it up as a park, with a proper boardwalk and trails."
MAYOR SAYS RIDING BEING IGNORED You don't have to tell French River Mayor Gisele Pageau about the importance of natural attributes and outdoor recreation. Her area depends on tourism and its waterways draw folks from near and far, even at this time of year. "I live right on the river, and every day the snowmobiles are zipping by," she said. "They make more noise than a boat trolling for fish, but it's nice to see because it means there are people coming in our area."
Historically the French River was the dividing line between southern and Northern Ontario. That changed under a previous PC government, when the region grew to include Parry Sound and Muskoka (the latter was subsequently booted out, but not before Gravenhurst got a major makeover to its waterfront, with help from coveted NOHFC funds). More recently French River has experienced a bit of a population boost, as southern Ontarians flee the concrete jungle for a quieter life amid fresh air a jungle of conifers.
"I think the pandemic did that, because people found out they could work from home and living out here is great because we have so much space," said Pageau.
While that influx is welcome, the mayor feels the region is still largely ignored by Queen's Park. "They haven't begun to give any notice to Northern Ontario," she said. "Some of the tidbits they give us are exactly that - tidbits. We're so different from southern Ontario, not just by population, but by needs, and you need a government that will recognize that."
Pageau said her municipality is in a fierce battle to keep its OPP station, which was on the verge of closing a couple of years ago, and to pay for infrastructure projects like a much-needed upgrade to the Noelville sewer system. "It's going to cost over $5 million, and we just don't have the capacity to charge our residents that money," she said.
The mayor blames the fiscal pinch on provincial downloading of services like public health and ambulance costs, which began back in the Mike Harris era, but has continued under the Ford government.
"We don't have any choice not to pay it - every year they give us a bill for about $2 million," she said. "We feel the government should take back what it is supposed to do, because health is a provincial responsibility, and then we could pay for the sewers and repairs on our roads."
RIDING AN NDP STRONGHOLD Nickel Belt has been an NDP bastion for more than half a century now, going back to the early 1970s, when Floyd Laughren wrested the riding from the Progressive Conservatives. France Gelinas was first elected in 2007 and would serve a sixth consecutive term if returned, which seems a strong possibility, although the race appears to be tightening between the popular incumbent and her PC challenger Randy Hazlett.
As of Wednesday, Pollster 338Canada had the NDP at 41 per cent of the vote in the riding, with the Tories just a few points behind at 38 per cent, although it still put Gelinas's odds of winning Nickel Belt at 69 per cent.
City councillor Natalie Labbee has thrown her hat in the ring for the Liberals, while Connie Hill is the Green candidate. There are also two fringe candidates in Paul Divincenzo (New Blue) and James Chretien (Libertarian).
Cost of living has been a dominant theme in this quick campaign, as has health care, with the leaders of both the NDP and Liberals highlighting a shortage of family physicians and putting forward their plans to address the problem.
It's certainly an issue in Nickel Belt, where many smaller communities don't have a single doctor, and even the larger ones have too few to adequately serve the people who live there.
"The Valley lost two doctors in a very short time and left 4,000 or 4,500 patients stranded within a couple of weeks," said Parent. "So there is definitely a need for more physicians."
Amanda Rainville, director of the Nurse Practitioner-Led Clinic in Capreol, said the services they offer are the only option people have to access primary care in the community.
"A doctor had retired just as we were coming on 13 years ago, and nobody has opened up shop since in Capreol," she said. "I think there's one other provider in Hanmer but she's at full capacity."
There are four nurse practitioners working out of the Capreol clinic, including Rainville herself, along with registered nurses and RPNs, a social worker and a dietitian, but even a team of this size is not adequate to meet the demand.
"We have about 800 people on our wait list and we can't intake any new patients," she said. "We are over capacity."
The clinic got some funds from the province a year ago but this was only for its operational budget and did not cover any staffing. "It's not continuous funding - just one-time," noted Rainville. "Four or five years back, we did receive compensation increases, but we haven't received anything since then."
She said the clinic would like to add two more NPs, as well as an extra registered nurse and social worker, plus a receptionist, in order to take on another 1,600 patients (it currently serves 3,200), but so far its application for provincial funding has been denied.
If she has one recommendation for whichever party forms the next government at Queen's Park, it would be to "invest in primary care," including not just nurse practitioners but also "physicians, nurses, dieticians, social workers, receptionists."
Those who work in clinics are underpaid compared to other settings, like hospitals and schools, she said, so a boost in remuneration would help keep these key points of first contact for patients adequately staffed, and the province should also continue to increase the number of seats available at universities for nurse practitioner students.
"I think they need to expand on existing teams, because it's been shown that team-based care is best for patients," she said. "Putting money towards the people who are already working here is really important, so we can retain them, and then trying to expand the number of physicians, so we are able to meet the goal of every Ontarian having access to a primary-care provider."
jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Rivers; Roads & highways; Elections

Document 14 of 281

Ontario election: In Sudbury, unions talk about long-term care

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-election-sudbury-unions-talk-about-long/docview/3169666813/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+In+Sudbury%2C+unions+talk+about+long-term+care&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Union of Public Employees says the Feb. 27 provincial election should also be about the current and future state of longterm care in Ontario.
The system of caring for those who can't care for themselves is experiencing further broken promises and chronic under-staffing, the union charged during a press conference in Sudbury.
The situation is not likely to improve based on today's funding priorities, the inability to attract, train and retain a skilled workforce, plus the poor pay for long hours, CUPE.
Registered practical nurse Jason Harasymchuk, speaking on behalf of CUPE 1182, implored voters to take a look at the track record in long-term care. In a release, Harasymchuk said the Ford government has failed to "make care and the lives of vulnerable residents better."
Some years ago, there was a legislated (but not implemented) commitment to four hours of care per day for each resident in longterm care, or LTC. Harasymchuk said what actually happens falls far short of that target.
Making things worse are the triple challenges of a growing demographic, reduced staff and bed shortages, said Harasymchuk, who has worked as an RPN for 13 years Like Ontario's hospitals - but magnified - is the mismatch of beds to a population of people 75 and older that has increased by 21 per cent from 2018 to 2023, the union said.
CUPE said the province's bed capacity has not kept up, adding Ontario is the province that spends the least on health care.
In a previous election, the Ontario PCs promised to "increase beds by 30,000 by 2028 and 15,000 by 2023/24," CUPE said. "But in their own 2024-2025 budget they reported that only 2,246 new beds have opened ... that amounts to a three per cent increase in the total number of LTC beds."
Harasymchuk said that in Sudbury, the fact Extendicare Countryside cannot accept new patients is putting an added strain on other facilities and their staff.
Countryside remains under an ongoing Ministry of Long-Term Care stop admissions order cannot admit new residents until care there improves and the order is lifted.
"After all that COVID has taught us about the gaps in care and staffing in LTC, Doug Ford promised to fix things," Harasymchuk said. "He's failed to deliver on this promise. How a government treats our most vulnerable and the workers who provide care for them, says a lot about their priorities."
Harasymchuk and his colleagues gathered at the intersection of York and Regent streets in the shadow of York Extendicare. Extendicare is a for-profit company based in Markham that operates 122 long-term care homes across Ontario, Manitoba and Alberta, providing 24-7 care and support to more than 17,000 residents. Harasymchuk also spoke of a pay system that is damaged and how this creates instability in the workers' lives. He said this industry largely exploits female workers, "without any plan to deal with overtime.
"Extra shifts and understaffing (are) rampant. Work is becoming increasingly part-time and casual with impacts resident care in an adverse way," he said.

Subject: Workforce planning; Provincial elections; Long term health care

Document 15 of 281

More Doctors, Safer Highways NDP Priorities; Ontario Election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-doctors-safer-highways-ndp-priorities/docview/3168908326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+Doctors%2C+Safer+Highways+NDP+Priorities%3B+Ontario+Election&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Note: The Sudbury Star has given candidates running in the Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings the chance to submit a column about why they are running and their priorities if elected.
FRANCE GELINAS My name is France Gelinas and I am running in the provincial election to represent the good people of Nickel Belt. I have been the Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) for Nickel Belt since the 2007 election.
I am a physiotherapist by profession, I worked in hospitals for over a decade. After doing a master's degree in business administration (MBA) at Laurentian University I started administrative work. I left the hospital sector for the community health sector. Before entering provincial politics, I was the executive director of the Community Health Centre in Sudbury. We focused on serving the francophone community in Greater Sudbury and surrounding communities.
The values I learned in the community health movement have shaped my focus for my time as the MPP for Nickel Belt. Respect, integrity, collaboration and accountability are the values my team and I have used to serve constituents and represent them at Queen's Park.
As the MPP for Nickel Belt, I have focused a lot on bringing equitable access to health care services to all the residents. There are currently 30,000 people in Sudbury and Nickel Belt that don't have access to a family doctor or nurse practitioner to handle their primary health care needs. This leads directly into the backlog at our hospital emergency room. Sick people that have no family physician end up waiting for hours at "emerg" because there is no other place for them to access health services.
This is part of the Ford government's agenda; underfund the public health care services so badly that people will be willing to try anything. For the Ford government. that means private for-profit health care services. It doesn't have to be this way. There are health-care organizations across Nickel Belt that would love to take on more patients if the government funded them; but those funding applications aren't approved or even acknowledged.
Nickel Belt is the seventh largest riding in Ontario. As the MPP, I drive to every corner of my riding. Complaints about winter road maintenance are top of mind for a lot of my constituents. We use these roads to shop, bring kids to school or simply head to work.
Unfortunately, the private contractors tasked to clear these roads are sometimes happier to risk a fine as punishment rather than follow the contracts they signed. It's wrong to have a private company thinking about their profits before road users' safety. It's time to put safety first and make the province responsible for winter road maintenance where contractors fail.
It has been over two years since the OPP investigation into untrained commercial vehicle drivers in this province but there is no evidence that anything has been done. My fellow NDP members and I have repeatedly asked the government at Queen's Park to make changes to driver training but our pleas have fallen on deaf ears. We need a government that takes highway safety seriously and I hope to be a part of that government.
The reason I'm running to be the MPP for Nickel Belt is simple: I enjoy helping people. Since I was first elected it has been my priority to listen to my constituents and try to help them, their family or their business. Whether it be an all-candidates debate, lunch at a seniors' club or at community events, I'm there, serving the people of Nickel Belt. It is an honour that I hope to continue to hold.
Nickel Belt deserves a representative that puts residents and their needs first, makes life more affordable, protects our public health-care system, makes our roads safer and fix our schools. I hope you'll choose me. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca MORE INFO France Gelinas has held Nickel Belt since 2007. Challenging her this election are Natalie Labbee (Liberal), Randy Hazlett (PC), James Chretien (Libertarians) and Paul DiVincenzo (New Blue Party). Ontario goes to the polls on Feb. 27.

Subject: Public health; Provincial elections; Health care; Roads & highways

Location: France

Title: More Doctors, Safer Highways NDP Priorities; Ontario Election

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.
