Document 37 of 281

Serre, Lapointe, the Liberals remain Sudbury area's best choices

Author: Arkilander, Alan

Publication info: Arkilander, Alan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/serre-lapointe-liberals-remain-sudbury-areas-best/docview/3165591607/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Serre%2C+Lapointe%2C+the+Liberals+remain+Sudbury+area%27s+best+choices&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Arkilander%2C+Alan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have been following the spate of letters to the editor arising due to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation as Liberal party leader and the prospect of an election this year.
There is no doubt that Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre was riding high in the polls and definitely was in majority government territory if the election had been held last fall.
As the Toronto Star's Susan Delacourt wrote recently, Poilievre's "anti-Trudeau campaign lacks a villain" now.
Poilievre will be facing a new Liberal leader who will discontinue the carbon tax and not proceed with changes to the capital gains tax.
If Mark Carney is chosen, we will have the former Bank of Canada governor, who kept us out of trouble in 2008 during Harper's years and later saved England's economy after Brexit. Carney is what I would call fiscally responsible, which many do not associate with this Liberal government.
Poilievre now calling the next Liberal leader "Carbon Tax Carney" will not get him far.
I was amused to read Dr. Ian Symington's letter wherein he, like Poilievre, misrepresents Liberal policies while offering no constructive alternatives, except for catchy phrases like "Axe the Tax", "Stop the Crime", and " Build the Homes".
(Symington is the Conservative candidate for the Sudbury riding.)
If you look at Poilievre's political career, it is not notable except he can retire now with a multi-million dollar pension. Other areas of concern are that Poilievre will likely cancel Canada's national child care system, cancel the new dental program and other social programs in the name of cutting costs and balancing the budget.
Also, we will likely face a move to return the retirement age, as it was under former Conservative Prime Minister Stephen Harper, to 67 years.
Locally another writer relies on a program called 338 CANADA to say the polls in Nickel Belt and Sudbury show our Liberal members of parliament, Marc Serre (Nickel Belt) and Viviane Lapointe (Sudbury), in third place with Conservatives likely to replace them.
I reviewed the website and discovered these are not polls. It is the creation of a Quebec political columnist where he projects electoral results based on opinion polls, electoral history and demographic data.
First, there have been no local published opinion polls; secondly, history indicates that a Conservative has never represented Nickel Belt or Sudbury since their creation.
There have been times when an NDP member has represented either riding, but otherwise, our representatives have been Liberal. The demographics also indicate that being bilingual is important.
Our sitting members have made significant contribu- tions to this part of northeastern Ontario and I believe their hard work will be recognized at the ballot box.
In terms of leaders, with Trudeau resigning, Poilievre will be facing a new leader with a new vision. What I recall most about Poilievre, besides his name-calling, is his delivering coffee and donuts to the Freedom Convoy occupiers that February in Ottawa.
Also, Poilievre continues to support that movement led by insurrectionists requiring that Trudeau be fired and having the Governor General replace the government with an unelected one.
Locally, we see them every Saturday in the parking lot across from Bell Park waving "F... Trudeau" and "Vote Trump" banners.
Presumably, 338 Canada did not include that in its demographics.
Again, in terms of history, when former Prime Minister Brian Maloney crushed the Liberals in the 1984 federal election, Sudbury and Nickel Belt did not elect any Conservatives despite Mulroney's popularity.
Viviane Lapointe and Marc Serre are still the best choices to represent us. Alan Arkilander is a Sudbury resident and lawyer.

Subject: Conservatism; Prime ministers; Elections; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Document 38 of 281

Choudhry to run for the Liberals in Sudbury; Ontario election Four people now running in the riding, now held by Jamie West for the NDP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choudhry-run-liberals-sudbury-ontario-election/docview/3165591600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Choudhry+to+run+for+the+Liberals+in+Sudbury%3B+Ontario+election+Four+people+now+running+in+the+riding%2C+now+held+by+Jamie+West+for+the+NDP&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals now have a candidate to run in the Sudbury riding for the provincial election on Feb. 27.
Rashid Mukhtar Choudhry, a local businessman, joins a race that already includes incumbent Jamie West for the NDP, Max Massimiliano for the Progressive Conservatives, David Robinson for the Green Party and David Sylvestre for the None of the Above Party.
Sylvestre, however, is not yet listed on the Elections Ontario website.
The Liberals have not formally announced that Choudhry is running, but he is listed as their Sudbury candidate on the party's website.
In Nickel Belt, three candidates are confirmed to run. France Gelinas is seeking to hold onto the seat for the NDP, while Liberal Natalie Labbee and Progressive Conservative Randy Hazlett hope to unseat her.
Speaking of Gelinas, the NDP candidate has opened her campaign office, located at 3030 Hwy 69 North in Val Caron, formerly the M&M Food Market.
Gelinas invites voters to drop in or call 705-588-2358 or 1-844588-235. ROBINSON TO RUN FOR THE GREEN PARTY IN SUDBURY David Robinson will once carry the Green Party's colours in a provincial election.
Robinson, an economist and Laurentian University professor, announced Thursday he will run for the Greens in the Sudbury riding.
Ontario voters go to the polls on Feb. 27.
In a release, Robinson said only the Green Party can effectively deal with climate change. "When we deal with the top-of-mind issues, we have to push toward climate adaptation," he said. "Shortrun panic will waste time and give us half-baked solutions. Even the tariff kerfuffle gives us chances to build a better, safer Canada."
The release said Robinson, who has run provincially and federally for the Greens in Sudbury in past elections without success, is "an economist who has made important contributions to the Sudbury economy.
"He identified and promoted the Northern Ontario Mining Supply and Service sector and proposed the Northern Ontario School of Architecture. He is a leading expert and teacher on Northern Ontario's economic development," the release said.
"No other candidate understands the economy and how governments work like David does," Green spokesperson Tree Rost said. "No other candidate comes close to understanding the climate crisis like he does."
Besides climate change, Robinson touched on a number of other issues in announcing he was going to run again. "The last three provincial governments created the housing crisis," he said. "Housing is a provincial responsibility. They abandoned Canada's historic public and social housing programs. Now, people are paying through the nose with rising prices and housing shortages. The Green Party has a plan that will work.
"This election is not about tariffs - it is happening because (Premier) Doug Ford doesn't want to face the music of his failure on every major portfolio from healthcare and housing to municipal taxes and dollar-a-bottle beer.'' Ford has said he called the election earlier than required despite having a majority government because he wants a strong mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump and his threats to impose tariffs on Canadian goods.
Star Staff sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 40 of 281

Ontario election: Washington-bound Ford talks tough on China

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-washington-bound-ford-talks/docview/3165450144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Washington-bound+Ford+talks+tough+on+China&amp;title=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 42 of 281

Situation at Sudbury's hospital frightens veteran health-care worker; Union leaders say Health Sciences North needs $22 million and 54 new beds 'right now' to end hallway medicine and a jammed ER

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/situation-at-sudburys-hospital-frightens-veteran/docview/3164731620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Situation+at+Sudbury%27s+hospital+frightens+veteran+health-care+worker%3B+Union+leaders+say+Health+Sciences+North+needs+%2422+million+and+54+new+beds+%27right+now%27+to+end+hallway+medicine+and+a+jammed+ER&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Suzanne Pinel-Asselin has worked in Sudbury's health-care system for more than three decades. She says what is happening today at Sudbury's hospital - and hospitals across Ontario - frightens her.
She spoke out Wednesday during a stop in Sudbury by the Ontario Council of Hospital Unions and the Canadian Union of Public Employees, who are working to make health care a central issue in Ontario's provincial election on Feb. 27.
The union groups say Premier Doug Ford has failed to end socalled hallway medicine in Ontario, which he promised in 2018. If anything, the situation has gotten worse, union leaders claim.
"What is happening is a huge concern," said Pinel-Asselin, who works in medical device reprocessing at Sudbury's Health Sciences North. "We are responsible for all surgical items, including those used in the operating rooms.
"We are a team of about 50 or 60. We are a 24-hour operation. We are seeing the backup (of patients). We know there is a crisis. I've been around for 34 years - since my late teens - so I have seen change.
"What is going on now is frightening," she said. "It is frightening for our seniors, for Generation X, for the younger people. Our wait times are long, our outcomes are worse.
"We need to see the provincial government step up and fund this the way they should."
Standing outside on Paris Street where Health Sciences North meets the Public Health Sudbury and Districts building, Doug Allan shared data on beds, wait times and what he called the empty promises of the current Progressive Conservative government.
'At Health Science North, there is a $22-million revenue shortfall and for North Bay, it is $10.2 million," said Allen, a Canadian Union of Public Employees researcher. "This hospital (HSN) is in an over seven per cent negative operating margin in the first half of the year.
Allan said an 85 per cent occupancy for any hospital is safe, but added HSN has particular challenges and simply doesn't have enough room. "HSN needs 54 extra beds right now," he said.
Allan said the backup of patients starts in the emergency room. "Across the province, it is a 20hour wait, but here in Sudbury, it is 27.3 hours. You enter and you have to wait 27.3 hours on average to get admitted ... that's more than three times the target of eight hours."
Allen said the unions do not blame HSN officials for the situation. "Ford ran on a promise to end hallway medicine in 2018. He doesn't talk about that any more," he said.
"Now we are at 1,860 patients per day - across the province - in hallways. We rented some stretchers today to make a point. We are here today to demonstrate the breadth of the crisis. There is an $800-million shortfall in the first half of the fiscal year" in terms of funding for Ontario's hospitals.
"We are 5,000 beds short across the province just to bring us back to where we were in 2005," Allen said, adding that an aging population coupled with population growth is putting pressure on Ontario's hospitals to cope.
Michael Hurley, president of the Ontario Council of Hospital Unions, said the union wants to make sure health care is a top-ofmind issue for Ontario's voters. "We are here to make sure in the shadow of possible (American) tariffs that the crisis in health care gets some prominence. The people in this building (HSN) are very concerned. It is operating well over capacity for a long time.
"There used to be other fully functioning hospitals (in Sudbury, which have since closed) ... One was down the road, and the other over there. They closed and consolidated.
"Now in Ontario on an average day, there are about 2,000 people on stretchers who can't get beds. These are people who are so sick ... but there is no bed."
Hurley was referring to the former General Hospital on Paris Street and the former Memorial Hospital on Regent Street, which were amalgamated with the former Laurentian Hospital in 1997. The Laurentian hospital site was expanded and now houses Health Sciences North.
Hurley said hospitals across Ontario lack beds, staff, resources and funding, including in Thessalon, for example, where all the acute care beds there have been closed.
"There are a quarter of a million people on lists for surgeries across Ontario," he said. "Many have waited beyond the medically recommended time for that surgery. And there are 2.5 million people without a family doctor, plus 50,000 people waiting for long-term care beds ... which we don't have."
Hurley and his colleagues are visiting two communities a day over 30 days to bring their message home during the provincial election.
He said Ontario needs more registered nurses, nurses and PSWs, but there is "no coherent" plan to recruit such staff. He called on all the parties to lay out a vision for better health care in Ontario.
"What we haven't heard from the parties is what kind of investments they are contemplating that would honour this commitment of ending hallway medicine and honouring the commitment that there should be, to each of us, if there is a surgery we are going to get it in a timely way. That's a normal expectation in a fairly wealthy society. We want detailed policies from the parties."
In an email, the Progressive Conservatives defended their record. "Facts matter and our PC team has made record investments in our healthcare system, investing $85 billion this year alone while getting shovels in the ground for over 50 hospital redevelopment projects, building on the 3,500 new beds we have added across the province since 2018, more beds than the Liberals built in over 10 years," the email said.
"At Health Sciences North, we have opened a new 52-bed Acute and Reactivation Care Centre, supported their expansion project with a $5 million planning grant, and increased their funding by nearly $150 million since 2018 - investments the NDP and Liberals have not supported.
"Sudbury deserves a strong government that will continue to make record investments in our healthcare system, to connect families to the care they need, when they need it and that is exactly what the Ontario PCs will deliver."
As for Health Science North, it said in an email that the hospital "continues to work closely with Ontario Health and the provincial government in addressing our capacity challenges."
Over the years, HSN leaders have said Sudbury needs more beds and that they were working with the province to deliver them.
The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government.
sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 43 of 281

Canadian pride grew this winter as tariff threats surged: poll; 44 per cent of respondents described themselves as 'very proud' Canadians

Author: Knight, Chris

Publication info: Knight, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-pride-grew-this-winter-as-tariff-threats/docview/3164143845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+pride+grew+this+winter+as+tariff+threats+surged%3A+poll%3B+44+per+cent+of+respondents+described+themselves+as+%27very+proud%27+Canadians&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Knight%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ifthere's asilver lining to be found in the recent threat of an all-out trade war between the United States and Canada, it would seem to be a resurgence of pride among Canadians. That has manifested itself in positive ways, including buy-Canadian initiatives, as well as negative acts, such as the booing of the U.S. national anthem at sporting events.
On Wednesday, Montreal's Fairmount Bagel started selling a "Canada Strong" variety of the popular baked good. In Ottawa, the makers of the "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat worn by Ontario Premier Doug Ford during a meeting of Canada's premiers has seen a huge jump in sales, while the hat's motto has become a social media hashtag.
Meanwhile, a moribund website, madeinca.ca has seen a spike in traffic as Canadian producers and consumers rush to advertise and locate locally manufactured products. The site now includes a grocery shopping guide.
Loblaw, Canada's largest grocery retailer, announced Sunday that it would try to leapfrog American purchases and buy from Mexico instead.
"Where we have had to purchase products made in the U.S., we will look for alternatives including products from Mexico which has also been subjected to these unnecessary tariffs," Loblaw CEO Per Bank said, adding that the U.S. decision was "wrong-headed."
On the social media site Reddit, the BuyCanadian forum, introduced in 2018 during another round of Trump trade tariffs, is seeing more comments as well.
And there are polling numbers to prove it. A recent survey by the Angus Reid Institute found that, between last December and this month, the number of Canadians describing themselves as "very proud" rose to 44 per cent from 34 per cent.
In the same period, those who said they had a "deep emotional attachment" to Canada rose by a similar amount, to 59 per cent in February from 49 per cent in December. And the number of people who thought Canada should join the United States fell to just four per cent, from an already meagre six per cent in December.
The swelling pride was not evenly distributed across the country, however. The poll found that the biggest increases were in British Columbia, Quebec and the Maritimes, which all saw rises of between 12 and 15 per cent on the "proud or very proud" and "deep emotional attachment" questions.
Smaller gains of between 3 and 9 per cent were seen in Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, while Manitoba alone saw a slight drop of about 3 per cent.
The pollster pointed out that the shift to greater pride in Canada also bucks a trend that has seen pride fall gradually over the last several decades. "Deep emotional attachment" was cited by 65 per cent of respondents in a 1991 Angus Reid poll, falling to 62 per cent by 2016 and just 49 per cent last year, before the recent rebound.
A variety of polls has shown the number of people who are "very proud" to be Canadian has fall- en slowly but steadily since the 1980s.
While it's impossible to say for certain whether Donald Trump's threats of tariffs and continued talk of annexation are the reason for Canadians' suddenly glowing hearts, the poll did find that the tariff issue was the most closely followed major news event since the onset of the COVID pandemic in 2020.
Fully 56 per cent of respondents said they were following news of tariffs very closely, while another 33 per cent reported following them closely, for a total of 89 per cent. This topped other recent events such as the last federal budget (62 per cent), the Israel-Hamas War (80 per cent), Russia's invasion of Ukraine (66 per cent) and the WE Charity scandal (73 per cent).
One has to go back to February of 2020 to find higher numbers, when 90 per cent were following the emergence of COVID-19 closely or very closely.
There is fear as well as general interest in the news of possible tariffs, with 3 per cent of respondents saying they expect to lose their job if tariffs are implemented, and a further 30 per cent saying they're concerned about what it would mean for their jobs.
The new poll was conducted online from Feb. 2 to Feb. 3, and carries a margin of error of plus or minus 2 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.

Document 44 of 281

Sudbury PC candidate says 'we need a seat' in government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-pc-candidate-says-we-need-seat-government/docview/3164143811/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+PC+candidate+says+%27we+need+a+seat%27+in+government&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Sudbury businessman running for the Progressive Conservatives in the Feb. 27 provincial election says the riding needs 'a voice at the table' if it wants to progress.
Max Massimiliano talked about why he's running as he opened his campaign headquarters on The Kingsway on Wednesday.
A father of three, Massimiliano co-owns Don's Pizza on Lorne Street and is "a proud first-generation son of Italian immigrants."
"I decided to run because we need a voice at the table with the party in power and I feel like the Sudbury area has been missing out on an immense amount of provincial funding that's gone to other Northern communities ... North Bay, Ste. Marie and Timmins have prospered over the years and we need a seat at that table."
The Progressive Conservatives now hold the North Bay, Ste. Marie and Timmins in northeastern Ontario. Two of those MPs - Vic Fedeli (North Bay) and George Pirie (Timmins) served in Premier Doug Ford's most recent cabinet.
The Sudbury riding is now held by the NDP's Jamie West, who has served two terms as MPP and was his party's Labour critic.
West is running again as is Dave Sylvestre for the None of the Above Party. The Liberals and Greens do not yet have a candidate in Sudbury.
The Tories last held the Sudbury riding when Jim Gordon, a popular former mayor, served two terms from 1981 to 1987.
Premier Doug Ford, who has called an election early (he didn't need to go to the polls until 2026), is hoping to win a third majority government. The PC's currently have 79 members in the provincial parliament, followed by the NDP at 28 and the Liberals at nine. There are six Independents and two Green party members.
I decided to run because we need a voice at the table with the party in power. The polls this time around suggest Ford will easily win the next election and form another majority government.
Ford said he called the election because he needs a strong mandate to deal with tariffs that American president Donald Trump is threatening to impose on Canada.
When asked about his priorities for Sudbury if elected, Massimiliano was not specific. "I think that there's many priorities for the city that need to be addressed and I'm just doing this for the whole of Sudbury, for all the constituents, for all Sudbury to make sure that it's a better place for myself, my kids and my parents to stay and live in," he said.
In an email sent by his campaign team, Massimiliano said he's "passionate about Sudbury's future, ensuring that our economy remains strong, our industries thrive, and the people who power them have the opportunities they deserve."
"He is genuinely proud of our hometown," the email added. "A dedicated father of three daughters, Max is actively involved in the community, coaching youth soccer and always supporting local initiatives through fundraising efforts."
PROVIN CIAL ALL- CANDIDATES MEETING FEB. 22 Voters in Sudbury and Nickel Belt will have the opportunity to see and hear candidates for both provincial ridings at a special meeting on Feb. 22 at the Canadian Legion branch 76, 1553 Weller St. in Minnow Lake. Liberal, Progressive Conservative, NDP and Green candidates have been invited to the event, which will begin at 1:30 p.m.
Candidates representing the major parties will have the opportunity for opening and closing remarks, and to answer questions from attendees. Other parties will have a table for material and can meet with those interested.
This event is sponsored by the local chapter of the Canadian Association of Retired Persons (CARP).
All are welcome to attend this free event.

Document 45 of 281

Cambrian College honours Sudbury's Geo Labs as top employer; College hosts career fair and names employer of distinction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cambrian-college-honours-sudburys-geo-labs-as-top/docview/3164142401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cambrian+College+honours+Sudbury%27s+Geo+Labs+as+top+employer%3B+College+hosts+career+fair+and+names+employer+of+distinction&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Northern Ontario's largest career fair got a little bigger this year. The annual career fair at Cambrian College grew to a full two days of programming.
The fair launched Monday with the day-long Employer Solutions Conference. The expanded programming included workshops; a panel discussion; dinner; and a keynote address from Sari L. Springer, founder and managing director at PembertonLane PC. Spring is an expert in human rights and employment law.
On Tuesday, thousands of students met with more than 130 employers on the fair floor. It was an opportunity for students to network and apply for that important first position in their chosen fields.
During the employer breakfast, Cambrian named this year's recipient of its annual Employer Award of Distinction. This award recognizes an organization, company or individual who has demonstrated exemplary support for Cambrian, its students and graduates.
The 2025 award was presented to Geoscience Laboratories (Geo Labs). Located in Sudbury as part of the Ontario Geological Survey, Geo Labs offers a comprehensive range of inorganic chemical and mineralogical services and analysis to clients in government, academia and the private sector. The data produced by Geo Labs is used for use planning and mineral exploration.
"We are very honoured to receive this award," Mario Paventi, manager of Geo Labs, said in a release. "We have a very strong partnership with Cambrian through offering co-ops and other learning opportunities to students, in addition to hiring Cambrian graduates from programs such as chemical engineering, mining engineering and environmental technician.
"On average, we hire six students or graduates a year for various roles. One-third of our fulltime technologists are Cambrian graduates, and over the past five years, all our full-time new recruits have come from Cambrian College programs. This underscores our dedication to nurturing talent and fostering long-term professional relationships with Cambrian College graduates."
Hadi Fergani, co-ordinator of Cambrian's chemical engineering programs, said the unwavering support of Geo Labs has "significantly enhanced the educational experience of our students and the overall quality of our program.
"Moreover, the generous donation of analytical equipment from Geo Labs has transformed our laboratory facilities. Their dedication to education, professional development and industry collaboration sets a remarkable example for others to follow."
sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar Star Staff

Document 46 of 281

Sudbury PC candidate says 'we need a seat' in government

Author: STAR11 STAR

Publication info: STAR.

Document 50 of 281

Make Ontario election a vote on health care; Doug Ford says its all about Trump. Don't believe the PC leader's ploy; Make Ontario election a vote on health care; Doug Ford says its all about Trump. Don't believe the PC leader's ploy

Author: Mohammed, Adam

Publication info: Mohammed, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/make-ontario-election-vote-on-health-care-doug/docview/3163298095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Make+Ontario+election+a+vote+on+health+care%3B+Doug+Ford+says+its+all+about+Trump.+Don%27t+believe+the+PC+leader%27s+ploy%3B+Make+Ontario+election+a+vote+on+health+care%3B+Doug+Ford+says+its+all+about+Trump.+Don%27t+believe+the+PC+leader%27s+ploy&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Mohammed%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is dragging Ontarians into a needless election in the dead of winter in pursuit of more power. The election call, more than a year ahead of time, is a cynical play for power, and on polling day, Feb. 27, Ontarians should rebuff him.
Ford says he needs a "strong mandate" to deal with the threat posed by U.S. President Donald Trump's plan to impose punishing tariffs on Canada, but he doesn't need any such mandate.
He already has it. In 2022, Ontarians handed him a huge majority, giving the Progressive Conservatives (PCs) 83 of the 124 seats in the legislature. Today, as a result of vacancies and other actions, the PCs have 79 seats, the NDP 28 and Liberals nine. The Green Party has one.
By any measure, that is as strong a mandate as any leader can get, and Ford has such control at Queen's Park, there is little he wants to do that he can't.
Besides, every political leader in Ontario fully understands the dire consequences for the province and Canada if the Trump tariffs are implemented. There is therefore every expectation that all political leaders will come together to take the necessary steps to protect Ontario. Nothing so far suggests otherwise, and there is absolutely no reason to call a snap election to seek a new mandate. Ford is merely exploiting the threat of tariffs to scare Ontarians. We should resist.
But since the election campaign is now fully engaged, we should oblige Ford by turning it into a referendum on his handling on the family-doctor crisis that has left 2.5 million people without primary care, a figure that could rise to more than four million by 2026.
Think of the hundreds of people lining up in bitter cold in Walkerton recently, hoping to find a doctor. It is therefore not surprising that a day before the election call, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced $1.4 billion in new funding over four years to connect two million people with a family doctor or primary-care team.
Of course, the announcement is an election ploy. The crisis has been boiling since Ford was first elected in 2018. The problem has become worse not better under his watch, so just days before the election campaign gets underway, presto, Ford finds more than a billion to throw at the problem. Raise the issue in the campaign, and Ford will point to the money. But there are no guarantees things will actually change.
The fact is, facing a weak opposition, ahead in the polls, and armed with a full war chest, Ford has been angling for this moment, and Donald Trump's tariff threats provided a convenient cover.
Over the next month, you'll hear time and again that Ford needs to get a bigger majority so he'll be in a strong position to face Trump and save Ontario. "I am asking the people for a strong mandate a strong, stable, fouryear mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration," Ford wrote in a recent op-ed.
"President Trump needs to know that for as long as he is president, I will be on the other side of the table ready to protect Ontario."
Don't buy it. It's just talk. The tariff war, if it happens, will not be Ford's to lead. As Ontario premier, he will have significant input into whatever position the feds adopt. But this is a federal responsibility, and you will notice that all the work so far has been done by federal officials in meetings and discussions with counterparts in the Trump administration. Like other provinces, Ontario has been an interested party watching from the outside.
When the time comes to face Trump, the person "on the other side of the table," won't be Ford, but the prime minister of the day.
That will be either the next Liberal leader, whoever that is, or Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Let Ontarians understand that on voting day, when they consider Ford's request for more power.
Mohammed Adam is an Ottawa journalist and commentator. Reach him at nylamiles48@gmail.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Americans+own+too+much+of+Canada%3B+Letters+to+the+Editor%3B+Americans+own+too+much+of+Canada%3B+Letters+to+the+Editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Poisson%2C+Ed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Beer Store in Ontario is owned by the Americans. It was originally owned by Labatts, Molson and Sleeman, but Labatt was bought by Anheuser Bush, Molson was bought by Coors, and Sleeman was bought by Japan's Sapporo. There is a small consortium of 30 companies that own 0.02 per cent of the company.
A few years ago, small breweries in Ontario were complaining to Premier Doug Ford that they could not get their product on the shelves at the Beer Store, so Ford made moves to allow beer to be sold in retail outlets, across the province.
The Beer Store still basically sets the price of beer sold in Ontario and sets fees for Canadian companies to put their products in the Beer Store.
It is amazing the depth that the Americans have gotten into our country, and will take years for us to root them out.

Document 55 of 281

Ford's early election call 'makes no sense:' Northern Ontario analyst; If the Conservatives lose seats, it will weaken premier's argument he needs a strong mandate to deal with U.S. tariffs, David Tabachnick says

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-early-election-call-makes-no-sense-northern/docview/3162490744/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+early+election+call+%27makes+no+sense%3A%27+Northern+Ontario+analyst%3B+If+the+Conservatives+lose+seats%2C+it+will+weaken+premier%27s+argument+he+needs+a+strong+mandate+to+deal+with+U.S.+tariffs%2C+David+Tabachnick+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Few Ontarians, it seems, are warm to the idea of a winter election.
David Tabachnick, a politics professor at Nipissing University, cited a recent poll that found more than three-quarters of eligible voters oppose a trip to the polls in February.
Part of that is likely just the tendency to hibernate at this time of year, he said, even though many people now vote early or by mail and outdoor queues are less common. Mostly, however, he thinks people simply don't buy the rationale being put forward by the Doug Ford government for the early writ drop - namely, that they need a mandate to fight the threat of tariffs from the U.S.
"It makes no sense," said Tabachnik. "Why call an election before we actually know that these tariffs are going to be imposed, or when and by how much? And also why would you want to be distracted by an election campaign for a month when you are supposed to be protecting Ontario?" (According to Associated Press, U.S. President Donald Trump will put in place 25 per cent tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico and 10 per cent tariffs on goods from China effective on Satur- day, the White House said, but it provided no word on whether there would be any exemptions to the measures that could result in swift price increases to U.S. consumers.)
Tabachnik said the snap election may well cost the PCs seats, which would only put them in a weaker position to negotiate on the province's behalf undermining the very case they are making for the election in the first place. "It just seems like a transparent power grab, like they're cynically taking advantage of the poll position they're in," said Tabachnick, adding the party likely also wants to avoid whatever bad news could come out of the RCMP inquiry into the Greenbelt land-swap scandal, whereas in his view "if there is criminality, the voters of Ontario should know about that" before being asked to make a decision on which party to send to Queen's Park.
An election coming ahead of the fixed date is also, however, not a huge surprise. The PCs were telegraphing this long before Donald Trump was elected as American president, through such populist moves as $200 cheques and beer in corner stores, not to mention a flurry of funding announcements.
The premier was on hand for one such event in Lively in October, where he touted a $35-million investment in local water infrastructure, while also deflecting questions from the media about the timing of the next vote - other than to say it wouldn't happen before the end of 2024. (By law, Ontarians don't have to vote until June of 2026.)
"I'm going to leave it with this, I can't help myself," said Ford at the tail end of that October press conference. "Folks, we need a voice down at Queen's Park for Sudbury. Because the guy you have down there is sitting in the corner playing cards ... We need a representative to be sitting at the table, jumping up and down about Sudbury."
The premier was of course referring to NDP member Jamie West as the card player in the wings, while suggesting a Progressive Conservative as his replacement, although whom that might be was not clear at the time.
It is now: Max Massimiliano, owner of Don's Pizzeria on Lorne Street, has been tapped as PC banner-bearer for Sudbury, according to the party's website. He also announced it on his own Facebook page, writing: "NEWS FLASH! I am the newly appointed PC Candidate for the upcoming provincial election! Please vote and support so we can have a strong voice in Queen's Park for Sudbury! It's Sudbury's Time."
In 2022, when Ontarians last went to the polls, the candidate was Marc Despatie, who finished a distant second to West, although his result was an improvement for the party over 2018, when Ford was first elected and Troy Crowder was the local candidate.
Sudbury typically goes either NDP or Liberal, at least in recent decades. Back in the 1980s, however, the riding did elect Jim Gordon, a self-described Red Tory, for two consecutive terms, and the city was also PC blue for a decade or so after the Second World War.
Nickel Belt, where Randy Hazlett has been confirmed again as the PC candidate, has been an NDP bastion for half a century. You have to go back the era of Elvis and The Beatles to find a Conservative (perhaps chosen in reaction to the all those beatniks and hippies threatening to upend society) holding the reins in that riding.
Hazlett placed far behind perennial favourite France Gelinas in 2022, but still, like Despatie, came second - well ahead of the Liberal candidate at the time, Gilles Proulx, and with a greater percentage of the vote than had been the case for the PCs in 2018.
As of press time, the Ontario Liberals had still not announced a candidate for either Sudbury or Nickel Belt and no one from the riding associations could be reached for comment, except that David Farrow, who ran last time for the Grits, confirmed it wasn't his intention to do so this time around.
"I will not be making any further comments on the OLP election campaign in Sudbury," he said in a message to The Star. "I am out of the running and continue to do the best job possible as a trustee and chair of the Rainbow District School Board."
It is also not yet clear who will be representing the Greens locally.
"Our candidate is still finalizing their paperwork," communicated Tree Rost, chief financial officer with the Sudbury Greens. "We'll reach out as soon as we can make that public."
David Robinson ran last time for the Green Party in Sudbury, while Glenys Babcock was the Green candidate in Nickel Belt.
The Greens retained their one seat in Ontario in 2022 in Guelph, home to party leader Mike Schreiner while the Liberals added just one to the seven they had before, with none of the eight being in Northern Ontario.
The Grits did finish second in the popular vote last time around, however, and have since chosen a new leader, in Bonnie Crombie, as have the NDP, in selecting Marit Stiles.
Tabachnik said it will be interesting to see if either these opposition parties are able to make some headway in the short amount of time they have left to get their messages out, and whether this will translate into actual gains or simply divide the vote in a way that benefits the incumbent party.
"I think the real question is how many seats are going to have vote-splitting between the NDP and Liberals and allow the Conservatives to win," he said. "You would expect the Liberals to have a bit of a resurgence after being absolutely wiped out, while the NDP really have trouble ever getting momentum one election after the other, so I think those kind of battles will be going on and the Conservatives can still win with a relatively small vote."
The Green Party has also enjoyed some success in Ontario, he noted, "so that's another splitting in the centre and the left, which I don't see happening so much on the right."
Tories have a strong lead At the same time, it's not so easy these days to slot parties into a firm place on the left-right spectrum, he noted.
"If Bonnie Crombie positions herself and the Liberal party as more centrist and centre-right, that might make a difference, but I really don't know what kind of campaign she's going to run," he said. "If I was Bonnie Crombie, I would definitely try to position myself in that territory, which I think Doug Ford has very successfully taken - he's really been governing as a centrist for the most part, except on a few issues."
The latest polling shows the PCs still have a solid lead but the Liberals closing the gap in many ridings, including in Northern Ontario.
Pollster 338Canada is only anticipating one seat will go to the Grits in the North, however, while projecting the Conservatives will add one to their existing six.
As of Thursday, the pollster had the PCs ahead in the region with 42 per cent of the popular vote, the NDP second at 29 per cent, and the Liberals trailing with 16 per cent.
At present, the NDP holds seven of the region's 13 seats, including both Sudbury and Nickel Belt.
According to the 338Canada data crunching, the Sudbury riding is currently leaning NDP again, but it's a close three-way race, with the incumbent at 34 per cent, the PCs at 30 per cent, and the Liberals at 25 per cent.
It has Nickel Belt more strongly shaded orange, with the NDP at 45 per cent of the popular vote and the Conservatives at 34 per cent. Were the election to be held today, the pollster puts the odds of France Gelinas winning the riding again at 95 per cent.
jmoodie@postmedia.com

Document 56 of 281

'We can't be paralyzed by fear' of tariffs, Sudbury business leaders advised; Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce guest speaker delivers message of optimism despite 'some destructive forces'

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-cant-be-paralyzed-fear-tariffs-sudbury/docview/3162490453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+can%27t+be+paralyzed+by+fear%27+of+tariffs%2C+Sudbury+business+leaders+advised%3B+Greater+Sudbury+Chamber+of+Commerce+guest+speaker+delivers+message+of+optimism+despite+%27some+destructive+forces%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is little doubt the threat of tariffs from American president Donald Trump is making businesspeople in Canada - and Sudbury - a little jittery.
But in a recent talk to the Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce, Jean-François Perrault, the senior vice president and chief economist at Scotiabank, struck a more optimistic tone.
"Inflation is kind of fixed and we are in transition from high interest rates to lower interest rates," Perrault told Sudbury's business leaders. "Those shifts are designed to stimulate growth. I am not saying the economy is in fantastic shape, but we are in the early stages of what should be a better world.
"The challenge is we have some destructive forces. We try to understand what Trump says and how seriously to take some of what he says. What kind of operating environment will we be in for the next few years is unclear.
"Retaliating over tariffs may make things expensive for us, too."
Trump has threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods starting Saturday.
Doug Ford, who is campaigning for re-election as Ontario's premier, posted on X that Canada needs to be ready to implement "a strong and forceful response," with "dollar for dollar" tariffs.
Employment Minister Steven MacKinnon, meanwhile, said Ot- tawa will be ready to hit back in a way Americans would feel should Canada be slapped with tariffs.
"One thing that you can be very sure about is that the Government of Canada is prepared, the Government of Canada will retaliate, the Government of Canada will protect workers throughout this process," he said at an unrelated news conference in Gatineau, Que.
Despite the threat of tariffs, Perrault said in Sudbury that with the right measures, Canada can weather the economic storm they will no doubt create.
While Perrault said Canada's strengths are our resources, innovation is important in improving our efficiencies. "We must not be complacent about our competitiveness. We can't be paralyzed by fear of the next four years. There is lots to be optimistic about. We are a natural resource powerhouse. We need greater investment."
Perrault acknowledged that homes, rent and groceries are expensive - as is fuel and energy - but that "opportunities are not out of reach."
Wealth accumulation over the last eight years by age groups, and especially younger people, has been measurable. Perrault sees the larger picture.
"Optimism? We see it in the economic data. We know the data points to an economy that is not great, but it is not horrible. People make their assessments of their well-being sometimes in very simplistic ways.
"It's not as bad as people think. Wages have been rising. There is no question food bank use is at record highs. Yes, there are folk who are struggling."
During the lunch hosted by the Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce, Perrault laughed heartily when he was called a guru, but clearly, he has the credentials.
"I started off in the public sector," he said.
Before joining Scotiabank, Perrault held prominent roles with the federal government, the Bank of Canada, the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.
During the mix and mingle, Greater Sudbury Mayor Paul Lefebvre highlighted that Sudbury, and the north generally, has the ingredients America needs to fuel its economy.
"There is much talk about critical minerals," Lefebvre said. "Sudbury has a role to play not just in the next four years, but nickel is an essential element for the future. Jobs here are top of mind for me."
Joanne LeBreton, second vice chair of the chamber's executive council, opened the event with a bilingual greeting. While this has been done before, this was the first luncheon that carried our dual languages throughout the talk and question and answer period.
Marc Lavigne, directeur general of Fondation franco-ontarienne, moderated the post-lunch conversation.
Lavigne noted that "we don't always have the opportunity to have a speaker of Jean-François' calibre." Switching agilely between French and English, Lavigne encouraged dialogue that highlighted Perrault's perspectives, insights and forecasts on the economy, financial markets and policy developments.
By the end of the fireside chat, as it was called, Lavigne felt cheered by Perrault's words.
"If Jean-François does not seem too concerned about Trump ... well, I am comforted."
The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government.
- with files from Postmedia sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 59 of 281

Ontario election 2025: What you need to know to vote; The Sudbury and Nickel Belt ridings are now held by the NDP

Author: Warren, Ken

Publication info: Warren, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-election-2025-what-you-need-know-vote/docview/3161646846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+What+you+need+to+know+to+vote%3B+The+Sudbury+and+Nickel+Belt+ridings+are+now+held+by+the+NDP&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Warren%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After several weeks of hints, allegations and things left unsaid, Ontario PC Leader Doug Ford finally made it official Tuesday afternoon, kick-starting the official process for a Feb. 27 election.
For those into ceremony, Ford asked Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont to agree to the election call. That set off the Writs of Election, the formal documents governing Ontario's 124 ridings.
Elections Ontario is the unaffiliated board that oversees all elements of the campaign. Greg Essensa has served as Chief Electoral Officer since 2008.
While the official news arrived Tuesday, the parties have been running campaign-like TV and radio ads for months, trumpeting themselves and bad-mouthing their rivals.
WHY CALL AN ELECTION NOW?
After winning a majority in the previous election on June 2, 2022, Ford and the ruling Progressive Conservatives weren't required to return to the polls until 2026. Ford, however, says he needs a new mandate "a strong, stable, four-year mandate -to outlive the risksposed by U.S. President Donald Trump and his threat to impose 25-per-cent tariffs on all Canadian goods. To maintain a majority, the Conservatives need to win at least 63 seats.
Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, NDP leader Marit Stiles and Green leader Mike Schreiner have all said an election now is an unnecessary waste of money.
On Wednesday, Essensa said the election would cost approximately $189 million.
A voting guide will be mailed to every household in the province between Feb. 6-12.
WHAT HAPPENED IN THE 2022 ELECTION?
The PC's won a resounding 83 of 124 seats, with a popular vote of 40.8 per cent. The NDP claimed 31 seats and 23.7 per cent of the voting share. Interestingly, while the Liberals claimed a higher popular vote than the NDP (23.8 per cent), they won only eight seats.
That election hardly excited the masses. The biggest disappointment of all was the fact that only 44.06 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots - the lowest turnout in Ontario election history. In 2018, when Ford won his first Ontario election, 57 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots.
The vote count will be intriguing, considering that the federal Liberals are currently undergoing their own leadership campaign, which will then be followed be a national election.
WHAT ARE THE NUMBERS IN THE ONTARIO LEGISLATURE NOW?
The PC's currently have 79 members in the provincial parliament, followed by the NDP at 28 and the Liberals at nine. There are six Independents and two Green party members.
WHEN DID THE OPP OSITION PARTIES CHANGE THEIR LEADERS?
The huge PC victory in 2022 spurred changes in the opposition ranks. Stephen Del Duca, who headed the Liberals in that election, has since been replaced by Crombie, a former Mississauga mayor who won the leadership in December 2023. Marit Stiles took over as NDP leader in February 2023, succeeding Andrea Horwath, who headed the party during the 2022 election.
WHAT ARE THE RIDINGS IN THE SUDBURY AREA AND WHO CURRENTLY HOLDS THE SEATS?
Algoma Manitoulin - Michael Mantha, a former NDP MPP now running as an Independent Nickel Belt - France Gelinas, NDP Sudbury - Jamie West, NDP WHO IS RUNNING AGAINST THE SUDBURY AREA'S INCUMBENT MPPS?
Algoma Manitoulin - Bill Rosen- berg for the PCs, Reg Niganobe for the Liberals.
Nickel Belt - Randy Hazlett for the PCs. The Liberals and Greens do not yet have a candidate.
Sudbury - Max Massimiliano for the PCs and Dave Sylvestre for the None of the Above Party. The Liberals and Greens do not yet have a candidate.
WHO IS ELIGIBLE TO VOTE?
Everyone who is 18 years old, lives in Ontario and holds Canadian citizenship. Most eligible voters will receive voter information cards (VIC) in the mail. They are expected to arrive in mailboxes between Feb. 17-22. The VIC includes details about what district to vote in and the site of the local election office. In order to vote, one piece of valid ID is necessary. The Elections Ontario website, www.elections.on.ca, includes information of what ID is acceptable and how to update change of address and other information.
How do I find out where my riding is?
To find your electoral district, enter your postal code in the Elections Ontario Voter Information Service online tool. This handy tool will show you your electoral district name, electoral district number, who the current MPP is, plus maps and more general information.
CAN YOU VOTE WITHOUT A VOTER INFORMATION CARD?
Yes, providing you have proof of residence and have valid ID and show that information to election officials on voting day.
Can you vote by mail? Yes, but in order to accomplish this, you must submit a vote-bymail application before Feb. 21 - available online - and provide ID with home address information.
But no, you can't vote online. HOW SOON CAN SOMEONE VOTE IN ADVANCE?
Residents are eligible to vote in advance at the local election office in their riding until 6 p.m. on Feb. 26. To do so, voters must present ID and fill out special ballot applications at local election offices. Voters can also go to the advance polling station in their riding between Feb. 20-22.
Is it possible to vote at home? Yes, in rare circumstances involving medical necessities. Voters must contact local election offices to explain the circumstances. If approved, two election officials will bring a voting kit to your home.
kwarren@postmedia.com

Document 60 of 281

Haven't got your $200 Ontario cheque yet? Don't panic

Author: Juha, Jonathan

Publication info: Juha, Jonathan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/havent-got-your-200-ontario-cheque-yet-dont-panic/docview/3161956846/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Hundreds of thousands of Ontarians have received their "taxpayer rebate," a $200 cheque promised last fall by Premier Doug Ford. If you haven't yet, don't sweat it, it's likely on the way. Here's what you need to know:

Identifier / keyword: hundreds; thousands; ontarians; received; taxpayer; rebate

Document 61 of 281

Chris Selley: Dangers are lurking in Doug Ford's Ontario election experiment

Author: Selley, Chris

Publication info: Selley, Chris.

Document 71 of 281

Province to open addiction treatment hub in Greater Sudbury by April; Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs are the province's response to the drug crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-open-addiction-treatment-hub-greater/docview/3160670788/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+open+addiction+treatment+hub+in+Greater+Sudbury+by+April%3B+Homelessness+and+Addiction+Recovery+Treatment+%28HART%29+hubs+are+the+province%27s+response+to+the+drug+crisis&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: STAR STAFF Greater Sudbury is getting a facility to treat people struggling with addiction and mental health issues.
Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced Monday the provincial government will spend $529 million to create 27 new socalled HART hubs across the province, including one in Sudbury that will be open by the spring.
Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs are the province's response to the drug crisis in Sudbury and across Ontario. Sudbury has one of the highest rates of opioid overdose deaths in the province.
The City of Greater Sudbury had funded a safe consumption site in 2023 that also offered treatment, but the province refused to fund it, so it closed early last year.
The new hub in Sudbury is part of the province's plan to support safer communities while also banning drug injection sites from operating within 200 metres of schools and licensed child-care centres.
Sudbury's HART hub will reflect regional priorities by connecting people with complex needs to comprehensive treatment and preventative services, the province said.
Planning efforts to create the HART Hub in Greater Sudbury are underway and the proposed services to be offered could include primary care; mental health and addictions services, including assessment and system navigation; mental health and addictions supportive housing; social and employment services; and services to meet basic needs.
"Our government is helping more people get the treatment and support they need, when and where they need it, by taking the next step to improve access to mental health and addictions services," Jones said in a release.
"Our investment to create a total of 27 new HART Hubs will keep communities safe and ensure timely and comprehensive access to mental health support, addiction care, primary care, supportive housing, and employment services, no matter where you live."
The 18 new HART Hub locations, including in Greater Sudbury, were chosen as a result of a provincewide call for proposals last summer. All HART Hubs will have the goal of being operational by April 1.
Earlier this year, the province announced that nine drug injection sites in Toronto, Ottawa, Hamilton, Kitchener, Guelph and Thunder Bay that are required to close due to being located within 200 metres of a school or licensed child-care centres have been approved for transition to a HART Hub. This brings the total number of HART Hubs across the province to 27, eight more than initially planned.
"We are investing more than any government in Ontario's history to create a nation-leading system of mental health and addictions care," said Michael Tibollo, associate minister of Mental Health and Addictions. "The new HART Hubs will give people struggling with addiction the support and treatment services they need to achieve lasting recovery."
The province said that with a focus on treatment and recovery, HART Hubs will not offer so-called safer supply, supervised drug consumption or needle exchange programs.
Through the Community Care and Recovery Act, the province has also prohibited municipalities from applying to Health Canada for an exemption for the decriminalization of drugs. The Act also prohibits municipalities from applying to Health Canada for funding or entering into an agreement with Health Canada to run safer supply services or applying for an exemption or renewal of an exemption to operate a supervised consumption site without prior provincial approval.
Greater Sudbury Mayor Paul Lefebvre said he is confident that Sudbury's HART Hub will make the community safer.
"This HART Hub funding demonstrates the province's commitment to helping us tackle the serious mental health and addictions challenges facing municipalities like Greater Sudbury," Lefebvre said. "This partnership bolsters my confidence in the success of transformative projects like the Lorraine Street Transitional Housing development.
"Together, we are not just addressing immediate needs - we are building sustainable solutions that will change lives and create a stronger, healthier Greater Sudbury. On behalf of the city, I want to thank Premier Doug Ford and the Government of Ontario for this much-needed funding. I am optimistic about the positive outcomes this collaboration will bring for individuals and families across our community." sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca

Document 73 of 281

Strong mandate needed to protect Ontario

Author: Ford, Doug

Publication info: Ford, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strong-mandate-needed-protect-ontario/docview/3160670625/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strong+mandate+needed+to+protect+Ontario&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Ford%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President Donald Trump is threatening steep and sweeping tariffs that would devastate Ontario's economy, putting 450,000 jobs on the line - threatening the livelihoods of workers on factory floors, construction sites and in local stores across the province and the families and communities that depend on them.
We need to be clear-eyed about the risk. Whether he imposes tariffs next week, next month or waits a year, President Trump's threats are not going away and are already taking a toll on our economy. He's promised to use economic force to get his way. He's even mused about taking over Canadian territory.
Canada is not for sale. Not to President Trump, not to anyone.
At a time when the federal government has left Canada drifting, exposed and vulnerable, President Trump will use every day he's in office to exploit any weakness he can find. To protect Ontario, I'm asking the people for a strong mandate - a strong, stable, four-year mandate that will outlive and outlast the Trump administration. President Trump needs to know that for as long as he is president, I will be on the other side of the table ready to protect Ontario.
We're entering a period of unprecedented economic risk and lengthy negotiations - against President Trump and with the federal government and other provinces. Responding to this challenge will demand extraordinary action: the need to invest tens of billions of dollars in unplanned spending and make tough choices to ensure Ontario's economy is the most competitive place to invest, create jobs and do business. This includes cutting red tape and streamlining approvals to get big things built faster. I'm prepared to make those choices. First, I need your support. The kind of action required to respond to President Trump's threats will impact the province's fiscal plan and our path to balance. As taxpayers, this is your money. Ontario is your province and this is your future. Your voice should be heard. At a time like this, in a democracy like ours, no politician should be against letting the people have their say.
With the trust and support of Ontario voters, we'll be prepared to give this everything we've got.
I'll continue to make the case to our American allies that we're so much stronger together. I will also be ready to do whatever is necessary to stand up for and protect Ontario, our workers, businesses and communities.
Make no mistake: Ontario won't start a fight with the U.S., but you better believe we'll be ready to win one.
Over the past seven years, together with workers, businesses and union partners, we've made enormous progress rebuilding Ontario's economy. After years of decline under the previous Liberal government, we're bringing back manufacturing jobs, with more than $45 billion in new investments in the first electric vehicle and battery plants in Canada, creating thousands of jobs. We're investing nearly $200 billion to build new roads, highways and the largest expansion of public transit in North America. We have a plan to deliver better health care and connect everyone in Ontario to a family doctor and primary care team. We're keeping your costs down by fighting the Liberal carbon tax, removing road tolls, scrapping the licence plate sticker fee and putting more money back into your pocket with $200 taxpayer rebate cheques.
All of this progress is now under threat from President Trump's tariffs. Team Ontario will answer the call.
I will never stop fighting to protect Ontario. With your support and with a mandate from you, the people, I will do whatever is necessary to win this fight. For our families. For our workers. For our businesses. For all of us. Doug Ford is Premier of Ontario and the Leader of the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario.

Document 77 of 281

Greater Sudbury to get more than $1.5M to keep homeless safe, warm

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greater-sudbury-get-more-than-1-5m-keep-homeless/docview/3159750532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greater+Sudbury+to+get+more+than+%241.5M+to+keep+homeless+safe%2C+warm&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Greater Sudbury has gotten another boost to help with homelessness.
The federal government announced this week it had signed agreements with nine additional municipalities in Ontario to deliver services to support those living without homes.
Tom Davies Square received more than $1.5 million to support those living in encampments, Sacha Novack, a spokesperson for the municipality, told The Star.
"Under the federal Unsheltered Homelessness and Encampments Initiative, the City of Greater Sudbury was approved to receive a total of $1,532,256 between Sept. 6, 2024, and March 31, 2026, to support those living in encampments, as outlined in the community engagement response plan," Novack said. "This funding will be used to support additional warming centre services at Energy Court and the Samaritan Centre over the winter months, as part of a system of engagement and support to persons living in encampments to transition to housing options."
The funding will be matched by provincial funding that is allocated for community outreach teams and client navigators who also work to support people living in encampments.
Novack said the city is aware of 200 people currently living in encampments across 42 locations.
In its most recent update of the by-name list, published on its website on Jan. 9, the city said "of the 292 individuals actively homeless, 93 are staying in encampments, 62 are unsheltered, 70 are in shelter and 67 are provisionally accommodated or 'unknown.' Of the individuals staying in encampments or unsheltered, some frequent shelter from time to time."
Greater Sudbury's shelters have been operating at capacity. From Jan. 6-12, there were 34 stays per night (average) at the Off the Street shelter, which has a capacity of 35 people. Another 28 people stayed at the Cedar Place shelter for women and families; the capacity at that shelter is 28. The Elizabeth Fry Safe Harbour House, which now has an expanded capacity of 26 people, received an average of 17 stays per night.
In addition to Greater Sudbury, the Liberals have signed agreements with Durham, Toronto, Peel, York, Hamilton, Niagara, Waterloo and Ottawa. Regina and Saskatoon also signed deals with the federal government. The federal government signed an agreement with London last month.
"Under these agreements, the federal government will provide a total of more than $91.5 million over two years. The municipalities will match federal funding," the Liberals said in a release. "In total, the federal government is delivering more than $88 million to 10 municipalities in Ontario and approximately $8 million to two cities in Saskatchewan. This is on top of what the federal government is investing through Reaching Home: Canada's Homelessness Strategy, a total of $1.6 billion in Ontario and $124 million in Saskatchewan."
This funding will support the implementation of community encampment response plans and "immediately provide additional support to people living without homes," the Liberals added. "Each plan is tailored to the specific needs of a community."
The money will be used to address a range of needs, including the creation of new shelter spaces; an increased number of options for those seeking transitional housing; and case management services to help people find and keep permanent housing.
The city of Peterborough has seen great success with its transitional tiny home development, which is located in a former parking lot. CBC News took viewers on a tour of the village last March (tinyurl.com/3d362rb8) and spoke with the mayor of Peterborough. He praised the program and said it was worth every nickel.
"It's worth every dollar; these are human lives," Mayor Jeff Leal told CBC. "One of the things we fundamentally believe in Peterborough is by making this investment, we're going to put individuals on a new trajectory in life."
It cost taxpayers $2.5 million to build the village, which includes accommodations for 50 people, laundry and washroom facilities, as well as wrap-around care services. It will cost about $2 million annually to operate.
While there are no plans in the works in Sudbury for a tiny home village, the city's transitional housing project on Lorraine Street will operate in a similar way to the program in Peterborough.
"There are currently no plans for the city to build tiny homes for people experiencing homelessness," Novack said. "Through the recommendations from the Roadmap to End Homelessness by 2030, the city is focusing on the development of affordable, supportive and transitional housing." sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar Star Staff

Subject: Federal government; Funding; Homeless people; Municipalities

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Document 78 of 281

Carney unconcerned about lack of experience; Liberal leadership candidate cites time at Bank of Canada, Bank of England, UN

Author: Lévesque, Carine; News11 News

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine; News. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-unconcerned-about-lack-experience-liberal/docview/3159750391/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+unconcerned+about+lack+of+experience%3B+Liberal+leadership+candidate+cites+time+at+Bank+of+Canada%2C+Bank+of+England%2C+UN&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine%3BNews&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The idea of becoming prime minister of Canada in a matter of weeks despite having never won an election does not seem to worry former central banker Mark Carney, who says he has started at the top many times in his career.
On his way to a private event in Ottawa with his supporters on Thursday, Carney briefly stopped to speak to reporters flanked with many ministers who have endorsed him.
"I've had many jobs where I came in at the top," he said, when asked what he would say to someone who thinks being prime minister is not an entry-level job. "I came in at the top of the Bank of England, Bank of Canada, in terms of the United Nations and climate change."
He served as Bank of Canada governor during the 2008 economic crisis before becoming governor of the Bank of England when the United Kingdom withdrew from the European Union. More recently, he served as the UN Special Envoy on Climate Action and Finance.
Carney insisted that he intends to put the economy "back on track" as a team.
"You see the team here," he said, pointing to the elected MPs standing behind him.
An hour earlier, the Conservatives had dispatched MP Michelle Rempel Garner outside of a nearby restaurant where they thought the Carney event was taking place to complain that he had not addressed the media since his campaign launch in Edmonton last week.
Until then, only the Canadian Press had managed to catch up with Carney, while he was skating the Rideau Canal on Wednesday to ask him about his plans for the carbon tax.
He promised to present a plan to address climate change "that works for everyone."
"This man ... could, quite possibly, become prime minister in about six weeks," said Rempel Garner on Thursday. "He's never held elected office of any form, he doesn't sit in Parliament, and he could be making Canada's policy at a time of national crisis.
"And yet, a week after launching his campaign ... we have heard absolutely nothing, zero, crickets from this man about what he would do as prime minister."
Liberal ministers and MPs who were entering the Carney event accused the Conservatives of being "afraid" of a new Liberal leader who is not Justin Trudeau.
"There's a lot of fear," said Employment Minister Steven MacKinnon. "I think Canadians know that the challenges we face around the world and here at home are going to require complex solutions by people of experience and not just pithy slogans."
Ontario Liberal MP Ryan Turnbull said Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre should indeed be afraid of an incoming opponent who has lots of economic experience.
"Mark Carney is a highly credible individual who has a reputation around the world for being able to deal with economic crises, and Pierre Poilievre is a career politician. He's got no experience," he said.
Rempel Garner said it is not about Conservatives, and that Canadians are frustrated.
"We are at a time where ... I have people in my community that can't afford to eat, and we've got these guys shuttering Parliament. We have tariffs ... being threatened on us by the American government, and they're having a cocktail party."

Five things to know about that $200 Ontario cheque in your mailbox

Author: Rivers, Hear

Publication info: Rivers, Hear.
