Document 81 of 281

For a change, Sudburians need to vote for the winning party

Author: Poisson, Ed

Publication info: Poisson, Ed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/change-sudburians-need-vote-winning-party/docview/3159026604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+a+change%2C+Sudburians+need+to+vote+for+the+winning+party&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Poisson%2C+Ed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It would seem that in the next few months, we will have a federal election in Canada and a provincial election in Ontario.
I am searching for the words to implore the people of Sudbury not waste their vote again and elect candidates who will not be part of the party who will win the election, form the government and decide where and when government money will be spent.
For decades, the people of Sudbury have voted to not have a member in Toronto of the governing party. And now there is four lanes to North Bay, and two to Sudbury, yet we have four times the population.
As well, the $15 million a year that the province was spending on Highway 400 upgrading to Sudbury has been taken by MPP Vic Fedelli, to his North Bay riding.
The province promised three years ago to four-lane Highway 69 but I have not seen a stone turned over yet.
All indications are that the Conservatives will win again in Ontario, as well the Conservatives are set for a landslide in the federal election.
People here have a history of voting for the bleeding-heart party, which has resulted in Sudbury having an undersized hospital and an undersized highway. They have done nothing for us. They say they are the party of the working man, then the one time they had power, all they did was take money from the working people and give it to the people who did not work. Sharon Murdock was the minister from Sudbury in charge of social services. When the NDP party left office, there were six times as many people on social services as when they were first elected.
Ontario politics has always been a winner-take-all proposition.
The governing party gets all the money for their ridings and everyone else is on their own.
The same applies to the Algoma Manitoulin riding. They have the oldest swing bridge in the world. They vote NDP as well.
Go with the winners.

Subject: Provincial elections

Re: 'Poilievre can't be trusted to negotiate with Trump: Sudbury MP,' Jan. 15

Full text: The hallmarks of a good person are honesty and credibility. Sadly, blatant and willful misinformation by Viviane Lapointe, Sudbury Liberal MP, do not measure up to the criteria; this from one who would love to bend at the trough again for four more years and thereby qualify for her government pension. She is putting out the typical self-serving Liberal fear-mongering that we have all come to expect come election time. Some of us remember the 2006 general election where the Liberal fear-mongering mantra of Paul Martin was: beware the PC's frightening "hidden agenda."

Furthermore, I have company in my assessment of Lapointe's comments: an informal poll carried out by The Sudbury Star shows that 65 per cent of those who read the MP's column do not agree with her.

Document 83 of 281

Trump holds off on hitting Canada with Day 1 tariffs; In November, Trump threatened to hit Canada with steep 25 per cent tariffs

Author: Duggan, Kyle; Karadeglija, Anja

Publication info: Duggan, Kyle; Karadeglija, Anja. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-holds-off-on-hitting-canada-with-day-1/docview/3157754344/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+holds+off+on+hitting+Canada+with+Day+1+tariffs%3B+In+November%2C+Trump+threatened+to+hit+Canada+with+steep+25+per+cent+tariffs&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Duggan%2C+Kyle%3BKaradeglija%2C+Anja&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: U.S. President Donald Trump held off on hitting Canada with damaging tariffs on his inauguration day.
Shortly before Trump was sworn into office Monday, an incoming White House official who insisted on anonymity pointed reporters to a Wall Street Journal report that said instead of tariffs Trump would instead sign an executive order launching an investigation into alleged unfair trade and currency practices by Canada, Mexico and China.
That suggests he plans to pursue a more deliberative approach to trade relations, as he moves to quickly implement his policy agenda through a raft of decrees.
In his inaugural address he did vow to boost domestic oil production and impose tariffs to "enrich our citizens." He talked about setting up an agency to collect tariffs but did not got into detail.
In November, Trump threatened to hit Canada with steep 25 per cent across-the-board tariffs in an executive order on his first day back in office.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has repeatedly said Canada is ready to respond with countermeasures if Trump makes good on that threat.
The federal government also promised to pump $1.3 billion in new resources into border security over the next six years in the hopes of convincing Trump not to impose tariffs.
Trump initially tied the tariffs to concerns about border security but later seemed more concerned with the trade imbalance between Canada and the U.S.
As Trump begins his second term as president, federal cabinet ministers are gathering in Montebello, Que., Monday and Tuesday to discuss the tariff threat.
Trudeau also met with the country's 13 premiers last Wednesday to discuss possible retaliatory actions. At the end of those meetings, he and 12 premiers released a statement pledging to present a united front to their American counterparts.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith opted out, saying she could not support any retaliation plan that included oil and gas.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Monday the tariff threat still needs to be taken seriously, despite the reports suggesting Trump will hold off.
"He's just adding more uncertainty, (saying) 'I may not do it today, I'll do it tomorrow. Maybe I'll do it in a month.'That doesn't help," Ford said, according to a CityNews report. "So we're going to take him serious."
Broad tariffs were among one of Trump's campaign tariffs but a new poll suggests there is limited support for them among Americans.
An Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research survey found almost half of U.S. adults oppose imposing tariffs on all goods brought into the U.S. from other countries - more than were in favour or were neutral on the question.
"I do think, and maybe it's just a hope, that Trump backs off from his most extreme rhetoric, especially on the deportation and tariff fronts," said Marvin Loh, senior macro strategist at State Street Global Markets.
The head of Canada's largest private-sector union said she's skeptical of the reports saying Trump won't proceed with tariffs, given how he imposed tariffs during his first administration.
"No one should be breathing a sigh of relief at all right now," Unifor president Lana Payne said Monday morning.
Payne said the "constant threat of tariffs" undermines investment and constantly puts Canada's economy in danger.
She said the solution is to invest in industrial plans for Canadian manufacturing, put supports in place to prepare for job losses and collaborate across political parties, business and organized labour.
"We have to come together in a profound way over the next four years to make sure that we're guarding against any future threat, but also making sure that we've got our act together in Canada," she said. "It's not going to stop here. The demands are going to grow." The Canadian Press, with files from The Associated Press, Bloomberg News

Subject: Political campaigns; Executive orders; Border security; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 84 of 281

U.S. tariffs would have a chilling effect on Sudbury's jobs, economy; Northern Ontario in general would be hit hard, analysts say

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-tariffs-would-have-chilling-effect-on/docview/3156925426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+tariffs+would+have+a+chilling+effect+on+Sudbury%27s+jobs%2C+economy%3B+Northern+Ontario+in+general+would+be+hit+hard%2C+analysts+say&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Folks in Sudbury, as elsewhere in the country, are worried about the impact a second Donald Trump term will have on the economy. Tariffied, you might even say.
The incoming U.S. president is threatening to tax a variety of goods crossing the world's longest undefended border by as much as 25 per cent, and metals and minerals could be among them. If so, that would certainly hurt the Nickel City and the region in general.
"It could be that mines have to downsize some of their activity and cut shifts," said Jean-Charles Cachon, professor of organizational strategy at Laurentian. "If they have to curtail production, that would have a trigger effect on the mining supply industry, as well."
Sudburians might also find the cost of everyday items goes up, yet again, although not because of COVID or a war in Ukraine this time.
"If they want to impose tariffs, it's just going to make inflation increase on both sides of the border," said Cachon. "We get 75 per cent of our food from the U.S., and imposing tariffs on our oil is going to increase the cost of agriculture in the U.S., so then we're going to import back further inflation from the goods that are produced."
Livio Di Matteo, an economics prof at Lakehead University, agreed a trade dispute could be chilling for Northern Ontarians, who have already seen what countervailing duties on softwood lumber can do to the resource economy.
"We don't know yet exactly what tariffs they are going to levy, or if it's across the board, but if they raise the price of our exports, we'll essentially be exporting less, and that will ripple back into the production side and probably cause some unemployment," he said. "There are all kinds of numbers floated out there, but if they come out with 25 per cent tariffs and we respond with 25 per cent tariffs, that's going to have a fairly large impact on our GDP growth and you'll definitely have a recession. But until we know exactly what they are going to do and how we are going to respond, it's all pretty much guesswork."
Addressing the media earlier this week, Premier Doug Ford said Ontario supplies nearly half - 46 per cent - of the nickel used south of the border. A lot of that, of course, comes from Sudbury.
Speaking at the same press conference, Ontario Mines Minister George Pirie said Canada and the U.S. enjoy "one of the most integrated and mutually beneficial economic relationships in the world. Both of our economies are deeply intertwined and we need to strengthen these ties now, more than ever."
Apart from mining a lot of nickel, Pirie said the Trillium Province produces more than 77 per cent of platinum-group elements in Canada, and a third of its copper. Ontario nickel, he added, is critical to the American aerospace and defence industries.
"Not only does Ontario possess vast mineral deposits, we also have a highly skilled mining workforce," he said. "Critical minerals are powering the technologies that will define the 21st Century."
Should Trump follow through on his tariff threats, Ontario would be "affected greater than any other province," according to Northern Development Minister Greg Rickford.
While Alberta - the biggest exporter (by far) of oil to the U.S. - also stands to get hit hard, "we have an auto sector that is comparable to the oil sector," said Rickford, during a visit to Sudbury on Thursday. "And our auto sector is relevant for Northern Ontario because the future vehicles are EV technology, driven by battery power, which makes mining more relevant than it's ever been - from mines to motors, as we say."
The province would obviously prefer to see no tariffs imposed at all on resources and products bound for U.S. markets, said the Kenora-Rainy River MPP, "but to the extent that they are, or will be, we just want to make sure that Ontario's interests are protected going into this - and from the perspective of Northern Ontario, that we're ready, and have all the tools in the toolbox, as the premier likes to say, to deal with these tariffs and protect our workers and business.
"We need to show the United States, in surgical detail, the importance and potential we have, and that we're ready to fight should these tariffs come into play."
The Ontario premier has mused publicly about ways to fight back, including flipping a switch on hydro exports to the U.S. Asked this week whether he might also shut down the supply of critical minerals, Ford was more circumspect, keeping his cards to his chest.
"Let's see what they're going to do on Jan. 20 (when Trump assumes office) and then we'll retaliate," he said.
According to Natural Resources Canada, the U.S. is Canada's top destination for mineral exports, representing 56 per cent of total shipments in 2023. (Other key buyers are China, the U.K., Japan, South Korea and Switzerland, but all five together don't even add up to half of that U.S. market.)
Canada maintains "a significant positive trade balance in minerals and metals with the United States," the department said in a December bulletin. In 2023, "the value of exports to the United States amounted to $83.8 billion, reflecting a five per cent rise compared to the previous year."
While iron and steel led the way, together accounting for almost a quarter of the exports, gold was also big ($12.5 billion, or 15 per cent of the total) and copper, which is harvested in Sudbury, was not insignificant ($4.9 billion, or six per cent).
"Canada also plays a crucial role as a supplier of critical minerals to the United States, including tellurium, niobium and uranium," said Natural Resources Canada.
Indeed, the U.S. is Canada's top trading partner in this area, with exports in 2023 amounting to $29.8 billion and "accounting for 59 per cent of Canada's total critical minerals exports," the report states.
The province is hoping to continue that cross-border flow, this past week pitching a "renewed strategic alliance" with the U.S., with critical minerals being a key plank of its broader plan, which includes enhanced border security, called Fortress Am-Can.
"The success of Fortress Am-Can depends on the critical minerals needed for new technologies, including advanced military technologies that will define geopolitical and economic security for the next century," Ford said in a statement.
The premier also wants to speed up the time it takes to get new mines in operation.
"At a time when China is winning the race to dominate these resources while also restricting the sale and shipment of critical minerals to the U.S., Canada and Ontario need to urgently get our critical minerals out of the ground, processed and shipped to the factory floors that are building for the future," Ford said. jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: International trade; Mines; Mining; Exports; Critical minerals; Natural resources; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Exports Tariffs

Location: China; United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Natural Resources Canada; NAICS: 924120

Identifier / keyword: folks; sudbury; elsewhere; country; worried; impact

Full text: For a political leader, few things are more attractive than riding to election victory on a wave of patriotic enthusiasm. It's an urge that Ontario Premier Doug Ford seems less and less likely to resist.

Document 86 of 281

Alberta is now the real threat to Canadian unity

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-is-now-real-threat-canadian-unity/docview/3156322130/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+is+now+the+real+threat+to+Canadian+unity&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, it's been assumed the biggest threat to Canadian unity comes from the province of Quebec. It hosted a separatist terrorist movement in the 1960s and is home to the sovereigntist Parti Québécois. It held two referendums on separation, the last of which saw the country hang together by less than one per cent of the vote. Today, the party tops the polls and is threatening to hold another referendum if it takes power next year.
But the game has changed, thanks to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. The biggest threat to the integrity of Canada no longer lies in the east, but in the west - namely, the province of Alberta. A showdown is looming between Edmonton and Ottawa over Canada's upcoming tariff war with the United States - one that will start this week, when provincial premiers meet with the federal government to hash out a "Team Canada" response to Trump's threats.
The battle lines are clear. Over the weekend, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that "everything is on the table" when it comes to countering Trump's tariffs, including withholding the four million barrels of oil Alberta ships south every day.
This caused a backlash from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who spent part of the weekend meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort. "We won't stand for that and you should never, ever threaten something you cannot do," she said. Smith reminded Joly that western Canadian crude flows to refineries in Ontario and Quebec through Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline, which runs through the U.S. Threats to cut off oil are thus "empty," she added, and would fuel a national unity crisis.
Smith is not wrong. If the federal government isn't careful, it could inflame the "Wexit" movement, which, for the first time, would be feasible, because it could find a willing partner in Washington. Trump thinks it would be great if Canada became America's 51st state. But what parts would he want? Downtown Toronto, with its core of NDP and Liberal voters? The province of Quebec, which speaks a different language? Nope. The prize would be Alberta, with its oil and conservative mindset. And its border with the Northwest Territories. If Trump got Alberta, his next stop would be striking a deal with the N.W.T., then on to neighbouring Yukon, to increase the U.S.'s access to the Arctic and its undersea oil reserves.
And how would Albertans feel about joining the U.S.? In a December poll, 30 per cent said the province would benefit, the highest percentage in the country. Thirty-eight per cent of Albertans also thought the province would be better off as its own country, while only 35 per cent of Quebecers felt the same about their province. A separate poll, released Tuesday, found that when asked if Canada as a whole should join the U.S., 18 per cent of Albertans agree - far from a majority, but the highest percentage in the country.
For this, we can thank Trudeau and Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault. Over the last decade, they've been determined to put Alberta's oil industry out of business. Between their federal carbon tax, incoming emissions cap and general denigration of the resource as dirty and shameful, it's no wonder the West feels disrespected.
It feels like a rerun of Pierre Trudeau's infamous National Energy Program, which caused thousands of Albertans to lose their livelihoods and spawned the bumper sticker, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark."
But Trudeau the father also was concerned about national unity and would roll over in his grave if his son's legacy was the breakup of Canada. As Trudeau the son prepares to exit stage left, our leadership cannot allow that to happen. Canada needs to present a tough, united front that respects all its provinces.
If we don't hang together, there is a real risk we will break apart. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 87 of 281

Barring Alberta oil exports to U.S. would lead to 'national unity crisis': Smith

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/barring-alberta-oil-exports-u-s-would-lead/docview/3155473088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Barring+Alberta+oil+exports+to+U.S.+would+lead+to+%27national+unity+crisis%27%3A+Smith&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith says that if the federal government imposes an export ban on Alberta oil going to the United States as a retaliatory measure against expected U.S. tariffs it would precipitate a "national unity crisis."
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith said Monday. "I can't predict what Albertans would do."
She warned that Ottawa would have a "national unity crisis on (its) hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. partners."
Smith made the remarks during a virtual press conference with reporters, which followed her meetings with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his West Palm Beach Mar-a-Lago resort and his golf club over the weekend.
Mélanie Joly, Canada's foreign affairs minister, was asked on CTV's Question Period talk show on Sunday if Canada would consider cutting off energy to the United States in the event of a tariff war, and responded, "Everything is on the table." Canada is preparing a list of U.S. products against which surtaxes could be levied, should Trump bring in his promised 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods after he's inaugurated next week. Canada made similar moves during negotiations in 2019 with the first Trump administration, adding more than $16 billion in surtaxes to a variety of American imports, including steel, aluminum, yogurt, toilet paper, dishwashers and various other items.
Joly's response echoes a pitch made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who threatened in mid-December to cut off energy supply to the northeastern United States, should the Trump tariffs become a reality. Smith has staunchly opposed such a measure but said Monday morning that if Trump imposes tariffs, there will need to be a Canadian response.
"We have to wait and see what he does, and then we have to have a measured and mature response to it that hopefully does not damage the relationship or damage Canadian consumers and Canadian businesses too much," Smith said.
Smith, who said she attended Trump's Saturday soirée as a guest of celebrity investor and former Conservative party leadership candidate Kevin O'Leary, described her conversations with Trump as "constructive," but also seemed resigned that, come Jan. 20, Trump will be going ahead with tariffs, regardless of what Canadian politicians do. She said that she is "not expecting" Alberta oil to be exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, although she remains hopeful that the United States will want to purchase more oil from Canada for its refineries in the Midwest.
"I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," Smith said.
The Alberta premier has been on an extensive hearts-and-minds campaign in the United States, meeting with governors and appearing on conservative-leaning news programs. She has been making the case directly that U.S. tariffs would increase energy costs to American consumers. She has, however, faced criticism that by boosting Alberta she may be undermining the broader Canadian response to tariffs.
"I'm hoping that by talking about the importance of energy, that we'll be able to avoid tariffs on every Canadian product," said Smith.
Since Trump first made threats of tariffs on Canadian exports, which would end more than three decades of free trade between the two countries, Canadian provinces and the federal government have scrambled to respond.
Trump has spoken of a "trade deficit" with Canada, and claimed that the United States is subsidizing Canada. He also suggested the tariffs were a pressure tactic to stop the flow of illegal migrants and drugs into the United States. Smith announced a $29-million border security plan, which would see a team of sheriffs patrolling Alberta's border with Montana.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with Trump at Mar-a-Lago, and a deputation of cabinet ministers has also travelled to Florida to meet with the incoming president's transition team. So far, there has been little indication that Trump has been persuaded to abandon his plan for tariffs, and it's all occurring against the backdrop of a domestic political crisis in Ottawa.
Last Monday, Trudeau announced that he would be resigning as prime minister, kicking off a race for the leadership of the Liberal party and proroguing Parliament until March 24. Smith has called for an immediate election to avoid what on Monday she called the "leadership vacuum" in Ottawa.
"We need somebody at the table who is able to engage with the (Trump) administration saying, 'You got a four year mandate. I got a four year mandate, let's deal.'And we don't have that right now," Smith said.
Canada's premiers and representatives from the federal government are meeting on Wednesday to discuss the tariff response. As well, a joint mission of provincial premiers will travel to Washington, D.C., on Feb. 12 to meet with Trump administration officials and talk trade. The Canadian economy, being orders of magnitude smaller than the United States', will be far more harmed by a prolonged trade war than the American economy will be, Smith said.
Research indicates that Canada would see tens of thousands of job losses, contractions in the country's GDP and, in the case of retaliatory tariffs, an increase in the cost of purchasing American-made goods.
"I think the solution is that we find ways to buy more American goods," said Smith. "We know the president's opening position, we've got to try to find a way to dig down and get to a resolution. And I think that will have to happen in earnest once the inauguration happens on Jan. 20."

Document 88 of 281

Supreme Court of Canada could hear 'generation-defining'climate case; One of the key players opposing the Ford government is Sophia Mathur of Sudbury

Author: Yadav, Anushka

Publication info: Yadav, Anushka. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supreme-court-canada-could-hear-generation/docview/3154284629/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+of+Canada+could+hear+%27generation-defining%27climate+case%3B+One+of+the+key+players+opposing+the+Ford+government+is+Sophia+Mathur+of+Sudbury&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Yadav%2C+Anushka&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is taking its fight against seven youth climate activists -including Sophia Mathur of Sudbury -to the Supreme Court of Canada after Ontario's highest court sided with the activists in October.
It's the latest attempt by the province to quash the case. Ford's government, which has shown environmental protection and climate change are low priorities, has used a climate change denier in court in an attempt to cast doubt on the youth's case.
Lawyers from both sides agree the case, if the court hears it, will be significant.
"This proposed appeal would ask this court to determine, for the first time, whether and to what extent the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms imposes obligations on Canadian governments to combat climate change," said Ontario's application for leave to appeal.
"This case presents an excellent opportunity for Canada's highest court to weigh in on the constitutional obligations of state actors in combating climate change -a recognized issue of national concern in Canada," lawyers for Ontario wrote in the application to the Supreme Court.
While the PC appeal to Canada's top court draws out the case further, lawyers with Ecojustice who are representing the youth are encouraged about what a victory could mean for future climate action.
"(This) opens the door to a generation-defining hearing before Canada's highest court - one that could lead to recognizing that governments have constitutional obligations to address one of the most pressing emergencies we have ever faced," Fraser Thomson, the Climate Director at Ecojustice said in a release. "The climate crisis isn't going away, and neither are we."
On Oct. 17, the Court of Appeal for Ontario overturned a previous lower court decision that dismissed the case and ruled in favour of the young applicants asking for stronger climate action in Ontario.
PLANS DISMANTLED The PCs came into power in 2018 and immediately dismantled plans to make Ontario's energy systems more sustainable and climate action stronger. Ford repealed the Climate Change Mitigation and Low-carbon Economy Act of 2016, reducing the carbon emissions target to 30 per cent below 2005 levels.
The previous goal was 37 per cent below 1990 levels by 2030.
Sophia Mathur, of Sudbury, who was just 12 when she first became involved in the climate justice movement, has been the face of the ongoing legal challenge since November 2019.
Alongside six other youth activists -Alex, Beze, Madi, Shaelyn, Shelby, and Zoe -Mathur argues that the reduced emissions target violates Ontarians'constitutional rights to life, liberty and security.
Since first fighting the provincial government five years ago, Mathur said she is "more determined than ever to see this case through ...
"The climate crisis is here and it is going to impact us all. I'm fighting for my future and for the future of the generations that come after me. My fellow applicants and I know what's at stake, and we are not backing down."
Using sections 7 and 15 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, the activists contend that children are disproportionately affected by climate change impacts, including poor air quality and mental health challenges, and they will bear the most severe consequences as environmental degradation worsens throughout their lives without strong, immediate intervention.
The Charter guarantees rights and freedoms to all Canadians, drawing parallels to other landmark human rights instruments worldwide, such as the U.S. Bill of Rights and the European Convention on Human Rights.
Ecojustice presented evidence showing that the province's reduced emissions target could allow an additional 30 megatonnes of carbon emissions annually by 2030 -an amount that further jeopardizes Ontario's ability to meet both national and global climate goals.
Canada has committed to reducing its greenhouse gas emissions by 40 to 45 per cent by 2030 from 2005 levels as part of its pathway to net-zero emissions by 2050. According to data released on Dec. 19, Canada's national emissions are currently only nine percent below 2005 levels.
The country has yet to submit its updated Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs), which are the emissions reduction targets central to the Paris Agreement.
ONTARIO A LARGE EMITTER Ontario remains one of the largest emitters of greenhouse gases in the country, producing 157 megatonnes of carbon emissions in 2022. This is almost identical to the emissions level of 158 megatonnes in 2017 before the province's cap-and-trade program introduced under Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government was scrapped by Ford through the Cap and Trade Cancellation Act in October 2018.
The young applicants sought an order "requiring Ontario to set a science-based emissions reduction target and to revise its climate change plan in accordance with international standards."
Instead, the Ford government attempted to strike down the case. On April 15, 2020, it filed a motion to dismiss the case, arguing it should not proceed to a full hearing. The youth applicants fought back.
In November 2020, the government's motion was denied and for the first time in Canadian history, a court acknowledged that climate change could potentially violate Charter rights, allowing the case to move forward.
After overcoming multiple procedural hurdles, the case proceeded to a full hearing in September 2022.
In a ruling last year, Ontario Superior Court Justice Marie-Andree Vermette dismissed the case, concluding that the provincial emissions target did not violate the Charter.
Thomson at Ecojustice has explained previously that the court believed that his clients had a "positive rights case which is a case that required the government to step into a problem that maybe it wasn't responsible for."
However, Justice Vermette recognized the significant gap between necessary global emissions reductions and the targets outlined in the provincial plan, describing it as "large, unexplained and without any apparent scientific basis."
The group appealed the decision and argued the case before Ontario's Court of Appeal in January 2024, which ultimately ruled in their favour, overturning the dismissal by the lower court.
LEGAL FIGHT CONTINUES However, the PC government -already under fire in the latest Ontario Auditor General's report for failing to educate citizens on the Environmental Bill of Rights and withholding key information on the environmental impacts of certain projects -has chosen to continue its legal fight.
On Dec. 16, the Ontario government applied for leave to appeal the Mathur et al. case at the Supreme Court, setting the stage for a potentially precedent-setting hearing at Canada's highest court.
In an interview, Thompson explained that the only way a party can appeal a decision from the Ontario Court of Appeal is by seeking permission from the Supreme Court of Canada. This process, known as "leave," means that Ontario must convince the Supreme Court that the case is significant enough to warrant its attention.
While many seek to have their cases heard by the Supreme Court, only a select few are granted leave. The Supreme Court receives as many as 600 applications for leave to appeal every year and grants around 80, according to its website.
An eight-week trial has been scheduled for October 2026.
When Ontario submits its request, the activists'legal team will respond but ultimately, the Supreme Court will decide whether to take the case.
Thompson describes the case as a classic David vs. Goliath battle, one they always anticipated would "likely end up at the highest court in Canada" given that climate change is both a matter of "fundamental national importance" and "an existential threat to humanity."
He said the Charter of Rights and Freedoms is designed to protect human rights. However, the actions taken by governments in fueling the climate crisis and failing to address it violate those fundamental rights, he said.
"And across the country, governments like the Government of Ontario have been fueling this crisis and not doing enough to stop it, and we know that this is having an impact on Charter rights across the country.
"This case is fundamentally about whether the Charter can protect those rights," Thompson said.
SIMILAR CASE IN THE U.S. In a similar case south of the border, 16 young people presented their case to the Montana Supreme Court in 2023, highlighting the economic and health impacts of spring floods, smoke-filled skies, extreme heat, and damage to Montana's landscapes.
On Dec. 18, the court upheld a historic District Court ruling, prioritizing children's constitutional rights over extractive industries. It confirmed that the state's greenhouse gas emissions are "proven to be a substantial factor" in causing climate harm and affirmed that Montana's constitutional right to a clean and healthy environment includes a stable climate system.
Thompson said he's optimistic since courts across the world are increasingly ruling in favour of the citizens because they are looking to unequivocal scientific evidence.
The Mathur case has been setting legal precedents throughout its time before the courts - including becoming the first climate Charter case to be heard and ruled on its merits.
If the provincial government is granted leave, this would mark the first time a climate case based on Charter rights is being decided at the Supreme Court of Canada. As a result, the decision would have precedent-setting implications, not only for Ontario but for all of Canada. With files from the Canadian Press The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. Anushka Yadav is based with the Pointer, a digital-only platform. For more, go to thepointer.com. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Emissions control; Activists; Greenhouse gases; Attorneys; Emissions trading; Carbon; Activism; Emission standards; Climate action; Human rights; Climate change; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Attorneys Emissions trading

Document 89 of 281

Lively man tries in vain to bring Nigerian doc to Sudbury; Foreign-trained physicians face too many barriers at a time when we could really use them, he argues

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lively-man-tries-vain-bring-nigerian-doc-sudbury/docview/3154284578/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lively+man+tries+in+vain+to+bring+Nigerian+doc+to+Sudbury%3B+Foreign-trained+physicians+face+too+many+barriers+at+a+time+when+we+could+really+use+them%2C+he+argues&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doctors are in short supply in Ontario, but bringing in expertise from abroad is far from a simple process, a Lively man has painfully learned.
"Navigating the system is unreal," said Garth Wunsch, who has been trying for years to help a Nigerian physician and his family relocate to Sudbury. "There's this almost dichotomy where Ontario has the needs and programs in place, but (immigrants) have to be permanent residents in order to work, and the federal government controls that."
Wunsch said his relationship with the Nigerian man goes back more than three decades. "At the time I was a member of the Congregational Church in Naughton and my pastor, who did missionary work in Nigeria, had adopted a little black boy," he said. "That boy befriended another boy who was selling peanuts in the streets."
Bassey Umoh, the street vendor, was just eight at the time, and effectively orphaned. His father had died and his mother, while still alive, couldn't afford to raise him along with several other children, so had placed him with an uncle.
"He had no hope of getting an education, but the pastor's wife took this little guy under her wing to homeschool with her own kids," Wunsch said. "Then their daughter took very ill in Nigeria and they had to come back to Canada, so Bassey was left stranded."
He wasn't entirely abandoned, however, as Wunsch, in collaboration with the pastor, began sending money to help with his schooling. He and fellow congregant Helene Pilon continued to do so after the clergyman passed away, supporting Umoh right through to his graduation from medical school.
"He's in his 40s now and has been a practising MD for 16 years," said Wunsch. "He wanted to come to Canada right after he graduated, and I said, 'Bassey, I think you should stay there, your country needs doctors.'Years go by, he gets married, has children, and life in Nigeria continues to deteriorate. So about five years ago we decided we would make an effort to bring him to Canada."
That involved a lot of paperwork and logistics, but everything, he thought, was eventually arranged. Umoh's wife Stella, who has an honours degree in biochemistry, was enrolled at Cambrian to take a PSW program, and her tuition was covered. Wunsch and fellow patrons even got as far as booking flights and securing an apartment for the couple and their two young kids.
But it all fell apart at the last minute. "The high commissioner to Canada turned down the study visa application, and everything unravelled from there," he said.
Wunsch said he explored multiple routes to find an immigration pathway, engaging help from the IVEY Group in Sudbury, which specializes in global recruitment, and consulting with people at the Northern Ontario School of Medicine. He also paid a visit to the office for MP Viviane Lapointe and spoke with a representative there.
So far, none of these efforts has paid off.
Wunsch acknowledged Bassey would have to go through an upgrading process - despite his experience and credentials, including a specialty in obstetrics and gynecology - before being licensed to practise here, which could take several years.
That part he understands, as other countries have different protocols and standards for medical grads, but he still feels there are far too many hoops to jump through to simply get someone with much-needed skills into the country.
"If they are truly serious and sincere about bringing these people here, take the hurdles down," he said.
Before the study visa was declined, Wunsch reckons he spent about three years wading through red tape, while the Umohs had to go through their own series of onerous steps.
"This whole process was horrendous," he said. "English was their first language, but they still had to do an English fluency test, and it was a 10-hour bus ride to get to the testing site."
The best solution would have been an express entry visa through the Federal Skilled Worker Program, but Bassey fell short on that application by "about 10 per cent," he said, "primarily because he was just a little bit overage to qualify for a certain number of points."
The Nigerian family - which now counts three children, all under the age of 10 - is "unbelievably disappointed" that their dream of moving to Canada has so far been denied.
"The situation in Nigeria is very volatile right now and they want their kids to have a future," said Wunsch. "For them it's scary because they are Christian, and Muslims are getting more radical all the time there. If it gets much worse they'll be able to apply on humanitarian grounds for persecution, but there is no specific threat to their lives at present."
Bassey is willing to fill a medical need anywhere in Northern Ontario but would prefer to be in Sudbury, where he could count on some support from his benefactor.
"He calls me 'uncle,'a term of endearment that many countries use," said the Lively retiree. "He would like to be near me so I can guide him along on this twisty path."
Wunsch recently turned 80, however, and is not sure how much energy he has left to put into getting the Nigerian 'nephew'situated in Canada, even though to him it's an obvious win-win.
"The system as set up seems to be designed for carpenters, plumbers and welders," he said. "It's not set up for doctors - and yet that's what they're saying we need. (Doug) Ford says we're going to hire thousands of doctors and nurses - where are you going to get them, off the shelf someplace?" Wunsch recently wrote a letter to The Star to share his frustration, hoping it might stir some conversation on the issue and possibly spur immigration reforms.
"I have no more doors to knock on, and don't know where else to turn," he said. "I'm out of options." jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Families & family life; Immigration

Location: Canada; Nigeria

Identifier / keyword: doctors; short; supply; ontario; bringing; expertise

Title: Lively man tries in vain to bring Nigerian doc to Sudbury; Foreign-trained physicians face too many barriers at a time when we could really use them, he argues

Document 90 of 281

Northern Ontario disproportionally impacted by homeless crisis: Report; Population of unhoused has shot up threefold over eight years, study finds

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/northern-ontario-disproportionally-impacted/docview/3154284460/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Northern+Ontario+disproportionally+impacted+by+homeless+crisis%3A+Report%3B+Population+of+unhoused+has+shot+up+threefold+over+eight+years%2C+study+finds&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new report confirms what many Sudburians have seen with their own eyes: homelessness has gone up, way up.
The study, titled Municipalities Under Pressure, reveals the unhoused population in Northern Ontario has tripled since 2016. In just the past two years alone, the number of people experiencing chronic homelessness in the region has nearly doubled.
The Northern Ontario Service Deliverers Association - which represents District Social Services Administration Boards across the region - engaged analytics firm HelpSeeker to undertake the study, in collaboration with the Association of Municipalities of Ontario and the Ontario Municipal Social Services Association.
Apart from quantifying the scope of the crisis, the report calls for an urgent investment of $11 billion to get homelessness down to a "functional zero" level (where it is rare and fleeting) by 2035.
Putting a firm figure on the number of homeless people in any given community is, of course, notoriously difficult.
The estimates in the recent report "capture known homelessness only, focusing on people known to the system through service use or enumeration efforts," the authors note. "Estimates do not accurately account for all hidden homelessness (e.g., couch-surfing) or for people in institutional settings, such as hospitals or correctional facilities. Similarly, populations living on reserve are not comprehensively represented."
Data collected by the City of Greater Sudbury indicates the homeless population locally has indeed grown over the past several years, although to an extent that likely understates the full problem, given many people escape tracking.
City spokesperson Sacha Novak said a point-in-time count taken this past October of "people known to be homeless within our community" put the population at 505 - a 25 per cent increase, roughly, from the 398 people found to be homeless during a similar exercise in October of 2021.
Homelessness has gone up across the province - by more than 50 per cent over the past eight years, and by 12 per cent since 2023 - the report notes, but there has been a disproportionate increase in Northern Ontario.
"When isolating data from Northern communities, a more pronounced increase emerges," the report states. "An estimated 5,377 people experienced known homelessness annually in 2024 - nearly triple the 1,771 recorded in 2016. This represents a 204 per cent rise over eight years, outpacing the growth in non-Northern areas."
Refugees in particular have begun to experience homelessness at an alarming rate - with the number shooting up nearly sixfold over the past four years. "Although the proportion of immigrants in the overall provincial population has not changed in recent years, the number of immigrants, refugees and asylum seekers enumerated among Ontario's chronic homelessness population has grown significantly," the report states.
The report also projects the current homeless population in our region could climb significantly over the next 10 years.
"The number of people experiencing known homelessness annually in Northern Ontario could range from 10,674 to 26,633 by 2035, depending on economic conditions," the study indicates.
In other words, it could double over a decade, from the current estimate of 5,377 people, or even more than quadruple.
Timmins mayor Michelle Boileau called these findings "deeply concerning," while pointing out the impact on Northern communities has already been "devastating."
Boileau, who also serves as chair of the Northern Ontario Service Deliverers Association, called on Queen's Park and Ottawa to take decisive action.
"Municipalities cannot face this alone," she said. "We need all orders of government to step up and make real commitments to end chronic homelessness across the province."
Al McNevin, mayor of Northeastern Manitoulin, said homelessness manifests itself differently in rural communities but in some ways becomes an even bigger problem, as small towns lack shelters and other resources present in larger centres.
"Our homeless population tend to couch surf with friends, live in trailers and sheds or other inappropriate dwellings," he said in a release. "We don't have shelters to refer them to."
The challenges in Northern Ontario are amplified, the report points out, by such factors as "geographic isolation, limited public transportation, inadequate infrastructure, and systemic inequities, including the overrepresentation of Indigenous populations in homelessness."
Apart from recommending $11 billion be spent over 10 years on housing and prevention measures, the report calls for $2 billion in targeted funding to address the issue of encampments.
Recognizing the unique challenges of Northern Ontario, the report recommends "allocating up to 20 per cent of these investments to address regional capacity gaps, infrastructure deficits, and the disproportionate impact on Indigenous populations."
Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles said the report speaks to the province's lack of action on the crisis.
"Encampments and homelessness in every single community are a stark reminder of this government's failure," she said in a release. "After six years, there is still no real plan to address the critical lack of affordable housing that is forcing so many of our neighbours out in the cold." The full report can be read by visiting tinyurl.com/3w4kth2w. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Shelters; Homeless people; Northern communities

Author: Initiative Reporter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/supreme-court-canada-could-hear-generation/docview/3154130506/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is taking its fight against seven youth climate activists - including Sophia Mathur of Sudbury - to the Supreme Court of Canada after Ontario's highest court sided with the activists in October.

Document 92 of 281

Gelinas to run once again for the Ontario NDP in Nickel Belt; She has won every provincial election since 2007

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gelinas-run-once-again-ontario-ndp-nickel-belt/docview/3153326082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gelinas+to+run+once+again+for+the+Ontario+NDP+in+Nickel+Belt%3B+She+has+won+every+provincial+election+since+2007&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: France Gélinas hopes to make it five in a row.
New Democrats in Nickel Belt have chosen Gélinas as their Ontario NDP candidate for the next provincial election. First elected in 2007, Gelinas has won four elections since then in a riding the NDP has dominated provincially for five decades.
In 2022, Gelinas took slightly more than 50 per cent of the popular vote, easily defeating her closest rival, Randy Hazlett of the Progressive Conservatives. Algoma-Manitoulin-Kapuskasing MP Carol Hughes and Chelmsford resident Claude Berthiaume nominated Gelinas.
"I am deeply honoured and excited to once again be nominated as the Ontario NDP candidate for Nickel Belt," Gelinas said in a statement. "Serving the people of this incredible community for 17 years and counting has been the privilege of a lifetime.
"I am inspired every day by the strength, resilience, and determination of the people of Nickel Belt. I am very confident that alongside the leadership, vision and dedication of our leader Marit Stiles, we will form a government that works as hard as Ontarians do.
"Together, we will work for a brighter, better future for everyone in this province."
The Progressive Conservatives now form the provincial government and do not have to call an election until June 4, 2026. However, there has been plenty of speculation in Queen's Park that Premier Doug Ford would like to call an election before a federal vote is held this year and before the Conservatives under Pierre Poilievre take power in Ottawa. Polls show Poilievre heading to an easy, even historic, victory.
When the provincial election is held, Stiles said she will be happy to have the veteran Nickel Belt MPP, who serves as the party's Health critic, running for the NDP. "France's work and dedication to her community is nothing short of exemplary," Stiles said. "She has been a fierce advocate for our public health care system and one of the strongest voices pushing back against Ford's privatization attempt.
"France always puts the needs of her community and our province before anything else. I am proud to have France on our team as we work to deliver a better, brighter future for the people of Ontario."
Gélinas noted that Nickel Belt is the seventh largest in the province, has 17 Ontario Parks and is comprised of over 30 communities including three First Nations.
She said as the New Democratic Party's Health Critic since being elected, she "takes pride in representing the party of Tommy Douglas, who brought us Medicare. France believes that Ontarians deserve a high-quality health care system, one that is based on needs and not on ability to pay."
Gelinas, who calls herself a proud Franco-Ontarian, lives in Whitefish with her spouse Keith. They have three children and 10 grandchildren.
She said she enjoys the outdoors, loves rowing, kayaking, snowboarding, snowmobiling and is always ready for an adventure.
While Gélinas was born and raised in Shawinigan, Que., she has an MBA from Laurentian University in Sudbury, is a licensed physiotherapist and an airplane pilot.
She has been the executive director of the Community Health Centre of Sudbury, served on the United Way's Citizens'Advisory Panel and as president of the Francophone Reference Group of the Northern Ontario School of Medicine. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 93 of 281

Sudbury's transitional housing project lacking, report suggests; Ontario's auditor general says province's decision to remove harm-reduction tools, such as safe consumption, is flawed and dangerous

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburys-transitional-housing-project-lacking/docview/3153325780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury%27s+transitional+housing+project+lacking%2C+report+suggests%3B+Ontario%27s+auditor+general+says+province%27s+decision+to+remove+harm-reduction+tools%2C+such+as+safe+consumption%2C+is+flawed+and+dangerous&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Greater Sudbury is taking further steps to address homelessness and substance misuse; however, the model it has adopted has been criticized by Ontario's auditor general.
The transitional housing project on Lorraine Street is nearing completion and should be ready for occupancy within the next few months. That project will include a Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hub, which includes companion services for those striving to overcome a mental health or addiction crisis.
It will not, however, include access to harm-reduction tools, including clean needles.
The Ontario government announced last week it would convert nine former supervised consumption sites, which were all funded by the province, to HART hubs. They will be located in Toronto, Ottawa, Hamilton, Kitchener, Guelph and Thunder Bay. Eventually, the province plans to establish 19 HART hubs across the province.
As the province notes (tinyurl. com/yc3t6yuv), "upon arrival, each client is welcomed and a member of staff discusses what they require. This provides a safe and welcoming space for the client to identify their needs and access immediate support.
"Often, due to their circumstances, clients will require basic needs (e.g. food, warm shower, primary care services, shelter) before accessing other services. Naloxone services are also provided where necessary. These allow for a client's immediate needs to be met and readies them for more treatment-focused services."
Following their visit to the hub, clients may receive support onsite or via "integrated care pathways," which involve primary care; mental health and addictions services, which could include detox; referrals; access to an emergency shelter; and case management.
"Clients who require longer-term services and supports can be supported to access a range of stability supports, including treatment services such as bed-based addictions services or supportive housing supports through the provision of rent supplements," the government notes. "These provide the necessary factors to prevent the client from experiencing a 'revolving door'of services."
The province hopes to transition the former supervised consumption sites by March 31. The hubs will be eligible to receive up to four times more funding from the province than they did previously when they functioned as consumption sites.
City officials said Greater Sudbury's safe consumption site was not eligible for funding through this stream, as it was never funded by the province. The facility, a trailer located in Energy Court, is currently being used as a warming centre for those who live unsheltered.
But Cassandra Maki, a spokesperson for the municipality, said Tom Davies Square "has collaborated with community partners to submit an application for a HART hub under a different funding stream, and the approval for these applications has not yet been announced."
Those partners include Shkagamik-Kwe Health Centre, Health Sciences North, the Sudbury/Manitoulin branch of the Canadian Mental Health Association, Monarch Recovery Services and Northern Initiative for Social Action.
The bulk of the services will be offered through the Lorraine Street project; however, Maki said "additional services will be offered through a downtown hub location, yet to be determined."
The goal of the hubs is to serve high-acuity clients (meaning patients who are in a dangerous condition) and to address the underlying issues that lead to homelessness. But they will not incorporate harm reduction mechanisms, such as a safer drug supply, the supervised consumption of drugs or needle exchange programs.
The hubs are intended to provide "low-barrier access to a range of locally identified services that best meet the complex needs of individuals who experience a range of overlapping issues and marginalization, including homelessness, substance use issues, unemployment and mental health challenges," Maki said.
However, closing supervised consumption sites and cancelling harm reduction protocols will undermine government efforts to address the opioid crisis, Shelley Spence, Ontario's auditor general, determined in a recent report.
Spence said the decision by Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones to shutter the supervised consumption sites by March and to prevent new sites from operating was made without public consultation or analysis of the impact.
"The audit shows the (government's) decision was made despite direct evidence that indicates safe injection facilities, which provide a controlled environment for people to consume substances in a supervised setting with sterilized equipment and overdose-prevention support, have reversed thousands of drug-related fatalities since their inception," Paige Peacock told The Pointer.
"It also found the province and Ministry of Health had not developed plans to mitigate the risks prior to making the decision, including increased overdoses and the financial and operational burden on emergency departments."
Advocates and those who work on the frontlines endorse harm reduction.
"Addictions and Mental Health Ontario has warned that 'Without access to harm reduction and addictions services, Ontarians are more likely to use alone, where they become increasingly susceptible to the often harmful and even fatal effects of an increasingly toxic drug supply'," Peacock wrote. "The organization has instead stressed the best way to protect people from the increasingly toxic drug supply is to expand access to harm reduction services."
The Star asked the municipality about the evidence that supports the HART hub care model; however, the city referred the newspaper to the government website. There is no information on this site alluding to the research that led the Ford government to adopt HART hubs.
"As indicated in the City of Greater Sudbury's roadmap to end homelessness by 2030, additional supportive housing, as well as access to mental health and addiction services, is required to meet the needs of the unhoused population within the community," Maki said. "The city will be seeking funding opportunities to meet these recommendations through all levels of government." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown BlueSky: @mkkeown.bsky.social Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Primary care; Emergency medical care; Drug overdose; Mental health; Addictions; Funding; Homeless people; Harm reduction

Identifier / keyword: greater; sudbury; taking; further; steps; address

Document 94 of 281

Trudeau put country first in stepping down, Sudbury MP says; Deferring to a statement released by his leader, the Conservative candidate in Sudbury called for 'a carbon tax election now'

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-put-country-first-stepping-down-sudbury/docview/3152323563/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+put+country+first+in+stepping+down%2C+Sudbury+MP+says%3B+Deferring+to+a+statement+released+by+his+leader%2C+the+Conservative+candidate+in+Sudbury+called+for+%27a+carbon+tax+election+now%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Monday he'll step down after his Liberal party chooses its next leader in a "robust, nationwide competitive process," telling reporters assembled at Rideau Cottage that he asked party president Sachit Mehra on Sunday to start preparing for the contest.
Trudeau also said that Governor General Mary Simon had granted his request to prorogue Parliament until March 24.
Not surprisingly, the reaction in Sudbury to Trudeau's decision to step down was mixed.
Sudbury MP Viviane Lapointe said Trudeau's resignation stemmed, ultimately, from his commitment to Canada.
"The prime minister has made a difficult decision. I respect his thorough, careful consideration of the issues and his deep regard for what Canadians are calling for at this time," Lapointe said in a statement. "His message is clear: country first."
Lapointe said Trudeau was one of the most progressive prime ministers since Lester Bowles Pearson, who served as Canada's 14th prime minister, from 1963 to 1968. She said Trudeau could point to several accomplishments during his nine years as prime minister.
He "successfully presided over the first Trump presidency; appointed the first gender-balanced cabinet; kept Canadians safe during a global pandemic; and implemented far-reaching progressive programs that have lifted millions of Canadians out of poverty," she commented.
"Regarding what comes next for the Liberal party, our steadfast commitment to Liberal values will guide our discussions and actions over the coming weeks."
Lapointe said as the Liberals seek out a new leader, she will work "to ensure we select a leader who understands the challenges and the opportunities for Sudbury and Northern Ontario. As the MP for Sudbury, I remain deeply committed to the work and will continue to advocate for the best interests of Sudbury."
Conservative candidate Ian Symington, a family doctor based in Copper Cliff, had a different take on Trudeau. He referred to a statement made Monday by party leader Pierre Poilievre.
"Now, while leaderless Liberals focus on saving their jobs and fighting each other for power, the country spirals out of control - an out-of-control housing emergency, an out-ofcontrol migrant crisis, an out-ofcontrol $62 billion deficit, not to mention tariff threats from the United States.
"Oh, and another NDP-Liberal carbon tax hike kicks in this April 1. Everything is out of control ... and now, the government is out-ofcontrol," Poilievre commented in the statement, noting "every Liberal MP in power today and every potential Liberal leadership contender fighting for the top job helped Justin Trudeau break the country over the last nine years.
"All Liberal politicians actively worked to pass into law the job-killing, inflationary carbon tax - a tax that Carbon Tax (possible leadership hopeful Mark) Carney endorsed in his book. All supported a law quadrupling the tax over the next five years.
"All voted for or actively supported Trudeau's out-of-control spending, debt and immigration. All passed Trudeau's housing policies that doubled the cost of homes. All Liberal politicians helped pass catch-and-release bail and house arrest for the most rampant re-offenders, policies that increased violent crime by 50 per cent, gun crime by 116 per cent and hate crime by 250 per cent."
Poilievre said the Liberals understand how "wrong this all is; that's exactly why they shut down parliament and paralyze government, all to save their own skins. This cannot go on.
"We need a carbon tax election now, to choose between the NDP-Liberal costly coalition that taxes your food, punishes your work, doubles your housing costs and unleashes crime and drugs in your community; or Common Sense Conservatives who will axe the tax, build the homes, fix the budget and stop the crime."
Poilievre used Trudeau's announcement to endorse his own policies and the politics of the Conservative Party.
"Canadians can take back control of their lives and their country; take back control of our border; take back control of immigration; take back control of spending, deficits and inflation; take back control of our streets by locking up criminals, banning drugs, treating addiction and stopping gun smugglers," he rallied. "We'll cap spending, axe taxes, reward work, build homes, uphold family, stop crime, secure borders, re-arm our forces, restore our freedom and put Canada first."
Trudeau, who has led the federal Liberals since 2013 and served as prime minister since 2015, said he will continue to lead the party until a successor is chosen.
Trudeau delivered the news outside Rideau Cottage, his official residence in Ottawa, on a cold and windy day that saw some of his speech notes fly from his wooden podium.
"I intend to resign as party leader, as prime minister after the party selects its next leader through a robust, nationwide, competitive process," said an emotional Trudeau.
"This country deserves a real choice in the next election, and it has become clear to me that if I'm having to fight internal battles, I cannot be the best option in that election."
The prime minister's announcement sets the stage for the Liberal party's first leadership election since 2013.
It will also be the party's first leadership race under a major 2016 rule change, which allowed anyone willing to register as a Liberal for free to vote in party contests. What happens next? Party president Sachit Mehra has said he'll call a meeting of the party's national board of directors, to be held this week. At the meeting, the board will set a leadership election date and appoint a committee to oversee the contest.
The board will also appoint a separate expenses committee to collect monetary deposits from leadership candidates and set spending rules. When will the leadership vote take place? The timing of the vote is already a matter of passionate debate in the party. One Liberal elder statesman, Eddie Goldenberg, has called for the Liberal caucus to quickly choose Trudeau's replacement within the next few weeks, ideally by the end of January.
Other Liberal insiders have said there's no way around a full membership vote, which would push back the timeline to the late winter or early spring.
The constitution sets out a campaign period of at least 90 days but allows the board to push the date forward under special "political circumstances." Who are the contenders? Cabinet members Melanie Joly, François-Philippe Champagne and Anita Anand are all thought to be interested in the job, as is ex-finance minister Chrystia Freeland. Ex-Bank of Canada Governor Mark Carney and former British Columbia Christy Clark are also rumoured to be organizing for possible leadership bids. -with files from the Canadian Press and Postmedia

Document 95 of 281

Sudbury will use extra provincial dollars to fix roads, sewers, water lines; The additional $2.7 million came as a pleasant surprise to the city, Mayor Paul Lefebvre says

Author: Carmichael, Harold

Publication info: Carmichael, Harold. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-will-use-extra-provincial-dollars-fix/docview/3152323543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+will+use+extra+provincial+dollars+to+fix+roads%2C+sewers%2C+water+lines%3B+The+additional+%242.7+million+came+as+a+pleasant+surprise+to+the+city%2C+Mayor+Paul+Lefebvre+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Carmichael%2C+Harold&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Greater Sudbury is getting an additional $2.7 million from the province through the Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund to put towards its infrastructure and social services.
The additional 12 per cent in funding, which will bring the city's 2025 Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund money to more than $24 million, was announced by Rural Affairs Minister Lisa Thompson at a press conference at Tom Davies Square on Monday.
"Ontario is indeed a place where we can raise a family, grow a business, and, most importantly, be proud to call it home," said Thompson, as she announced the additional funding. "Northern and rural communities matter. There's that vibe we are feeling. There are so many diverse opportunities we all want to be supportive of ...
"Right across the province, we have diverse communities that are working hard ... We want to make sure we don't miss anything (opportunities) that's on the horizon."
Greater Sudbury is one of 44 per cent of Ontario municipalities that receive Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund funding and get at least 10 per cent of their fiscal budget from the province, noted Thompson.
"Local economies matter: Premier (Doug) Ford gets it," said the minister.
Mayor Paul Lefebvre said the additional funding will be put towards infrastructure such as sewer, water and roads projects, as well as social services.
"It's a very significant funding boost for us," he said. "This funding is coming at a crucial time. We are investing in housing, infrastructure, roads, strengthening the foundations of the community ... This is a good example of partnership."
After the press conference, Lefebvre said the additional $2.7 million came as a surprise as the city's Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund funding has not changed for at least a decade.
He said the $2.7 million will go into the $50-million social services and infrastructure budget.
"We really appreciate it," he said.
hcarmichael@postmedia.com X: @HaroldCarmichae
