Document 96 of 281

This was the moment Trudeau told us he was done

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-was-moment-trudeau-told-us-he-done/docview/3152323448/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+was+the+moment+Trudeau+told+us+he+was+done&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau didn't decide to resign this past week, sources say. The decision was made weeks ago, in plain view.
And few noticed. It happened Dec. 18. On that date, Trudeau's PMO abruptly cancelled interviews with multiple media outlets: Global News, CBC, CTV, Radio-Canada, TVA Nouvelles, along with what was to be a joint interview with CityNews and Omni Television. The move was unprecedented.
Few Canadians understood the significance of that decision. But senior Liberals knew it almost certainly meant the government end of Trudeau's reign had arrived.
One very senior Liberal, who has years of experience with different prime ministers and their offices, said that is the moment when Trudeau had truly decided to go. "No leader has cancelled year-end interviews, ever," said the Liberal. "They're a tradition. They're important, because lots of Canadians watch them. And Justin cancelled."
Trudeau's behaviour in the days that followed did nothing to alter that decision, sources said. Trudeau made some canned remarks at the Liberal Christmas party on the Hill, and then just a few words to the media after he shuffled his cabinet for the last time. He then got on a Challenger jet and flew to British Columbia to ski.
Few, if any, heard from Trudeau during the crucial days when he needed to be working the phones to save his leadership. Trudeau mainly communicated only with the small circle who remain loyal to him, and with family members. Some urged him to stay and fight.
But Trudeau had no fight left in him. Everywhere he looked, sources said, his prospects were bad and getting worse. President-elect Donald Trump was mocking him on the world stage, calling him the governor of the 51st state. Caucus members - including the crucial Atlantic, Quebec and Ontario caucuses - started to demand publicly that he step aside. And an Angus Reid Institute poll was issued, suggesting the Liberals had fallen to only 16 per cent support nationally.
That's not all: Liberal leadership campaigns started organizing, more or less openly, and talking to the media - anonymously, of course - about their prospects in the post-Trudeau era. Among them was Dominic LeBlanc, who Trudeau considers one of his closest friends, and Chrystia Freeland, the former deputy prime minister who had dramatically quit Trudeau's cabinet two days before he cancelled his year-end interviews.
"He's human, you know," said another Liberal insider. "He was down. He asked his circle (of advisers) if there was any way to hold on and avoid a full caucus revolt. They came up with nothing."
"It had nothing to do with (Conservative Leader Pierre) Poilievre," said one Liberal. "He still thinks that Poilievre is a pipsqueak."
But the parliamentary holiday recess was like bankruptcy, another Liberal said. The process of unravelling is very slow, and then it suddenly reaches its grim conclusion very, very fast.
"He knew it was all over," said the senior Liberal. "His kids were saying to him, 'Dad, it's time to go home.'" Don't now expect an avalanche of patronage appointments, the sources agree. That was the main factor that defeated former Liberal leader John Turner in 1984. Liberals have been mad enough at Trudeau as it is, they say, and he doesn't want to make them any angrier.
As for the future, Trudeau is not worried.
His family's wealth have made him a multimillionaire, and he will be paid handsomely on the book and speech circuit. He has no shortage of job prospects, either. Some suggest a soft landing at the United Nations, but other sources doubt that.
Whatever happens, Justin Trudeau has finally taken what his father memorably called a "walk in the snow." He loved the job of prime minister, and he felt he was doing right by Canadians.
But Canadians clearly felt otherwise. And in mid-December, when no one was really looking, Justin Trudeau finally took note, and made his decision. Warren Kinsella is a Canadian lawyer, author, musician, political consultant, and commentator.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: justin; trudeau; decide; resign; sources; decision

Sudbury taxpayers will help pay for underwater parking lot in Toronto

Author: Gupta, Akshay

Publication info: Gupta, Akshay. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-taxpayers-will-help-pay-underwater/docview/3152323409/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: I am concerned about the Ontario government's plan to build a huge private spa at Ontario Place. It is estimated that every taxpayer in the province will pay at least $65 so that a foreign-owned private spa can have an underwater parking lot.
The deal with Doug Ford's government also includes a 99-year lease to Therme, an Austrian company, with all the details kept secret. The plan includes removing hundreds of trees, with an environmental assessment being deemed unnecessary for this project.
I am very concerned that the provincial government is wasting our taxes on something that will only be enjoyed by the very few. Public money would be better spent on health care, affordable housing, roads and infrastructure for the benefit of the entire province.
Please contact your MPP to oppose this wasteful plan. Akshay Gupta Toronto

Document 98 of 281

Conservatives polling well in new Sudbury East- Manitoulin-Nickel Belt riding

Author: Castonguay, Adam

Publication info: Castonguay, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/conservatives-polling-well-new-sudbury-east/docview/3151505713/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservatives+polling+well+in+new+Sudbury+East-+Manitoulin-Nickel+Belt+riding&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Castonguay%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the Liberal/NDP coalition government falling apart, a federal election might come early in 2024, but many people are still surprised to learn that new electoral districts come into pplay with the next election, increasing the total number of MPs to 343, up by five from the current 338.
More ridings, unfortunately, won't mean more representation for Northern Ontario, which will be sending one less MP to Parliament.
Electoral districts covering Greater Sudbury are an expanded Sudbury riding and the new Sudbury East-Manitoulin-Nickel Belt riding.
The latter doesn't just have a big name but covers a huge area from as far west as Cockburn Island to West Nipissing in the east, to North of Gogama, surrounding the expanded Sudbury riding that took in Garson, Falconbridge, Coniston, Wahnapitae and most of the South End.
Voters can search their address through Elections Canada and the Redistribution website to confirm what riding they fall into.
Candidates for the redrawn Sudbury riding are Dr. Ian Symington for the Conservatives, Nadia Verrelli for the NDP, and incumbent Viviane Lapointe for the Liberals.
Candidates for the new Sudbury East-Manitoulin-Nickel Belt riding are Andréanne Chénier for the NDP and the sitting Nickel Belt MP, Marc Serré, for the Liberals.
The Conservatives, who are polling strong in the riding, have yet to select a candidate but several people, all from Greater Sudbury (and the writer Adam Castonguay included), are vying for the position and an exciting nomination contest is expected.

Document 99 of 281

'The silence is deafening'; More Liberal MPs are demanding Justin Trudeau make a decision

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/silence-is-deafening-more-liberal-mps-are/docview/3151505307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+silence+is+deafening%27%3B+More+Liberal+MPs+are+demanding+Justin+Trudeau+make+a+decision&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Prime Minister Justin Trudeau returning from vacation on Friday, some Liberal MPs say the time has arrived for him to face Canadians and say whether or not he intends to resign.
Trudeau has spent the past two weeks since former finance minister Chrystia Freeland's stunning resignation largely out of the public eye, at first staying behind closed doors and then setting off on his annual Christmas vacation, this time to B.C. Trudeau had said he would use the time to reflect on his future.
In speaking to fellow Liberals since Parliament broke for its holiday recess, Helena Jaczek, an Ontario MP who has called for Trudeau to quit, said what has become noticeable now is his silence.
"We want to keep our party together, and at this point, we're not hearing from the prime minister," she told Postmedia in an interview.
"I think everyone understood the need for some reflection, but it is what, more than two weeks now since Chrystia Freeland resigned. And so what I'm hearing that's a little different is, 'well the silence is deafening. Where's the leadership in a time of crisis?'" Another Ontario Liberal MP, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, said the calls for to Trudeau to resign are coming from every corner, whether it's from residents who are not usually close followers of politics or "dyed-inthe-wool Liberals."
That MP said time is running out before others in caucus get prepared to add their name to the roster of roughly 20 Liberal MPs who have gone public with their desire for Trudeau to resign, adding the fact that the prime minister has yet to make a decision "is almost as bad" as the blow Freeland dealt by resigning on the day she was set to deliver the government's fiscal update, announcing her move publicly in a scathing letter.
"This isn't just about Liberal political intrigue," the MP said. "Canadians are looking at a situation where they genuinely have a concern about who will be leading the country and what the plan is going forward on confronting the Trump threat."
Regional caucus chairs were set to meet on Friday to discuss the requests to hold a national caucus meeting.
Of the 51 out of 75 Ontario Liberal MPs who met after the House of Commons broke for the holidays, most said that Trudeau should resign - a message that Jaczek says has been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office, citing information from the group's chair. Ontario Liberal caucus chair Michael Coteau had yet to respond to a request for comment from Postmedia.
"As has already been widely reported, there was a consensus. Obviously, that's not unanimity, but there was a consensus that the prime minister should resign," said Jaczek.
A Quebec Liberal MP confirmed the sentiment that Trudeau must go is also shared by members within its provincial caucus, although its chair, Stéphane Lauzon, clarified in a statement posted to X that no letter has been signed or circulated by its caucus since they last met on Dec. 11, which was several days before Freeland resigned.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long shared the letter sent by the Atlantic Liberal caucus chair, Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois, asking that Trudeau step aside, telling Postmedia that caucus members feel "disillusioned" that the prime minister has not yet responded to the growing calls.
"People are beyond mad now," Long says, "I think they're just really sad that the prime minister and in particular (the Prime Minister's Office) and those around him have allowed this to happen."
"Good leadership, of course, should have a lot of reflection, but good leadership also acknowledges when his caucus and, more importantly, Canadians, want to hear from him."
Long, who was one of the earliest to publicly call for Trudeau to quit following the party's surprise byelection loss in the former Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's last June, said he expects Trudeau ultimately will resign.
"There is no other option," he said. "It's time to get out of fantasyland and get back to reality and reality clearly states that the prime minister's time is up."
Other Liberal MPs believe he will do the same, including B.C. MP Ken Hardie who said he believes Trudeau will do so "for the good of the country," as the Liberals enter an election year where they are set to face Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has been riding high in public opinion polls since summer 2023, while Trudeau has been unsuccessful in his attempts to gain back any favour among Canadians.
Hardie pointed to a retreat the Liberal caucus had that year in London, Ont., where MPs "were assured at that time that there was a path forward."
"The leader and the Prime Minister's Office, they're pretty smart people so (we) said, 'OK, fine, let's see what that path forward is.'Well, so far we haven't seen it."
While Hardie said he believes Trudeau deserves to take the time he needs to reflect on his next steps, "the sooner the better" it would be to present a plan to Canadians.

Randall Denley: How Justin Trudeau ruined Doug Ford's plan for an early election

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-how-justin-trudeau-ruined-doug/docview/3151435986/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+How+Justin+Trudeau+ruined+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+for+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For awhile, 2025 was shaping up to be a great year for Ontario Premier Doug Ford. Things were lining up nicely for a widely expected spring election. There was the feel-good advertising blitz, the $200 cheques for nearly every Ontarian, the promised gridlock-busting tunnel under Highway 401, the early arrival of a new era in alcohol retailing.

Document 101 of 281

Will transportation woes ever be fixed?; Recent developments are a mixed bag for travellers

Author: Boutilier, Éric

Publication info: Boutilier, Éric. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-transportation-woes-ever-be-fixed-recent/docview/3147828134/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+transportation+woes+ever+be+fixed%3F%3B+Recent+developments+are+a+mixed+bag+for+travellers&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Boutilier%2C+%C3%89ric&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The American industrialist and business magnate Henry Ford once responded "You can't build a reputation on what you are going to do" when asked about his professional achievements.
It's a fitting analogy to describe how Parliament Hill and Queen's Park continue to sputter when attempting to run, re-establish or regulate public transportation.
For decades, numerous studies have been published on how to re-imagine the way people travel across the country and the province.
Whether by bus, plane, train or even highway expansion, politicians of all stripes claim they want to reduce gridlock in urban areas and improve mobility in rural and remote regions.
Yet, for all the initial hype surrounding these government-led initiatives, progress has often been sluggish. In some instances, projects have stalled or were outright abandoned. As a result, there remains:
Unprecedented delays and congestion at Pearson International Airport.
Endless debate on high-speed versus high-frequency rail.
Severely late intercity trains.
Impassable roads due to collisions and weather conditions.
Many setbacks on Toronto's Eglinton Crosstown LRT, Ottawa's O-Train and the now defunct London GO Train pilot.
Increased isolation in Northern Ontario due to reduced or discontinued motor coach, train and air services.
Who'd have thought getting around would be so difficult and complicated, a quarter of the way into the 21st century? It's been a rocky few months for the nation's passenger rail carrier, VIA Rail Canada.
Much was written about its Montréal-Québec City route, with customers being stranded for 10 hours and a parliamentary committee summoned to get to the bottom of things.
It's ironic because the federal government doesn't bat an eye whenever The Canadian - VIA's Toronto-to-Vancouver service - encounters similar delays through Northern Ontario, or when the Sudbury-White River train is cancelled at a moment's notice without alternate transportation.
The company has more pressing concerns, though: speed restrictions imposed by Canadian National Railway on its new Siemens Venture fleet.
It's kind of an existential crisis because these units are supposed to make train travel more dependable.
I hope Metrolinx and Ontario Northland pay close attention to the lawsuit filed by VIA, because this might impact redeployment of the Northlander.
Despite promising billboards, platform and shelter announcements between Matheson and Temagami, and ongoing reconstruction of the North Bay rail yard bypass, the Siemens train sets ordered by the province will face the same issues as VIA if they must also slow down at each railroad crossing that CN owns all the way to Toronto. Several questions remain unanswered:
When in 2026 will the Northlander return?
When does construction begin on the Timmins-Porcupine station, and the platforms and shelters between South River and Washago?
Will there be access to washroom facilities?
Will passengers seamlessly transfer between ONR trains, busses and local transit?
How scalable is the service? Earlier in 2024, the Township of Brock produced a report detailing the need for a stop in the community of Beaverton.
Consider there's a 110-km gap between the two nearest stations (Gormley and Washago), it seems like an odd decision to bypass the fourth largest catchment area on this route - roughly 26,000 people within a 15-minute drive of Beaverton.
Moreover, why doesn't The Canadian stop here, at one of the GO stations north of Toronto, and at other reasonably-sized communities further up the line (Bala and French River)? These are missed opportunities.
Transportation in this country is at a crossroads.
Prime Minister Justine Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford each have an opportunity to steer Canada and Ontario in the right direction. Their decisions will ultimately determine whether public transit has a bright or gloomy future.
Choose wisely.
Éric Boutilier is a columnist for Northern Tracks, a self-published blog related to intercity transportation in Northern Ontario.

Document 102 of 281

More Greater Sudburians using transit than ever, numbers show; $14 million over 10 years from the federal government will allow city to add buses, including electric ones

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-greater-sudburians-using-transit-than-ever/docview/3147828116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+Greater+Sudburians+using+transit+than+ever%2C+numbers+show%3B+%2414+million+over+10+years+from+the+federal+government+will+allow+city+to+add+buses%2C+including+electric+ones&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A financial boost from the federal government could be a real game-changer for Greater Sudbury's transit service, Mayor Paul Lefebvre said Thursday.
Sudbury MP Viviane Lapointe, Lefebvre and Nickel Belt MP Marc Serre met with media at the Lorne Street fleet centre for a $14-million funding announcement - about $1.4 million annually for 10 years, beginning in 2026.
The money, funnelled through the baseline funding stream of the Canada Public Transit Fund, will be used to further modernize the transit system and to "help advance key improvements to GOVA."
This stable and predictable funding will help the city plan, upgrade, replace or modernize current transit and active transportation infrastructure, the Liberals said in a release.
Lefebvre said Tom Davies Square will use some of the money to grow the municipal fleet and to purchase electric vehicles as they become available.
"With more ridership, we need more buses," he said. "As we're doing that, we're going to be transferring from diesel to electric buses over the next 10 years; we're starting that transition. The challenge is, there are not enough electric buses being manufactured at the same time. It's kind of a chicken-and-egg."
Lefebvre said the city also plans to modernize the GOVA app, so that users can track buses in real time.
"We're really looking at modernizing the way we offer the service, and expanding the service as much as we can," he commented.
There is a need to continue to modernize and grow the fleet. As Lefebvre noted during Thursday's announcement, staff predict 6.2 million rides on GOVA Transit in 2024 - up one million rides from the previous year.
"The service is more reliable and there's more frequency," Lefebvre said Thursday about the boost in ridership. "If residents do not feel they can rely on buses, they'll look for alternatives. We have been increasing the bus frequency, as well as the number of bus routes over the past years."
Lefebvre said the city has spent years building trust with transit users. An overhaul of the system a few years ago - complete with councillors acting as ambassadors on day one - was a positive first step.
Now, residents know they can rely on the service and "it's affordable, especially when you compare to the alternative - taking your own vehicle," Lefebvre commented. "We really feel the increased ridership is based on more confidence in the system. The buses are there and they can rely on that."
Currently, the city fleet includes 63 regular buses, as well as 15 GOVA+ buses, which services Sudburians will physical and mental challenges. Lefebvre said there are plans to grow the fleet by ten.
The transit system is a lifeline for many residents, Lefebvre said, especially those who do not own a vehicle. He said expansion would not be possible without the assistance of senior levels of government. Ridership only covers about 30 per cent of costs. Lefebvre said subsidies account for the remaining 70 per cent.
"I'll be honest with you," Lefebvre said. If the federal and provincial governments were not involved, "we'd be at half the service. If not, the municipal (tax) levy would be even more than it is today. It's a partnership between all three levels of government."
He pointed to the evolution of the transit service over the past 25 years.
"We were not at 6.2 million 25 years ago - not at all. The frequency of buses was not there and reliability was always a challenge," he said of the service of yore. "Now we're really trying to connect the city."
That remains a challenge for a municipality as large as Greater Sudbury. The city is massive - the largest by land mass in Ontario and the second-biggest municipality in Canada. It extends from Beaver Lake to Kukagami Lake Road, and as far north as Levack.
Lefebvre said city staff will focus on connecting outlying communities to the core, as well as some of the routes within the former city.
"There are some routes in the old city that are packed," he said. "What we're trying to do is encourage more transit use, not less, so that means more frequency of buses. It's when they can't rely on it that people are discouraged."
Serre said the investment will be good for the environment.
It will "reduce traffic; cut air pollution; and improve access to essential services, family and friends," he commented.
"Upgrading transit infrastructure supports housing growth, increases capacity and enhances social inclusion, making life more affordable for everyone."
The Canada Public Transit Fund is the largest public transit investment in Canadian history. It is designed to meet the unique needs of all communities - from metropolitan areas to mid-size and smaller communities. This includes rural, remote, northern and Indigenous communities.
"The fund will deliver stable funding for municipalities and (provide) transit agencies with the resources they need to plan and implement key public transit projects that will meet their needs for decades to come," the Liberals said in a release.
Lapointe said the federal government launched the public transit fund in July, in response to demands from growing cities.
"That means there's a greater demand on transit systems," she said. "What we are trying to do is help municipalities deal directly with those types of increases - that's what this fund is for."
For her, a good transit system hinges on reliability.
"That's more important than ever now, as we see people really struggling with the increased cost of living," Lapointe said. "You have more and more people who are relying on the public transit system. It's a way to help people directly. ... It's more important now than ever."
Lapointe said stable and predictable funding for the next decade will help the city with planning and decision-making. Ultimately, it is about accessibility, convenience and affordability for residents, and Lapointe said Thursday's announcement will allow GOVA to "expand and meet the growing needs in the community." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown BlueSky: @mkkeown.bsky.social Facebook: @mkkeown

Your Independent Grocer in Lively under new management; Roger and Nancy Beaulieu welcome customers starting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/your-independent-grocer-lively-under-new/docview/3144465146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Your+Independent+Grocer+in+Lively+under+new+management%3B+Roger+and+Nancy+Beaulieu+welcome+customers+starting&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Your Independent Grocer has announced the grand opening of Roger and Nancy's Your Independent Grocer, which was held on Dec. 13.
Located at 65 Regional Road 24 in Lively, the newly renovated store is under new management, offering customers an expanded assortment of grocery and essential items to help customers bring more to the table.
"We are excited to welcome the greater Sudbury community like Lively, Walden, Atikameksheng Anishinabek and Naughton to our newly renovated store," store owners Roger and Nancy Beaulieu said in a joint statement. "From the carefully curated product selection to the warm and inviting atmosphere, we want every customer to feel welcomed, valued and inspired when they walk through our doors."
Roger and Nancy's Your Independent Grocer will feature a full assortment of produce, meat, dairy, seafood, baked goods, organic and gluten-free products, as well as multicultural products from multiple cuisines.
The store also features a "robust" selection of ready-to-eat and ready-to-heat meals, including fresh pizza, flatbread pizza, subs, sandwiches, pasta dishes and more.
Roger Beaulieu has been in the grocery business for more than 30 years, starting as a meat cutter and progressing to an assistant store manager before opening his first franchise store in 2017.
Nancy's retail career began in 2017 and has covered various parts of grocery retail.
As part of the store's commitment to supporting the local community, it will donate $1,000 to Northern Ontario Families of Children With Cancer (NOFCC), an independent Canadian volunteer-based registered charity established in 1998 by a group of parents of children with cancer.
The NOFCC aims to provide hope, support, education and advocacy to families who have a child with cancer.
Roger and Nancy's Your Independent Grocer is open Sundays from 8 a.m. to 7 p.m., Mondays from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. and Tuesdays through Saturdays from 7 a.m. to 10 p.m. About Loblaw Companies Limited:
Loblaw Companies Limited is one of Canada's food and pharmacy leaders, as well as its largest retailer and private sector employer.
With more than one billion transactions each year at its 2,500 stores and national e-commerce options, Loblaw brings food, pharmacy, beauty, apparel and financial services to customers through many of Canada's favourite and most-trusted brands: President's Choice, No Name, Loblaws, Shoppers Drug Mart, No Frills, Real Canadian Superstore, T&T, Joe Fresh, PC Express and PC Financial.
The company's loyalty program, PC Optimum, has more than 16 million active members and is one of Canada's largest and best-loved reward programs. Star staff sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Document 107 of 281

Liberal minister says sales tax reprieve will be good for Sudburians; 'I think it's always a good day to provide support to Canadians, especially during expensive times,'Jenna Sudds says

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberal-minister-says-sales-tax-reprieve-will-be/docview/3144464700/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+minister+says+sales+tax+reprieve+will+be+good+for+Sudburians%3B+%27I+think+it%27s+always+a+good+day+to+provide+support+to+Canadians%2C+especially+during+expensive+times%2C%27Jenna+Sudds+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you haven't got your Christmas tree yet, or anything to put beneath one, your procrastination will actually pay off in a bit of savings.
A two-month sales tax reprieve on many items - including takeout food, toys, books and, yes, conifers - kicks in this weekend, just in time for us tardy shoppers.
"Undoubtedly there are many folks in Sudbury and across the country who are having a tough time with affordability challenges, so this is a measure that will really help to address that, particularly through the holiday season and the winter, which is typically an expensive time of the year," said Jenna Sudds, minister of Families, Children and Social Development.
While the break on HST and GST will shrink revenue for federal coffers, Sudds said the government felt it was doable given the current state of the economy.
"We are seeing inflation rates back on target and five consecutive cuts of interest rates, coming down faster than anywhere else - granted we know it is taking a while for Canadians to feel that," she said. "But we're in a strong position to be able to move this forward, and frankly I think it's always a good day to provide support to Canadians, especially during expensive times."
The measure could be particularly welcomed by parents of young kids. Not only are toys free from tax, but so are diapers, car seats, and clothing and footwear for children.
Pet parents will be disappointed, as kibble, catnip and Kongs are not on the list of tax-exempt items. On the other hand, you could still get a pup cup at Salute or Starbucks while also getting yourself a beverage and treat without paying tax.
Most groceries are already untaxed but some items that typically are will now be cheaper to ring through the cash. These include prepared foods (like sandwiches, salads and pre-made meals), as well as energy bars, baked goods, granola bars and gift baskets.
Books, magazines and newspapers are also getting a tax break - as long as they are the old-fashioned printed variety - and you can enjoy your reading material with a tax-free bottle of beer, goblet of wine, or mug of java. But not rum and eggnog, or at least not the first part, as spirits and liqueurs did not make the cut.
While many see the move as more of a gimmick than a gain, and it could cause some pricing and reckoning headaches for retailers, Sudds believes the break is being welcomed overall and many businesses are anticipating more traffic in their aisles and drive-through lanes.
"I've certainly been having some conversations with small businesses, and as recently as Wednesday the CRA (Canada Revenue Agency) has published online additional guidance to make sure businesses have the information they need to administer this," she said. "But I've also had some really encouraging conversations from retailers and restaurants who have shared their sales have been down and they are encouraged by the prospect of folks being motivated to make these additional expenditures."
The minister also noted the tax relief is just one step of several the government has taken to make life more affordable for Canadians, pointing to the Canada Child Benefit as an example.
"I'm sure there are thousands of families in Sudbury who are receiving this monthly benefit," she said. "If you are getting the maximum, that's almost $8,000 a year per child, so this is real, tangible support."
She also highlighted efforts to make child care and dental care more affordable, as well as a school food program the feds recently inked with Ontario. "All of these measures, stacked one on top of the other, are helping to address the challenges that we know families and individuals are facing."
Meanwhile, Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre has, in her view, ironically and hypocritically opposed the HST hiatus.
"He self-proclaims he wants to 'axe the tax'but did not choose to support axing this tax," she said. "It became very evident as we were moving this work through the House of Commons that he would rather play political games with this. He has turned his back, frankly, on the people of Sudbury and across this country by not supporting this tax break for Canadians."
Poilievre, a vocal advocate for cutting taxes, has said the GST break "isn't a tax cut."
"This is an inflationary, twomonth temporary tax trick that will drive up the cost of living," Poilievre said last month.
"My tax cuts are not just about lowering costs, they're about sparking more production. By axing the carbon tax, our businesses can hire more workers and produce more goods. By axing the sales tax, we're going to get 30,000 extra homes per year," Poilievre said, referencing his proposals to scrap the federal fuel charge and the GST charged on new home builds under $1 million.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, meanwhile, is promising to remove the GST from what he called daily essentials if his party forms the next federal government, CBC Radio has reported.
"New Democrats are going to give you a break on your daily essentials and monthly bills," Singh told an audience last month at the Canadian Club in Toronto.
"We are going to take the GST off of everyday costs like heating your home, your cell phone bills, your internet, food that you buy at the grocery and your children's needs."
Local MP Viviane Lapointe said she knows from talking to Sudburians that affordability is a top concern, and she is pleased the GST break will help ease some of that pressure.
"It's wonderful to see everyday essentials like groceries, children's clothing, and even Christmas trees become more affordable for families," she said in a statement. "The inclusion of things like prepared foods and children's toys will bring some relief to parents and caregivers.
"I'm truly hopeful this will make the holidays a little brighter for Sudbury families, and I'm proud to support a policy that puts the needs of families first." -with files from Canadian Press jmoodie@postmedia.com

Document 109 of 281

Greater Sudbury to encourage fourplex development in the city; Developers can move ahead with a project without needing to get a rezoning first

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greater-sudbury-encourage-fourplex-development/docview/3143458991/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greater+Sudbury+to+encourage+fourplex+development+in+the+city%3B+Developers+can+move+ahead+with+a+project+without+needing+to+get+a+rezoning+first&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city is tweaking its housing rules to promote fourplex development in residential areas.
On Monday, the planning committee approved changes put forward by staff that will permit four-unit dwellings "as of right" - meaning a project can move forward without a rezoning application, whether it is a new build or conversion of an existing home.
The trend has been pushed by the Justin Trudeau government and allies who see this type of housing as a good way to address the so-called "missing middle" between big apartment towers and single-family homes, but it has its detractors, too. Some residents fear the character of neighbourhoods will be unfavourably altered and some premiers, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, have fought against this being imposed on municipalities.
(Ford has said that while he's in favour of four units on a single property, he's against the idea of four-storey buildings in neighbourhoods that are typically made up of single-family or semi-detached homes.)
There is a lot of federal money available, however, for cities that get on board with the plan.
The $6-billion Canada Housing Infrastructure Fund, launched in the spring, is available to municipal projects that "directly support the creation of new housing supply and improve densification," noted Ed Landry, a senior planner with the city. To access this money, however, "municipalities must have zoning in place and in effect for four units as-of-right."
At its Nov. 12 meeting, council directed staff to amend a policy it had recently adopted for third units to "allow for four units as-of-right, where appropriate," said Landry.
The proposed changes to the city's Official Plan and zoning bylaw will "allow a maximum of four units on a fully serviced parcel within the city's settlement areas, or three units within a primary building where there is a unit in an accessory building," he said. "All other zoning standards are proposed to stay the same - for example setbacks, building heights, parking requirements, etc."
Should the new rules earn council approval, staff will still "monitor development applications, such as zoning bylaw amendments and minor variances, to see if further changes are required," he said.
Planning staff recommend the policy, if implemented, be reviewed by the end of 2025. Coun. Rene Lapierre said he supported the direction being taken but wanted clarity on what steps a property owner would have to go through to convert a home into four units.
"Can somebody just go ahead and do it and decide their house is now four apartments?" he asked. "Or is there an application process required with a building permit and building code still relating to that?" Kris Longston, director of planning services, said the changes presented Monday only pertain to zoning and would not exempt someone from needing a building permit.
"You'd still be required to apply for a building permit to convert them, and that process would check to make sure other zoning requirements were met, such as parking, setbacks, height," he said. "What this change would do is remove the requirement to go for rezoning. But you would still have to comply with the Ontario Building Code."
Matt Labonte, a citizen who has previously run for council, expressed some concerns about how effective the move will be in solving the housing crisis, as well as concerns around safety.
"I just want to make sure we're all clear here that the rezoning is mostly about propping up property values, not increasing actual housing that's available," he said. "You're talking about taking properties that are single homes, in a lot of cases, and turning the investment value from, say, $2,400 to rent a house, to $1,200 a unit for four units. When you change the math on it, you increase the land value."
Labonte said he hoped the committee did not view the proposal as a "magic bullet to solve affordable housing issues, because it doesn't, and we still need to have non-market housing investment in Sudbury."
Those undertaking renovations also need to be supervised more closely, he suggested, to prevent shoddy work and potential tragedy.
"A lot of these homes, you end up with someone who has been told by a real estate agent that buying a second house and renting it out is a good investment," he said. "But they're not getting the proper support and in many cases oversight from the city on how to be a landlord and how to do the conversion properly.
"A lot of contractors will go in and say you're not taking out any structural stuff or changing the outside, so we don't need a permit. They go in and don't do the fire separation, the connected fire alarms, and so forth, and when the city goes in to inspect, it all gets torn out."
Labonte said he's encountered duplex conversions "where they had junction boxes completely buried in the wall," among other fire hazards.
"The city hasn't been that great on keeping up on this stuff to begin with, so if we're going to encourage people to convert their single-family home, we have to make sure the city is there not just for enforcement but to educate them, so they're jumping into this blind, and to make sure they're not getting their advice for dealing with tenants on Facebook," he said.
Others are more enthusiastic about the plan.
"This proposal supports the need for more housing - and specifically, smaller, more affordable units - in our community," said the Coalition for a Liveable Sudbury in a letter to council. "It also supports our community's climate goals."
The group pointed to an objective laid out in the Community Energy and Emissions Plan to "achieve energy efficiency and emissions reductions by creating compact, complete communities through infill developments, decreasing dwelling size through an increase in multi-family buildings, and increasing building type mix."
The change would "support more affordable housing, and more complete and walkable communities," said the coalition. "It would make it possible for more people to live in existing neighbourhoods, in more housing types and options."
It is also extremely important that the city have access to the Canada Housing Infrastructure Fund, the group said, and a fourplex policy is a prerequisite to any application to the CHIF.
"This type of support is very much needed to add badly needed affordable housing," the letter from the coalition states. jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Councils; Building codes; Neighborhoods; Affordable housing; Building permits; Planning; Zoning

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Document 110 of 281

Nadia Verrelli to run for Sudbury NDP in next federal election; 'We're going to fight to make life more affordable for Sudbury families'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nadia-verrelli-run-sudbury-ndp-next-federal/docview/3143458849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nadia+Verrelli+to+run+for+Sudbury+NDP+in+next+federal+election%3B+%27We%27re+going+to+fight+to+make+life+more+affordable+for+Sudbury+families%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sudbury New Democrats nominated Nadia Verrelli on Saturday as their candidate for the next federal election.
"I'm thrilled to have Nadia join the New Democrat team," NDP leader Jagmeet Singh said in a press release. "The NDP is the party of the working and middle class, and Nadia is joining the fight to bring costs down for them.
"For too long, Liberals have let Northern Ontario down. They're too weak to take on corporate greed and prices in the north have skyrocketed as a result. But the Conservatives now vowed to cut housing, pensions and dental care at a time when people need help the most. Those cuts will hurt people, and we're going to fight to stop them."
Verrelli also represented the NDP in the 2021 federal election, finishing behind Liberal candidate Viviane Lapointe and ahead of Conservative candidate Ian Symington in a tightly contested race.
Verrelli is a professor in the law and justice program at Laurentian University and "is passionate about good public healthcare, fighting the climate emergency and championing Franco-Ontarian culture." She is an advocate of the New Democrats'plan to remove the GST off daily essentials such as children's clothing and monthly bills, like home heating, internet and cell phone bills.
"It's an honour to run for the NDP," Verrelli said. "Northern Ontarians are struggling right now. All families are finding it hard to keep up. The Liberals in Northern Ontario have disappointed people - they let corporate greed drive up prices, then pushed back again and again against NDP plans to deliver relief, agreeing to half-measures like the winter tax holiday instead of permanently taking GST off essentials.
"The NDP believes in the Canadian value of taking care of each other - that's why we fought and won free dental care and pharmacare, and that's why we're going to fight to make life more affordable for Sudbury families." Star staff

Document 111 of 281

Doug ford is leading the way; Opinion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-is-leading-way-opinion/docview/3141997025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+ford+is+leading+the+way%3B+Opinion&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has had a successful run since being elected in 2018, but perhaps more than anything else, he deserves credit for expanding the subway system in Canada's largest city.
The Ontario Line will introduce nearly 16 kilometres of track to the fastest-growing city in North America, but it wasn't easy. Initially conceived as the Downtown Relief Line, it has been mired in politics and bureaucracy for decades - until Doug Ford came along and made it happen.
Without Ford's initiative, the Ontario Line would still be aspirational rather than under construction, although that seems lost on Toronto's chattering liberal class, for whom swallowing cyanide might be preferable to bestowing even a soupçon of credit upon a Conservative.
The Ontario Line is no doubt a legacy project for a premier who will be remembered for building the most significant infrastructure project the city has seen in decades.
Ford is presently being chided from all angles for his stance on bicycle lanes in Toronto, even though it's one of the most congested cities in the world. It doesn't take an urban planner to figure out that augmenting mass transit, not expanding underused bike lanes (which are used by a mere 1.2 per cent of the residents, according to census data), is the solution.
Nearly two decades after the Ontario Liberals introduced the Places to Grow Act, in part to curtail congestion, the tribulations of navigating Toronto's factory farm-like conditions have exposed kinks in the legislation's armour.
Bloor Street is down to a single lane in either direction along the hyper-dense expanse west of Spadina Avenue. Further to the north, Eglinton Avenue has been saddled with a bike lane that virtually no one uses but has somehow managed to make traffic around the notoriously congested Allen Road intersection even worse than before.
Ford's initiatives have nevertheless done precious little to stem the barrage of criticism he receives on a quotidian basis. And the Toronto Star, which dropped the mitts against the Ford family long ago, is unsurprisingly front and centre.
It recently ran a column accusing Ford of trying to hoodwink voters with trivialities like permitting grocers and convenience stores to sell alcohol. It's a shrill rebuke that reeks of the same patronizing paternalism that's mugged the province's hard-working taxpayers of the same consumer liberty that neighbouring Quebec has enjoyed from what seems like time immemorial.
The Star's petty, low-rent reprimand ignores the fact that Ford is merely adhering to arguably the most salient principle of free-market capitalism: competition between vendors results in lower consumer prices.
The Star must have forgotten that its columnists have also advocated for the exact thing Ford codified into law, having noted in the past that the Beer Store is a foreign-owned "cartel-cum-monopoly" and that its stores feel like "Stalinist relics ...
designed by Communist apparatchiks for the beer-swilling lumpenproletariat."
The Star might need a primer on its own founding principles and mission statement, which says that it believes in "individual and civil liberties," and works to "focus public attention on injustices of all kinds." Those "injustices" apparently don't include corporations colluding to price-gouge increasingly immiserated consumers.
The reality is that such free market policies augment razorthin profit margins for entrepreneurs, many of whom pour their life savings into ventures like neighbourhood convenience stores, and in case the chattering liberal class in Toronto hasn't noticed, everything is more expensive.
That includes small businesses'overheads, but alcohol sales will help - and considering that you'd be hard-pressed to find a pint anywhere in Toronto for under $9, Ford's push to increase competition and choice is great news for consumers and businesses alike.
The portion of Highway 401 running through Toronto is North America's busiest and the bane of the city's drivers. Weekday or weekend, day or night, drivers can expect a slow commute.
Ford has proposed building a tunnel below the highway to augment traffic flow. It's an ambitious project that comes with considerable risk - just look at Boston's "Big Dig," which suffered years of delays and billions in cost overruns. Yet Ford's army of media critics have only admonished his idea instead of offering their own solutions to the city's traffic woes.
Ford is a lightning rod for Canadian media, most of which has a liberal bias that perhaps explains why they're more interested in tearing him down than proffering policies of their own. They offer criticism without any real solutions of their own, which reeks of partisanship and does little to further public discourse.
Urban planners and left-wing commentators are fond of gaslighting Canadians by pointing to bike-lane usage in cities such as Beijing, Paris and Amsterdam, where temperatures rarely dip below freezing, and using the "If you build it, they will come" argument.
But the fact remains that Canada does not have the bike culture of a country like China, and no matter how bad traffic gets, the prospect of riding a bicycle through the snow when it's -20 C outside will remain unappealing to most Canadians.
Unfortunately, Ford's critics seem completely devoid of solutions of their own, repeating tired clichés about how bike lanes are the answer to all our problems and building new roads will only invite more traffic.
Toronto fancies itself Canada's answer to Manhattan - and in a way it is, if exorbitant rent and gridlock factor into the equation - but if that dream ever becomes reality, Ford's push to modernize transit and ease congestion will have played an outsized role.

Subject: Consumers; Convenience stores; Roads & highways

Location: Canada; North America

Document 112 of 281

Provincial program to add four family doctors in Sudbury next year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-program-add-four-family-doctors/docview/3141996650/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+program+to+add+four+family+doctors+in+Sudbury+next+year&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: STAR STAFF Four internationally trained family doctors will set up practices in Sudbury next year, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced on Thursday.
Based on the average attachment rates of family doctors, this means over 4,800 more people will be connected to primary care in the community, Jones said in a release.
"Our government is continuing to take bold and innovative action to ensure that everyone who wants a primary care provider can connect to one," said Jones, who is also Ontario's deputy premier.
"Through the Practice Ready Ontario program, we are taking another step to connect more people to world-class primary care right in their own communities now and for years to come."
Through the Practice Ready Ontario program, the Ontario government said it is breaking down barriers for internationally educated physicians to practice family medicine in Ontario.
The province launched the Practice Ready Ontario program in 2023 to bring more internationally educated physicians into Ontario's healthcare workforce faster by removing the requirement to complete unnecessary re-education programs.
The announcement by Jones comes a week after Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie told The Star that more than a quarter of Sudburians are without a family doctor.
Crombie said an internal government report leaked to the Liberals shows that nearly 50,000 Sudbury residents are not signed up to a family doctor. The document indicates 130,480 people in Greater Sudbury, out of a population of 179,831 (based on a 2023 estimate), are currently rostered with one of 96 physicians.
That means 49,351 - about 27 per cent - do not have a family doctor.
"That's more than one in four, and it's higher than the provincial average," said Crombie.
The Liberal leader said the situation will get worse in Sudbury and across Ontario as family doctors retire. Province-wide, more than four million Ontarians (out of a population of about 16 million) don't have a doctor, and three million are at risk of losing the ones they do have, the stats show.
"These numbers are staggering, and they're quite frankly worse in the North," Bonnie Crombie told The Star.
Jones, however, said Thursday that starting in 2025, four internationally trained physicians with training in family medicine and who have completed their field assessment will begin practising as a family doctor in Sudbury.
Each internationally educated physician who participates in the program is required to complete a 12-week assessment to ensure they have the skills and competencies needed to practice in Ontario, the government said. This program also requires physicians to complete a three-year return of service as a family doctor in a rural or northern community.
To ensure the incoming doctors are prepared, the assessment includes training in all aspects of rural family medicine across a variety of practice settings. This includes an office, hospital, emergency department, and long-term care and home care settings.
By the end of 2025, the Practice Ready Ontario program will add 100 family doctors in rural and northern communities, enhancing access to primary care across the province.
"This builds on the considerable investments the government is making to close the gap for the remaining 10 per cent of people who want to connect to a primary care provider, including increasing the number of primary health care teams and making the largest expansion of medical schools in over a decade," the government's release said.
As announced in the 2024 Fall Economic Statement, the province said it is spending an estimated $88 million over three years to expand Learn and Stay grants for 1,360 eligible undergraduate students who commit to practise family medicine with a full roster of patients once theygraduate.
The government estimated this will connect an additional 1.36 million people to primary care based on average attachment rates for family doctors.
The funding will cover all tuition and other direct educational costs like books, supplies and equipment in exchange for a term of service as a physician in any community across Ontario. QUICK FACTS
Only candidates who met the registration requirements and safeguards set by the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario were selected to participate in the program. These safeguards are based on national Practice Ready Assessment Standards established by the Medical Council of Canada.
Ontario said it leads the country in how many people benefit from a long-term, stable relationship with a family doctor or primary care provider. Since 2018, more than 15,000 physicians have joined the healthcare system, including a nearly 10 per cent increase in family doctors.
Ontario also said it has opened two new medical schools and expanded medical school seats, adding 260 undergraduate seats plus 80 undergraduate seats from York University as well as 449 postgrad positions plus 102 postgrad positions from York University. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Primary care; Medicine; Northern communities

Document 113 of 281

Focus of Trump tariffs should be China, Mexico - not Canada: Ford

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan.

Document 114 of 281

Sudbury advocate - now a mom - cheers improved fertility services in city; Nickel Belt MPP, however, says the Ford government needs to do a lot more to give Northern Ontario families full access

Author: Stradiotto, Laura

Publication info: Stradiotto, Laura. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-advocate-now-mom-cheers-improved/docview/3141293907/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+advocate+-+now+a+mom+-+cheers+improved+fertility+services+in+city%3B+Nickel+Belt+MPP%2C+however%2C+says+the+Ford+government+needs+to+do+a+lot+more+to+give+Northern+Ontario+families+full+access&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Stradiotto%2C+Laura&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An advocate for better fertility care in the North says the situation is beginning to improve for women struggling with infertility in the region.
Renee Higgins gave birth to a healthy baby boy a few weeks ago and is on cloud nine with recent developments she says will benefit those seeking fertility treatment in the North. Higgins has been pushing for better access to fertility care after spending more than $100,000 in treatment, medication and travel to southern Ontario where she underwent numerous cycles of in-vitro fertilization.
A few days after her son Jackson was born, the Ontario government announced $150 million over two years to improve fertility services, along with a tax credit for Ontarians paying for treatment out-ofpocket.
Then, mere days after this funding announcement, the Markham Fertility Centre revealed it was forming a new partnership with Well Health Diagnostic Centres in Sudbury to begin offering monitoring services here as a way to reduce the travel burden.
The Markham clinic is also working with other partners to improve fertility care in Whitby, Barrie and Orillia.
"If this model is successful, it could possibly roll out to further Northern Ontario cities and maybe even across Canada," said Higgins.
Markham Fertility Centre's medical director Dr Meivys Garcia credits Higgins as the driving force and inspiration behind the change. Garcia sees many patients from all over Northern Ontario access care at her clinic as it is the closest geographically to the region. Higgins was one of her patients, too.
Garcia says she knows many patients like Higgins make multihour drives, through all types of weather, to access services at her clinic. She understands the impact this has not only on finances, but mental health. "As a team, we wanted to do something to alleviate that stress," said Garcia. "We know that infertility is stressful to begin with."
Starting in spring 2025, Sudbury residents struggling with fertility and who are patients of the Markham clinic can stay in Sudbury to access diagnostic and monitoring services at Well Health on Larch Street. Egg retrieval and insemination will still have to be done in Markham, said Garcia.
"We are hoping those services can be accessible to any fertility clinic, not just Markham Fertility Centre," she added. "We don't want to be exclusive; we want to help anyone who lives in the North and wants to access fertility satellite monitoring imaging for their fertility treatment."
Garcia is asking for support from the provincial government to hire a nurse and purchase equipment. However, the project is moving forward regardless of government support, she said.
Garcia also said that the services could be made available to other fertility centres in the south or patients could pay a user fee to access the care if they are patients of other fertility clinics. Patients typically pay fees for monitoring services anyway, she said.
"We are committed to go ahead regardless, but funding would make it easier for patients to access it," said Garcia.
Garcia said she would like to be able to offer full IVF services in the North, but "we don't have the funding for that now."
If the partnership in Sudbury is successful, Garcia said she hopes to open more monitoring services in the North.
"Our goal with Markham Fertility is to, as much as possible, have patient appointments in town," said Karla Paquette, Well Health's regional manager of the north region.
"Markham Fertility was the first one to approach us, but we are open to any organization looking to branch into the North."
Paquette said there is space available in their downtown clinic. Once equipment is purchased and a nurse is hired, the services will be made available. Paquette anticipates that will happen in March or April 2025. Fertility care patients will be able to access ultrasound services from the clinic's existing equipment, although Paquette was quick to note that scheduling will not impact other patients'timely access to scans.
Nickel Belt NDP MPP France Gelinas, who is also the official opposition health critic, says the recent partnership doesn't go far enough in creating parity for fertility care between north and south regions of Ontario.
Patients still need to travel to southern Ontario to access full care for IVF. "We have one fertility clinic in Sudbury and they have a partnership with Anova Fertility, so that all the testing is done in Northern Ontario, but you still have to go to Toronto for the egg retrieval and insemination," she said. And the same will be done for patients of the Markham clinic, she added.
"For us in Northern Ontario, nothing changes," she said, adding that the health system granting Northern Ontarians access to fertility care has not changed.
"The other thing that doesn't bring equitable access is that the government gives a certain number of covered procedures for each of the different companies in Toronto. Then, it's up to them to decide how many of those they will use for people in the North.
"I would like the government to step in to put in equitable access ... and say how many have to be done for people in the North. But now it's up to the goodwill of the existing private for-profit providers."
Many Ontarians cannot afford to pursue fertility treatment beyond what is covered by OHIP and while Gelinas says in the perfect world, fertility care should be in the hands of public health, she doesn't see that happening under the Ford government.
"We have a long way to go," she said. "Just think about it: If you have respiratory system that doesn't work, you will be treated, diagnosed and then you will be put on a wait list for a lung transplant. If you're digestive system doesn't work well, they have tests, medication and possibly surgery ... but if it's your reproductive system, it's not covered." The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Bluesky: @sudburystar.bsky.social X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Fertility; Reproductive technologies; Infertility; Funding; In vitro fertilization

Document 115 of 281

Fresh off bonspiel win, tanner horgan chats positional change

Author: Pascal, Randy

Publication info: Pascal, Randy. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fresh-off-bonspiel-win-tanner-horgan-chats/docview/3134433239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fresh+off+bonspiel+win%2C+tanner+horgan+chats+positional+change&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=Pascal%2C+Randy&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I really think, for whatever reason, that this is kind of my new calling, my new position."
It might be hard for many local curling folk to fathom teenage Tanner Horgan uttering these words in contemplating a move from his long-standing role as a team skip to the front end.
But now, blessed with the wisdom of 26 years, the bulk of which have been spent curling at a relatively elite level, and having enjoyed a great deal of success on the ice but always looking for more, Horgan projects a confidence in throwing second stones for the John Epping rink that also includes Jake Horgan at vice and Ian McMillan at lead.
Of course, the emotional lift that comes with the Team Epping triumph at the recent Stu Sells Living Waters Collingwood Classic and the knowledge the foursome is right in the thick of the race for an automatic entry into the Olympic Trials in 2025 certainly helps in allowing the graduate of Lockerby Composite, who now resides in Mississauga, to feel pretty darn good about the way things are going for his new squad this year.
"We played really well, building momentum throughout the event," Horgan said this week. "We're starting to get into a groove heading into the playoffs and we were playing really well on Sunday."
Adding the accomplished talents of John Epping to a trio that benefitted from plenty of pre-existing familiarity was going to entail a period of adjustment, though Horgan and company are thrilled at how quickly it's all come together.
"John is getting better at reading everyone's releases and we're moulding more towards a team identity in terms of how we want to throw the rocks, how we want to play," explained Horgan, a sixtime Northern Ontario junior curling champion from 2014 to 2019.
"The biggest thing in the past couple of events is that John now has an expectation of what's coming at him."
As for the move from skip to second, a positional tangent that Horgan seemed sincerely interested in plunging into when we last chatted in the spring, the results may have even exceeded his expectations.
"I am probably enjoying it even a little but more than I thought I was going to."
That said, the journey to this revelation is not one the young man who qualified for the Brier for the first time in 2023 as a member of the Darren Moulding NOCA rink would have predicted.
"I thought it would take me quite a bit of time to get used to the sweeping, used to judging rocks," Horgan said. "The bigger learning curve for me is having to see the game from the other end of the ice. I am so used to reading the ice and putting the broom where I want, cheating the ice a bit here and a bit there.
"I think I have been slowly getting my throwing back to how I want it to be. I think John would agree that our last event was probably my best event."
Representing the Northern Credit Union Community Centre in Sudbury this season, Team Epping cruised through the A draw in Collingwood, posting wins over Landan Rooney, 7-3, and Nova Scotian Owen Purcell, 5-2.
The playoff encounters went every bit as well as the team eliminated Jayden King in the quarter-finals, 7-4, Scott Howard in the semis, 8-2, and topping the Swiss rink skipped by Michael Brunner in the championship tilt, 8-1.
DOUBLES TEAM STILL IN HUNT Out in Moncton, the local mixed doubles team of Samantha Digiglio and Brendan Rajala was still in the hunt heading into play Friday afternoon at the Canadian U21 Mixed Doubles East Qualifier. A total of 33 teams are on hand, battling it out to try and secure one of four berths at the inaugural U21 Mixed Doubles Nationals in March.
Another 40 teams are in Brandon, Man. to take part in the West Qualifier.
On Tuesday evening, the Digiglio-Rajala tandem put up points in each of the first four ends, cruising to a 9-2 win over Isabelle Daigle-Sean Blythe, but were bumped to the B bracket following a tough 6-4 loss to Ashley Siddall and John Siddall.
The Sudbury duo put another notch in the win column, trimming Kakabeka Falls curlers Lily Wright and Brayden Sinclair, but continued to teeter-totter thanks to a 6-4 setback at the hands of Charlotte Johnston and Kyle Stratton.
Early Friday morning, Digiglio and Rajala survived their first win-or-you're-out test, bouncing Sophie Blades and Cruz Pineau 8-1, but then had to fend off Ella Lenentine and Jack MacFadyen to secure one of eight playoff spots that will produce four qualifiers.
LOCALS IN NORTH BAY Finally, much closer to home, a handful of curlers with local ties are in North Bay this weekend, site of the Montana's Mixed Doubles Bonspiel, as Kira Brunton, Amanda Gates, Dustin Montpellier, David Daoust, Manon Paquette and Megan Smith are all on hand for the 16-team event.
The Gates-Montpellier entry will kick things off against Lauren Wasylkiw and Shane Konings, while Team Paquette-Daoust draws the Chinese twosome of Ziqi Dong - Xuequing Fei.
Kira Brunton teams with Wesley Forget to face Katie Ford and Oliver Campbell, while Megan Smith is once again with Doug Thomson, battling it out against Mackenzie Daley and Kyle Vainio. Randy in the Rings runs regularly during the winter curling season.

Subject: Teams

People: Smith, Megan
