Document 116 of 281

Liberals, Ford government squabble over family doctors in Sudbury; Opposition leader says leaked report shows more than a quarter of Sudburians don't have a family doctor; government says that's misleading

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ford-government-squabble-over-family/docview/3134433189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%2C+Ford+government+squabble+over+family+doctors+in+Sudbury%3B+Opposition+leader+says+leaked+report+shows+more+than+a+quarter+of+Sudburians+don%27t+have+a+family+doctor%3B+government+says+that%27s+misleading&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than a quarter of Sudburians are without a family doctor, according to data obtained by the Ontario Liberal Party.
And the situation could get worse: nearly 12 per cent of the city's physicians are expected to retire in the next few years.
Province-wide, more than four million Ontarians (out of a population of about 16 million) don't have a doc, and three million are at risk of losing the ones they do have, the stats show.
"These numbers are staggering, and they're quite frankly worse in the North," Bonnie Crombie told The Star.
The Liberal leader pointed to the situation in Sault Ste. Marie, where some 10,000 patients were orphaned in a wave of physician departures and retirements. "That could happen anywhere," she said.
In Sudbury, nearly 50,000 residents are not signed up to a family doctor. "That means if you need a doctor's note for work, you don't have access to that person," said Crombie. "You can't get ODSP if you don't have a family doctor. You can't get your annual physical, your mammogram. A walk-in clinic isn't going to do that for you."
Some people have turned to nurse practitioners for their health-care needs, but while this can be a good alternative, there is also a limit to their availability.
"Unfortunately, there are only 5,000 of them in Ontario, and there are only 24 publicly funded nurse practitioner clinics across the province," said Crombie. "The rest is fee for service. So the access to public health-care is limited."
The figures on the family doctor crunch came from an internal government report that was leaked to the Liberals, the party's leader said.
"A concerned insider who works in the Ministry of Health found the numbers quite startling and couldn't get anyone's attention," said Crombie. "They knew this was a priority for us, so we did the analysis and went forward with it."
The document indicates 130,480 people in Greater Sudbury, out of a population of 179,831 (based on a 2023 estimate), are currently rostered with one of 96 physicians. That means 49,351 - about 27 per cent - do not have a family doctor.
"That's more than one in four, and it's higher than the provincial average," said Crombie.
Barrie, by comparison, has a slightly smaller population (164,487) but more residents on a physician roster (135,178), leaving 17.8 per cent who are not. North Bay is also better off, with 49,096 of its 58,388 people (84 per cent) connected with a doctor, and 16 per cent still looking for one.
The report predicts at least 2,336 physicians, serving roughly three million people province-wide, could retire in the next five years. That will strand quite a few more patients unless as many new doctors graduate from med schools or come from abroad in the meantime.
The analysis further shows that "almost half the municipalities in Ontario don't have a family doctor signing up new patients," noted Crombie. "So that means they either aren't taking new patients or perhaps they are working in a different discipline - maybe they are hospitalists or anesthetists, but they're not taking on patients."
Out of the 445 communities included in the report, 205 of them "don't have a doctor rostering - which means signing up or registering - new patients," said Crombie.
The Liberal leader contended the Doug Ford government has kept the extent of this crisis hidden, while also doing little to alleviate it.
"Funding has been cut in our health-care system, and I truly feel that they are deliberately creating this crisis so they can justify the move to more private medicine," she said.
Crombie also pointed to the increase in hallway medicine - patients in hospital being treated in unconventional spaces due to a lack of beds - and long wait lists for MRIs and CT scans as evidence of the Conservatives failing to deliver on the health-care front.
The government counters that it has done plenty to address the doctor shortage, among other health priorities.
"Since 2018, our government has added a record 15,000 new physicians to our workforce, including a 10 per cent increase in family doctors," said Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Health.
The Conservatives also take issue with the stats made public by the opposition, saying they don't tell the whole story.
"The data used by the Liberals is incomplete and fails to account for millions of patients receiving primary care," said Jensen. "Their figures leave out important primary-care practices like salaried doctors, Community Health Centres, and Nurse Practitioner-Led Clinics that connect millions of people, including in the Sudbury region, to high-quality primary care."
Ontario is actually "leading the country," according to the health ministry, "with nearly 90 per cent of Ontarians having a primary-care provider."
The government said it has simultaneously expanded med school capacity and filled new residencies.
"While the Liberals were in power, they slashed 50 medical residency seats, reducing the physician workforce by hundreds of doctors," the spokesperson said. "We have launched the largest medical school education system expansion in 15 years."
Other steps include: "breaking barriers for internationally educated physicians through programs like Practice Ready Ontario; making historic investments to expand interprofessional primary care teams; connecting 330,000 more people to primary care; and taking action to expand the learnand-stay grant to connect 1.36 million more people to primary care," said Jensen.
The 2024 budget also includes a significant investment to connect 600,000 more people to primary care, the spokesperson noted.
"In the North, our government is adding 44 undergraduate and 63 residency positions at NOSM (Northern Ontario Medical School), where a majority of residency students are studying family medicine," Jensen added. "And we have expanded the Northern Ontario Resident Streamlined Training and Reimbursement (Nor-Star)."
This program supports "increased access to Northern Ontario learning experiences for medical residents and fellows from Canadian medical schools," according to the province, "while also enabling immediate and longer-term physician support" to communities in the Northeast and Northwest. jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Nurse practitioners; Primary care; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Physicians

Document 117 of 281

Trudeau defends $250 rebates; PM accuses Tories and NDP of turning their backs on working people

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-defends-250-rebates-pm-accuses-tories-ndp/docview/3134432691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+defends+%24250+rebates%3B+PM+accuses+Tories+and+NDP+of+turning+their+backs+on+working+people&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.6&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau defended his government's decision to dole out $250 cheques to working Canadians, arguing that many of them have been left out of targeted programs meant to help seniors and young families in recent years.
Speaking in Prince Edward Island on Friday, Trudeau said he had been hearing from Canadians across the country who do not qualify for any of the federal government's initiatives, whether it is a boost in seniors'pensions, child benefits or childcare spaces.
"I regularly meet a 26-year-old grocery worker, or a 32-yearold school teacher with no kids, or someone in their 40s who's working in a factory, or a 50-yearold plumber whose kids have all grown, who says, 'OK, but we're having troubles too,'" he said.
Trudeau said those Canadians are feeling "squeezed" even though they are working hard and feel like the federal government is not "showing up" for them.
"Seeing hard-working Canadians who get up, go to work every day and make our economy and our communities successful, and recognizing them specifically ... and showing that we see them, that's what this is all about," he added.
Trudeau announced last week that the government would be pausing the GST/HST on select items, including beer, takeout food, children's toys and Christmas trees in time for the holidays, but also send out a new rebate for working Canadians in the spring next year.
Those rebates are expected to be sent to 18.7 million Canadians who worked last year and made $150,000 or less. They will cost $4.68 billion to taxpayers.
In the past week, federal Liberals faced fierce pressure from the NDP, the Bloc Québécois and even some in their own caucus for the $250 cheques to include retirees, people with disabilities and other vulnerable groups who did not earn an income in 2023.
As a result, the federal government opted to table a bill to solely pass the GST/HST holiday, leaving any legislation and debate on the $250 cheques for another day.
A Liberal source said the reason for splitting the measures in two was to make sure the tax break would be in effect by Dec. 14. In comparison, the government has a lot more time to make sure the cheques go out to Canadians as they are expected next spring.
Trudeau would not say when his government would be tabling legislation for the rebates, but expects they will be rolling out in April 2025.
He also said his government will look at "different ways" of helping seniors and "all sorts of different people."
Trudeau also blasted opposition parties for not supporting his government's current proposal on the cheques, arguing that they are against working people.
"To see the NDP, which was supposedly the party of workers, turn its back on hard-working Canadians and say, 'no, we shouldn't be delivering this check unless we expand it massively,'is not recognizing working Canadians for the challenges they're facing."
He accused Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of "pretending to care about workers and care about affordability." Poilievre has criticized the Liberals'latest ideas as "irresponsible and inflationist" and said the carbon tax increase on April 1 will raise the cost of living. P.E.I. Premier Dennis King held a press conference alongside Trudeau to announce his province is the latest to agree to join the federal government's National School Food Program after Newfoundland and Labrador, Manitoba and Ontario.
King said he and Trudeau talked about the upcoming tax holiday. He said P.E.I. would "willingly participate" in removing the harmonized sales tax for products selected by the federal Liberals during the holidays and until Feb. 15, 2025. National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Federal government; Rebates; Canadians; Holidays & special occasions; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Tax increases

Identifier / keyword: prime; minister; justin; trudeau; defended; decision

Title: Trudeau defends $250 rebates; PM accuses Tories and NDP of turning their backs on working people

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: B.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 30, 2024

Dateline: Ottawa

Section: Canada

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Document 119 of 281

Liberals out of touch with ordinary Canadians Re: 'Sudbury column: Bandaid solutions to Liberalcaused problems,' Nov. 26.; Letters to the editor

Author: Ray, Vincent

Publication info: Ray, Vincent. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-out-touch-with-ordinary-canadians-re/docview/3133885920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+out+of+touch+with+ordinary+Canadians+Re%3A+%27Sudbury+column%3A+Bandaid+solutions+to+Liberalcaused+problems%2C%27+Nov.+26.%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Ray%2C+Vincent&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dr. Ian Symington (Sudbury PC candidate for Sudbury riding) has hit the proverbial nail squarely on the head. The economic and social problems besetting Canada today are due in large part to the egregious mismanagement of this country's affairs by a Liberal government high on theatrics but short on fiscal talent and completely out of touch with everyday hard-working Canadians.
Little wonder that recent reputable opinion polling shows Justin Trudeau as one of Canada's leastliked prime ministers in recent Canadian history.
Folks should not be seduced by a temporary two-month GST holiday on some goods, wine and beer, Christmas trees and restaurant meals (as if most of us can afford restaurant meals).
Folks, none of these Christmas goodies are coming out of Justin Trudeau's (or the Liberals') personal chequing account. The money is coming directly out of our own pockets. What a shameful exercise is being perpetrated on the Canadian taxpayer - we are being bribed by our own hard-earned money.
The whole thing smells; it is repugnant and an insult.
The short-sightedness and vote buying do not end here: the $250 to be sent to those who were employed taxpayers in 2023 would be much better applied and be of much greater social good if this special cheque went to every family, sole-support parent and individual on social assistance benefits (welfare) - they are our indigent neighbours who could really benefit by the extra cash. And why not put these billions of dollar giveaways to help the homeless and our depleted food banks now being used in record numbers? Simply put, folks, the Liberal giveaways are not being targeted to those of our society most in need because very few of them bother to vote, and the Liberals know that fact.

Document 120 of 281

Sudbury families who lost three kids to drunk driver urge holiday safety; Caitlin Jelley, Steven Philippe and Jazmine Houle died in 2009 when they were hit by a drunk driver while walking home in Hanmer

Author: Carmichael, Harold

Publication info: Carmichael, Harold. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-families-who-lost-three-kids-drunk-driver/docview/3129080079/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+families+who+lost+three+kids+to+drunk+driver+urge+holiday+safety%3B+Caitlin+Jelley%2C+Steven+Philippe+and+Jazmine+Houle+died+in+2009+when+they+were+hit+by+a+drunk+driver+while+walking+home+in+Hanmer&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Carmichael%2C+Harold&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just over 15 years have passed since three teens were struck and killed while walking in Hanmer on Father's Day 2009.
For Lisa Jelley, the pain of losing her daughter Caitlin has never left.
"It's hard to believe it's been 15 years since our children were taken as a result of an impaired driver," she read from a joint statement by the Jelley, Phillipe and Houle families on Thursday near the intersection of Municipal Road 80 and Centennial Drive, where the fatal crash occurred.
She was speaking during a press conference marking the launch of local 2024 Festive Reduce Impaired Driving Everywhere (RIDE) spot-check programs. "Sadly, we continue to see (impaired driving) every day."
Jelley said that while the memory of the loss of her daughter and friends Steven Philippe and Jazmine Houle might help to prevent a similar tragedy, she noted the huge memorial sign behind her featuring the faces of the three teens serves as a permanent reminder to passing motorists and others not to drive impaired.
"It's one way we hope to get people to remember," she said.
Jelley was accompanied by Corrine Williamson (Jazmine's mother), as well as Daniel and Jocelyne Philippe (Steven's parents).
Only Jelley addressed the press conference.
In a recent letter to the editor of The Sudbury Star, however, Williamson expressed her frustration at recent court cases in which motorists charged with impaired driving offences plead to the lesser charge of careless driving.
"Judges should not drop charges; clearly the judges were never personally impacted by an impaired driver," she wrote. "A vehicle can be used as a weapon, when sober. So why is it not a weapon when someone is operating it while impaired? "It's the same as firing a loaded gun. Would you bring a loaded gun to a party? Probably not.
"Then why do people bring their vehicles? Leave your friggin'vehicle at home. It's so easy to get home safely while you are sober, before having your first drink."
Early Father's Day morning in 2009, Jazmine Houle, 15, Caitlin Jelley, 15, and Steven Phillipe, 16, were walking home after seeing a movie when they were struck and killed by Nicholas Piovesan.
Piovesan, 26, who ended up crashing into a home nearby, had just left a local bar and restaurant.
Piovesan later pleaded guilty to three counts of criminal negligence causing death and received a seven-year jail sentence -at the time, the harshest penalty ever given to a drunk driver involved in a fatal motor vehicle collision in Canada.
The tragedy led the Houle, Jelley and Philippe families to form the group Action 6/21 to raise awareness about the dangers of impaired driving.
The Greater Sudbury Police Service 2024 Festive RIDE program will run now until the first week of January. Funded through provincial grants and donations, the special spot checks are in addition to regular spot checks conducted by traffic division officers.
As of Oct. 30, 2024, Greater Sudbury Police had charged 216 drivers with impaired driving-related offences. The total consisted of 133 drivers impaired by alcohol, 72 by drugs, and 11 who refused to provide breath samples.
For all of 2023, a total of 308 drivers were charged with impaired driving-related offences by Greater Sudbury Police. Insp. Bob Norman of the police service said the tragedy on Father's Day 2009 "reminds us of the deadly toll impaired driving takes on friends, families, and communities. These are tragedies that never should have happened."
Newman said the service is committed to preventing such future loss of lives due to impaired driving.
"Impaired driving is not an excuse," he said. "It's not a minor crime. It's a criminal act, a conscious decision made by others ... Driving impaired is a deliberate choice with potentially deadly consequences."
Newman said it's important that motorists who suspect they have come across an impaired driver report it immediately.
"The entire community needs to support us and help us do our jobs," he said. "If you see someone who may be drinking, call 911. It saves lives. That's the bottom line ... Every call to report impaired driving is a step toward a safer community."
Acting Sgt. Serge Sicard of the Ontario Provincial Police West Nipissing Sudbury detachment said the OPP's Festive RIDE program will kick off on Nov. 21 and run through to Jan. 5.
"The OPP remains committed to stopping impaired driving offences through public education and enforcement," he said.
The OPP West Nipissing Sudbury detachment held 158 Festive RIDE spot checks from Nov. 12, 2023, to Jan. 1, 2024, with 12 drivers charged with 20 impaired driving offences. Officers checked 195 vehicles.
Lesli Green, who heads Safe Ride Home Sudbury, now in its 26th season of offering free rides home during the holiday season, agreed there is no excuse to drive impaired.
"Our community has seen way too many horrific similar tragedies," she said. "We are here to support the families here today. We know that there's lifelong consequences."
Green added the holidays would be safer "if everybody would just remember to always plan ahead and never drive impaired."
Options to get home safely from holiday celebrations, she noted, include lining up a ride with Safe Ride Home Sudbury on weekends, public transit, calling a taxi and having a designated driver. hcarmichael@postmedia.com X: @HaroldCarmichae

Subject: Press conferences; Automobile drivers; Drunk driving; Vehicles

Document 123 of 281

Sudbury needs to get government members

Author: Poisson, Ed

Publication info: Poisson, Ed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-needs-get-government-members/docview/3128481387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+needs+to+get+government+members&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Poisson%2C+Ed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 124 of 281

Downtown Sudbury group backs mayor on dealing with addiction crisis; It also calls for provincial funding for the city's nowshuttered safe consumption site

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/downtown-sudbury-group-backs-mayor-on-dealing/docview/3126479941/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Downtown+Sudbury+group+backs+mayor+on+dealing+with+addiction+crisis%3B+It+also+calls+for+provincial+funding+for+the+city%27s+nowshuttered+safe+consumption+site&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Downtown Sudbury has penned an open letter to Mayor Paul Lefebvre and city council following news last week the mayor had signed a letter to Premier Doug Ford asking him to invoke the notwithstanding clause as needed to address homelessness and tent encampments.
"The Downtown Sudbury BIA recognizes the complex and growing challenges surrounding homelessness, mental health and addiction in our city. We understand any issue warranting the use of the notwithstanding clause is not one to be taken lightly," Jeff MacIntyre, executive director of the BIA, wrote. "As a community, we have witnessed and participated in numerous efforts to address these issues. We've examined models from around the world with varying degrees of success, but have also seen these models struggle in Canada, where adjustments are required to align with our Charter."
The BIA noted the most successful places have carefully balanced "the rights of the community with those of the individual."
The notwithstanding clause is Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. It gives provincial governments or the federal government the ability to override certain portions of the charter for a period of five years.
Lefebvre told The Star that within this context, it would provide governments and institutions with the power to coax individuals into treatment, even against their will, as it would override their right to refuse medical treatment.
"While we believe individuals should have autonomy over their health, society also needs protection when someone becomes violent or harmful to others," Mac-Intyre wrote. "This subset, though small among people experiencing homelessness, has a tangible impact on the daily lives of the community, including other members of the homeless population.
"This group is currently slipping through a significant gap between our justice and healthcare systems, and making it even more difficult to help others who are experiencing homelessness access resources."
The BIA said it believes it is unacceptable to criminalize mental health issues; however, "we need options that protect public safety - an option our current system fails to provide."
The group also said it believes treating addiction as a health issue is essential to solving current challenges.
"Criminalizing drug use has been shown to fail repeatedly. At the same time, unrestricted, open drug use cannot function within a healthy community," MacIntyre wrote. "A balance must be struck between criminalizing those suffering from severe addiction and allowing drug use in spaces like schoolyards or day cares.
"Recent experiences with supervised consumption sites have shown that current rules for public drug use are too permissive; even with access to safer sites, users often prefer street use over restrictive consumption site rules."
The BIA argued the system currently in place lacks a middle ground by either criminalizing use or permitting it, in unrestricted ways.
"The notwithstanding clause is not a tool to be used casually, but we have seen attempt after attempt to address this issue fail, with tragic consequences," Mac-Intyre wrote. "This situation is a clear emergency requiring action beyond the measures cities across Canada have already tried. Mental health workers, police and frontline service providers need appropriate tools to assist those in need.
"We understand and share the concerns raised by the mayor and fully support his commitment to finding effective, compassionate solutions."
Downtown Sudbury said it believes a multi-faceted approach that hinges on funding, harm reduction and empowerment will be required to properly address homelessness, mental illness and addiction.
The group is also advocating for the return of the city's safe consumption site, which was located in Energy Court, behind the grocery store on Lorne Street. The site was shuttered earlier this year after its funding ran dry and the provincial government failed to provide new monies.
"We urge all levels of government to prioritize and invest in accessible and comprehensive mental health and addiction services. This includes readily available treatment options, supportive housing and harm reduction initiatives," MacIntyre indicated.
"We support harm reduction strategies that prioritize the well-being of individuals struggling with addiction, while minimizing the impact on the downtown community. This includes initiatives such as supervised consumption sites and expanded access to naloxone."
The BIA said that to properly address the crisis, all levels of government need to collaborate with service providers, businesses, as well as the community at large.
"We are committed to working alongside all stakeholders to develop and implement sustainable solutions. Downtown Sudbury remains dedicated to fostering a safe, inclusive and vibrant downtown for everyone," MacIntyre concluded. "By addressing the root causes of homelessness, mental health and addictions, and implementing compassionate, effective strategies, we believe we can build a stronger and more resilient community." sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Addictions; Mental health care; Funding; Homeless people; Mental disorders; Harm reduction; Drug use

Location: Canada

Document 125 of 281

Sudbury seniors responsible for their deaths, city says in lawsuit response; Municipality also blames landlord, tenants where the fire started and others for tragedy that killed George Dodge and Lucy Kulik in 2022

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-seniors-responsible-their-deaths-city/docview/3126479257/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+seniors+responsible+for+their+deaths%2C+city+says+in+lawsuit+response%3B+Municipality+also+blames+landlord%2C+tenants+where+the+fire+started+and+others+for+tragedy+that+killed+George+Dodge+and+Lucy+Kulik+in+2022&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 2.5 years ago, two people died in an early-morning fire at a Paris Street apartment building. Their families are still seeking answers.
George Dodge, 85, and Lucy Kulik, 81, were killed on March 27, 2022, when fire and smoke engulfed a few units within the Banyan Apartments. They lived in unit 116.
The two left behind large and loving families, and lots of questions. Kulik had three children of her own - Kim, Ken and Karen - as well as three grandchildren. Dodge had two kids - Carrie and Loring - three grandchildren and two great-grandchildren.
Both were predeceased by their long-time spouses and met each other later in life.
Family members met with city officials to learn what happened and what went wrong; however, their questions were not answered adequately, they say. Earlier this year, the two families launched a lawsuit, collectively seeking $2.5 million.
There are a number of defendants named in the lawsuit, divided into three umbrella groups - the City of Greater Sudburmy; tenants of unit 118, where the fire started; and the landlord.
The defendants include the Greater Sudbury Fire Service and the City of Greater Sudbury; Amanda Commanda, who lived in unit 118, where the fire started; John and Jane Doe, who also occupied unit 118; Ceming Investments Inc.; John Doe maintenance company; Cemcor Apartments Inc.; 1525 Paris St. Ltd.; Gord Stauffer, the platoon chief the night of the fire; as well as four unnamed firefighters.
None of the allegations in the statements of claim or defence have been proven in court.
The lawsuit says the families of Dodge and Kulik have suffered as a result of their deaths (the statements regarding the elderly couple are identical).
"The plaintiffs have suffered a loss of care, guidance and companionship as a result of the death of Lucille (and George). These plaintiffs have suffered a permanent and deep loss of an integral member of their close-knit family," the statement of claim indicates.
"Lucille (and George) contributed significantly to their enjoyment of life, sense of happiness and the cohesiveness of the family. They will never full recover from their loss. These plaintiffs have also incurred funeral, burial, travel, as well as other various expenses to enable them to adjust to their loss. They have also suffered pecuniary losses."
Additionally, the lawsuit noted the two families "suffered a mental distress injury."
Brian L. DeLorenzi of the Sault Ste. Marie-based law firm, O'Neill DeLorenzi Nanne, is representing the City of Greater Sudbury and the fire service; Stauffer; as well as the four firefighters.
All aforementioned defendants deny all of the allegations contained in the statement of claim.
"These defendants deny that the plaintiffs are entitled, at law or otherwise, to maintain an action for damages against these defendants as alleged, or at all, and puts the plaintiffs to the strict proof thereof," the statement of defence notes. "These defendants plead and deny there were any actions and/or conduct on their party, or on the part of anyone for whom they are responsible, whether agents, servants and/or employees, and whether by way of misfeasance and/or non-feasance, which in any way caused or contributed to the incident referred to in the statement of claim."
As part of the statement of defence, DeLorenzi also filed a crossclaim naming as defendants Commanda, John Doe and Jane Doe; 1525 Paris St. Limited; Ceming Investments Inc.; Cemcor Apartments Inc.; and/or the John Doe maintenance company.
The city and its employees are seeking compensation under the Negligence Act, as well as "contribution and indemnity under the common law and equity for any amounts" for which they may be responsible.
Additionally, the city is seeking compensation for "the costs of the main action, plus all applicable taxes; the costs of this cross-claim, plus all applicable taxes; and such further and other relief" the court may deem appropriate.
In the statement of defence, De-Lorenzi argues that "at all material times, the premises in question were in full compliance with all provisions of the Fire Protection and Prevention Act."
The statement also argues that any non-compliance "did not cause or contribute to the fire, which forms the subject matter of these proceedings, or the plaintiffs'damages herein."
DeLorenzi argues the fire service performed in a "competent, thorough and professional manner, in keeping with the standard prevailing in the province of Ontario."
They responded to the fire "expeditiously, professionally and with adequate resources."
DeLorenzi argues Dodge and Kulik were responsible for their own deaths, as a result of "a pre-existing casualty, injury or illness," and/or a "novus actus interveniens." This is a legal term to describe an "act or event that breaks the causal connection between a wrong or crime committed by the defendant and subsequent happenings, and therefore relieves the defendant from responsibility for these happenings," the Oxford Reference indicates (tinyurl.com/4xnepr5w).
Dodge and Kulik were located, several hours after the fire was extinguished, in their bedroom. The coroner determined both died of smoke inhalation.
When she was found, Kulik was lying on her left side in a semiprone position. She was on the floor beside the bed. A green hand towel with what appeared to be soot was crumpled near her face. Dodge was sitting in a rocking chair near the bedroom window, which was open.
"These defendants plead the plaintiffs'damages, if any, are not the result of any negligence, breach of duty or want of care on the part of these defendants, or anyone for whom they are at law responsible for," the statement of defence notes. "In the event this honourable court should find the plaintiffs did sustain the injuries, losses and/or damages as alleged, or at all, which is not admitted but specifically denied, these defendants plead that such injuries, losses and/or damages were caused or contributed to by the negligence of the late plaintiffs, Lucille Marielle Kulik and/or George Murray Dodge."
Both octogenarians, Dodge and Kulik did have some health challenges. They both lived with hearing loss and they had mobility issues. Dodge used a cane and Kulik, a walker. Carrie Moussa, Dodge's daughter, told The Star both suffered from bad arthritis. Kulik also suffered from coronary artery disease and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. She required oxygen.
DeLorenzi denies the city or its staff were in any way responsible for the deaths of Dodge and Kulik; however, he submits that any injuries, damages or losses "are the result of injuries and accidents suffered by them prior to or after" the fatal fire.
He also argues that losses were the result "of infirmities, psychological, emotional and physical problems of the plaintiffs that pre-date or post-date the said occurrence and is unrelated to it."
DeLorenzi argues the damages sought by the two families are "excessive, exaggerated and/or too remote to be recoverable at law."
Finally, the statement of defence argues the two deaths resulted from the negligent acts of Commanda; John Doe; Jane Doe; 1525 Paris St. Limited; Ceming Investments Inc.; Cemcor Apartments Inc.; and/or John Doe Maintenance Company. mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Lessors; Cardiovascular disease; Fires; Older people; Defense; Apartments; Negligence; Injuries

Award-winning Giants coming to Sudbury outdoor film festival; The Banff Film Festival honoured the Australian feature, The Giants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/award-winning-giants-coming-sudbury-outdoor-film/docview/3125913941/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Award-winning+Giants+coming+to+Sudbury+outdoor+film+festival%3B+The+Banff+Film+Festival+honoured+the+Australian+feature%2C+The+Giants&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The world's best adventure and environmental films are headed to Northern Ontario this weekend for the fourth edition of the SOAR Film Festival at the Indie Cinema, 162 Mackenzie St.
Winners at the Banff Film Festival were announced over the weekend. One of the top awards went to the Australian feature The Giants, set to show on the big screen in Sudbury this coming Saturday night.
The Giants follows the story of Bob Brown - an environmentalist whose activism is documented over five decades. Brown takes a run for political office, becoming the first and only elected member of the Green Party to then hold the balance of power in Australian parliament.
Brown's activism extends to his personal life, as he comes out as a gay man in the 1970s. He is an important public figure on many fronts. His courage and dedication to stand up for his values is inspiring. The Giants won best environmental film in Banff. The Sudbury screening will be the film's Northern Ontario premiere.
Banff hosted several world premieres, including For Winter, SOAR's opening feature (Friday at 6:30 p.m.); and Wildflowers, which is featured in SOAR's Women In the Outdoors programme, taking place Nov. 10 at 2 p.m. Both of these Ontario premieres will be followed by a live Q&A, moderated by Beth Mairs.
Call Me Moab screens Saturday at 4 p.m. and will be followed by a local panel. This mountain biking film, based in Utah, follows a former tech industry worker who decides to radically change his life by re-organizing his means of income around the biking scene in Moab, his adopted home.
Local panellists are drawn from among a cluster of folks who have found a way to make a living doing what they love. They will include local artist Gillian Schultze; canoeist, professor, outdoorsman and author Kevin Callan; and John Lalonde, proprietor of Sessions Ride Company.
SOAR has organized a free high school screening on Friday that will bring together outdoor education and media arts students to screen Keepers of the Trail. A discussion will follow with filmmaker David Hartman (Haywire Media) and doc subject Kielyn Marrone of Lure of the North.
Both guests are graduates of Laurentian University's outdoor adventure leadership program. Marrone, based near Espanola, is best known as a top-three finalist on the show Alone.
SOAR Film Festival is pleased to announce the addition of three new film awards this year: the audience-choice Best of the Fest; best cinematography; and best student film.
LU will host a special screening of student adventure films on Nov. 9 as part of SOAR. One hundred per cent of ticket sales will go towards their expedition trips, set for 2025.
To learn more about the SOAR Film Festival, go to sudburyindiecinema.com. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar Star Staff

Document 128 of 281

Minister says housing funds at risk under Poilievre's plan

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-says-housing-funds-at-risk-under/docview/3124210183/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+says+housing+funds+at+risk+under+Poilievre%27s+plan&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing Minister Sean Fraser is asking around 140 municipalities to speak out about his Conservative opponent's pledge to cut a federal housing program, a promise which one mayor says has raised questions about its impact on existing deals.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre announced last week that if Canadians were to elect his party, he would tackle housing affordability by slashing the GST on home purchases under $1 million.
He pledged that a future Conservative government would pay for it by cutting the Canada Housing Infrastructure Fund as well as the Housing Accelerator Fund.
The federal government says what Poilievre is talking about is cash Ottawa has already committed to provide cities through deals struck under the $4-billion program, which promises to give municipalities housing money in exchange for local officials reforming zoning and other bylaws to speed up construction.
Under existing agreements, municipalities must provide the Canadian Mortgage and Housing Corporation updates on how many building permits they issue, which could result in funding being held back if officials determine local governments are not meeting their agreed-to targets.
On Monday, Poilievre's office confirmed that the cash Ottawa has committed to provide municipalities for years to come would be cut under his proposal. Last year, the Conservative leader unveiled his own approach for incentivizing cities to speed up housing construction, pledging to give those that do a "building bonus" and to levy a penalty against those who fail to meet certain targets.
Under the existing accelerator program, the federal government has struck 178 agreements with 140 municipalities, 37 Indigenous communities and one for the province of Quebec.
In letters sent to each of those municipalities last week, Fraser warned how the money Ottawa has promised them is now at risk under Poilievre's pledge.
"I would also encourage you to take this opportunity to inform members of your community about the impact this cut would have to your plans to build more homes," Fraser wrote in one such letter to Markham, Ont., Mayor Frank Scarpitti.
London, Ont., Mayor Josh Morgan, who chairs the Big City Mayors'Caucus with the Federation of Canadian Municipalities, said Poilievre's proposal raises questions about how it "would work in practice."
His was the first city to strike a deal with Ottawa under the accelerator program.
"Cities like London and others have existing deals with the federal government to produce tangible results, and we expect to be measured on producing those results," he said in a recent interview.
"I think the questions are mainly around how would this actually play out with existing contracts and existing commitments we make. There would be some uncertainty there about the paths we're going down, the plans that we're making, the plans that we're currently executing."
Scott Aitchison, a Conservative MP from Ontario who serves as the party's housing critic, says in a statement that Poilievre's plan to cut the GST on home purchase under $1 million would provide "real relief directly to Canadians," compared to the Liberals'plan, which he said amounted to giving "billions of taxpayer dollars to municipal gatekeepers who block home building and raise taxes and fees."

Subject: Mayors

Document 129 of 281

'People are dying. I'm tired of it,'Greater Sudbury's mayor says; He and other mayors in Ontario want to force drug addicts into treatment, if necessary, because the 'status quo is not working'

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/people-are-dying-im-tired-greater-sudburys-mayor/docview/3124210148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27People+are+dying.+I%27m+tired+of+it%2C%27Greater+Sudbury%27s+mayor+says%3B+He+and+other+mayors+in+Ontario+want+to+force+drug+addicts+into+treatment%2C+if+necessary%2C+because+the+%27status+quo+is+not+working%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-11-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Paul Lefebvre said Monday he is fed up with losing so many community members to addiction and mental illness.
Lefebvre was one of a dozen mayors last week who asked Premier Doug Ford to act immediately to address homelessness and the growing number of tent encampments in cities across the province.
It was a controversial move and the evidence suggests forcing people into treatment is ineffective; however, Lefebvre said municipalities need to try something different.
"What is our option? The status quo? The status quo is not working either," he said. "People are dying. I'm tired of it."
A letter made public Thursday - signed by Lefebvre and the mayors of Windsor, Pickering, Barrie, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Brampton, Brantford, Clarington, St. Catherines, Oshawa and Oakville - called on Ford to invoke the notwithstanding clause, if necessary, to "help municipalities with issues related to mental health, addiction and homeless encampments."
They said they want to work with Queen's Park to "realize positive changes to very complex issues."
The notwithstanding clause is Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. It gives provincial governments or the federal government the ability to override certain portions of the charter for five years.
Lefebvre said within this context, it would provide governments and institutions with the power to coax individuals into treatment, even against their will, as it would override their right to refuse medical treatment.
The mayors called on the provincial government to intervene in any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate, dismantle and prohibit encampments.
But Lefebvre said in Greater Sudbury, the clause would only be invoked under very specific circumstances and would not be used to empty encampments.
"We have individuals who are very sick, who have severe mental health and addiction issues, and that almost have brain injuries because of that," he said. "Currently, what's going on is that they often require medical services. They get to the hospital and the frontline workers ask them to stay, but they refuse them.
"Often, they go back on the streets. It's a perpetual cycle. This is not the majority of the population. These are very high-acuity individuals who are really suffering and cannot support themselves. Often, they're being refused at the soup kitchen, refused housing. We've offered them housing in the past, but they've refused. Often, these are the individuals who pass away from these drug addictions."
Lefebvre said it is untenable to continue to allow the cycle to be perpetuated.
"Are we on the right path to help them out, by keeping them in this perpetual cycle of overdosing, going to the hospital and back on the street?" he asked.
Lefebvre said a line in the sand has been drawn. While he knows not everyone will agree with his actions last week, or the intentions of the letter, he said he has heard from too many families who have lost loved ones to addiction.
"They tell me 'if my child, or brother or sister would have received these treatments, they'd probably be alive today'," he said.
At least they would stand a better chance. There is no hope for recovery after death.
"At what point do we, as a society, say we need to help these vulnerable people," he commented. "It's not about incarceration. It's about providing them with services."
Lefebvre said the municipality is providing transitional and supportive housing, with more to come, as well as shelter beds and warming spaces; "however, for me, it's the ones who refuse these services. How do we help them? How do we reach them to prevent death? That's why I signed this letter when I saw it."
The mayors also asked Queen's Park to enhance the "existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care, and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions."
They also called on the province to create a drug and diversion court system and to fund it adequately "to allow a meaningful focus on rehabilitation," rather than incarceration.
The mayors also requested the province amend the Trespass to Property Act to create a provision for repeated acts of trespassing. They want the police to be able to arrest those who trespass repeatedly and they believe the penalty should include a period of incarceration; however, the mayors also said they want trespassers to progress through the aforementioned diversion court system.
Lefebvre said city hall has no intention of evicting residents from tent encampments. There are outreach workers and client navigators who have built and maintained relationships with encampment residents, who will continue to educate people on the housing options available and to "assist them. There's ongoing advocacy and trying to support them where they're at and trying to get them out of that situation. Encampments are not safe situations at all."
Finally, the mayoral dozen called on Ford to "enact legislation providing clear guidance regarding the open and public use of drugs being prohibited in the same manner as the open consumption of alcohol."
Lefebvre said the social issues facing municipalities are complex, but cannot be ignored.
but cannot be ignored.
"I have heard from countless families who feel powerless, grappling with the heart-wrenching reality of watching their loved ones struggle with severe mental health challenges and debilitating addictions," he said in a statement published Friday. "The current system is not equipped to address the growing need for comprehensive treatment options, especially for those facing severe and chronic addiction who are refusing intervention.
"We need to ensure people struggling with mental illness and addiction access the support they need when they need it. The data surrounding the opioid crisis locally demonstrates a worsening situation, and people are continuing to die at an alarming rate."
Lefebvre said municipalities are overwhelmed by the crisis. They need the help of senior levels of government.
"We're overwhelmed with the needs we're seeing and coming into winter," he said, noting that despite increasing shelter spaces and social housing options locally, "the numbers keep increasing."
A survey conducted last month indicated at least 500 homeless people are living in Greater Sudbury, although that list includes individuals who are in jail or in hospital. The list, published in mid-October, indicated there are currently 310 actively homeless individuals on the list.
There are more than 260 Crosses for Change on display at the corner of Brady and Paris streets, each one for a loved one lost to overdose. The first cross was planted in 2020.
Public Health Sudbury and Districts reported last week an increase in the toxicity of the drug supply.
"A drug warning was issued on Oct. 23 due to an increase in drug poisonings (overdoses)," Public Health posted on X. "We continue to receive reports of increased drug poisonings in our area. Be cautious if using drugs and please distribute this information widely to help save lives."
Lefebvre said there are times when people need to be forced into treatment, even when they resist.
"There must be a way to get individuals into supportive treatment and rehabilitation programs, even if involuntarily, when their safety and well-being are at risk," he said. "This is a human crisis that requires urgent action, and a key issue the health care system needs to address."
Lefebvre said he and the other signatories are asking the province to "support us in saying nobody can refuse medical services when there is a dire need in a very precarious situation. We're seeing it over and over again."
Lefebvre said he and his colleagues recognize the gravity of invoking the notwithstanding clause. It is not a step they took lightly and Lefebvre said it will only be used under "extreme circumstances."
But it is one tool at their disposal. Lefebvre said the scenarios "playing out across Ontario demand swift and decisive action, requiring the full spectrum of tools available to address it effectively and compassionately."
There is a small percentage of the population Lefebvre believes "require the services they're not accessing, and they're going to keep cycling through the system or they're going to die. So what do we do?" Lefebvre said he and his mayoral colleagues are trying to protect the lives "of those who are vulnerable, and ensuring their safety and dignity. Expanding these critical services will not only relieve pressure on families who are left without options, but also reduce the strain on our emergency response systems, hospitals, policing and judicial systems. We need a proactive approach that focuses on rehabilitation, care and long-term recovery."
The local situation offers some hope, Lefebvre said, noting some progress has been made with transitional housing.
"Through dedicated efforts to support those willing to accept intervention, we have been able to provide supportive transitional housing for individuals in need, and provide critical wrap-around services. This includes access to mental health care, addiction services and other essential resources that help people rebuild their lives," he said.
"We have shown that though these issues are complex, with the right partnerships and resources, real change is possible. We cannot abandon those individuals who have refused intervention."
Lefebvre said he stands in solidarity with mayors from across the province "in urging the provincial government to take decisive action on the critical issues of mental health, addiction and homelessness - issues that affect the safety and well-being of our entire community." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Document 130 of 281

Trojan Horse visits HSN to protest privatization of surgeries; Hospital unions, health coalition demand investment in public hospitals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trojan-horse-visits-hsn-protest-privatization/docview/3122816028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trojan+Horse+visits+HSN+to+protest+privatization+of+surgeries%3B+Hospital+unions%2C+health+coalition+demand+investment+in+public+hospitals&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A 15-foot Trojan Horse symbolizing threats to medicare rolled onto the doorstep of Health Sciences North on Tuesday.
The giant prop has been making the rounds of Ontario hospitals as part of a campaign by CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions and the Ontario Health Coalition to protest a government plan to privatize surgeries.
In Greek mythology, soldiers hid inside a statue of a horse to invade Troy.
"The Trojan Horse represents a gift, which, if accepted, threatens the recipient," said Kevin Cook, first vice-president of OCHU-CUPE, in a release. "The false promise here is that privatizing surgeries is a solution to long waits. In fact, privatization redirects money and staff from public hospitals to private, for-profit clinics."
Cook said wait times in the public system will only get longer if this strategy is implemented, as staff shortages lead to service closures. "Meanwhile, these private clinics charge out-of-pocket costs, which are unaffordable for most people," he added. "Ultimately, they will reduce access based on need, lengthen wait times and weaken our public hospital system."
CUPE points to a recent study published in the Canadian Medical Association Journal, which indicates "surgical rates for cataract surgeries went up by 22 per cent for the wealthiest people in Ontario, while decreasing by nine per cent for the lowest income-earners," according to the release.
"This is in line with the experience in other countries which have experimented with private delivery of surgeries," said the coalition. "The study backs research by the Ontario Health Coalition showing that patients have been charged up to $8,000 at private clinics."
According to the union and OHC, "these charges are often manipulative and unethical, and in many cases are a flagrant violation of our public medicare laws."
The Doug Ford government has been "expanding private delivery of services predominantly offered in public hospitals, including cataract surgeries and diagnostic tests," the release states. "This fall, the government will be issuing new licences to private clinics and providing them funding to perform 100,000 MRIs and CT scans."
Natalie Mehra, director of OHC, said private clinics can be two to three times more expensive than public hospitals.
"The Ford government is taking our public tax funding for health care away from our local hospitals to give it to more costly for-profit clinics," she said. "Even worse, for-profit clinics threaten public medicare and cause hardship for patients, charging the elderly on pensions thousands of dollars unlawfully for needed surgeries and manipulating them to pay for unnecessary add-ons."
The organizations are instead demanding the government invest more in public hospitals - which already have the infrastructure to offer more services, they say, but lack resources - and improve staffing and capacity.
"Ontario now funds our public hospitals at the lowest rate in Canada, with the lowest staffing levels and bed capacity across the country," said Cook. "Yet, the Ontario Conservatives are shifting more than a billion dollars per year away from our public hospitals to private, for-profit clinics, hospitals and staffing agencies."
The solution, he said, is to add enough new hospital beds and staff over the next 10 years to meet the needs of an aging and growing population.
The government "must also close the loopholes that exempt some occupations from paying the health tax that would generate more than enough revenue to offset these costs," added the CUPE leader.
The Trojan Horse visited Espanola last week, parking outside that community's hospital on Friday morning, and is now bound for North Bay, where it will be stationed Wednesday outside the constituency office of Conservative MPP Vic Fedeli.
Hannah Jensen, director of communications for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, told Postmedia in a statement that the Ford government is spending more money than ever on public health.
"Ontario is proud to have one of the largest publicly funded healthcare systems in the world, and under the leadership of Premier Ford, our government has made record investments in our publicly funded healthcare system to connect more people to the care they need when they need it," the statement said.
"Since 2018, we have increased the healthcare budget by over 31 per cent, investing over $85 billion into the system this year alone. We have increased our investment across the hospital sector by four per cent for a record two years in a row, we are getting shovels in the ground for over 50 hospital development projects across the province and have added 49 new MRI machines and 50 CT machines at hospitals across the province.
"This is in addition to the nearly $1 billion to support the innovative ideas of hospitals to make it faster and easier to access surgeries and procedures ... The actions our government is taking have led Ontario to achieve some of the shortest wait times in Canada, with nearly 80 percent of people receiving their procedure within clinically recommended target times." sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca Star Staff

Subject: Workforce planning; Hospitals; Privatization; Health care policy; Eye surgery; Medicare

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Hospitals Privatization Medicare

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Ontario Health Coalition; NAICS: 813319

Document 131 of 281

Sudbury's new rehab hospital site begins taking in patients; St. Joseph's

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburys-new-rehab-hospital-site-begins-taking/docview/3122815958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury%27s+new+rehab+hospital+site+begins+taking+in+patients%3B+St.+Joseph%27s&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Patients in Sudbury needing longer-term rehabilitation care have a new permanent, modern hospital site where they can recover.
The first patients from the Clarion Hotel arrived at the St. Joseph's Continuing Care Centre building on Lasalle Boulevard on Monday when the new site officially opened.
"We look forward to his site being a model and a gold standard for how rehabilitative care can be delivered," said Kari Gervais, the president and chief executive officer of the St. Joseph's Health Centre of Sudbury.
"We've been at the Clarion since April 2020."
In addition to the Lasalle site of the St. Joseph's Continuing Care Centre, the health centre operates a similar site on South Bay Road, which has 64 rehab beds.
The St. Joseph's Health Centre also runs St. Joseph's Villa on South Bay Road and Villa St. Gabriel Villa in Chelmsford. These are long-term care homes.
Of course, the Clarion on Elm Street was never meant to be used for anything more than a temporary location. During the early days of COVID-19, the hospital and the St. Joseph's collaborated to rapidly respond to the threats of the time.
During its four years in operation, the Clarion location, which had 60 beds, served more than 1,500 patients. St. Joseph's staff work to restore the health of patients so they can go home after experiencing, for example, injuries from a fall. This is not long-term care, but a transition from hospital to home.
Gervais said patients were "clearly excited" by the Lasalle site. "There are a lot of smiles upstairs. They are happy about the bright space and big windows, the single rooms."
The wide hallways and purpose-built spaces were highlighted often as each speaker came to the podium during the opening ceremonies.
"Our staff have been on site now for almost four weeks now ... easing themselves in to know how to adjust workflow and figure out how they are going to function in the new space compared to the previous one. They have to decide how to best use all this space." The facilities include a rehabilitation gym, a dining area, a healing garden, and a multi-faith worship space.
"This facility represents our commitment to innovation in health care," Gervais said.
Following the opening ceremony, tours were offered to those in attendance.
David Long, director of mission at St. Joseph's, also celebrated the transfer. "We have had our first group welcomed (Monday) ... It is going to be a stepped process of two weeks to complete the process. We never done this before.
Mind you, transfers happen all the time between locations."
Long is referring to opening one facility and closing another.
"We probably will write an after-action report on how we did it successfully. Here is how we made it an easier process ... It helps us learn as an organization."
This week's opening is only the first phase of the project. "The second phase of an additional 36 beds has begun. We are under construction," Long said.
Once complete. St. Joseph's will have 72 beds by 2025.
Nickel Belt MPP France Gélinas MPP praised the Lasalle site and St. Joseph's generally.
"We have been in need of this type of service for such a long time," said Gelinas. "To have the Sisters of St. Joseph behind this is just phenomenal.
"This is the way to go. Most frail elderly people want to go back home. I saw the physio and therapy areas on my tour and know it will help patients regain their independence and return home. The place has been built to achieve this.
"As the (NDP) critic for health in Ontario, I have no problem saying thank you when things are good and this is wonderful. The design and all of this works. I guarantee you people that might have needed long-term care will be able to go back home because that is where they want to be.
"If I have to be critical, I would say we should have done this 10 years ago. But we are here today and I am really proud of what they have done."
David McNeil, president and CEO of Health Science North, agreed the facility will ease pressure on Sudbury's hospital by diverting patients to Lasalle.
"Absolutely, yes," McNeil said.
"The expansion to health care in our community for elderly patients and meeting the needs of our community is essential. "St. Joe's is an important partner with HSN ... they do excellent work. Their leadership in making this happen is well appreciated."
The new Lasalle site was developed with $20 million in funding from the provincial government. Sylvia Jones, Ontario's minister of Health, attended the opening ceremonies.
"Our government is making historic investments in Ontario's health care system to expand access to care and make it faster and easier for people, of all ages, to connect to the care they need, closer to home," Jones said.
John Roininen is a family member of a patient who previously received care at the Clarion Hotel.
He is an advocate for the new rehabilitation hospital.
"My mother received exceptional care at the Clarion site," Roininen said. "The level of attention and compassion she experienced was truly incredible.
We are deeply grateful to the entire team for their dedication and support during her time there.
"With the opening of this new rehab hospital site, we're excited about the opportunities ahead for our community of care in Sudbury, enhancing our ability to support and serve even more families on their paths to recovery."
For more information about St.
Joseph's Continuing Care Centre and its services, visit www.sjsudbury.com. The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 135 of 281

Provincial water funding historic, Greater Sudbury mayor says; Premier Doug Ford says money to expand water system will clear the way for the construction of 3,300 new homes

Author: STAR11 STAR

Publication info: STAR. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-water-funding-historic-greater-sudbury/docview/3121816263/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+water+funding+historic%2C+Greater+Sudbury+mayor+says%3B+Premier+Doug+Ford+says+money+to+expand+water+system+will+clear+the+way+for+the+construction+of+3%2C300+new+homes&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=STAR&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says the almost $35 million his government is giving the City of Greater Sudbury to upgrade its water system will clear the way for the construction of 3,300 new homes in the city.
"We're working with our municipal partners here in Sudbury and across Ontario to get more homes built faster," Premier Doug Ford, who was in Sudbury on Monday, said in a release. "With billions of dollars in investments in housing-enabling infrastructure, our government is helping get more shovels in the ground to keep the dream of homeownership alive and help more families across Ontario find a home that meets their needs and their budgets."
The funding is being delivered through the first round of investments under the Housing-Enabling Water Systems Fund, which includes $970 million to help municipalities build, repair, rehabilitate and expand drinking water, wastewater and stormwater infrastructure that will enable the construction of more homes.
The City of Greater Sudbury will use the money to upgrade components of the Lively-Walden Wastewater System, including lift station and water collection system improvements and the expansion of the Walden Wastewater Treatment Plant.
The project will support the city's growing population, unlock more housing opportunities and support economic growth, the province said.
Mayor Paul Lefebvre called the funding historic: "The largest onetime funding commitment we've ever received from the province. It will unlock 585 housing units immediately and pave the way for thousands more, helping us continue to exceed our housing targets as our city experiences record growth.
"This investment means more than just new homes; it ensures that families can thrive, businesses can expand and Greater Sudbury will remain a hub for talent and opportunity."
In response to the significant demand for investments in drinking water, wastewater and stormwater infrastructure to enable the construction of more homes, and after consulting with municipalities, the provincial government is allocating an additional $250 million to the Housing-Enabling Water Systems Fund.
This brings Ontario's total investment in the fund to $1.2 billion and the province is currently accepting a second round of applications through the fund until Nov. 1, 2024.
Under the second intake, $120 million of the $250 million will be reserved for projects in small, rural and Northern municipalities without housing targets.
The provincial government is investing nearly $2 billion in housing-enabling infrastructure funding, including $1.2 billion through the Housing-Enabling Water Systems Fund, in addition to the province's Building Faster Fund, which rewards municipalities for progress toward their housing targets.
The province has made building new homes a priority in response to the high cost of housing in Ontario.
"Building Ontario means building more homes, and housing cannot be built without connections to reliable drinking water, wastewater treatment and stormwater management facilities," said Kinga Surma, the minister of Infrastructure.
"Through the Housing-Enabling Water Systems Fund, our government is making important investments in municipal water infrastructure projects, like the Lively-Walden Wastewater System here in Sudbury, which will lay a strong foundation for much-needed new homes while keeping residents safe and healthy." sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Drinking water; Infrastructure; Water treatment; Stormwater management; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Document 136 of 281

Ford's bike lane promise so popular, opposition won't attack

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-bike-lane-promise-so-popular-opposition/docview/3121816005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+bike+lane+promise+so+popular%2C+opposition+won%27t+attack&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's promise to stop the expansion of bike lanes and even rip out some existing ones is so popular that the opposition doesn't want to speak against it.
Sure, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have been dismissive of Ford's bike lane plans, but they haven't really attacked the idea.
Can you really blame them? Most people in this province, roughly 75 per cent, get to work in their own car or as part of a car pool. Just shy of 15 per cent commute via public transit, five per cent walk and just over one per cent commute to work or school by bike.
In January, in most of the province, that number is zero.
Meanwhile, we have bike lanes popping up in municipalities across the province, but especially in Toronto. Spend any amount of time watching the number of cars versus the number of bicycles and you'll see a massive, lopsided discrepancy.
Cars are stuck in traffic, while the odd bike that does show up whizzes past with no concerns.
What's worse, for much of the day, especially in Toronto, these bike lanes are mostly used by Uber Eats and DoorDash delivery riders. We're spending huge sums of money to subsidize getting people their burgers and butter chicken delivered faster.
Meanwhile, Doug Ford has been doubling down on the issue, saying at a speech to the Empire Club in Toronto last week that he wants to do more than just stop expansion.
"It isn't enough to keep an eye on future bike lanes," Ford said. "We need to and will remove and replace existing bike lanes on primary roads that are bringing traffic in our cities to a standstill."
Those should sound like fighting words to the NDP and the Liberals and yet, they aren't taking the bait. Pressed on the issue Thursday morning during an interview on Newstalk 1010, Stiles expressed sympathy with frustrated drivers.
"I think a lot of people are frustrated, I am too, you know, and sitting in bumper-tobumper traffic, like a lot of new listeners right now, I'm sure, and bike lanes and construction are a big part of the traffic," Stiles said.
She went on to note that the Bloor bike lane runs right through her riding without saying if she supported or opposed the bike lane. That saw host John Moore push Stiles for an answer he didn't really get.
"I guess I would ask you quite blankly, if the bike lane is in your riding, would you go to bat for it, or is the premier right that it needs to go?" Moore asked.
Stiles answered that there have been several iterations of the bike lane, that it has been tweaked and perhaps needs to be tweaked some more.
"I don't think that the premier should tear that out, I don't think it's helpful to people," she said in a less than full-throated defence.
Crombie, meanwhile, has spent most of her time answering questions on this issue claiming Ford wants to be mayor of Toronto. There's some merit in that, the premier spends far too much time focused on Toronto compared to the rest of the province, but Toronto is the biggest and most important city in the entire country, never mind the province.
But the claim that Ford should simply focus on improving public transit, as Stiles and Crombie have done, is laughable.
The Ford government is currently building Toronto's first major subway expansion in decades with the Ontario Line. They're extending the current subway system north and are putting billions into new cars for the existing Line 2.
They've pledged billions for Ottawa's LRT expansion, an LRT system expansion in Mississauga and Brampton and a new LRT line in Hamilton. They have dramatically expanded the GO Transit system of commuter trains and buses across southern Ontario faster than anyone expected.
The opposition parties aren't attacking Ford's plans for bike lanes for one simple reason - they know Ford's plans are popular with voters. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Automobiles; Public transportation; Traffic; Bicycling
