Wordstock Sudbury runs Nov. 1-3 at Place des Arts; This year's festival will showcase more than 20 Canadian authors and present 15 engaging sessions that celebrate the literary arts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wordstock-sudbury-runs-nov-1-3-at-place-des-arts/docview/3121815897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wordstock+Sudbury+runs+Nov.+1-3+at+Place+des+Arts%3B+This+year%27s+festival+will+showcase+more+than+20+Canadian+authors+and+present+15+engaging+sessions+that+celebrate+the+literary+arts&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Excitement is building as the 11th edition of the Wordstock Sudbury Literary Festival approaches. From Nov. 1-3, readers, writers and lovers of words will gather at Place des Arts for three days of literary love.
This year's festival will showcase more than 20 Canadian authors and present 15 engaging sessions that celebrate the literary arts. There's something for everyone, whether you're a book lover, an aspiring writer, or looking to connect with a literary community and participate in thoughtful discussions.
The festival includes Northern Ontario authors, including: - Kim Fahner with her debut novel, The Donoghue Girl; - Melanie Marttila; Sudbury's poet laureate, - Alex Tétreault; - ecopoet sophie anne edwards; and - award-winning Manitoulin Island author Danielle Daniel.
Cobalt-based author Charlie Angus, a nationally recognized politician (he is the NDP MP for Timmins) and musician, is returning to the festival with his latest book Dangerous Memory: Coming of Age in the Decade of Greed.
The annual poetry slam takes place on Nov. 2 from 9-11 p.m. Jennifer Alicia Murrin, two-time national poetry slam winner and storyteller, is joining as the emcee, along with two feature poets.
The festival will have award-winning spoken word poet Fareh Malik, winner of the RBC PEN Canada New Voices Award and the Austin Clarke Prize in Literary Excellence.
Local poet and past winner Lindsay Mayhew will also appear.
For those interested in competing, find out more on the ticketing page. Admission is $5.
Emily Austin, an award-winning novelist and poet, will be sharing the stage with fellow novelist and poet Kate Cayley in the session Queer Reflections, which takes place on Nov. 2 at 3 p.m.
The festival also features two exclusive masterclasses for writers: Immersive Nature Writing Foray with poets Ariel Gordon and Yvonne Blomer; and Laws and Best Practices That Influence Professional Writers with John Degen, executive director of The Writers Union of Canada.
There are multiple ticketing options, including reduced rates for students and individuals on a fixed income. The all-access festival pass includes access to all panel discussions, events and two exclusive masterclasses for writers.
The three-day festival pass includes access to all panel discussions and events, but excludes masterclasses.
The program book can be found at tinyurl.com/fv5etj2r. It includes detailed descriptions of events, a full line-up of 2024 guest authors and ticket prices. To view the schedule at a glance, go to tinyurl. com/4up6vxfp. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 140 of 281

Local curlers always on the move

Author: Pascal, Randy

Publication info: Pascal, Randy. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/local-curlers-always-on-move/docview/3120963483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+curlers+always+on+the+move&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=Pascal%2C+Randy&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Abby Deschene is a curler on the move.
Actually, this winter in particular, there are several moves in store for the graduate of both Lockerby Composite and Laurentian University.
Though she has resided in Timmins for past three years, moving there for work, Deschene remains a familiar face around local curling clubs on weekends or as time permits.
A member of the Lauren Mann rink during the 2023-24 season, the 23-year-old has connected this year with a rink that is "a little geographically scattered," as she explained with a laugh.
Skip Shelley Hardy, a veteran of the Newfoundland and Labrador Scotties Tournament of Hearts for two decades or so, now calls Arnprior home. Third Stephanie Mumford, stationed in Sarnia, is a twotime medal winner at the World Curling Championships (2013, 2014) as the fifth for the Rachel Homan rink.
Lead Stephanie Corrado of Hamilton has multiple appearances at the Ontario Scotties to her credit. With curling resumes of this stature on her new team, small wonder that Deschene was ready to forego the position of skip where she has been seen more often than not in recent years.
"I really liked their goals and what they're about - and I had been looking to move away from back end for a while now," said the product of a curling family who helped her team to a bonspiel win at the Stroud Sleeman Cash Spiel last weekend.
"It aligns with what I was looking for."
Deschene has enjoyed success at second throughout her career, filling that slot with the Bella Crosier Laurentian Voyageurs foursome that claimed bronze at the U Sports Championships in Sudbury in 2023 and also thriving in the front end of the Sydnie Stinson Lockerby rink that walked away with OFSAA gold in Fort Frances in 2018.
"It's definitely a different view of shots and what shots you typically see in that position," Deschene said. "There are a lot more hits at second position."
Competing in their third bonspiel of the year, Team Hardy snuck in a few days of practice time in St. Thomas, 30 minutes or so south of London, in preparation for Stroud - and also to cope with the realities of competitive curling life away from the larger centres in early fall.
"One of our biggest challenges right now is not all having ice locally at our home clubs leading into this event," said Deschene, who welcomed the arrival of the 2024-25 sheets at the McIntyre Curling Club in the past week or two. "We're trying to build consistency in our deliveries despite a lack of practice at this point in the season.
"With every event, we've gotten slightly better on our early season technical side."
The Hardy quartet opened in Stroud by winning handily over the Dominique Jean crew from Montreal, but were bumped down to the B draw following a 6-4 loss to Katie Ford of Guelph. From there, the qualifying berth in quarter-final action was booked as a result of triumphs over both Jillian Uniacke and Lauren Mann.
An 8-2 victory over Heather Heggestad of Barrie was the last of the comfortable margins for Hardy and company as their semifinal with Jo-Ann Rizzo of Mississauga went to an extra end, 6-5 - the same score by which the champs would prevail in the final over Chelsea Brandwood of Niagara Falls.
"We were happy that we were in every game," Deschene stressed. "We lost one game on the last rock, but we were able to build on what we did in previous events."
Deschene, Hardy and squad will join other teams with Sudbury connections at the Women's Curling Stadium Tour in North Bay from Nov. 8 to 10 at the Granite Curling Club.
Locals come close at FISU qualifiers Also on the move last week were four curlers representing Laurentian University at the 2024 FISU Mixed Doubles Qualifier in Edmonton. The pairings of Mya Smith and Olivier Bonin-Ducharme, and Piper Lehtimaki-Croisier and Ian Deschene came up short of advancing to the FISU Games in Torino, Italy, in January of 2025, but will now focus on OUA championships and beyond post-Christmas.
The 2025 OUA Women's Curling Championships will be held in Sudbury as the Idylwylde Golf and Country Club welcomes teams from across the province to the region from Jan. 31 to Feb. 2, with a berth at nationals in Lethbridge, Alta., on the line.
NCUCC TO WELCOME MIXEDTEAMS Still with curling in Sudbury, Northern Credit Union Community Centre will be the site this weekend for the NCU Mixed Doubles Open as 18 teams gather to battle it out for a purse of $6,200.
The local contingent will include Samantha Digiglio/Brendan Rajala, Valerie Ouimet/Luc Ouimet, Manon Paquette/David Daoust and Megan Smith/Doug Thomson, among others, though the competition has a sprinkling of an international flavour.
The Japanese duo of Chiaki Matsumara and Yasumasa Tanida join Americans BriAnna Weldon and Sean Franey as the imports in the 2024 field.
Also on the go, beginning on Sunday, are the defending women's world championship team of Rachel Homan, Tracy Fleury, Emma Miskew and Sarah Wilkes, currently in Alberta for the Pan-Continental Championships, remaining in the province for the Canadian Open Slam in Nisku, Alta., from Nov. 5 to 10. Randy in the Rings runs every other week during the winter curling season.

Document 144 of 281

Timing around India affair raises flags

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/timing-around-india-affair-raises-flags/docview/3117816683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Timing+around+India+affair+raises+flags&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, Canada announced the expulsion of six Indian diplomats over allegations of foreign interference. In a news conference, RCMP Commissioner Michael Duheme warned of widespread violence, homicides and a public security threat linked to agents of the Indian government. New Delhi has rejected the allegations, and sent six Canadian diplomats packing in return.
The accusations are appalling, and no country should be targeting the citizens of another on foreign soil. But why are these revelations coming out now? On the surface, they provide a great distraction from the weekend's attempted internal coup on the prime minister. The story of a revolt among Liberal MPs, 30 of whom apparently signed a letter asking the PM to quit, hit the news cycle on Saturday. Then, presto: on Monday, the RCMP dropped its bombshell, allowing the PM to take to the airwaves and sound all grave and solemn and prime minister-like, defending Canadian sovereignty.
But that would be too obvious. So let's look at what else is going on in Ottawa, namely the Hogue commission on foreign interference. On Friday, the commission heard from Public Safety Minister Bill Blair that his office sat for 54 days on a warrant to investigate Ontario Liberal MPP Michael Chan, accused of doing China's bidding in Canada. Blair offered "no explanation" for the delay, and neither did his chief of staff.
Three days later, boom: India stands accused of being the major agent of transnational repression in Canada and the focus switches from the government's failings to the murderous machinations of New Delhi. And who is responsible for ensuring that the RCMP is "effective, accountable and addresses the government's priorities?" You guessed it: Bill Blair.
According to Chapter 5 of the 2023 NSICOP report on the federal policing mandate of the RCMP, while there exists a core concept of police independence, the minister provides direction on "national security investigations in sensitive sectors," including issuing directives to the RCMP "to implement special measures when investigating terrorist offences or activities which have an impact on fundamental institutions in Canadian society, including academia, politics, religion, the media and trade unions."
I'm not saying India isn't engaged in transnational repression, but the timing of this exposure is highly convenient for both the minister and the government. It's also highly convenient for China, which relishes any chance to bash India, its chief rival for dominance of the Indo-Pacific.
For whom is it inconvenient? Not just India.
It's also inconvenient for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives. Left-wing media outlets made noise last month about alleged Indian efforts to build a "war chest" of funds to disparage Trudeau and help elect Poilievre. The same month, the Hindustan Times reported that Poilievre told Canadian Hindu media that, "Anti-Hindu and Hinduphobia agendas have no place (in Canada). I detest how the prime minister has divided our people. We were getting along in this country. Now look, everybody is fighting."
And then there's NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who, until August, was Trudeau's loyal coalition supporter. When he was deputy leader of the Ontario NDP in 2015, Singh spoke at a Sikh "sovereignty rally" in San Francisco that venerated violent Khalistani leader Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale, and accused India of "trying to wipe us out." In an interview on a Punjabi station in Canada in 2018, when asked to comment on Khalistani separatist referendums, Singh said, "In any place if people seek freedom, it is their right to seek freedom."
Why is India apparently willing to kill to crush the Khalistani movement? Sikhs have been demanding a separate state in the Punjab since India was partitioned in 1947. In the 1980s, the Khalistanis engaged in major acts of terrorism, including the bombing of Air India Flight 182 in 1985, which killed 329 people and was attributed to Sikh extremists in Canada. Today, Khalistanis regularly hold referendums in the U.S. and Canada on creating a separate state, with the goal holding an independence vote in the Punjab in 2025.
None of this is an excuse for murdering Canadian citizens. But nor should it be an excuse for this government to play diaspora politics and shield its own misdeeds. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Referendums; Murders & murder attempts; Politics; Prime ministers; Transnationalism; Sovereignty

Location: China; Canada; India

Identifier / keyword: canada; announced; expulsion; indian; diplomats; allegations

Title: Timing around India affair raises flags

Document 145 of 281

Alberta conservatives have 'moved the dial'

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-conservatives-have-moved-dial/docview/3117816584/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+conservatives+have+%27moved+the+dial%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: These are triumphal times for bedrock Alberta conservatives.
Even their enemies can't doubt it: Conservatives have "moved the dial" on every key issue, from tough sovereignty-based stands against Ottawa to health-care reform and other issues.
Twenty years ago, then-PC premier Ralph Klein almost got run out of the province for suggesting a Third Way in health care through some private options.
Now, the door to private care swings wide open.
This political landscape seemed fantastical only a decade ago.
Ted Morton, ex-PC finance minister who ran afoul of then-premier Ed Stelmach, is getting a hero's welcome at events plugging his new autobiography, Strong and Free.
In the introduction, Morton wrote, "Suddenly, many of the Alberta Agenda - a.k.a. "Firewall" - policies and ideas I had championed unsuccessfully had now been supported by two successive conservative majority governments.
"... Reforms that were on the periphery of Alberta provincial politics 20 years ago are now front and centre."
Morton was referring to the "Firewall Letter" he co-authored in 2001 with Stephen Harper and other prominent conservatives. They sent it to Klein, who squirmed.
The letter shocked the country. Critics called it extremist, un-Canadian, and other names far less civil.
Today, the key measures are United Conservative Party policy, reality or aspiration: provincial pension, provincial police, massive shakeup to health care, sovereignty-based legislation barring municipalities from dealing with Ottawa, and more.
Every revolution has its sacrifices. Morton ran for the PC leadership twice in 2006 and 2011, losing both times.
The Alberta centre would hold through Alison Redford, the brief and tragic tenure of Jim Prentice, and the government of Rachel Notley's NDP.
Then conservative forces merged as the UCP under Jason Kenney in 2017. The fiercest battles were now internal. In 2022, they culminated in Kenney's resignation and Danielle Smith's shocking capture of the UCP leadership.
Smith's political life had seemed to be over after she and most Wildrose MLAs jumped to the PCs in 2014.
But she became premier by harnessing the power Morton began to unleash in 2001.
One interesting character in all this is Rob Anderson, who became Smith's chief of staff this week. A close friend of the premier, he's now the second-most powerful political person in Alberta.
As a young lawyer and PC MLA, he always advocated fierce resistance to Ottawa.
In 2010, he crossed the floor to Wildrose with fellow MLA Heather Forsyth.
Though not obvious then, they had launched a decadelong internecine conservative battle.
But in 2014, he went along with Smith to join the PCs.
"When I first broke into politics I was very idealistic and a bit naive," Anderson says now.
"I certainly did take a lot of chances and made a lot of mistakes. The floor-crossing from Wildrose was a massive misjudgment. But you learn from these things, and hopefully I'm a better person and public servant because of it."
In 2021, he did something that proved crucial to Smith's ultimate success.
With Barry Cooper and Derek From, Anderson wrote the Free Alberta Strategy. It called for an Alberta Sovereignty Act, raising separation as a legitimate last resort if no settlement with Ottawa could be reached.
Smith latched onto the sovereignty agenda and plunged into the UCP leadership race. Her stance consumed the campaign, sharply separating her from the other candidates.
Anderson insists he didn't have that much influence.
"There were a lot of good ideas out there. How many papers have been written on this? You know, dozens and dozens.
"She took not just the Free Alberta strategy, but the Firewall letter and many other thoughts on this, combined them and made them her own, and she's been very successful with it."
Ted Morton, Danielle Smith and Rob Anderson were dismissed as fringe figures for years. Now they've won, and don't plan to lose again. Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Herald. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Conservatism; Health care policy; Politics; Sovereignty

Identifier / keyword: triumphal; times; bedrock; alberta; conservatives; enemies

Title: Alberta conservatives have 'moved the dial'

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 17, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Document 146 of 281

Townships formally oppose transfer of radioactive waste; Nairn-Hyman and Baldwin send letter, resolution to Premier Ford

Author: Jim Moodie Nairn-Hyman; Baldwin

Publication info: Jim Moodie Nairn-Hyman; Baldwin.

Abstract: None available.

Full text: townships have jointly opposed a transfer of radioactive material to a former mine site near Agnew Lake.
In a resolution passed earlier this month, the two municipalities express serious concerns regarding environmental and health risks of the project, particularly the potential contamination of Agnew Lake - a critical drinking water source for many residents in the region.
The Ministry of Transportation is planning to transport 34,000 tonnes of niobium rock from the North Bay area to a tailings storage site at Agnew Lake, which was formerly home a uranium mine.
The niobium derives from the defunct Beaucage Mine near North Bay and has been classified by the province as naturally occurring radioactive material, or NORM, as the (somewhat misleading) acronym goes.
According to a fact sheet from MTO and the Ministry of Mines, the waste rock will "help to repair the existing cover on the Agnew Lake tailings management area, increasing the thickness of the cover and and ensuring ongoing stability and safety at the site."
Clean sand and topsoil will also be placed overtop of the niobium, adding an additional layer that will facilitate growth of vegetation, according to the ministries.
Niobium contains "very low levels of naturally occurring radiation," the province said, and "is not considered a hazardous substance."
Nairn-Hyman and Baldwin townships are not so sure of that.
In their resolution, the municipalities state they "reviewed reports regarding the Beaucage Mine and waste site and concluded that the materials that are proposed to be transported to the (Agnew Lake) site are not accurately categorized as NORM but are mine tailings containing hazardous heavy metals, including niobium and uranium."
The resolution asserts both councils are unified in their stance to "formally oppose the transportation and deposition of these materials to our area," arguing the Agnew site should "be remediated using clean materials to mitigate existing environmental impacts, rather than accepting additional hazardous materials that could exacerbate contamination and environmental damage."
The councils have demanded transparency and accountability from the province, requesting that the Ministry of Mines, Ministry of Transportation, and the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission provide clear answers to the municipalities'questions and notify them of any forward movements on this project.
The joint resolution has been forwarded to Premier Doug Ford, relevant government ministries, First Nations leaders, the Ontario Ombudsman, environmental law organizations, and other stakeholders.
The two townships also started a petition back in August, which has garnered more than 340 signatures.
The petition will be presented to the Legislative Assembly of Ontario by Algoma-Manitoulin MPP Michael Mantha on behalf of the councils and residents. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Mines; Councils

Passport clinics: Sudbury Liberal MP Viviane Lapointe will host passport clinics 1-7 p.m. Oct. 17 at Science North, 100 Ramsey Lake Rd., and 10:30 a.m.-2:30 p.m. Oct. 26 at the Greater Sudbury Public Library's New Sudbury branch, 1346 Lasalle Blvd. The clinics are first come, first served. A photo service will not be available on site at either clinic, so official passport photos must be acquired in advance. The clinics will offer public guidance on passport applications, a secure courier service to Passport Canada, and a 25 business day processing time, plus mail time. For more information, call 705-673-7107.

New or gently used winter clothing including coats, hats, gloves, scarves, and other cold-weather essentials are being sought. Public donation sites are currently being finalized and will be announced in the coming weeks, along with details on the distribution process.

Charity: Sean Morton, senior pastor of All Nations Church, is encouraging community members to support the annual Mountain of Mittens campaign in Sudbury. Monetary donations can be made by visiting allnationschurch.ca, and children's mittens or gloves can be dropped off at the church located at 414 St. Raphael St. The campaign ends on Nov. 15.

Radio: Sudbury's community radio station CKLU 96.7 caters to all music tastes. The Sounds of Scotland airs Sundays at 10 a.m. and Wednesdays at noon. Maestro's Picks runs Sunday at noon. Broadway fans are in their element on Mondays at 11 a.m. as they tune into Sounds of Broadway. Allan Walsh's take on the jazz world in Jazz Sounds is always worth listening to on Wednesdays at 2 p.m. JazzMania airing Saturdays at 6 p.m. is equally informative and entertaining. Most CKLU radio shows are aired twice weekly. Check out the schedule at cklu.ca and listen to what you love.
Spirituality: Father Raymond Akor of Christ the King Church downtown invites you to his uplifting sermons, both online and in person. All welcome. Sermons are 12:15 p.m. Tuesday to Friday, 5 p.m. Saturday, and 10:15 a.m. Sunday. For more information, call 705-674-6447.
50/50 draw: The Sudbury Manitoulin Children's Foundation has announced its online 50/50 raffle to raise money for children in need. Funds will go directly into helping marginalized children in the districts of Sudbury and Manitoulin attend camp, sports, or leisure activities. They will also help eligible wards attend post-secondary education by emptying the organization's waitlist.
50/50 raffle: The Sudbury Manitoulin Children's Foundation has joined Sudbury's online raffle fun for the community to help children in need have the chance to be kids. Each month a new raffle will be held, with one lucky winner receiving 50 per cent of the total jackpot earnings. Draws take place on the first of the month after ticket sales from the previous month close. Tickets can be purchased in packages of 3 for $10, 10 for $20, 50 for $50, or 200 for $100. They are available at www.rafflebox.ca/raffle/smcf.

Education: Indigenous learners 17 years and older can earn credits towards their high school diploma at the N'Swakamok Alternative School located in the N'Swakamok Native Friendship Centre. To learn more about the N'Swakamok Alternative School, contact Kelly-Lee Assinewe at 705-674-2128 or visit nfcsudbury.org.
Mining: Workplace Safety North will hold an Introduction to Seismic Risk in Underground Mines course, Oct. 22-24. Mining engineer and retired Laurentian University professor Marty Hudyma will review seismic risk management in hardrock mines, including rock mechanics, rockbursts, and rock engineering. The cost is $1,500 Register by contacting Workplace Safety North at 1-888-730-7821 or 705-670-5707.
Environment: Junction Creek Stewardship Committee presents Creatures of the Night Paint Social and Bat Quest to raise money for bat and wildlife research. Friday, Oct. 25, at the Living with Lakes Centre, 840 Ramsey Lake Rd. The event goes from 6:30 to 9 p.m. Cost is $50 and people must register in advance. To register, contact Jaclyn at communications@junctioncreek.com.
Music (register) Northern Lights Festival Boréal is one of the most emblematic festivals on the Canadian cultural scene and the longest consecutive music festival in Canada. It is pleased to announce applications to perform at the festival, which takes place from July 4-6, 2025, are now open. Artists and agents can now submit their proposals on the festival website. For more information, go to nlfb.ca. Application forms are available at tinyurl.com/43mjz5r4. Applications are due by Dec. 31.

Business: Ontario Chamber of Commerce President and Chief Executive Officer Daniel Tisch Echevarria will be the keynote speaker at the Greater Sudbury Chamber of Commerce's Annual General Meeting at Bryston's-on-the-Park in Copper Cliff on Oct. 24. Doors will open at 5 p.m. for networking time, followed by dinner at 6 p.m. Tickets are $95 for members and $140 for non-members. For tickets or more information, call 705-673-7133, ext. 219.

General admission tickets are $55 plus taxes and fees, with seating first-come, first-served. Online tickets are available through Eventbrite. Prefer to skip the online fees? Held at the Polish Combatants Hall, 291 Albinson St., the evening features Latin, ballroom, swing and jive dance music with DJ Andre, as well as gift bags for everyone, midnight appetizers, prizes and more. Tickets are $50 each and can be purchased under Special Events at connectedlivingyoga.ca. For further information, contact Condon at 705- 677-6494.
Books: Tender, beautiful and unflinching, Sylvia Barnard's second historical fiction, A Hyphenated Life, is an expertly woven coming-of-age story of Angelika Langer, the daughter of German immigrant parents living in a Northern Ontario mining town in the 1960s. Barnard is returning for a series of book signings and readings: Nov. 5 at 6 p.m. Greater Sudbury Public Library Valley East branch, 4100 Elmview Dr. in Hanmer: Nov. 6 at 6 p.m. Main branch of the Greater Sudbury Public Library, 74 Mackenzie St.: Nov. 21 at 6 p.m.

Community: Sudbury's oldest service organization, the Rotary Club of Sudbury, will honour Lesli Green, founder of Safe Ride Home Sudbury, with a prestigious Paul Harris Fellowship. A luncheon will take place on Nov. 7. The luncheon will take place at the Radisson Hotel, 85 Ste. Anne Rd. Tickets are $75 per person, with tables of eight available. Contact lisademers705@gmail.com by Oct. 24 to purchase your tickets. Proceeds from the event will be split equally between The Rotary Foundation and Safe Ride Home Sudbury.
Dance: Northern Latin Congress, Nov. 9: Zoi Monroy invites anyone who loves dance to the Sixth Annual Northern Latin Congress on Nov. 9 at the Days Inn (formerly the Northbury Hotel) on Brady Street. Ticket prices range from $25 for the Saturday night performances to $125 for the VIP workshops, performances and the dance. Go to zoimonroy.com/events for tickets and information.
Circus: Tickets for Cirque Musica Holiday Wonderland, which plays at the Sudbury Arena on Nov. 21, can be purchased online at greatersudbury.ca/tickets, by phone at 705-671-3000, or in person at 240 Elgin St. at the arena. For more information visit, www.cirquemusica.com.
Cinéfest Sudbury is bringing to the big screen Woman of the Hour, the directorial debut of Academy Award-nominated actress Anna Kendrick. It will screen at SilverCity Sudbury on Nov. 27 at 7 p.m. The 1970s-set drama is screening as the final film of this year's Red Carpet Patron program. Woman of the Hour is the stranger-than-fiction story of an aspiring actor (Kendrick) in 1970s Los Angeles and a serial killer in the midst of a years-long murder spree. Their lives intersect when they are cast on an episode of The Dating Game. Individual tickets are on sale now with limited availability at cinefest.com; at the Cinéfest box office, 40 Larch St., unit 103; or by calling 705-688-1234. All Red Carpet patrons are granted complimentary admittance.

Document 150 of 281

Sudburians 'fed up' with what's happening in Ontario: labour leaders; The president of the Ontario Federation of Labour holds town hall meeting with union members in Sudbury

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburians-fed-up-with-whats-happening-ontario/docview/3110869983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudburians+%27fed+up%27+with+what%27s+happening+in+Ontario%3A+labour+leaders%3B+The+president+of+the+Ontario+Federation+of+Labour+holds+town+hall+meeting+with+union+members+in+Sudbury&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Federation of Labour brought its The Ontario We Need tour to Sudbury, but it's not just labour issues on the minds of those who gathered in Copper Cliff.
"This is a big road trip," said Jessica Montgomery, president of the Sudbury and District Labour Council, referring to Laura Walton, president of the Ontario Federation of Labour, who attended the meeting. "The objective is to see and hear about the issues in our communities. Not just labour issues.
"Affordability, housing costs, opiates - including in the workplace - and families with autism on waitlists for services are just some of the challenges," Montgomery said. "We have special needs in the north. Access to doctors and distance to treatment is another problem.
"When our local CT (computed tomography) scanner was down, people were being rerouted. We should not have to go to Toronto to access basic medical care."
A series of posters at the event included one that stated "Health care should be where we need it."
The OFL has launched The Ontario We Need tour as a cross-provincial circuit. Walton said the meeting at the CUPE Local 4705 town hall was a chance for OFL to listen and engage.
The town hall conversations started right after Labour Day and runs to early October. The visits included stops in Belleville, Thunder Bay, Oshawa, Kingston, Ottawa, Sault Ste. Marie, Windsor, Kitchener-Waterloo and Barrie, and end in Peel.
"It is good we are seeing the federation leadership here in Sudbury. The potential of a provincial election is on everyone's mind," said Max Lafontaine, vice president of CUPE Local 4705, which represents City of Greater Sudbury workers.
Officials said Sudbury is the biggest hub and concentration of unionized employees in Ontario. "Sudbury is a union town," Montgomery said. So it was only natural this stop was on the agenda, she said.
Walton said Ontario is facing a host of challenges. "From the cost of living to the health care crisis to overcrowded classrooms, we know people in this province are struggling," she said. "More importantly, we know the solutions can be found in our communities. That's why we're travelling across the province to hear from community members.
"I am up here a lot. Sudbury is a model for Ontario on fairness. But we see folks here fed up with what is happening. They are coming out and they want to make change ... they are developing tangible actions. We find many of the challenges are common, but how they address them in each community is unique."
Jackie Taylor also arrived from Toronto. "I am the executive vice president of the OFL. I come from the health-care industry and am a Steelworker. We need to be able to be here and engage. We want to know what matters to members here."
NDP MPPs France Gelinas (Nickel Belt) and MPP Jamie West (Sudbury) were also present. West offered his observations. "These kinds of meetings are super important. It is an interesting format. It's not just the problems, but we get to talk about what we can do about them.
"At first, people say, 'there is nothing we can do,'then together we see there are things we can do. Be involved, educate each other, and make change, he said "Who would think workplace violence would be a common theme in a variety of sectors? We can learn from each other and know you are not the only one. The pinch of prices ... even the year I was elected, I've been hearing about that more and more. Affordability is the number one item for everyone. We don't want to feel gouged. The prices today don't make sense to me.
"When we are talking about people not having doctors, out of the blue, the premier (Doug Ford) offers us beer at corner stores. That is not one of the top three issues.
"And at what did that cost? Now there is the proposal to build a tunnel underneath (Highway) 401 (to ease traffic congestion in Toronto). It is unbelievable. No details "I am one of the many who don't have a family doctor. I found out at the (Ontario Medical Association) meeting that my doctor retired. My kids don't see themselves owning their own home. There is a crunch everywhere. That's why it is important for Laura to be here -out of the 905 and 416 -and listening."
The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 151 of 281

Discovering the dark secrets of Stirland Lake Residential School; Accent; Sol Mamakwa, now a New Democrat MP, went there; 'I think Stirland Lake is one of the great open secrets we never talk about'

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/discovering-dark-secrets-stirland-lake/docview/3110869980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Discovering+the+dark+secrets+of+Stirland+Lake+Residential+School%3B+Accent%3B+Sol+Mamakwa%2C+now+a+New+Democrat+MP%2C+went+there%3B+%27I+think+Stirland+Lake+is+one+of+the+great+open+secrets+we+never+talk+about%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When I stepped out of the truck, the first thing I noticed was the silence.
Above the 52nd parallel, things seem bigger. The skies seem infinitely expansive, the forests are impenetrable and the lakes feel monstrous.
The silence is big, too. The buildings scattered along the northeast shore of Stirland Lake, about 185 km north of Pickle Lake, are in various states of disrepair. There are a few rusting, forgotten fire hydrants on the property and so many remnants of the past.
The small details in the big picture of Wahbon Bay Academy - like the worn and well-loved ping-pong paddle - whisper of the children who were impacted by their stay at the school.
Old, ramshackle buildings are clustered along pitted gravel footpaths. There are several small buildings that look like single-storey homes. I am told these were dormitories for boys. At the end of one road is a larger building that was used as a dormitory for girls, when they arrived near the end of the school's existence.
Upstairs in one building is a small chapel. A book of hymns, Life Songs No. 2, rests on the altar, awaiting parishioners.
The relics of the past are on full display - there are typewriters and old desks reminiscent of the 1980s; an old table tennis set; a former science lab, still outfitted with amber bottles and pipette cleaners; children's desks arranged in neat rows; and shelves overflowing with outdated textbooks, including Modern Biology and The Methods of Science.
There is an AV room filled with the technology of the day and there is a large gymnasium, still outfitted with hockey sticks, basketballs and volleyballs. The day we visited, it was flooded with natural light.
There is also a wall calendar, still open to June 1991.
Stirland Lake Residential School is located about two hours north of Pickle Lake, which is known as Ontario's last frontier. It is remote.
Also known as Wahbon Bay Academy, the school was operated by the Mennonites under the banner of the Northern Youth Program.
It opened in 1971 as a boys-only school and operated for 20 years, until it finally shut its doors in 1991.
The Cristal Lake Residential School, a companion school for girls about 70 km south of Stirland Lake, operated from 1976 until 1986, when the two schools merged at Wahbon Bay. BEATEN BLACK AND BLUE Non-Indigenous residents of Pickle Lake told me this was a different kind of residential school. They said it was not oppressive, there was little to no abuse, and students chose to attend. I was surprised to learn some teachers who formerly taught at Stirland Lake still live in the community of Pickle Lake.
But Sol Mamakwa, the New Democrat MPP representing the riding of Kiiwetinoong, says the school had problems.
"You'd get a detention for speaking your language; there was very hard labour in the wintertime, whether it was cutting logs or stuff like that," he told me. "You're not allowed to speak your language. That's so wrong.
When you have these Christians - Mennonites - when they give their hardest when they strap you, what does that say? Is that easier?" Kiiwetinoong is vast. It extends from Sioux Lookout north to Hudson Bay, and from Webequie First Nation to the Manitoba border. Mamakwa is from Kingfisher Lake First Nation, a 143-km drive along the winter road from its junction with the Northern Ontario Resource Trail (NORT Road). The community is only accessible with a vehicle via ice road. Otherwise, it can be reached by plane.
Mamakwa was educated at the primary level in an Indian Day School. When he was 15, his parents sent him to Stirland Lake Residential School. He completed Grades 9 and 10 at the school, from September 1984 to June 1986. Mamakwa completed the last two years of his secondary studies in Sioux Lookout.
During his first year at Stirland Lake, he lived in Diamond Willow House.
"There was a group of us Grade niners who stayed in a building - maybe 10 or 12 of us there," he said. "When I visited back in February 2023, it was the first time I went back. I didn't remember much of Grade 10. I don't know why."
Mamakwa said strapping was a concern amongst students, as they deemed it excessive and overly harsh.
"When you're a kid - 12 to 14 years old, you just accept that's the way things are, you know?" he said.
Mamakwa's two older brothers also attended Stirland Lake. While he said his parents sent their children willingly, there really were no alternatives.
"I was just happy to attend school at that time," he said. "I cannot say that I was happy to go to high school - it wasn't like that."
Amenities at Stirland Lake were sparse. Mamakwa told his colleagues in the provincial legislature last year he had a bunk bed and a single drawer in a chest of drawers.
"We were constantly watched by staff; they censored our letters home to our parents, reading every word we wrote," he told his colleagues. "The older boys were heavily punished, sometimes for no reason. They would be beaten and strapped until they were black and blue."
Mamakwa also mentioned that Ralph Rowe was a regular visitor to the campus. He said the Anglican priest visited the school by float plane "to administer to the Anglican boys." Rowe was convicted of nearly 60 sex crimes and is believed to have sexually assaulted as many as 500 boys in remote northern communities.
"He was a notorious sexual offender," Mamakwa told other MPPs.
Mamakwa is only 53 years old, but he said nearly half the people featured in his yearbook have passed away - often a result of suicide, violence or addiction.
"Why have so many left us?" he asked his colleagues. "Their spirits were broken. They could not carry on. Why? Because of the Indian residential schools."
Mamakwa has begun talking openly about his experiences at Stirland Lake Residential School because he says not talking about the past is imperilling the future.
"I'm just hearing this now; I'm just hearing about these issues now from people who are adults, who are just starting to talk about it," he said. "It's been a secret for a long time. It was something we never talked about."
Mamakwa says life at Wahbon Bay Academy made him numb. But sharing his lived experience with others is part of the healing process.
"I think not talking about it is not the best way," he commented. "I think Stirland Lake is one of the great open secrets we never talk about. The number of people I went to school with - they don't even talk about it. I only started to talk about it because I want others to start talking about it."
First impressions of Stirland LakeAs soon as I realized I would be in the area of Stirland Lake, I knew I had to make time for a tour.
I hatched a plan three years ago to drive to the end of NORT Road, formerly tertiary Highway 808, and to paddle Koral the Kayak on Lake Windigo.
The NORT Road, which is only paved for about 30 minutes north of Pickle Lake, ends at Lake Windigo, itself three hours north of the tiny town.
Situated north of the 52nd parallel, it is the farthest north you can drive on an all-season public road in Ontario. Three winter roads branch off the NORT Road, servicing at least 17 communities scattered across northwestern Ontario.
While I was adventuring along the NORT Road last month, I stayed with a fellow named Brent at Mawley Lake. Brent is a teacher in Pickle Lake, but spends his summers on Mawley, a 20-minute drive from Stirland Lake. He knows everyone in the area and reached out to the caretaker family. They were travelling, but told Brent he could show me around.
The land is owned these days by Weagamow Lake First Nation, but they employ the family who lives on-site to maintain the buildings and the sprawling property. Parts of the former school are still used for healing circles and retreats.
Boys from remote communities all over northwestern Ontario arrived to the shores of Stirland Lake. At the time, the school was only accessible via float plane. Scared students, far from home and community, stepped out of the plane and onto a small wooden dock. First impressions would have been made on that dock. FIGHT F OR RE COGNITION The National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation notes students at Stirland Lake Residential School protested in 1987 "against what they considered excessively restrictive conditions at the school.
After a cooling off period following the protest, a number of students did not return. The school closed in 1991 when Northern Youth Programs decided to withdraw permanently from providing residential schools programs."
The deputy leader of the NDP still recalls when administrators forced him to cut off his hair.
Mamakwa left Stirland Lake a year before the revolt, but he had friends who were still students at the school when their peers protested.
"There were so many strappings that were happening and students got tired of the strapping," he said. "The students got fed up, so they stood up to the staff."
Indigenous communities had to fight to have Stirland Lake and Cristal Lake recognized as part of the residential school system.
"In October 2007, Windigo First Nations Council, supported by Nishnawbe Aski Nation (NAN), formally requested that the Stirland Lake and Cristal Lake residential high schools be recognized under the Indian Residential Schools Settlement Agreement (IRSSA)," Algoma University notes in a digital archive, accessed at tinyurl. com/37v2yfw3. "In the spring of 2008, the request for recognition by Windigo and NAN was rejected by Canada. In December 2008, Windigo and NAN filed a motion to the Superior Court of Justice of Ontario, asking the court to exercise its power under the IRSSA by adding the two schools to the list of recognized Indian residential schools within the meaning of the IRSSA."
Wahbon Bay Academy was officially recognized as a residential school in 2011.
The administrators of the Northern Youth Program issued a formal apology to former students of Wahbon Bay Academy and Cristal Lake Residential School.
"We have heard the expressions of genuine pain in what you have said about the schools and your experiences there. We are doing our best to understand you and to empathize with you on your journey to healing and resolution. We sincerely acknowledge and validate your perspective on your school experiences," the statement reads (tinyurl.com/2sevbmun).
"For the times when we physically inflicted pain or added to the pain of your soul by our actions, we are sorry. For the times when we underestimated or ignored the impact on you of your separation from your family, we are sorry. For the times when our ignorance or negligence caused you to suffer additional emotional and physical pain at the hands of other students, we are sorry.
"For the times when school personnel were not properly screened, and when personnel were not adequately trained to relate to you in culturally appropriate ways, we are sorry. For the times we acted as though we were culturally superior to you, we are sorry. For the ways in which we co-operated with the national plan to force your assimilation into Canadian society, we are sorry."
The statement concludes with a pledge from staff.
"We pledge ourselves to the ongoing healing process by offering ourselves to you for private conversations, or with a third party present, as you wish," they say. "Please consider our apology and our sincere desire for a successful healing journey."
Mamakwa says he was one of the lucky ones. He believes the hardships he endured while studying at Stirland Lake prepared him for his career in politics.
"My parents always took me out on the land in the springtime, for two months of the year," he told me. "I already had my language, my hunting, my identity and ways of life. There was no way this residential school could change me."
To learn more: The Knowing is currently streaming on CBC Gem. It is the story of journalist Tanya Talaga and her family's 80-year search for family matriarch Annie Carpenter. As she digs, she uncovers a story that is deeply intertwined with Canada's residential school system. mkkeown@postmedia.comX: @marykkeownFacebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Families & family life; Students; Children & youth; Dormitories; Schools; Sex crimes; Roads & highways; Northern communities

Document 152 of 281

Jamie West to run again for the NDP in the Sudbury riding; Labour activist first elected in 2018

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jamie-west-run-again-ndp-sudbury-riding-labour/docview/3110869904/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jamie+West+to+run+again+for+the+NDP+in+the+Sudbury+riding%3B+Labour+activist+first+elected+in+2018&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jamie West will once again carry the NDP's colours in the Sudbury riding in the next provincial election.
Party members nominated West, the current Sudbury MPP, at a meeting on Wednesday.
"I am honoured by this nomination, and by the trust that New Democrats here in Sudbury have placed in me once again," West said in a release. "I grew up (in Sudbury), raised my family here, and I know firsthand the challenges our community faces. Sudbury is a community that cares. We stand up to support and protect our most vulnerable people, we unite around common causes, and we look out for our neighbours."
"I will always be proud to amplify and echo your voices at Queen's Park and to fight for the things that matter most, whether that's a family doctor for everyone, affordable housing, more support for mental health and addiction, safer classrooms, or more childcare spaces. The people of Sudbury deserve a government that cares about them and puts them first.
"As a blue-collar worker, a northerner, and a New Democrat, I will never shy away from the fight to give people the life they deserve, because it's time for better."
Marit Stiles, Ontario NDP leader, said she welcomed West's nomination.
"Jamie is a relentless fighter for workers across the province," Stiles said in a statement. "Jamie's strong roots in the labour movement and commitment to making sure that the people of Sudbury get their fair share has made him a force to be reckoned with at Queen's Park.
"Whether it's fighting for local health care in Sudbury, or taking on Ford for unfair labour practices - Jamie never shies away from a challenge if it means a better, brighter future for our province. I am proud to have Jamie carry the NDP banner in Sudbury."
West is the official Opposition Critic for Labour, Training and Skills Development. He said he fought for better health care, more affordable housing, safer workplaces, and improved access to French education and services.
He also said he has legislation supporting workers'rights, including anti-scab legislation, legislation to support healthcare workers, and for the declaration of Injured Workers Day in Ontario.
First elected as Sudbury MPP in 2018, West worked for Vale Limited's smelter operation in Sudbury and served as president of the Sudbury and District Labour Council.
He also taught labour studies at Laurentian University.
The 44th Ontario general election is tentatively scheduled for June 4, 2026. However, there has been speculation that Premier Doug Ford, who is riding high in the polls, may call an election before that. sud.editorial@sunmediaca @SudburyStar

Document 153 of 281

Column: Palliative care in Sudbury should be more than about dying; Field should also be considered an equal partner for curative medicine

Author: Zalan, Peter

Publication info: Zalan, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/column-palliative-care-sudbury-should-be-more/docview/3110869182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Column%3A+Palliative+care+in+Sudbury+should+be+more+than+about+dying%3B+Field+should+also+be+considered+an+equal+partner+for+curative+medicine&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Zalan%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are only 241 palliative care physicians in all of Ontario. It is a misunderstood specialty.
I will attempt to define it in its modern context. There are only 131 experts in geriatrics who specialize in the care of the elderly in Ontario. Somewhere from 1.35 million to 2.3 million people in the province do not have a family doctor in our province. Serious new government investment in these three professions needs to be made as soon as possible. These physicians are the orphan children of our health-care profession.
When I was a medical student in the 1960s, there were no cardiac care units. After a heart attack, patients were sent to an open ward and placed on oxygen. There was little treatment besides nitroglycerine under the tongue, digitalis, rest and pain relief. Life expectancy was 71.
We offered caring and comfort but little in the way of curing.
Over the next 50 years, medical discoveries revolutionized the management of heart disease. Pacemakers kept the heart beating. Open heart surgery opened blocked vessels; transplantation replaced the failing heart with another human heart; and most recently, the surgical implantation of mechanical devices to assist the failing heart with the pumping of blood, all to increase survival and improve the quality of life.
Living longer does not always equal living better, because of the burden of other life-limiting illnesses such as COPD, cancer, Alzheimer's and frailty -none of which are curable with today's medicine.
However, they can often be controlled for years with good medicine and symptom management. In other words, palliative care.
The delivery of health care has become complicated. The patient may have a family doctor: one who has known him intimately for many years. In which case, he is blessed.
Or, he may have no access to a family doctor or nurse practitioner and gets care in walk-in clinics and emergency departments. I well remember meeting such patients with complicated stories in the Emergency Department. Meeting them for the first time, in crisis.
Me, as the intensive care physician, they needing immediate rescue from death, with no evidence of a health plan and no direction from family or friends. This is not the proper way to look after our seniors.
There are 22,000 medical specialists in Ontario who are divided into 47 distinct specialties. These physicians have extensive training in diagnosing and treating specific complex medical conditions.
Any one patient may need to be helped by a number of different physicians.
One of these 47 specialties is palliative care (PC). Historically, PC has been viewed as an alternative to curative therapies. It involves in the development of an advanced care plan after review of your wishes for health care in case you become incapable of directing your own care.
It confirms your choice for a substitute decision-maker, the person who will speak on your behalf if you become incapable. It undertakes end-of-life planning, should this become necessary.
It is now recognized that palliative care should be implemented much earlier in the course of chronic diseases than near the end of life. In the case of dementia, it may be a decade or more before death.
The core principle of palliative care is facilitating effective collaboration between the ill person, the family and the health-care professionals involved in care. Early in the course of a chronic illness -take heart disease for example -primary care would coordinate care, with both the cardiologist and palliative care physician acting in a back-up capacity as required.
As the disease progresses, the cardiologist would assume leadership for care. As curative options decrease and symptom burden increases, specialty palliative care (sPC) would become the coordinator for the patient, focusing on quality of life, including physical, psychosocial and spiritual factors.
Specialty palliative care began with a focus on cancer. Half of cancer patients now receive consultation for palliative care. Yet fewer than 10 per cent of heart failure patients are referred.
The burdens of pain, anxiety, fatigue and breathlessness are often worse than those of even metastatic lung and pancreatic cancer. Because of advancements in medication and technology, patients can survive longer with their chronic diseases. It means incorporating measures to reduce hospitalizations, maintaining independence, decreasing frailty and improving the quality of life for both patients and their families. There is a lot to do.
Life expectancy has reached 83.
The need for palliative care is expected to increase dramatically over the coming decades as our population ages.
The label of palliative care is off-putting to many physicians, patients and families. It is misunderstood to be care done only when a cure is no longer possible and a person is at the end of their life.
In fact, good palliative care started earlier in the illness journey has been shown to provide better communication between health care and family, less anxiety and depression and in some cases, it even extends life. It should be recognized as an equal partner to curative treatment.
There are three physicians on staff at Health Sciences North who have specialized in palliative care out of the 468 specialists on staff. There is one clinical nurse specialist in palliative care.
These realities need to change. Dr. Peter Zalan, a retired intensive care physician, is the former head of medical staff at Health Sciences North.

Subject: Cancer; Patients; Emergency medical care; Quality of life; Life expectancy; Health care; Chronic illnesses; Cardiovascular disease; Palliative care; Health sciences; Older people; Heart; Intensive care; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Physicians

Company / organization: Name: Health Sciences North; NAICS: 622110

Identifier / keyword: palliative; physicians; ontario; misunderstood; specialty; attempt

Document 157 of 281

Premiers push back on refugee resettlements

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-push-back-on-refugee-resettlements/docview/3106293186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+push+back+on+refugee+resettlements&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is looking at taking the hundreds of thousands of refugee claimants in Ontario and Quebec and moving them across the country.
It's an idea that already is being met with resistance from premiers who say the feds should deal with their own programs.
"Alberta's government is opposed to the federal government's plan to relocate tens of thousands of asylum claimants to Alberta, especially without any financial assistance to support the province in doing so," Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said in a statement Thursday.
Smith joined New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs, who said during a speech on Wednesday that the federal government was looking to move 4,600 refugee claimants to his province. Higgs said during his speech that New Brunswick is a welcoming place, but for the federal government to move so many people there without providing financial support is unacceptable.
"This sudden and unilateral proposal by Ottawa is deeply concerning, and I feel obligated to share the challenges it will bring to our province," Higgs said.
What would those challenges be? For a start, provincial welfare payments would go through the roof - one senior Ontario government official shared that one in four people on welfare in that province is an asylum seeker. That fact alone they said was costing the provincial government $500 million per year.
In a province like New Brunswick, those figures would be lower, but still a strain on the system.
It is the strain on school programs, on the welfare system and social services in general that has led to Quebec's government and the Bloc Québécois in Ottawa to push for relocation. According to a document viewed by my Postmedia colleagues at National Post, the Trudeau Liberals propose to send 32,500 asylum seekers to British Columbia, 28,000 to Alberta, 4,952 refugees to Nova Scotia and 4,600 to New Brunswick.
The redistribution idea is being pushed by Quebec, where 37,780 refugee applications were filed between Jan. 1 and July 31, that's equivalent to the population of Sorel-Tracy in a year, just from refugee claimants.
Ontario wouldn't receive any of the resettled people from Quebec, because in that same period, Ontario has seen 55,700 people claim asylum, more than the population of North Bay.
Here's a crazy idea, instead of spreading the problem around, how about fixing it? The Trudeau government allowed the illegal border crossing at Roxham Road to fester for years, claiming it couldn't be fixed until it was. Now, instead of people walking across the border, they are flying in from around the world and claiming asylum as soon as they arrive.
The Trudeau government has made it easier to enter Canada with moves such as lowering or eliminating visa requirements or making it easier to enter as a foreign student or temporary foreign worker. That has seen an upshot in asylum claims, including from places like India, which has seen a huge uptick in asylum claims by people allowed in on work or study visas.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller hasn't said the numbers reported by National Post are false, but he has said comments by Premier Higgs were "irresponsible" and that the "allegations by Premier Higgs are largely fictitious."
Clearly, the Trudeau government is trying to move people around the country rather than fix the problem by dealing with a broken visa system and holes in the work and study programs. Thankfully, while the feds won't try to fix the problem, some premiers are standing up and saying no.
"The Trudeau government's unrestrained open border policies permitting well over a million newcomers each year into Canada is causing significant challenges, and it's simply not sustainable," Premier Smith said.
She pointed out that such moves will increase the cost of living and strain social services.
It's time to end Trudeau's open border policies, but it might take an election to do so. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Federal government; Political asylum; Decades

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 158 of 281

Time is up for the Trudeau Liberals; Letters to the editor

Author: Saumier, Glen

Publication info: Saumier, Glen. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-is-up-trudeau-liberals-letters-editor/docview/3106293132/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+is+up+for+the+Trudeau+Liberals%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Saumier%2C+Glen&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is either too conceited or too self-absorbed to realize his time as leader of the federal Liberals is up.
The train has already left the station. Canadians are ready for change and Liberals have zero chance of forming the next federal government. The NDP will also suffer losses as Canadians will blame Jagmeet Singh for supporting the unpopular Liberals.
It's amazing how things have changed. In 2015, Trudeau was worshipped like a God. Women wept in his presence, and Canadians surrounded him in malls just so they could touch the agent of change and the hope for the future.
Who could forget "we are here to help the middle class and those who want to join it." It almost seems laughable now because today there are fewer Canadians within the middle class.
Canadians are struggling.
The high cost of living, unpopular taxes and the unattainable first home have turned people against the Trudeau Liberals. These days, Justin is greeted with protests and angry mobs shouting expletives wherever he goes. And the Liberals still don't get it. They are out of touch and Canadians are out of patience.
The Conservatives will get their kick at the can and spend the next eight to 10 years undoing Liberal policies. Ten years from now, Canadians will again demand change and vote the Liberals back in. And they will spend another eight to 10 years undoing Conservative policies.
See how it works. Politics in Canada is cyclical and power is shared by two parties. And it will never be the NDP. Ontario tried that once. Glen Saumier Sudbury

Subject: Canadians; Middle class

Identifier / keyword: justin; trudeau; conceited; realize; leader; federal

Title: Time is up for the Trudeau Liberals; Letters to the editor

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 17, 2024

Dateline: Sudbury

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08392544

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 159 of 281

Poilievre says he won't introduce anti-union policies; Labour chief says it's hard to take Conservative leader at his word

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-says-he-wont-introduce-anti-union/docview/3104764351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+says+he+won%27t+introduce+anti-union+policies%3B+Labour+chief+says+it%27s+hard+to+take+Conservative+leader+at+his+word&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is pledging not to introduce the kind of union-opposed legislation he championed as a Harper government member of Parliament if he becomes prime minister.
The surprising pledge is part of what he calls an evolution in his thinking on labour unions.
And it comes as Air Canada is prepared to start shutting down its operations beginning Sunday if the Air Line Pilots Association issues a 72-hour strike notice. There has been pressure from business groups across the country for the federal government to intervene and prevent the strike.
In 2012, Poilievre and the rest of the Conservative Harper government brought in back to work legislation for those same pilots before they even took to picket lines. He said this week the pilots are negotiating for better wages and argued nothing should stand in the way of that.
In a statement to Postmedia, Poilievre said he is now pro-union and wouldn't introduce those bills again.
"Hundreds of visits to workshops, factories, union halls and training centres have taught me a lot and helped me evolve my thinking," he said. "A Common Sense Conservative Government will not introduce or pass bills C-377, C-525 or right to work laws. Period. This commitment will be written in my election platform."
Poilievre was largely silent on the threat of a work stoppage at CN and CPKC rail companies, which the Liberals sent to binding arbitration after a one-day strike last month.
Poilievre heaped blame on the Liberals and NDP for driving up the cost of living over the last nine years and said there is clearly a need for unions.
"Billionaires and multinationals have gained power and wealth from the money-printing inflation that balloons their assets while robbing buying power from workers after nine years of the NDP-Liberals," he said. "It will take strong unions to reverse these losses and to fight for wage and pension gains and better working conditions."
Poilievre also pointed to private member's bills his MPs have championed including one requiring pensions take precedence in any bankruptcy, a bill requiring airlines to play flight attendants for work before the plane takes off and his call for tariffs on Chinese made steel.
"I don't understand why it is that Canadian pilots are paid so much worse than American pilots," he said this week. "I would call on Air Canada to negotiate in good faith with the pilots. We're not going to support preempting those negotiations."
While they were in government, the Harper Conservatives also brought in legislation that was denounced by labour groups. Bill C-377 required unions to publicly disclose their finances and Bill C-525, which changed the voting process for joining a union, requiring a secret ballot and a higher threshold for success.
Those bills both passed as private member's legislation while the Conservatives were in power, but were repealed in 2016 after the Liberals came into office. Poilievre spoke out against the repeal, arguing the government was forcing people into unions by taking away the secret ballot.
"The government's problem is with the outcome. The government might not be happy that when workers are given the choice through a democratic vote, they opt not to unionize. However, that is the choice of the workers, not the choice of the government," he said in Parliament in 2016.
The Conservatives'most recent policy book, amended at last year's convention, includes commitments to those policies, but Poilievre says a Conservative government he leads won't introduce them.
He also pointed out that his party recently voted for the government's anti-replacement worker legislation - commonly referred to as an "anti-scab" bill.
Siobhán Vipond, executive vice-president with the Canadian Labour Congress, said it's hard to take Poilievre at his word on this issue, because he has such a long record of anti-union behaviour.
"We're very wary about this idea of words that are empty if you don't fundamentally understand the role that unions and workers have in a country right now," she said. "It's just really hard to trust that, knowing when you actually had the power, what you did with it."
Vipond said even just a month ago Poilievre was silent when the government sent railworkers to binding arbitration after a oneday lockout.
"We're not talking historical. It was a month ago, the railworkers weren't worth speaking up for a month ago," she said. "If that is what he's going to do, we're going to need to see more than one opportunistic statement in the media."
Vipond said when employers believe the government is prepared to legislate back to work, they are much less likely to negotiate.
Dan Robertson, a former chief strategist for the Conservative party, said unions were considered an adversary to the party and not worth pursuing previously but that changed over time.
"Five or six years ago, there was just a 'unions are bad'opinion that was pervasive in the Conservative movement and party, and it was very, very difficult to overcome," he said.
Robertson said that changed when Conservatives looked closer and realized whatever the views of union leadership, blue-collar union workers shared many of the same values as party members.
"Conservatives recognize that communities need strong institutions and among them are labour unions. They provide workers with power. They afford solidarity, mutual aid and representation all of which benefits workers and their communities," he said.
In the 2022 election, several private sector unions endorsed Ontario Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative party, Robertson said that was proof this approach could also benefit Conservatives electorally.
He said there are still Conservatives upset at the party's outreach to labour, but they can't argue with the results. rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 160 of 281

'A horrible way to experience health care': Hallway medicine not going away, despite political promises

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/horrible-way-experience-health-care-hallway/docview/3103494271/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+horrible+way+to+experience+health+care%27%3A+Hallway+medicine+not+going+away%2C+despite+political+promises&amp;title=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Patients are spending more time in hallways and "unconventional spaces" at some Ottawa hospitals than they were in 2018 when Ontario Premier Doug Ford vowed to end hallway medicine.
