Document 161 of 281

Sudbury needs 1,400 more hospital beds, union says; Union researcher also says Ontario needs billions more in health-care spending, 34,000 additional staff and 4,600 new hospital beds

Author: Carmichaelhealth, Harold

Publication info: Carmichaelhealth, Harold. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-needs-1-400-more-hospital-beds-union-says/docview/3102812541/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+needs+1%2C400+more+hospital+beds%2C+union+says%3B+Union+researcher+also+says+Ontario+needs+billions+more+in+health-care+spending%2C+34%2C000+additional+staff+and+4%2C600+new+hospital+beds&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Carmichaelhealth%2C+Harold&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health Sciences North in Sudbury needs 1,400 beds over the next decade to keep up with the demand for services, a union researcher says.
Doug Allan said Monday that pressure on the hospital will only grow as the city's population ages and because Health Sciences North serves northeastern Ontario, as well as Sudbury.
Health Sciences North now has about 600 beds at its Ramsey Lake Health Centre site.
Allan, a Canadian Union of Public Employees senior researcher, also noted Sudbury's hospital had a shortfall in its 202-24 budget and needed an additional $348,000 from the provincial government in emergency funding to balance its books.
He said the hospital could use another 1,400 beds or 140 a year over the next decade.
Allan has just compiled a report looking at the hospital funding across the province. He said Ontario's hospitals need an immediate annual cash infusion of $2 billion to restore their emergency departments and add beds and staff to address shortages and return patient care to proper levels.
"Ontario is experiencing a hospital crisis," Doug Allan said at a press conference in Sudbury on Monday. "There have been thousands of closures of hospital emergency departments, cases of patients being treated in hallways, and hospital bed shortages that drive the emergency department and hospital closures.
"The current plan of this government will make this situation much, much worse if it's not changed. We have changed it in the past. We can change it in the future."
According to Allan, Ontario hospitals need an additional 34,292 full-time employees to address staffing shortfalls to bring levels up to what exists in other provinces.
"We have high (hospital) job vacancies in Ontario, he said. "It has just gone up and up and up ... (The hospital sector) not a good place to work because of the shortfalls and capacity issues." In his report, Allan noted that according to Statistics Canada, Ontario hospitals currently have 23,330 job vacancies, a figure that is 534 per cent higher than in 2015 when there were about 3,635 employees in late 2015.
Allan said that in 2023, Ontario had 34,931 hospital beds or 2.23 beds per 1,000 people. That's down 12 per cent, he said, from the 2.53 beds per 1,000 mark in 2005. Ontario's population, meanwhile, has increased 24.5 per cent.
"It's way off the average of developed economies -4.1 beds per 1,000 people, he said. "This (funding) crisis has shrunk the beds per capita to 2.23 from 2.53." An additional 4,699 beds, suggested Allan, would be needed to bring things back to the 2005 level.
Ontario hospital underfunding is only getting worse due to an aging population, added the senior researcher.
"We are in an aging society," said Allan, noting that 60 per cent of hospital beds are now tied up with people aged 65-plus.
Adding some 3,500 hospital beds would begin to address the bed shortages, he said.
To make matters worse, Allan said 43,000 Ontarians are now waiting to get into long-term care institutions. The province said it would open some 15,000 new longterm care beds by 2023, he said, but the total only ended up being 2,246.
'That (figure) doesn't even include the beds that were closed, 600 in Toronto alone, with other beds expected to be lost in places such as Brantford and Niagara Falls," he said.
Michael Hurley, president of the Ontario Council of Hospital Unions of the Canadian Union of Public Employees, said the hospital funding crisis affects all Ontarians but the province doesn't seem interested in correcting it. The union represents about 30,000 hospital workers provincewide.
"With a one-quarter increase in people waiting for surgery, (the government) must be open to fixing the problem," he said. "We estimate what is needed is $2 billion in extra funding per year. People are paying for it in safety, waiting for surgeries that should be addressed in a timely way."
The Canadian Union of Public Employees forecasts that five times more hospital beds will be needed than planned for by the Ford government -a shortfall of 13,800 hospital beds -by 2032. As well, there could be a hospital staff shortfall of 80,000 by then.
Allan's report concluded that "Ontario lacks hospital capacity. Under (Premier Doug) Ford, hospital capacity has fallen behind the growth in need. (The) Ford government plans for the future, especially for the next several years, will see capacity fall behind at an even more rapid pace. The Ford government capacity plan has to improve.
"To begin this process, the government must at the very least fund hospitals to maintain their current service levels. That would require the four per cent funding to offset population, aging, and utilization. Inflation would also have to be offset, meaning that hospital funding to maintain service levels should be in the seven per cent range. That would mean a provincial funding increase of $2 billion. To improve hospital capacity (e.g. by returning beds per capita to 2005 levels) would mean significantly more funding."
Hurley said that in the months leading up to the next provincial election in mid-2026, he hopes Ontarians will collectively demand that the hospital underfunding issue be addressed by the province.
"We hope this (report) will resonate with people whose family members are suffering and not getting treated, not getting into beds, and being provided with services that they should," he said.
A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said told Postmedia that the provincial government under Ford has made "record investments" in the health-care system, including $85 billion this year, a 31 per cent increase from 2018.
"We have increased our investment across the hospital sector by four per cent for a record two years in a row," Hannah Jensen said in an email.
"Over the last two years, we have registered a record number of new nurses, adding 32,000 new nurses, with another 30,000 studying nursing at one of Ontario's colleges or universities."
The province is working on more than 50 hospital development projects and has added 3,500 hospital beds since 2020, Jensen said. hcarmichael@postmedia.comX: @HaroldCarmichae

Document 162 of 281

'We are not the Justin Trudeau Party of Canada'; Liberal lawmaker thinks prime minister staying on hurts his party's chances

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-are-not-justin-trudeau-party-canada-liberal/docview/3101776501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+not+the+Justin+Trudeau+Party+of+Canada%27%3B+Liberal+lawmaker+thinks+prime+minister+staying+on+hurts+his+party%27s+chances&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Liberals set to gather next week for a caucus retreat, one of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's MPs is once again calling for a change in party leadership.
New Brunswick Liberal MP Wayne Long first called for a change of leadership after the Liberals'loss in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection in June. The downtown Toronto seat had been considered a safe seat for the party and the loss to the Conservatives led to many whispered calls for change.
Long was the only sitting MP to publicly urge Trudeau to step down at the time. This week, he said he still feels it's time for the prime minister to go after nine years in office.
"I haven't changed my position one iota. The prime minister does need to step down for the good of our party and for the good of the country," he said. "We are not the Justin Trudeau Party of Canada. We are the Liberal Party of Canada."
Long was first elected in 2015, but he is not running for re-election. He has opposed his own government several times before, on the Energy East Pipeline, on proposed tax changes for private corporations and, most recently, on the government's decision to phase out salmon farms that operate in B.C. waterways.
Calgary Liberal MP George Chahal called for a broader caucus meeting soon after the byelection defeat, but one did not take place. Trudeau has spoken with individual caucus members over the summer and dropped into meetings of both Ontario and Quebec MPs. Long said he spoke with Trudeau over the summer and believes the prime minister understands his position.
He said Trudeau has served the country well, navigating the country through the pandemic, bringing in programs like subsidized child care and dental care and achieving strong economic performance post pandemic, but he said every politician's career has an end date.
"I like the prime minister. I think he has done great work, but I also recognize that we can't win the next election under his leadership," said Long.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has maintained a sizable lead in public opinion polls, as much as 20 percentage points, for more than a year. Long argues with a change in leadership, the Liberals could win a fourth term.
"I believe Pierre Poilievre is absolutely beatable, and I would argue there hasn't been a leader of the official opposition who is as disliked as Pierre Poilievre," he said.
When Liberals begin three days of meetings in Nanaimo, B.C., on Monday they will be discussing a different parliamentary landscape, now that NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has announced he is leaving the confidence and supply agreement that kept the minority government in power for the past two years.
The move puts the Liberals on shaky ground as the House of Commons resumes sitting this month, needing the support of at least one other party to pass legislation and keep the government from falling in confidence votes.
The Liberals'national campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst also announced Thursday he was stepping down, leaving a vacancy in a key position just as a threat of an early election has been heightened. Long said that is another major setback for the party.
In a statement, Broadhurst cited the toll two decades and five national campaigns have taken on himself and his family.
He said the upcoming federal election could be the most critical federal campaign of his life, and the party deserves a campaign director who can bring more energy and devotion to the job.
Long said he knows that as a backbench MP who is not re-offering he can be ignored. But he said he has gained some political skills over nine years in office and believes Canadians have simply become tired of the prime minister.
He said he hopes his colleagues speak up because absent a change, the party could be headed to disaster.
"I continue to hope some of my colleagues will be a little more vocal and say what is needed to be done," he said. "I think we are sleepwalking to disaster if we don't read the room as a party." Postmedia News, with a file from The Canadian Press rtumilty@postmedia.com

Subject: Aquaculture; Leadership; Prime ministers; Pandemics

Document 163 of 281

Feds want to be landlords

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feds-want-be-landlords/docview/3098556997/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+want+to+be+landlords&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Everyone agrees that Canada faces a housing shortage. But it's not a shortage of homes: it's a shortage of homes people can afford. In Toronto, for example, there is currently a glut of condos on the market. Prices have fallen as investors off-load properties: high interest rates mean their mortgage payments exceed the rents they can get.
Homes have become unaffordable because of the disconnect between wages and prices. The average price-to-earnings ratio in Canada more than doubled from 3.2 in 1980 to 6.7 in 2020. Vancouver currently has the highest price to earnings ratio, at 12.3, followed by Toronto at 9.3. To put that in perspective, to buy an average house in those cities in July 2024, you need a household income of $208,000 and $226,000, respectively. Not exactly middle-class.
Now, the federal government claims to have an answer. The Liberals propose to convert unused public lands and buildings to housing - an idea the Conservatives have been floating for a couple of years. But here's the twist: residents won't own the land the home is built on. Instead, the government will lease the land to developers so it can maintain ownership and "keep home prices low."
In other words, it plans to get into the condo business, which private developers are abandoning because the market is saturated. In Toronto, several condo projects have gone into receivership in recent years, including The One, a supposedly "iconic" tower at the corner of Yonge and Bloor.
The Liberal leasehold project was actually announced back in April just after the federal budget. But now it's been repackaged in a shiny new video featuring the prime minister dumping on the Opposition for proposing to "sell it all to developers." Instead, Trudeau presents long-term leasing as the saviour of the Canadian housing dream, while failing to mention its downsides.
Long-term leasing doesn't build equity at the same rate, because you don't own the land your home is built on. And like in a condo, your fees, a.k.a. rent, can rise unexpectedly, not just because of need, but by government fiat. When the state is your landlord, the feeling of powerlessness is palpable.
Ask the leasehold cottage owners in Ontario's public wilderness parks, who for years felt like a "sword of Damocles" sat over their head as the provincial government decided whether to renew their leases. Or leaseholders of recreational properties in Saskatchewan forced to move when the First Nations who leased them their lands changed the terms. Or the leaseholders in British Columbia who faced a 180 per cent rent hike when their property assessment increased.
On top of this, Trudeau claims that the government will build the "right kind" of homes (i.e., "affordable" homes). The government already has several projects underway, which Housing Minister Sean Fraser showed off in another shiny video. But how will the government keep these homes "affordable?" Will it sell at below market rates? Will it cut corners in construction? Will it means-test buyers? And what happens when they sell? The shiny videos do not address these questions. They are a public relations response to the Conservatives'biggest issue: the sense that the Canadian dream is slipping away. A big part of that dream has always been home ownership, something that young people now say is out of reach. And when that dream slips away, so do the votes attached to it, as the Liberals are seeing at the polls.
Of course, the real way the government needs to address the housing crisis is on the demand side. They are finally doing this, by reducing the number of low-wage temporary workers in Canada that ballooned over the past few years, as well as cutting off a veritable deluge of international students. That announcement didn't get a shiny video, however, just a little mention.
Why? Because curtailing immigration runs counter to the Liberal narrative that diversity is our strength.
By failing to manage that key file, this government has done a disservice to all Canadians, new and old. And now it wants to lease them their house. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Housing prices; Condominiums

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing prices

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: everyone; agrees; canada; faces; housing; shortage

Title: Feds want to be landlords

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 29, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08392544

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Opinions

ProQuest document ID: 3098556997

Document 164 of 281

'There's only one taxpayer'? Not quite

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/theres-only-one-taxpayer-not-quite/docview/3098556966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+only+one+taxpayer%27%3F+Not+quite&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Denley%2C+Randall&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's only one taxpayer. It's an adage that attempts to explain taxation in the simplest possible terms, but the problem is that it's neither entirely accurate nor particularly useful.
Local federal cabinet Minister Jenna Sudds recently used the phrase while deflecting the City of Ottawa's request that the federal government pay its fair share of property taxes. Sudds said, "We must remember that there is only one taxpayer," then urged the city government "to manage their money well and spend it wisely," just like the federal government does.
Citizen columnist Brigitte Pellerin recently explored the one-taxpayer concept and found it a bit silly for one level of government to seek money from another because it all comes from you and me in any case. Pellerin asked, "Is there a magic money tree under the Peace Tower that no one told us about?" It seems that there is and it's bearing an amazing amount of fruit. How else to explain the ability of Justin Trudeau's government to spend about $40 billion more this year than it will actually take in? Talk about managing our money wisely.
Things are a little different at the municipal level. By law, the city can't budget for a deficit and its problem is that there is only one tax, not one taxpayer. This year, Ottawa expects to raise just over $2.1 billion in property taxes. Its next biggest source of revenue is about $994 million in user fees.
The premise of a municipal budget is that not much will change from one year to the next. When the city takes on more, as it has with the LRT, then that equation rapidly gets out of balance. So does the city budget and there is no easy fix.
While the city has only one tax, the number of taxes levied by the federal and provincial governments is limited only by their imaginations and their political nerve.
The idea that you and I ultimately pay all taxes ignores the substantial sum that both governments collect in corporate taxes. The feds plan to rake in $90.7 billion this year, the province $24.9 billion.
Then there are all the other revenue sources. The federal government collects customs duties, excise taxes and profits from Crown corporations. The GST alone is estimated to bring in $54.1 billion this year.
The province has a health tax, Crown corporation income, a land transfer tax and its share of the HST. That sales tax will generate about $39 billion this year.
The provincial government even dips into the property tax. The average urban residential property in Ottawa pays about $4,325 in municipal property taxes annually. This same property pays an additional $635 in provincial education taxes, collected as part of your property tax bill.
Let's not forget the magic money tree. Both the federal and provincial governments routinely run deficits, something the Doug Ford government finds mildly regrettable and the Trudeau government accepts as a fact of life. In effect, there are no real restraints on federal and provincial spending.
It's a bit rich to see our city government criticized for its spending when municipal governments are the only ones that have to make difficult choices and accept fiscal discipline. The federal and provincial governments have reserved the most lucrative forms of taxation for themselves and they still can't come close to balancing their books, even when economic growth drives up the tax take every year.
The nation-wide problem with municipal transit deficits suggests an imbalance between cities'resources and their responsibilities. It's a problem that could be fixed with a sum of money that's modest by federal and provincial standards.
Without federal and provincial help, Ottawa will be faced with a transit budget shortfall that will average about $140 million a year until it's fixed. The city will have to increase property taxes substantially, make big service cuts or some combination of the two.
Surely the "one taxpayer" wouldn't want to see that, especially knowing how much money the Trudeau government squanders on things that offer the public little value at all. Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist and author. Contact him at randalldenley1@gmail.com

Subject: Taxation; Budgets; Property taxes; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Budgets Property taxes

Identifier / keyword: taxpayer; adage; attempts; explain; taxation; simplest

Document 166 of 281

Debt Clock comes to Sudbury, sounding alarm about Ontario's $400-billion debt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/debt-clock-comes-sudbury-sounding-alarm-about/docview/3097644925/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Debt+Clock+comes+to+Sudbury%2C+sounding+alarm+about+Ontario%27s+%24400-billion+debt&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In bringing its Debt Clock to Sudbury, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation hopes to push the Ontario government to pay down debt.
The clock, which shows Ontario's $400-billion debt go up in real-time, was in Sudbury last week.
"The provincial debt is already more than $400 billion and it's going up by tens of millions of dollars every day," Jay Goldberg, CTF Ontario director, said in a release. "Every dollar Queen's Park racks up in debt today is a dollar plus interest that taxpayers will have to pay back tomorrow."
The Debt Clock is a huge digital counter mounted on the side of a moving truck. The Canadian Taxpayers Federation said that each Ontarian's share of the provincial debt is more than $28,000.
The Debt Clock shows the debt increasing by $768 every second.
By the end of this year, Premier Doug Ford will have added more than $86 billion to the provincial debt, the federation said.
Provincial debt interest charges will cost taxpayers $14 billion this year, it added.
"The Ford government is spending more money on debt interest than post-secondary education," Goldberg said. "The province is wasting too much money on debt interest charges so Ford must cut spending and balance the budget."
The Debt Clock will make stops across Ontario to sound the alarm about the rising provincial debt.
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation said it is Canada's leading non-partisan citizens'advocacy group fighting for lower taxes, less waste and accountable government.
Founded in 1990, the federation has more than 235,000 supporters and seven offices across Canada. Free-will, non tax-receiptable contributions fund the Canadian Taxpayers Federation. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar Star Staff

Document 167 of 281

Provinces lead on cannabis reform while feds dither; A lack of meaningful reform since legalization risks tarnishing PM's legacy

Author: Khan, Omar

Publication info: Khan, Omar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-lead-on-cannabis-reform-while-feds/docview/3097644156/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+lead+on+cannabis+reform+while+feds+dither%3B+A+lack+of+meaningful+reform+since+legalization+risks+tarnishing+PM%27s+legacy&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Khan%2C+Omar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the Cannabis Act became law back in 2018, it was hailed by the federal Liberals as a bold move that would keep cannabis out of the hands of youth, protect public health and safety, and reduce criminal activity. The idea was that by creating a regulated industry, illicit operators would either jump on the bandwagon and go legal or be pushed out of the market by a newly created legal sector. Some progress has been made on this front.
A 2022 study by Deloitte estimated that between 2018 (legalization) and 2021, the industry contributed $43.5 billion toward Canada's GDP while sustaining over 150,000 jobs nationally. Despite this, almost six years on, the industry continues to face serious challenges, with many feeling that the federal Liberals have washed their hands of the industry they helped to create.
According to Statistics Canada, the illicit market still accounts for about 35 per cent of total sales nationally. Another recent study by Deloitte found that the average price in the illicit market remains 20 per cent lower than in licensed stores, which deters price-conscious consumers from going legal. This poses real risks to Canadians as revenues generated by the sale of illicit market cannabis often find their way into the pockets of organized crime.
Despite this, the federal government continues to ignore the advice of the House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance, which in February recommended adjustments to the excise tax structure aimed at improving the ability of the legal cannabis sector to compete with an entrenched illicit market.
And product from the illicit market is risky. A 2022 study by the National Research Council on behalf of the Ontario Provincial Police found that a random sampling of seized illegal cannabis contained high levels of unauthorized pesticides. Add to this, the fact that many of the products they sell are packaged to look like popular junk food brands to appeal to youth, and it's clear that more work is needed.
After the creation of a Cannabis Industry Forum within the Department of Innovation, Science and Economic Development this past February, some industry players expressed cautious optimism about increased federal engagement. But the minister has yet to attend a meeting, signalling political indifference to an already skeptical stakeholder group.
The good news is that Canadian provinces, many of which have Conservative or NDP governments, have decided to fill the void created by federal inaction and have begun taking decisive steps to help ensure the long-term viability of their legal operators.
In September 2023, for instance, Ontario reduced the average wholesale mark-up it charges licensed producers. It then doubled the number of stores a licensed retailer can operate and allocated $31 million over three years to fund a Provincial Joint Forces Cannabis Enforcement Team targeting illicit market stores and websites across the province.
The Alberta government has boldly allowed licensed retailers to sell products at festivals or trade shows closed to minors - opening up another opportunity for responsible consumer engagement. In June of this year, the province announced that certain legally licensed retailers could sell private label products, usually sold at lower prices to appeal to a more cost-conscious consumer.
In 2023, Manitoba eliminated a six-per-cent levy on legal cannabis sales, which had driven prices up unnecessarily.
As helpful as these provincial changes are, they cannot fully compensate for a lack of federal action.
In March of this year, the recommendations of a statutory review of the Cannabis Act were released (more than a year late), with the federal government's expert panel confirming that "... the illicit cannabis market remains entrenched, and too many illicit retailers continue to operate both online and physical stores." Legitimate cannabis retailers, who are on the front lines of the fight against illegal operators, know this all too well with more and more cannabis companies filing for bankruptcy protection in 2024.
Cannabis legalization remains a cornerstone achievement of the Trudeau Liberals, but the lack of meaningful reform and engagement with industry since legalization risks tarnishing this legacy. With a federal election just over a year away, there is still time for federal action to help ensure the sustainability and success of an industry they helped to create. Only time will tell if they follow the lead of their provincial counterparts.
Omar Khan is the Chief Communications and Public Affairs Officer at Calgary-based High Tide Inc., the second-largest retailer of legal cannabis in the world by store count. He previously served as Chief of Staff to Ontario's Minister of Health and Long-Term Care and Minister of Economic Development and Trade, and Senior Adviser to Ontario's Attorney General.

Document 172 of 281

It's time for Algoma-Manitoulin's MPP to resign and apologize

Author: Farquhar, Ruth

Publication info: Farquhar, Ruth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-algoma-manitoulins-mpp-resign-apologize/docview/3094966684/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+time+for+Algoma-Manitoulin%27s+MPP+to+resign+and+apologize&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Farquhar%2C+Ruth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 2023, our NDP MPP, Mike Mantha, was removed from caucus over alleged workplace harassment. Although no details of the harassment were released to the public at that time, he was now sitting as an Independent MPP with no party affiliation.
It's difficult at the best of times to have our concerns heard in the Ontario Legislature but now it would be even harder for Mantha to do so.
I also felt that the NDP did the right thing to remove him following the investigation that seemed to be quite thorough and which included three witnesses who had direct knowledge, video evidence, email exchanges, text messages and online communications.
At that time, Mantha said very little about the accusations on the advice of his lawyer. Recently, however, due to an arbitration decision between the NDP and CUPE, the details of the alleged workplace harassment have been made public.
First of all, Mantha has been our (Algoma-Manitoulin) MPP since 2011. He is well-known and, obviously, well-liked, winning four consecutive elections in Algoma-Manitoulin.
Following his ousting from the NDP, he carried on as if nothing had happened. Attending pretty much every event on the Island that he could, as the year went on rumours started surfacing (as they do in small communities) that he had been set up by his own party, which I dismissed as ludicrous and still do.
Now we have gotten details of the alleged harassment, which includes Mantha pressuring an employee to have "non-consensual" sexual interactions with him. The arbitration heard he asked her to send explicit pictures of herself and that he engaged in several incidents of unwanted physical and sexual contact, notwithstanding her protests that he stop. It also heard that he advised her to tell him about any sexual dreams about him and he made comments about her appearance, among other things.
Following the removal of Mantha from the NDP in 2023, he is quoted on CBC saying, "I've taken responsibility for my actions and I will learn from them. I will become a better person and I will move on and serve the people of Algoma Manitoulin to the best of my abilities."
Following the most recent release of details of the alleged harassment, I attempted to reach Mantha and was referred by his office to this statement: "The last two years have been very challenging, but it is time to move past this matter. While I disagree with some of the characterizations made, on the advice of legal counsel I will not be commenting further at this time. It is my honour to represent the people of Algoma-Manitoulin. I will fulfil my role as MPP for the remainder of this term and leave the decision on my future in the hands of the voters of Algoma-Manitoulin."
Okay, Mike, how have you taken responsibility? You have never said anything about being sorry, even if your lawyer says not to say sorry to your alleged victim because she could sue you or perhaps even press criminal charges.
You could at least apologize to your constituents who voted for you as a member of the NDP.
You say that the last two years have been challenging. Really, for who? I guess you mean for yourself because there has been no indication that you think it might have been challenging for anyone else, like maybe the victim? You also say we need to move on. Yeah, sure, you would like it if we never mention it again. I wonder if all of the people who feel betrayed by your behaviour are able to move on? All of this is not taking responsibility. It's asking us - the ones who voted for you - to ignore everything and forget about it. Not going to happen.
Attending every parade, fair and birthday party on Manitoulin Island will not make this go away. It is time for you to resign. Ruth Farquhar is a freelance writer based on Manitoulin Island.

Location: Manitoulin Island

Identifier / keyword: mantha; removed; caucus; alleged; workplace; harassment

Document 173 of 281

Sudbury's Science North, tourism sector impresses federal minister; 'I think there are a lot of untapped jewels of tourism here'

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburys-science-north-tourism-sector-impresses/docview/3093996805/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury%27s+Science+North%2C+tourism+sector+impresses+federal+minister%3B+%27I+think+there+are+a+lot+of+untapped+jewels+of+tourism+here%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Joining the many Ontario Liberals in Sudbury this past week was an MP from Quebec who actually spoke with the media.
Tourism Minister Soraya Martinez Ferrada, who represents the Montreal riding of Hochelaga, made some time for The Star just prior to boarding a plane to Toronto late Wednesday afternoon.
She said she had come to the Nickel City at the invite of local MPs Marc Serre and Viviane Lapointe, and learned a lot during her three-day stay.
"As you know, Sudbury has a reputation from the mining sector of being industrial, but it has changed a lot and has lots to offer to the tourism sector," she said. "And I was really glad to be able meet with stakeholders and to see this for myself."
Ferrada visited Science North, participated in a round table with representatives from local and regional tourism groups, met with entrepreneurs, and made a trip to the A.Y. Jackson lookout trail at Onaping Falls.
She said she was impressed by the volume of visitors to the science centre, which sees about 400,000 annual admissions in a market of fewer than 170,000 people.
"That's more than double the population," she remarked. "A lot of places don't have those numbers. That shows people are believing in the organization and people are coming from from other places to see it, which is great."
She also got a sense of the other attractions, both natural and cultural, in the city and its surrounding area.
"I think there are a lot of untapped jewels of tourism here," she said. "I can't wait to see how the sector is going to work on it in the future."
While the City of Lakes boasts plenty of outdoor recreation opportunities, in both warm and wintry months, the tourism minister said its year-round urban attributes should not be discounted.
"There's the seasonality of the region that could be developed, and I think they are working on that," she said. "But the urban sector of Sudbury could also be developed. The cultural sector is very strong, the francophone culture here is very strong. There are so many opportunities."
Ferrada said she had passed through Sudbury before but this was the first time she had lingered in the city.
"I'm looking forward to coming back soon, visiting with my other colleague Terry (Sheehan, who represents the Sault), and seeing what it looks like here in the fall maybe." jmoodie@postmedia.com

Subject: Tourism

Document 174 of 281

'A lot more to be done,'Nickel Belt MP says of Liberal government; Liberals MPs from Ontario are meeting to Sudbury to plan ahead, Marc Serre says

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lot-more-be-done-nickel-belt-mp-says-liberal/docview/3093996758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+lot+more+to+be+done%2C%27Nickel+Belt+MP+says+of+Liberal+government%3B+Liberals+MPs+from+Ontario+are+meeting+to+Sudbury+to+plan+ahead%2C+Marc+Serre+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The prime minister made a quick stop in Greater Sudbury on Wednesday but did not make time to speak with the public.
Nickel Belt MP Marc Serre confirmed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was in town to visit with members of the Ontario caucus during their annual summer retreat.
But Serre said Trudeau's schedule would not permit media availability. The Star contacted the prime minister's office seeking an interview, but the request was denied.
"Let's be fair, there's no prime minister who has come to Northern Ontario more than he has. He's been here often. We've made announcements - Maley Drive, Laurentian. He was just here in June and we went to an old age club," Serre pointed out. "This is the summer, so with the by-election in St. Paul, there was a need to meet with the MPs."
With more than 50 MPs representing Ontario ridings in the conference room, Serre said Trudeau had to prioritize information-gathering over media scrums.
"It was a great opportunity for the PM to come and talk to us, and to plan for the fall session. The prime minister's schedule to cover the country is difficult, but I think his priority was hearing from caucus members," Serre commented.
The Nickel Belt MP said Trudeau met with the caucus to "listen to the concerns we've heard over the summer, and it's an opportunity to reshape things in the fall and for the next parliamentary session."
Serre said he and Sudbury MP Viviane Lapointe are co-hosting about 50 members from the Liberal party for the two-day retreat.
"We planned an Ontario caucus retreat here in Sudbury. We picked Sudbury because we know the importance here of bringing all the Liberals together and being the hub of the north," said Serre, treasurer of the caucus. "We have a good mix of rural and urban, and mining, critical minerals, tourism, health care, so we're trying to bring that voice."
The caucus is meeting in the Nickel City over two days to discuss issues of importance to Ontarians. The Nickel Belt rep said he has heard common concerns from his constituents surrounding housing; the cost of food; health care and access to family doctors; youth employment; and climate change.
"There are a lot of concerns overall that we're trying to address," the veteran politician said. "I'm hearing a lot of concerns about affordable housing, so that's been how can the government work more closely with the province and municipalities. Housing is a big issue, as you know. We know the cost of living and food prices are a big issue, so how do we deal with those big companies - we talked about that. We need to find a way to lower prices."
An MP since 2015, Serre said he continues to work on behalf of his constituents "to get tangible results." He said as a caucus, members "are trying to find solutions."
Serre emphasized the importance of working together to solve some of the big issues of the day.
"We need to find solutions. We need to work with municipalities and the province versus a lot of that American-style politics the Conservatives seem to have," he said. "We're trying to do the best we can. There's a lot more to do. We've got a lot of improvements in certain areas. It's been tough for the past few years for Canadians and for people in the north. We know the north is always hard hit."
Serre said caucus members, especially those in Northern Ontario, want information on how the federal government can work more collaboratively with Queens Park to solve the doctor shortage.
The Liberal party maintains several caucuses that meet regularly to discuss issues of regional or thematic importance. In addition to the Ontario caucus, there are caucuses representing the Atlantic region, Quebec, the western provinces and British Columbia. There is also a women's caucus, as well as groups representing rural constituents and the Indigenous communities across the country. Each caucus presents weekly to the prime minister and cabinet.
Serre expects the next federal election will be called in October 2025. That means this iteration of the government will be the first minority government to serve a full four years.
"We've still got a lot of work ahead of us; I'm going to work hard. I'm proud of the record I've done locally and our team has done across Northern Ontario, but there's a lot more to be done," Serre commented. "But with a minority government, you never know." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Strategic materials; Prime ministers

People: Serre, Marc

Document 175 of 281

Trudeau visits Sudbury, holds no public meetings, ducks the press

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-visits-sudbury-holds-no-public-meetings/docview/3093996694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+visits+Sudbury%2C+holds+no+public+meetings%2C+ducks+the+press&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Conservatives blasted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for visiting Sudbury on Wednesday without holding any public meetings or talking to the press.
"Justin Trudeau took a surprise trip up to Sudbury to appease his Liberal MPs from Ontario at their swanky retreat before the summer ends, where they will discuss ways to make life more costly and miserable for Ontarians, especially those in Northern Ontario," the Conservatives said in a release.
"Of course, he didn't want you to know about it so he can avoid any and all security for his disastrous policies such as imposing his punishing carbon tax which has driven up the cost of gas, home heating, and groceries."
Ontario Liberals met in Sudbury this week without any public notice. Trudeau's trip to Sudbury was only announced late Wednesday with few details.
An official with the Prime Minister's Office said it was an "in and out" visit with no public events. The Star asked for an interview with the prime minister, but it was declined.
(Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, who is also the Finance minister, came to Sudbury on Thursday. Like her boss, she held no public events and did not speak to reporters.)
In any event, the almost-secret trip to the city gave fodder to the Conservatives. "These lavish getaways don't come cheap to the Canadian taxpayer," the party said in its release. "When Justin Trudeau and the Liberals jetted off to St. Andrews, (N.B), in September 2022, they billed taxpayers over $428,000 for the trip (not including the Prime Minister's security or his private jet. "Then in 2023, they went to London, Ontario and burned another $314,552 that we know about so far.
"But this won't be the only time Trudeau treats himself and his MPs and then sticks Canadians with the bill this year. It has been revealed the Liberals will also be treating themselves to a lavish retreat on Vancouver Island in September once they come back from their summer vacation.
"While Trudeau and his desperate caucus are spending tax dollars on these retreats, Canadians are struggling to simply get by."
The Conservatives then shared some numbers they said show that Trudeau's "disastrous policies ... are making life more difficult for Canadians and people in the Sudbury area."
They include: -According to the Canadian Real Estate Association, the Benchmark Price of a home in Sudbury in October 2015 was $239,500, compared to $462,800 in June 2024, an increase of 93 per cent.
-According to the CMHC, housing starts in the Greater Sudbury area totalled 268 in 2023, down from 282 in 2022 and down from 434 in 2021.
-According to the Report Card on Homelessness for 2023, there were 237 people experiencing homelessness in Greater Sudbury, a jump from 164 people in 2022.
-According to the Report Card on Homelessness for 2023, despite the Liberal promise to eliminate chronic homelessness, the number of chronic homeless persons in Greater Sudbury jumped from 143 in 2022 to 196 in 2023.
-According to the Report Card on Homelessness for 2023, the number of encampments in Greater Sudbury has jumped from 25 as of the end of 2022 to 113 as of the end of 2023. (More recent statistics from the City of Greater Sudbury put that number at 49 encampments and at least 200 homeless.)
The Conservatives also blamed Trudeau for a dramatic rise in crime. "Our communities have never been less safe and while Trudeau's Justice Minister had once tried to tell Canadians it was all in their heads, the facts don't lie, and Trudeau's chaos, crime, and disorder are running rampant across our country," the release said.
Under Trudeau's "watch" from 2015-2023, violent crime in Sudbury has risen 108 per cent, sexual assaults 158 per cent and extortion 365 per cent.
Nationally, the Conservatives said violent crime is up 50 per cent, sexual assaults 75 per cent, violent firearms offences 116 per cent, extortion 357 per cent, auto theft 46 per cent, murders 28 per cent and gang-related murders 78 per cent. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Canadians; Extortion; Sex crimes; Violent crime; Prime ministers; Homeless people; Housing starts

Document 178 of 281

We need a federal government with priorities, please

Author: Symington, Ian

Publication info: Symington, Ian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-federal-government-with-priorities-please/docview/3093355050/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+a+federal+government+with+priorities%2C+please&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Symington%2C+Ian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's been a busy month for anyone following politics here in Sudbury. Last week, Pierre Poilievre toured the north, meeting large crowds of people who are looking for answers and solutions to the high cost of living, the lack of housing and the feeling they aren't being heard by their representatives in Ottawa.
If there was a simple way to describe what I noticed when people from across Sudbury came out to meet Pierre, it was that they want change. They want a government in Ottawa that will deliver on what it promises, leaders that serve Canadians and not special interest groups and, most importantly, a prime minister who cares about the cost of things. One that won't tax you more to deliver less and also prioritizes how Ottawa spends your tax dollars.
We've seen a lot of waste over the last few years. What we haven't seen is any real results or accountability from those who were elected to spend your money wisely.
This week we will have about 70 Liberal MPs and staff here in Sudbury. They will all have spent a great deal of your tax money to travel here with all of their staff members. They are surely here to ask each other how they can win the next election.
When they consult, they consult each other, likely while enjoying fine dining. In contrast, Pierre Poilievre has been visiting every small town in Northern Ontario to ask you what is best for Canadians. He has been to Sudbury at least five times in the past two years meeting with our community leaders and, more importantly, any one of us who wants to attend his events. His level of engagement with voters is unmatched and this is why he is so well supported.
Everywhere I go these days people are now engaging with me to show support. I was at the Sudbury Cubs reunion this weekend; it was great to see the guys again and I was overwhelmed with well wishes for the campaign. In my office, more and more patients are making a point to tell me they are behind our team.
People are approaching me at the hospital, in grocery stores, hardware stores, coffee shops and restaurants with words of encouragement. I have heard so many positive stories about Conservative support from all corners of our city. In the lunchrooms underground, our hardworking miners are all of a sudden engaging in political discussions and we are gaining their support.
Back on surface, the diversity of support we are gathering is unprecedented, from blue-collar to whitecollar workers, union members, professionals and business owners, born and raised Sudburians to entire new communities of recent immigrants, even my own colleagues in the medical field, who may have leaned Liberal in the past but are now declaring their support for us.
Young voters are our fastest growing supporters. My social media is exploding with new followers, connections and friend requests. No matter the neighbourhood, I have great interactions while door knocking across this city. The list goes on and I thank everyone for their confidence in our team and know that we are listening.
Currently, we have a government without leadership, priorities or productivity. The Trudeau Liberals are out of touch and it is time for the change we all heard from voters across Northern Ontario when Pierre was here earlier to actually listen to what Canadians really want.
Canada deserves better.
Sudbury deserves better. Let's call an election and get back on track. Ian Symington is the Conservative candidate for the Sudbury riding. He is a married father of three children. He grew up in Copper Cliff where he now practises medicine. Before medicine, he was a secondary school teacher in Sudbury. He is an avid volunteer and community leader.

Subject: Leadership; Voters

Document 179 of 281

Western Canada's forest fires yet another warning shot

Author: Orlando, Cathy

Publication info: Orlando, Cathy. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/western-canadas-forest-fires-yet-another-warning/docview/3092491450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Western+Canada%27s+forest+fires+yet+another+warning+shot&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Orlando%2C+Cathy&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The eerie orange sun at sunset is a reminder that Western Canada is yet again on fire. I live in the boreal forest and forest fires terrify me. Do they terrify you, too? After becoming a climate reality leader in 2008, I was invited to join Greater Sudbury's Climate Adaptation Panel. I served as co-chair in 2010 and 2011. For three years, alongside experts, we reviewed data predicting future climate scenarios.
Two things were clear 15 years ago: Alberta would become drier, hotter, and be burning by the mid-2020s due to fossil fuel pollution if humanity continued its reliance on fossil fuels. Humanity possessed all that we needed to transition away from fossil fuels.
Despite the warnings and the hope, the world and Canada ignored the signs, with Alberta and Saskatchewan leading the way in undermining collective progress made by other provinces and territories through their significant provincial carbon footprints.
To be clear, provinces hold the power with regard to their firefighting services and energy production.
The summer of 2023 marked a record-breaking wildfire season in Alberta, which should have been a wake-up call. And what has Alberta Premier Danielle Smith done? In 2023, she paused 118 renewable energy projects worth $33 billion and jobs to keep 24,000 people working for a year. Despite the fact the province had a surplus budget in 2023, she barely reversed the massive cuts the United Conservative Party made to wildfire services in previous years.
She opposes the federal cap on emissions in the oil and gas sector despite the fact Alberta has its own cap on the books.
Sadly, she is a lead cheerleader in the "Axe the Tax" campaign on carbon pricing, too. This is by far the most efficient means of reducing pollution from fossil fuels and is doubly helpful during the food affordability crisis by providing an income supplement while reducing global heating gases.
Last month, Alberta was in the midst of a record-breaking heatwave and had 176 active fires, 54 out of control.
These more intense heat waves and wildfires are not natural - this is climate change endangering our lives and the people and places we love, including the UNESCO World Heritage Site of Jasper National Park, which receives over two million visitors annually. This is the equivalent of: - The Pyramids of Giza cracking and falling apart due to unnatural weather. - Stonehenge tumbling down due to erosion from unnaturally dry, hot weather, climate change-induced torrential downpours, and burrowing badgers that now thrive in warmer conditions. - Venice going underwater. - Ellis Island and Liberty Island in New York City washed away by rising seas and storm surges during hurricanes. - The ancient Cedars of Lebanon, some over 3,000 years old and of biblical significance, perishing if the planet gets too hot.
All these treasures are likely to disappear. We must act now. Later will be too late.
Note that Ontario, under Premier Doug Ford, has joined the ranks of Saskatchewan and Alberta in becoming a climate laggard. Research "Mathur et al vs His Majesty the King in Right of Ontario" for more information.
Jasper was a warning shot from the heart of the beast that has been deceiving us for decades.
Why is this happening? We are in a fossil-fueled crisis caused by decades of lies from the fossil fuel industry.
What to do? Forgive yourself if you have been duped, then get angry.
Demand that all politicians hold the fossil fuel industry accountable for what they have done to our world. If you live in a community that has been devastated by unusual weather patterns, consider suing. A quarter of all Americans live in jurisdictions that are suing big oil over lying to the public.
The eerie orange sun at sunset is a stark reminder of Western Canada's ongoing fires. Let it also be a beacon, urging us to demand accountability and real action against the fossil fuel industry that continues to endanger our world. Cathy Orlando of Sudbury is the program director at Citizens' Climate International.

Subject: Forest & brush fires; Records & achievements; Fossil fuels; Climate change

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada; Western Canada

Document 180 of 281

Northeastern MPP says he will let voters decide his political future; Algoma-Manitoulin MPP pressured staffer for sexual interactions: arbitrator

Author: Kelly, Brian

Publication info: Kelly, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/northeastern-mpp-says-he-will-let-voters-decide/docview/3092491416/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Northeastern+MPP+says+he+will+let+voters+decide+his+political+future%3B+Algoma-Manitoulin+MPP+pressured+staffer+for+sexual+interactions%3A+arbitrator&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kelly%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Algoma-Manitoulin MPP Michael Mantha pressured a female constituency worker for "sexual interactions" and wanted her to send him sexually explicit photos, a probe into his alleged behaviour found.
The veteran provincial politician also required the Elliot Lake-based employee to hug him regularly and tell him of any sexual dreams she had about him, the investigation determined. The woman was sexually harassed by Mantha "on a regular basis."
The woman "raised concerns" about an unsafe work environment in August 2022. She went on a paid leave of absence at the same time.
The Ontario New Democratic Party caucus has a collective agreement with the Canadian Office and Professional Employees Union Local 343. Mantha's constituency office fell within the scope of that pact. Two grievances were filed alleging a failure to keep a workplace free of harassment and being terminated without just cause.
Mantha was removed from the NDP caucus in the spring of 2023 pending the probe's results. A third-party investigation by Mireille Mortimer, of MortimerKhoraych Workplace Investigations, was done from Jan. 31 to July 6, 2023. She was retained by the NDP caucus.
The constituency worker, who is only identified as the grievor in arbitrator Jasbir Parmar's release, Mantha, his lawyer and three witnesses were interviewed. Text messages, online communications and "substantial" video evidence were reviewed.
Mortimer's findings were shared with the party caucus in July 2023. A summary was shared with the worker, Mantha and Local 343 a month later.
Mantha was permanently removed from the NDP caucus in August 2023. He continues to represent his riding as an independent.
"The Mortimer investigation concluded, on a balance of probabilities, that allegations of workplace harassment, discrimination on the basis of sex, abuse of authority and creation of a toxic workplace, had all been substantiated," said Parmar.
Mantha's actions contravened his party's workplace harassment, violence and discrimination policy.
The NDP caucus terminated the constituency worker in January, saying that because Mantha was no longer an NDP MPP, her position did not fall under the collective agreement with the political party.
Other constituency workers in Mantha's office lost their jobs at the same time.
Local 343 grieved the woman's firing, however.
Parmar found the worker did not lose her job for just cause.
"Rather, as with the other constituency assistants in the office, the grievor's employment with the ONDP caucus was frustrated, through no fault of her own, following the removal of MPP Mantha from the ONDP caucus based on the substantiated findings that he had engaged in sexual harassment, workplace harassment, discrimination on the basis of sex, abuse of authority and the creation of a toxic workplace in relation to the grievor during the period of her employment," said Parmar.
How the woman's job concerns and workplace harassment grievance are to be dealt with is up to the NDP caucus and Local 343.
The arbitrator will get involved if the two groups can't agree on what to do. The woman wants her job back "and damages for any losses that may have occurred," said Parmar.
Mantha called the last two years "very challenging.
"But it is time to move past this matter," he said in a statement released on the weekend. "While I disagree with some of the characterizations made, on the advice of my legal counsel I will not be commenting further at this time."
Mantha will "leave the decision on my future in the hands of voters in Algoma-Manitoulin."
Mantha has served the riding since defeating long-time Liberal incumbent Mike Brown in 2011. He was a constituency assistant to NDP MP Carol Hughes for four years. Mantha did not participate in the arbitration hearing. btkelly@postmedia.com On X: @Saultreporter

Dynamic Earth to build $3.27M facility to train future mine, skilled workers; It would process up to 35,000 youth and apprentices over five years, provincial government says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dynamic-earth-build-3-27m-facility-train-future/docview/3091244424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dynamic+Earth+to+build+%243.27M+facility+to+train+future+mine%2C+skilled+workers%3B+It+would+process+up+to+35%2C000+youth+and+apprentices+over+five+years%2C+provincial+government+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Science North will get more than $3.27 million from the provincial government to build a training facility at Dynamic Earth.
The new facility will train up to 35,000 youth and apprentices over five years in mining and the skilled trades.
The money will come from the provincial government's Skills Development Fund and is part of almost $17 million in funding handed out on Friday during an announcement held in Thunder Bay.
"We're giving workers in Northern Ontario the support and investments they need to find good-paying jobs in in-demand careers," Premier Doug Ford said in a release.
"By working closely with our partners in the North, including First Nations, labour unions and the private sector, we can continue to expand the critical sectors like mining, construction, forestry and energy that will power Ontario's growth and prosperity."
The Sudbury-based Northern Centre for Advance Technology, or NORCAT, will get $580,000 to deliver training programs focused on skills and safety knowledge related to careers in forestry, mining, exploration and energy development.
Officials from Science North and NORCAT said they welcomed the funding.
"As Northern Ontario's premier earth sciences and mining attraction, the potential for the Dynamic Earth expansion project to inspire youth and underrepresented groups to consider a career in mining and the skilled trades is significant," said Ashley Larose, Science North's chief executive officer.
"We are grateful for this critical support ... that will allow Science North and Dynamic Earth to engage youth in modern mining technologies, create innovative opportunities for career exploration and enhance capacity in Northern Ontario to build a pipeline of young, diverse talent to meet the workforce needs of the mining industry now and in the future."
Jason Bubba, NORCAT's chief operating officer, said the agency has had success with its Skills Development Series programs in Thunder Bay. "These programs are not just about training, they're about connecting skilled workers with real job opportunities in mining, exploration, construction and forestry," he said.
"With the support from Ontario's Skills Development Fund, we've been able to prioritize training for underrepresented groups, ensuring that a diverse and inclusive workforce can thrive in our northern communities. Our hands-on, practical approach equips participants with the skills employers are looking for, helping to strengthen the local economy and provide sustainable career paths close to home."
Agnico Eagle Mines Limited, meanwhile, will get $10 million to train people for careers in mining, in partnership with Matachewan First Nation, Taykwa Tagamou Nation and Moose Cree First Nation, which are located in northwestern Ontario.
Also receiving money are the Sheet Metal Workers Union (SMART) Local 397 (just over $1 million), Keewaytinook Okimakanak ($1 million) and the International Association of Bridge, Structural, Ornamental & Reinforcing Ironworkers Local 759 ($644,461).
"Our government is working closely with industry to address labour challenges across Ontario," said Mines Minister George Pirie. "This $17 million investment through the Skills Development Fund will train more than 36,000 workers and Indigenous People throughout the North and help connect them to employers in expanding industries like mining, which needs more trained and skilled workers.
"These targeted investments will ensure we can meet industry labour needs while upskilling people and giving them access to great paying careers."
Greg Rickford, the minister of Northern Development and the minister of Indigenous Affairs and First Nations Economic Reconciliation, said the Ford government continues to make "targeted investments" that contribute to long-term economic growth and opportunities for Northern and First Nation communities.
"We understand that meaningful reconciliation requires an all-of-government approach and the partnerships we are announcing today with local employers, First Nations and community organizations in the North demonstrates our commitment now and into the future," he said. Star staff sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 183 of 281

Ontario nurses support national pharmacare program

Author: Casas, Maria

Publication info: Casas, Maria. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-nurses-support-national-pharmacare/docview/3090785256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+nurses+support+national+pharmacare+program&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Casas%2C+Maria&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An open letter to Prime Minister, Justine Trudeau, Prime Minister of federal Health Minister Mark Holland, Sudbury MP Viviane Lapointe, and Premier Doug Ford: The federal government has been discussing a national pharmacare program for many years. RNAO has been advocating for this all along and continues to do so.
We are pleased to note that Bill C-64 was finally passed at second reading in June of this year.
A significant proportion of Canada's population includes immigrants, racialized Canadians, and others who do not have insurance to cover the cost of prescriptions. As a result, many forgo the care they need and go on to develop worsening conditions, often chronic illnesses, that could have been prevented had they received appropriate treatment from the start. This also puts strains on our emergency rooms and hospitals. Diabetes medications, as one example, would be covered in the national program and represent a significant step towards a universal and comprehensive pharmacare program.
To proceed with the implementation of the program, the Bill must receive royal assent, requiring agreement from all provinces and territories. We are calling on the government to collaborate in good faith and for Premier Ford to make it happen for Ontario as soon as possible.
Political action executive network officer, Registered Nurses' Association of

Document 184 of 281

Protecting Trudeau is ruining political careers; Blaming others for PM's failings won't fool voters

Author: Snobelen, John

Publication info: Snobelen, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protecting-trudeau-is-ruining-political-careers/docview/3090785216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protecting+Trudeau+is+ruining+political+careers%3B+Blaming+others+for+PM%27s+failings+won%27t+fool+voters&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Snobelen%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Ontario premier David Peterson told me he quit politics for health reasons. He said the people got sick of him.
Last week, U.S. President Joe Biden quit the 2024 election race, mere weeks before his party's nomination convention. He said he was leaving to protect democracy.
Well, maybe. But there are some other good reasons why Biden belatedly and seemingly begrudgingly left the race.
Running for office isn't a personal decision.
It starts with an individual making the commitment to begin the journey but, early in that process, it becomes by necessity a group effort.
It takes people and money to win a campaign.
Biden was bleeding support when he finally got the message his time was up. He may not have run out of ambition, but he was running out of cash.
Political leaders in Canada face a similar dilemma. They can't win on their own. Canadian elections are cheaper and raising cash isn't as crucial as it is in the United States.
But leaders need the support of people beyond the cadre of camp followers who run elections for a living. They need the party faithful to believe, win or lose, they represent the future of the party.
Justin Trudeau has lost that support. For months, Trudeau has known most Canadians will not vote for him in the next federal election. You don't need to be a political savant to figure that out.
Canadians are simply sick of him. No amount of foreign costume parties or fake cultural divides will solve that.
No doubt the politicos who have tied their futures and their income to the Trudeau brand are still plotting and scheming to find a way to some sort of victory, even if that victory is just keeping Trudeau in power for a little longer. But, it is a vain and selfish effort.
Protecting Trudeau has already ruined the political lives of some of the brightest lights in the Liberal tent. Jody Wilson-Raybould, Jane Philpott and Bill Morneau were once the best and brightest in the Trudeau cabinet. Now they are political roadkill, sacrificed to protect Trudeau.
Now the Trudeau brain trust, desperate to find someone not named Trudeau to blame for a byelection loss and sagging polls, have set their sights on Chrystia Freeland. That's right, the new target for blame is none other than the deputy PM and finance minister.
A couple of weeks ago, leaks from senior officials in the prime minister's office suggested Freeland was to blame for the government's misfortunes. Apparently, she is just not a good communicator.
This is a classic political hatchet job. First, Freeland's abilities or lack thereof aren't some new discoveries. She has been Trudeau's minister of everything for years. Second, the world's best carnival barker couldn't sell Trudeau's economic plan.
Covertly attacking Freeland demonstrates the Trudeau government has turned its fire inward. That's not a winning strategy.
It's also strong evidence Trudeau has lost his party. He is, now and forever, yesterday's man in the Liberal party.
Next week at the Democratic Party convention, people will say nice things about Joe Biden. But, if Kamala Harris loses the election to Donald Trump, Democrats will blame Biden for being the stubborn man who stayed well past his time.
Someday soon, the Liberals may feel the same way about Trudeau. His time in office will be forever marred by his dismal treatment of the best of his cabinet. John Snobelen was a cabinet minister in the Conservative government of Ontario premier Mike Harris from 1995 to 2002.

Subject: Elections; Political parties

Location: United States--US

People: Biden, Joseph R Jr

Identifier / keyword: former; ontario; premier; david; peterson; politics

Ontario Premier requests Wasaga beach visitors not poop in the sand
