Document 215 of 281

Sudburyarea woman teaching the film industry the value of the 3Rs; Andrea Sacchetto says more companies looking to implement environmentally sustainable practices

Author: Stradiotto, Laura

Publication info: Stradiotto, Laura. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburyarea-woman-teaching-film-industry-value/docview/3064443008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudburyarea+woman+teaching+the+film+industry+the+value+of+the+3Rs%3B+Andrea+Sacchetto+says+more+companies+looking+to+implement+environmentally+sustainable+practices&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Stradiotto%2C+Laura&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Living off-grid requires discipline and commitment, two principles that have helped transform a lifestyle into a business for Andrea Sacchetto.
For most of the year, aside from winter when she stays with family in Sudbury, Sacchetto lives off-grid in Field, located in West Nipissing.
She purchased the property in 2016 and built a cabin with someone who shared her vision for creating a simple and meaningful life.
While the partnership later dissolved, Sacchetto remained committed to the off-grid lifestyle.
"The rewards on a personal level are immeasurable," she said. "With my parcel of land and my very own zen zone, I have been able to spend most of my time on my property and still be able to return home to Sudbury to care for my mother during the winter months."
It was that life that influenced her next career path.
Last year, a one-week program offered by Cultural Industries Ontario North (CION) to train for work in the film industry's art department piqued her interest.
"I absolutely loved it," said Sacchetto, who has worked in creative design, independent filmmaking and journalism over her career. "I've been part of the arts community forever ... it was right up my alley."
One course in the program offered by Ontario Green Screen spoke to Sacchetto's passion for living a minimalist and balanced life with nature. Ontario Green Screen is a collaboration between government, industry partners, unions, guilds and trade associations. The aim is to promote environmentally sustainable film production practices; it also provides training to help the industry achieve targets.
"I found that really interesting because I live by that out here anyway," said Sacchetto. "When I'm in Field, I'm off-grid, I don't have running water or electricity. I upcycle many things, build things out of discarded items I find in the landfill."
She has built furniture, and even a shed, from reclaimed wood.
The push to make film and television production more environmentally sustainable is gaining momentum, with the overall goal of reducing the industry's carbon footprint. Whether through diverting waste - both food and materials - from landfills or reducing energy consumption, Saccheto said that Ontario Green Screen is setting the tone in the film industry in the province.
Once she completed the CION program, she landed a job in the art department for a Hallmark movie, transforming downtown Sudbury into a Christmas scene last summer.
"Unless there's an exceptional amount of pre-planning, which is almost next to impossible when you are doing set dressing - that's just the nature of doing creative work- there is a lot of waste happening on the set," she said.
So, in addition to her job on set, Sacchetto found herself following the crew around with a recycling bin, doing what she could to reduce waste and promote recycling.
A few months later, Sacchetto attended CION's Cinema Summit. She was specifically interested in a panel discussion on how to work in the green economy of sustainable filmmaking.
"That one hundred percent set my brain in that direction," she said about the panel discussion. Sacchetto saw an opportunity as there was no one working to help film production companies in Northern Ontario become more sustainable.
"I dove in and started studying sustainability in film production on my own," she said.
Shortly after, Sacchetto worked with a French production company looking for a sustainable production lead in Sudbury.
"One of the big things in regard to film production is proper waste sorting," she said. Sacchetto said it's typical for a film crew of 30 to 40 people to produce about 10 garbage bags of waste a day.
"The attempt to recycle is always there but not enforced," she said. "The concept of composting was non-existent."
Composting through a third party was not available in Sudbury, so Sacchetto found a company in Sturgeon Falls that sold food-grade food barrels and purchased them out of pocket to stand as food composting receptacles.
She asked Greater Sudbury staff to speak to the film crew about proper sorting for recycling and the reasons behind the practice. Then the crew was asked to bring in refillable water bottles instead of relying on cases of pre-filled water bottles to stay hydrated.
Through her efforts, at the end of 24 days of filming, there were 16 bags of garbage, 26 bags of compost and 36 bags of recyclables.
Without her effort, "all of that would have gone into the landfill," she said.
"Generally, most people want to be environmentally and socially conscious. They just don't have all the information they need to do so."
That's when Sacchetto came up with the idea to start providing consulting services and created her business, Happy Planet Sustainable Production Services.
Promoting sustainable film production goes beyond recycling and composting, though. Entire film sets often land in landfills. Sacchetto said she is working to develop a circular production economy where companies are provided storage space for materials so that materials can be repurposed and reused for one film project to the next.
"There are a lot of potential avenues, it's just not the norm in this industry," she said. "However, there are huge changes coming" as more production companies are developing sustainable film production practices to track their carbon footprint.
Sacchetto anticipates those practices to become the norm or mandated. In the meantime, she is refining her business model while providing in-kind services in the community, most recently, supporting Northern Lights Festival Boreal in developing sustainable practices for their annual event.
The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 216 of 281

Sudbury to get a slice of the electric battery production chain; Wyloo Canada announces plans to build a battery materials processing facility in the city

Author: Carmichael, Harold

Publication info: Carmichael, Harold. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-get-slice-electric-battery-production/docview/3062370331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+to+get+a+slice+of+the+electric+battery+production+chain%3B+Wyloo+Canada+announces+plans+to+build+a+battery+materials+processing+facility+in+the+city&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Carmichael%2C+Harold&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wyloo Canada plans to build a downstream battery materials processing facility in Sudbury to process nickel ore from its Eagle's Nest nickel mine in the Ring of Fire area of northwestern Ontario.
Kristan Straub, Wyloo Canada's chief executive officer, in an interview following a press conference at Cambrian College's eDome on Wednesday, said it will cost as much as $900 million to build the facility. It would open in 2030 in conjunction with the start-up of the Eagle's Nest nickel mine.
"We are working toward our Eagle's Nest project's start of 2030, the proposed plant being ready to operate and accept materials for the electric battery process," he said. "It's a processing plant. With the smelting facilities you have here, you get matte (that needs to be further smelted elsewhere)." Straub on Wednesday also announced a memorandum of understanding with the City of Greater Sudbury and two First Nation communities to buy land and build the facility somewhere in the city.
When up and running, the Wyloo processing facility will be the first mine-to-precursor cathode active material (pCAM) integrated solution -low-carbon nickel sulphate and nickel dominant pCAM, which are key ingredients for electric vehicle batteries.
That's a technical way of saying the plant here would take nickel from the Ring of Fire and process it so it can be used to make electric batteries for vehicles.
"It's a hydro-metallurgical process," said Straub. "The nickel is removed from the materials (ore) and put into solution ... The aim of the facility is to provide nickel for the battery vehicle sector in the supply chain."
Straub, who grew up in Sudbury, added that cobalt, which is also part of electric battery production, will also be processed at the facility.
Asked how many jobs would be involved at the processing facility, Straub replied several hundred.
As for a location for the facility, one has not yet been selected.
"Our intention is to pair development of this (processing facility) with development of the Eagle's Nest development," said the chief executive officer.
Earlier, Straub told the more than 100 people on hand for the announcement that the processing facility will provide the missing piece in Ontario and Canada's plans to develop a national electric vehicle battery supply chain.
He noted that 600 models of electric vehicles are now being produced around the world and about 250,000 vehicles are being produced annually.
He said the Eagle's Nest development will provide the bulk of the nickel for processing at the Sudbury facility, with the balance coming from other nickel producers and recycled battery materials.
"With Eagle's Nest as our anchor, combined with third-party feed from other North American sources, we are building enough capacity to meet 50 per cent of the nickel demand from the ($40 billion in) announced electric vehicle investments (in Canada)," he said.
Wyloo Canada is a subsidiary of Wyloo, an Australian mining company.
"This is a great day," said Mayor Paul Lefebvre, who served as emcee for the announcement. "I'm very excited and look forward to working with all of you in the future."
Gimaa (Chief) Craig Nootchtai of the Atikameksheng First Nation said it was good that his First Nation has been involved with the proposed processing facility.
"We all want to see growth, substantial growth, and the economic opportunities this will bring," he said. "But, we also want to take care of the land, that this is sustainable, and we protect it for future generations ...
"It is crucial we support this so we can help grow this undertaking and be a part of it for generations to come ... We support growth as long as we are part of the active process from start to finish."
Wahnapitae First Nation Chief Larry Roque asked why southern Ontario should get so many economic opportunities with the electric vehicle industry in Canada when "the minerals are from the North. We should get jobs in the North. We always are looking for opportunities. We don't want to sit by and watch things. We want to be involved."
"We know that critical minerals are the key to the future and we know this project will unlock that and fuel the supply chain," said Sudbury Liberal MP Viviane Lapointe. "It is evident Sudbury is poised to play a major role (in the electric vehicle industry)." "This is an opportunity that has been long in the making," added Nickel Belt Liberal MP Marc Serre. "We have to take this opportunity to provide the generational opportunities to provide the supply chain."
Serre said that "what's good for Greater Sudbury is good for northeastern Ontario and Canada." He said Northern Ontario has had to compete with southern Ontario to land the processing facility that will fill in the gap with the supply chain in the electric vehicle industry.
"it's about time we sat down and worked together to make this happen," he said. Wyloo Canada Chief Executive Officer Kristan Straub announced that a downstream battery materials processing facility will be built in Greater Sudbury and operational starting in 2030. Harold CarmichaelSudbury Star/Postmedia Network Ontario's minister of Mines, George Pirie, called the announcement "a tremendous opportunity. It took probably years of work to get this far."
The minister said it was only right that Sudbury, a community with so much mining industry expertise and experience, in addition to being home to Vale and Glencore mining and smelting operations, landed the processing facility.
"Sudbury is a hub for critical minerals," he said, "It's appropriate this is happening in Sudbury."
Pirie also said it was good that a memorandum of understanding that included Sudbury's two Indigenous communities -Wahnapitae First Nation and Atikameksheng First Nation -was part of the announcement. He said a decade ago, that might not have been the case.
The minister also said the processing facility will produce nickel for electric vehicle batteries in a clean fashion, as opposed to nickel coming from China via Indonesian mines. There, coal powers the mining operations, with tailings dumped into the ocean.
"Here, it will be produced in a clean facility with participation with the First Nations and all partners are totally committed to ensuring it's produced sustainably," he said. "We can fill the gap in the supply chain right here where the value will be added and where it should be happening." hcarmichael@postmedia.com X: @HaroldCarmichae

LILLEY: No, Doug Ford isn't about to call an early election

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-no-doug-ford-isnt-about-call-early/docview/3061489022/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+No%2C+Doug+Ford+isn%27t+about+to+call+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Sudbury+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We aren't headed towards an early election in Ontario any time soon, but Premier Doug Ford is having fun keeping people guessing. Last week, Ford was asked at a news conference about early election speculation, and he wouldn't rule out an early vote.

Document 218 of 281

Ford delivers on beer and wine promise, but with a catch

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-delivers-on-beer-wine-promise-with-catch/docview/3060981802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+delivers+on+beer+and+wine+promise%2C+but+with+a+catch&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You'll be able to get beer, wine and cider in corner stores by the end of the summer, Premier Doug Ford announced Friday.
The announcement moves up the changes by a significant margin with the province having previously only said the move to expanded sales would happen before Jan. 1, 2026.
The cost of that move, though, is raising questions, but proponents say the benefits are clear - more jobs and more tax revenue for the government.
Starting Aug. 1, grocery stores that already sell booze will be able to expand their offerings including ready to drink cocktails like a Caesar in a can and larger cases of beer. After Sept. 5, convenience stores will be able to do the same while new grocery stores and big box stores like Costco can come online as of Oct. 31.
One thing raising questions is a pledge to pay The Beer Store up to $225 million over the next 19 months for what are described as "added costs."
Those added costs will include ramping up distribution and recycling facilities to deal with all the new stores. A new agreement between the province and TBS states that "to facilitate the use of TBS and its efficient recycling and distribution systems for the benefit of Ontario on terms acceptable to the parties."
The agreement also specifically states that TBS must keep 386 retail stores open until at least Jan. 1, 2026.
In part, this $225 million is for services TBS will be providing to the province, like recycling. It will also in part be about subsidizing unprofitable stores and preserving jobs for the next 19 months.
Some of these costs were going to be incurred no matter when the expansion of booze sales happened. The province already pays TBS to run the recycling program for them, so those costs would have to be paid, but clearly some of this is paying off The Beer Store for ending their contract early.
Supporters of the plan to expand sales say the financial impact will be significant, including increased taxes for government.
A study on the economic impact of these changes conducted for the Convenience Industry Council of Canada predicts between 7,000 to 7,500 jobs created. Most of those would be in retail but they also project 500 to 800 in what are described as "middle income jobs."
The study also projects $165 million spent on installing coolers and other infrastructure. As for taxes, the study estimates more than $200 million in annual tax payments being generated with 69 per cent going to the provincial government.
"Our plan will create new growth opportunities for local brewers, wineries, retailers and small businesses. It's going to support local jobs and most importantly, it's going to give people more choice and convenience," Ford said.
The move to expand alcohol sales was fought for fiercely by the convenience store industry and by craft brewers who see this as a real opportunity for expansion. By contrast, the changes have been fought for years by The Beer Store and its owners Labatt, Molson and Sleeman.
Right now, Ontario has the fewest retail alcohol stores per capita, excluding bars and restaurants, in all of Canada. There are just 2.4 stores per 10,000 population in Ontario compared to 21.1 in Newfoundland and Labrador and 12.2 in Quebec.
After these changes, the province expects there to be 9.4 retail stores per 10,000 population.
Ontario is finally catching up to other provinces and many states south of the border. It's a change many politicians have promised but never delivered.
Ford now can claim credit for doing what others couldn't. Whether the public will toast him or have a sour taste in their mouths over the $225-million payment is an open question. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores; Costs; Grocery stores

Document 219 of 281

Grant allows Sudbury Shared Harvest to reach more people; It receives a $67,000 grant from the Ontario Trillium Foundation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grant-allows-sudbury-shared-harvest-reach-more/docview/3060981793/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grant+allows+Sudbury+Shared+Harvest+to+reach+more+people%3B+It+receives+a+%2467%2C000+grant+from+the+Ontario+Trillium+Foundation&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Armed with a $67,000 grant, Sudbury Shared Harvest was able to reach more young people and teach them about urban agriculture.
"This is a fantastic boost towards both increasing volunteer engagement and expanding experiential education programming for youth," Jessica Blaauw, chair of Sudbury Shared Harvest, said in a release. "The funding allows us to more than double the number of people impacted this year."
Sudbury Shared Harvest coordinates volunteer involvement to increase local sustainable food production and knowledge of what can be grown in Sudbury's climate, how to use what grows well here and the benefits of using ecological production methods.
Funds from the grant were used to engage personnel and expertise to help it continue providing strong support for its volunteers, while increasing its reach and the effectiveness of its programs in the community.
To learn more about the group's work, see www.sudburysharedharvest.ca.
Sudbury MPP Jamie West this week met with the team at Sudbury Shared Harvest to hear more about the $67,000 Resilient Communities Fund grant it received in 2023 from the provincial government's Ontario Trillium Foundation.
"The Sudbury Shared Harvest had to pivot how they provided their programs during the pandemic; this grant allowed them to expand on their mission by engaging even more students virtually," said West, an NDP MPP.
"Today, I got to visit one of their food forests and see first-hand how the knowledge of food helps enhance our overall community health."
Neil Lumsden, the minister of Tourism, Culture and Sport, also praised the work of Sudbury Shared Harvest.
"Non-profit organizations across Ontario deliver programming that makes a difference," Lumsden said. "That's why funding that my ministry is providing through the (Trillium Foundation) is so important. Our government wants to ensure that these programs and spaces remain the heart of communities across our province."
The Ontario Trillium Foundation is an agency of the Ontario government with a mission to build healthy and vibrant communities across the province. Last year, OTF invested more than $110 million into 1,044 community projects and multi-sector partnerships.
Those projects aim to enhance economic well-being, foster more active lifestyles, support child and youth development, provide spaces for people to come together and connect, and create a more sustainable environment.
Visit otf.ca to learn more.
sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 220 of 281

Today in history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/today-history/docview/3060981533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today+in+history&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1808, Canadian explorer Simon Fraser began a trip down the British Columbia river that would bear his name. In 1898, the Shroud of Turin was photographed for the first time. Many believe the shroud shows the negative image of the crucified Jesus Christ. In 1927, the Canadian Old Age Pension Plan was approved by the House of Commons. It was to be paid to those in need over the age of 70, in co-operation with participating provinces.
In 1929, the first all-colour talking picture, "On with the Show," opened in New York. In 1934, the Dionne quintuplets were born in Callander, Ont. Annette, Emilie, Yvonne, Cecile and Marie were the first quints to survive more than a few days. The Ontario government placed them in a specially-built hospital, where the children were put on public display. More than three million people came to watch them play behind a one-way screen. Their mother fought for nine years to regain custody, but the family reunion in 1943 was not successful.
In 1998, the three surviving sisters and their families received $4 million compensation from the Ontario government for their childhood mistreatment. In 1937, Neville Chamberlain became prime minister of Britain.
In 1954, U.S. president Dwight D. Eisenhower signed a bill which added the words "under God" to the Pledge of Allegiance. In 1958, the Presbyterian Church in the U.S. merged with the Presbyterian Church of North America to form the United Presbyterian Church in the United States of America. In 1959, American space officials recovered two monkeys who splashed down after a 2,700-km flight in the nose-cone of a Jupiter rocket. In 1967, Englishman Francis Chichester completed the first solo, around-the-world boat voyage when he returned to Portsmouth, England. In 1972, the Duke of Windsor - who had abdicated the British throne in 1936 to marry the woman he loved, American divorcee Wallis Simpson - died of cancer in Paris at age 77. In 1977, more than 160 people died when fire raced through the Beverly Hills Supper Club in Southgate, Ky. In 1981, Poland's Stefan Cardinal Wyszynski died.
A member of the Polish resistance in the Second World War, he later cautiously supported such movements as the Workers'Defence Committee, Solidarity and Rural Solidarity, which sought greater freedom from the late 1970's until the collapse of Communist rule. In 1985, the Ontario NDP and Liberals signed a pact that ended the Conservatives'42-year reign and led to the resignation of Frank Miller as Tory leader. He was premier for only a few months. In 1987, Mathias Rust, a 19-yearold West German pilot, stunned the world as he landed a private plane in Moscow's Red Square after evading Soviet air defences. In 1988, the Canadian aerosol industry announced it would ban CFCs (chloro-fluorocarbons) from spray cans. The man-made chemicals were the main cause of damage to the ozone layer that protects Earth from excessive solar radiation.
The aerosol industry planned to ban CFCs for almost everything except some medicinal sprays, where the chemicals were deemed to be essential. In 1989, Global TV reporter Doug Small and four other people were charged with possession of stolen goods, and two were charged with theft, in connection with the leak of the federal budget. In 1995, Jacques Villeneuve became the first Canadian winner of the Indianapolis 500 auto race. In 1998, Canadian-born actor Phil Hartman was found shot to death in his California home in a murder-suicide.
His wife Brynn shot and killed herself as police were removing the couple's two children from the home after reports of gunfire. Hartman, who was 49, was a regular on TV's "News Radio" and "Saturday Night Live." He also appeared in more than 20 movies. In 2000, the remains of an unknown Canadian soldier were interred at the National War Memorial in Ottawa after being returned from Vimy Ridge, France. In 2003, goaltender Patrick Roy officially announced his retirement after 18 NHL seasons, four Stanley Cup championships and three Conn Smythe trophies as the most valuable player in the playoffs.
In 2005, Justin Trudeau married Sophie Gregoire in Montreal. In 2007, the Liberals won a stunning victory in the P.E.I. election, ending 11 years of Tory rule. Robert Ghiz - the son of former premier Joe Ghiz - and the Liberals took 23 seats, while the Tories won just four. In 2008, the newly elected Constituent Assembly of Nepal declared the country a republic and abolished the 239-year-old monarchy. In 2010, Prime Minister Stephen Harper bestowed honorary Canadian citizenship upon the Aga Khan, a billionaire philanthropist and spiritual leader of 20 million Muslims worldwide. In 2010, the iPad was finally available at Canadian stores. The tablet computer was first released in the U.S. about eight weeks earlier and quickly sold more than a million units. In 2011, Egypt lifted a four-yearold blockade of the Gaza Strip, greatly easing travel restrictions on the 1.5 million residents of the Palestinian territory in a move that bolstered the Hamas government while dealing a setback to Israel's attempts to isolate the militant group. In 2014, author and poet Maya Angelou, who rose from poverty, segregation and violence to become a force on stage, screen and the printed page, died at age 86. In 2018, the Stratford Festival's opening night of its 2018 season was cancelled and all its buildings evacuated after a bomb threat, which turned out to be a hoax. In 2018, Simplii Financial and the Bank of Montreal reveal cyber attackers had broken through security and were threatening to release the account information of 90-thousand Canadians. In 2019, Canadian country songwriter Ralph Murphy, who penned lyrics for Shania Twain and Randy Travis while earning a reputation as a Nashville mentor, died at age 75. The England-born musician, who also produced for Canadian rock act April Wine, had been hospitalized after suffering numerous health issues. He died with his two children and wife by his side. Murphy was inducted into the Canadian Country Music Hall of Fame in 2012. In 2020, the mayor of Minneapolis appealed for calm after a second night of violence left one person dead. People protesting the death of George Floyd set fires and looted businesses. In 2021, the chief of a First Nation in B.C. said the finding of the remains of 215 children on the site of a former residential school in Kamloops was "an unthinkable loss that was spoken about but never documented." Chief Rosanne Casimir of the Tk'emlups te Secwepemc First Nation said the remains were confirmed with the help of a ground-penetrating radar specialist.
Radar expert Dr. Sarah Beaulieu said "disruptions in the ground" suggested the presence of unmarked graves. (Beaulieu later revised the number to 200, adding they should be considered "probable burials" until excavation confirms they are human remains.) In 2021, Liberal MP William Amos said he was deeply embarrassed after being caught unawares on camera once again. Amos apologized for urinating during a virtual session of the House of Commons, saying he didn't realize he was visible to fellow parliamentarians. In 2023, a rapidly spreading wildfire, fed by strong winds and tinder-dry woods, destroyed homes and forced the evacuation of several suburban communities northwest of Halifax.

Jazz Sudbury Youth Band performs June 15; State of the arts

Author: Straughan, Judi

Publication info: Straughan, Judi. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jazz-sudbury-youth-band-performs-june-15-state/docview/3059204929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jazz+Sudbury+Youth+Band+performs+June+15%3B+State+of+the+arts&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Straughan%2C+Judi&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sudbury Arts Council congratulates the Jazz Sudbury Youth Band, a group of more than 20 talented young musicians, on achieving a gold standard at MusicFest Nationals this past weekend.
Entering its 52nd celebration year, MusicFest Canada's The Nationals brings thousands of students and educators from across the country together over one week to celebrate excellence in musical performance.
The band, comprised of passionate talented youth from Sudbury and Espanola, travelled to Toronto to perform and receive its adjudication as part of a roster of musical groups from across the country, The Jazz Sudbury Youth Band performs a public concert on June 15 at 7:00 pm at Sudbury Secondary School. The program also features the seasoned Sudbury Jazz Orchestra.
Entrance is by donation. -The Music Continues: The Sudbury Youth Orchestra concert, directed by Jamie Arrowsmith, presents Expressive Landscapes, art-inspired music performances, May 26, 2:30 pm at St. Peter's Church, 203 York St. Admission is by donation.
Sudbury Symphony Orchestra presents a concert of Beginnings and Endings in C on June 1, 7:30 p.m. at Place des Arts, 27 Larch St. Featured guests are Duo46.
Jazz Sudbury Wednesday Jazz features Jacob Starling on May 29 at the Place des Arts Bistro from 6-8 p.m. Stay informed by visiting the Jazz Sudbury website.
On Thursdays, Dorland Music presents jazz from 7-10 p.m. at the Night Owl Speakeasy. Artie Roth is the featured guest on May 23, with Steve Koven on May 30. Check out the Night Owl Speakeasy website for complete information.
-May 24-25, La Slague: Every year, La Slague discovers rising stars at a unique event. La Slague describes it as "an outdoor music festival inside one building," Place des Arts. The event has concerts taking place throughout the building.
Friday, May 24, is hosted by Anique Granger. An intimate concert brings together Yves Doyon, Michel Laforge and Tricia Foster.
Saturday, May 25, the party continues with Mehdi Cayenne, Bermuda, Prairie Comeau, and The Phonophile. Tickets are available at laslague.ca Concurrently at Place des Arts, the Galerie du Nouvel Ontario will present three video installations -Origines (2021), Émergence (2017) and Whitewash (2016) -by Nadine Valcin, famous Canadian director, producer and writer of Haitian origin.
The Panache Bookstore will also be open during La Slague presentations.
-May 24, Friday Night Make-Believe: It's happening once again, hilarious live improv theatre at the Indie Cinema on Mackenzie Street. This month, oddhawks Performance Company takes the audience's suggestions and mines for comedy gold -because it's Sudbury and they are contractually obliged to mention mining.
The talented cast has performed around the world -from St. John's to Victoria, from the North West Territories to Florida, from New York to L.A., from Canada to China to Kuwait! When they ask for a location -chances are, they've been there.
Tickets are $15 for general admission, and $10 for students and seniors.
-May 28, Wordstock Trivia Night: Wordstock Sudbury's second annual trivia night fundraiser on May 28 is the perfect opportunity for a fun night out while showing off your book smarts.
Brendan Roy from Nickel City Trivia tests the teams'book and author knowledge covering various genres and themes, both classic and contemporary. Prizes, books and goodies await as well as a silent auction in support Wordstock Sudbury Literary Festival.
To reserve a table for your team, call the Townehouse Tavern, 705-674-6883.
-Afternoons with Artists: An art mentorship program is offered on Thursday afternoons by artists Jennifer Johnson and Carmen Martorella.
Each session is 1-4 p.m., costs $25 and is held at the Carmichael Community Centre, 1388 Bellevue Ave. For complete information, contact Carmen at Carmenartist13@gmail.com.
-May 29, Cinefest presents Kanaval: Cinéfest Sudbury's Red Carpet Patron Program presents the award-winning film "Kanaval" at 7 p.m. on May 29 at SilverCity Cinemas.
In the early 1970s, after leaving their troubled hometown in Haiti, a young boy and his mother settle in a small rural village in Quebec where the boy counts on his invisible friend to understand the new world around him.
Nominated for three 2024 Canadian Screen Awards, Kanaval is presented in French and Haitian with English subtitles. Tickets are on sale at cinefest.com.
-May 29, Come on Over: Dieter Buse invites everyone to the launch of his latest book, "Come on Over", 6:30 p.m. at Living with Lakes, 840 Ramsey Lake Rd., where he will be in conversation with CBC Radio's Markus Schwabe.
Dieter describes his book as "A unique, single-volume historical and cultural compendium" filled with fascinating stories. The reader will discover information about the statues of significant explorers, about where to swim with a polar bear, about where the first Hardy Boys books were written, and about Winnie the Pooh, Grey Owl and Shania Twain. It promises to be a captivating read.
Books are available for purchase at the event. Snacks and beverages are available.
-June 1, This is My Story (I Swear it's True): Some of the names of the storytellers have just been released for this evening of personal true stories, a live-story telling event in support of CKLU 96.7 community radio.
Kevin Closs, Jenny Lamothe, Bill Sanders, Anna Barsanti, Greg Tremblay and Alex Tétreault are some of the storytellers who pull back the curtain on an incident in their lives.
Years ago, what exactly happened to singer Kevin Closs who found himself stranded on a remote Northern Ontario road in the dead of winter? Bill Sanders says his five years on Code Lake could be called Green Acres" or maybe not.
There are times when a phone call is just a phone call. There are other times that one call changes your life forever. Anna Barsanti received that phone call sixteen years ago.
What could possibly go wrong when you meet your idol? Ask Greg Tremblay. Things don't always go exactly as expected.
Peter Williams hosts CKLU's night of live storytelling on June 1, 7 p.m. at Indie Cinema on Mackenzie Street. Tickets are $25 and available at cklu.ca and at Jett Landry Music on Lasalle Boulevard.
(cash only).
-June 12, Here Comes the Sun: It's time to get outside and enjoy a performance at Sudbury's outdoor theatre The Refettorio on Durham Street in downtown Sudbury.
It's the music of The Beatles, presented by YES Theatre.
Join musicians and singers in a night of nostalgia, sing-alongs and a celebration of the timeless music that continues to captivate and inspire generations.
Don't worry about rain. Ticket holders will be notified of cancellations via email and will be automatically rebooked free of charge.
Those who cannot attend the date chosen by YES Theatre can exchange tickets for an alternate date/time pending availability.
If no tickets remain or a final performance has been cancelled, a refund will be provided.
Go to showpass.com for tickets.
State of the Arts is a bi-monthly article by the Sudbury Arts Council. To become a board member, inquire at sac.communicate@gmail.com

Document 223 of 281

Again, Canada is spiralling into disorder

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/again-canada-is-spiralling-into-disorder/docview/3055829180/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Again%2C+Canada+is+spiralling+into+disorder&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four people killed, including a baby and his grandparents. A horrific head-on collision on Canada's busiest highway that could have been even more deadly, according to eyewitnesses. All caused by a driver fleeing police after allegedly holding up a liquor store.
The driver, international student Gagandeep Singh of Milton, was out on bail for multiple charges of theft and robbery.
According to police, he was also under investigation for carjacking and subject to a driving ban.
The case sparked outrage not just because it took the lives of three innocent people, but because it feels like this was a tragedy waiting to happen.
A week prior, Nirmal Singh (no relation), of Brampton, was arrested for ignoring his fourth lifetime driving ban. Since 2017, Singh has been convicted of 35 Criminal Code and eight Ontario Provincial Offences Act charges, including impaired driving, dangerous driving, flight from police, theft of a motor vehicle, assault, criminal harassment, and obstructing a peace officer.
In November 2023, another man, Rafael Maia, of Mississauga, received a two-year sentence after ignoring nine lifetime driving bans, and committing 10 additional offences. He had been (finally) held in custody for 19 months leading up to his trial, where he was found guilty.
In the House of Commons last week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre accused the government of favouring criminals and causing tragedies with their "catch and release" bail policies.
Asked about this, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland accused the Conservatives of scoring "cheap political points" off a family's grief, and then cited recent reforms the Liberals made to the bail system, reforms that didn't prevent Singh from getting his freedom despite the string of charges against him.
The reality is this: The family of baby Aditya Vivaan Golkunath wouldn't be grieving if Singh hadn't been out on bail.
And for years, bail has become easier to get thanks to Liberal policies, specifically Bill C-75, which in 2019 legislated a "principle of restraint" for police and courts to ensure that "release at the earliest opportunity is favoured over detention."
The "unintended consequence of the reform" according to former B.C. Attorney General Murray Rankin, was that more repeat offenders, some accused of random violence, ended up on the streets, and committed more crimes. In December 2022, a man out on bail, with a warrant out for his arrest and a lifetime firearms prohibition, murdered OPP officer Grzegorz Pierzchala. That spurred provincial and territorial leaders to write the federal government asking they reverse the onus for bail in cases where a firearm is used.
The Liberals responded with Bill C-48, which this January brought in a reverse onus for violent crimes involving weapons, including firearms, knives and bear spray. The Liberals also enacted a requirement that courts consider if the accused's criminal record includes any past convictions for violent offending. But judges still have a lot of discretion when it comes to granting bail, and other laws have been undone by the courts on the basis they violated the charter rights of the accused.
In response, Poilievre has mused about using the notwithstanding clause to enact criminal justice reforms that would be immune to court challenges - leading the Liberals to accuse him of wanting to curb all manner of other freedoms in the process.
It's useful to remember why the Tories brought in tough-on-crime laws in the first place. They did so after another family lived a terrible trauma in 2005. Jane Creba, a 15-yearold high school student, was killed in a brazen gang shooting while out doing her Christmas shopping. Creba's killing capped a year of increasing violence, and voters said enough.
In 2005, there was a sense that Canada was spiralling into disorder.
Today, it feels the same. There is no consequence for holding up a liquor store, for failing to obey a driving ban, for spewing hate. There is no incentive to obey the rules. When anything goes, everything goes. People just do whatever the hell they want.
And a family is left grieving a loss they can never repair. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Bans; Liquor stores; Robbery; Murders & murder attempts; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Liquor stores

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: people; killed; including; grandparents; horrific; collision

Document 224 of 281

Today in history

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/today-history/docview/3054708140/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today+in+history&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In 1904, the first Olympic Games to be held in North America opened in St. Louis. In 1907, Anna Jarvis arranged for a special church service in Philadelphia to honour mothers, an idea that caught on and became Mother's Day. In 1940, future Tory leader and prime minister John Diefenbaker first took his seat in the Canadian House of Commons. In 1940, the Netherlands surrendered to invading German forces during the Second World War. In 1948, British rule in Palestine ended and the independent state of Israel was declared. It was led by David Ben-Gurion.
At the end of the Second World War, an independent state was suggested for the combined 1.5 million Palestinian Jews, European Jews and Jews in Arab countries. The United Nations proposed in 1947 to divide Palestine into Jewish and Arab states, with a small international zone to include Jerusalem. The Arabs rejected partition and attacked Israel as soon as it came into being. The ensuing war saw Israel greatly expand its boundaries from what the UN proposed. In 1955, representatives from eight Communist bloc countries, including the Soviet Union, signed the Warsaw Pact in the Polish capital.
In 1956, the chaotic and contentious pipeline debate began in the House of Commons. The Liberal government caused an uproar by limiting each stage of debate on a bill loaning Trans-Canada Pipe Lines $80 million to build a natural gas pipeline from Alberta to Ontario. The controversy helped cause the Liberals'defeat in the 1957 federal election. In 1973, the United States launched "Skylab I," its first manned space station. In 1981, a Vatican spokesman reported that Pope John Paul was strong enough to recite prayers and impart blessings to his doctors and nurses.
The pontiff had been shot the previous day. In 1984, Jeanne Sauve was sworn in as Canada's first female governor general. In 1986, a blizzard with 80 km/h winds and knee-deep snow surprised south-central Alberta. In 1987, actress Rita Hayworth died at the age of 68. In 1992, former Soviet president Mikhail Gorbachev addressed the U.S. Congress, urging it to approve aid to the people of the former Soviet Union. In 2001, Canadian author Alistair Macleod's novel, "No Great Mischief," won the world's richest literary prize for a single work of fiction. The IMPAC Dublin award was worth $172,000.

Subject: World War II; Jewish people; Papal visits

Location: United States--US; Canada; Israel; Union of Soviet Socialist Republics--USSR

Document 225 of 281

Trudeau, Singh have led parties to 50-year-low poll numbers: study

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-singh-have-led-parties-50-year-low-poll/docview/3053714005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%2C+Singh+have+led+parties+to+50-year-low+poll+numbers%3A+study&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new data analysis from the Angus Reid Institute has combed through 50 years of political polls and uncovered some historical trends and oddities. Most recently, it found that the popularity of Canadian federal party leaders is at a five-decade low. And that's not one of them - it's all of them. UNPOPULAR LEADERS Data gleaned from ODESI - the Ontario Data Documentation, Extraction Service and Infrastructure Initiative - found that, going back to 1974, there has never been a time when the leaders of the three main federal parties have polled as low as this year.
As of April 2024, the pollster determined, the most popular federal party leader, Pierre Poilievre of the opposition Conservatives, has a net rating of -12 points, calculated by combining favourability with unfavourabililty in polling numbers.
And he's the most popular. New Democratic Party Leader Jagmeet Singh has a net rating of -14, which is his worst in seven years as party leader. And Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of the governing Liberals has a net approval rating that is downright chilly at -38. TRUDEAU TRENDING DOWN The prime minister's numbers become even more dire when one looks at the trending direction. In data from this decade, Liberal and NDP leaders start out with net positive numbers of around 10 in 2020, while the Conservative leader (then Erin O'Toole) has a rating of -43.
However, by the following year, Trudeau had dropped to -9 while Singh was at just 6, and O'Toole had risen to -28.
The next three years saw the Conservative numbers increase in each poll (though never crossing into positive territory) while Trudeau and Singh continued to sink ever lower.
NEW DEMOCRAT S REACH ALL-TIME LOW Historically, the New Democrats have almost never strayed into negative territory, and remain relatively popular in opinion polls, even though they have never won a federal election.
"In 2011 Jack Layton (NDP), Michael Ignatieff (Liberal) and Stephen Harper (Conservative) were all in negative territory, but the intensity of dislike towards Layton was relatively slight," the Angus Reid Institute notes in its analysis. "In the late 1980s both John Turner (Liberal) and Brian Mulroney (Conservative) were heavily disapproved of, but (NDP) Ed Broadbent soared in public opinion polls."
More recently, however, numbers for the NDP leader (Singh since 2017) have been heading down and staying down. After positive figures in 2020 and 2021, the NDP fell to -1 the following year, and then an even zero. In the latest numbers, Singh has the distinction of holding the worst rating ever, at -14.
Why so low? "It appears that the NDP's supply and confidence agreement with the deeply unpopular Liberal government has increased Singh's profile and influence enough that he, too, is now garnering considerable criticism," the Institute suggests. CHRETIEN LEADS THE PACK At the other end of the popularity ladder is Liberal Jean Chretien, who in 1994, early in his 10 years as prime minister, scored a record-setting 42 on the scale.
In fact, throughout his time as prime minister, he consistently pulled in positive numbers, dipping as low as 14 and as high as 34. Liberal numbers wouldn't fall below zero again until 2003, the same year Paul Martin took over as prime minister and party leader.
TALE OF TWO TRUDEAUS In 1974, when data collection began, Liberal leader Pierre Trudeau had already been prime minister for six years, and his first number was a respectable 14, though still lower than the NDP's David Lewis (17) and the Conservatives'Robert Stanfield (18). But the bloom was clearly off the rose; in subsequent years his popularity would plummet.
In 1976 he scored a dismal -25 during a period of high inflation, before struggling back to numbers around zero through the rest of the decade, and then a drop in 1981 from which he never recovered. That year saw Trudeau post a -19, which sank to -34 and then -33. In 1985 the Liberal numbers rebounded to -3, but they were no longer Trudeau's Liberals, as John Turner had taken the helm.
A similar fate seems to be befalling Trudeau's son, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. In 2014, the first year after he took the party reins, he scored a rating of 7. Two years later, as prime minister, his score was 29, and it stayed positive for each of the next two years.
Since 2021 it has been mired in negative numbers. And just as Singh has brought the New Democrats to its lowest rating since 1974, Trudeau's current -38 is a low-water mark over the same stretch of time.
In its analysis, the Angus Reid Institute notes: "Prime Minister Trudeau scores his lowest rating in his run as Liberal leader in April, with just 28 per cent approval. What these historic data show is usually the decline of one federal leader engendering the rise in favourability for an alternative; see the elder Trudeau's fall and the corresponding rise of Brian Mulroney. Our fractured and divisive politics, however, appear to have created an era where all leaders can remain under water at the same time."

Subject: Conservatism; Popularity; Prime ministers

People: Mulroney, Brian; Trudeau, Justin

Document 226 of 281

Opposition leaders outline priorities at Sudbury event; Crombie and Stiles share their visions for Ontario at FONOM conference

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-leaders-outline-priorities-at-sudbury/docview/3053001022/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+leaders+outline+priorities+at+Sudbury+event%3B+Crombie+and+Stiles+share+their+visions+for+Ontario+at+FONOM+conference&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The next provincial election does not take place until June 2026, but already political leaders are making their pitches.
Bonnie Crombie, leader of the Ontario Liberal party, and Marit Stiles, leader of the provincial New Democrats, stopped by the annual conference of the Federation of Northern Ontario Municipalities on Tuesday to share their visions for Ontario.
Stiles said she wants to include northern leaders in decision-making.
"The sad truth is that elected leaders in Toronto and at Queens Park are disconnected from the North," Stiles commented. "I've personally never felt like Queens Park is further away from northern Ontario than it is right now. They have a habit of turning the other cheek to issues that are unique to this region of northeastern Ontario. They show interest from time to time, but not when push comes to shove."
Stiles said under her leadership, "I'm going to bring the North into the fold at every single conversation."
She said there is a need to maximize every social and economic opportunity, while creating good-paying jobs.
This year's FONOM conference was hosted at the Holiday Inn on Regent Street. More than 250 delegates, including many elected officials, from northern Ontario gathered to listen to speakers; meet with provincial ministers; and share ideas.
To a round of applause, Stiles said one of her priorities is the completion of four-laning along Highway 69.
"We need to make sure our roads and our streets and our highways are free from danger," she shared. "Parents should feel safe driving their young ones to school, or waving them off if they get on a school bus. Seniors deserve to feel safe behind the wheel, as do new drivers, and truckers too. It shouldn't be too much to ask."
Stiles said the NDP would also ensure snow removal in Northern Ontario mirrors that in the south. She said unfortunately, the Conservatives voted against an NDP motion that would have enacted equitable snow removal on highways.
"I don't really think that people's safety should be a partisan issue," she offered. "These are simple solutions and they shouldn't take decades to get them."
Stiles pointed to the tent encampments scattered throughout Greater Sudbury and many other communities as evidence of the provincial failure to provide adequate affordable housing. She said the government has only built 1,100 affordable units across the province since 2018, even though it promised to build thousands more.
"How is that possible? They promised to build nearly 20,000," Stiles noted. "That's less than six per cent of the promised housing target."
Stiles said the NDP would work with municipalities to double the supply of non-profit homes.
"The Ontario NDP, we are not afraid to spend money on housing," she said. "Not only are we building new housing, we have to make sure people don't lose their homes. It's critical. It's simple stuff - keeping people housed."
Like Stiles, Crombie talked about putting power back into the hands of northerners. She said northern leaders should be empowered to solve northern problems.
"You want a partner at Queens Park. You want and deserve to have a strong voice and a seat at the table - that's not asking too much," Crombie said. "You deserve collaboration and respect, and a good working relationship with the provincial government. We need a new deal for cities and municipalities so you have stable, predictable and reliable funding."
Crombie also said the north needs a dedicated infrastructure fund, "so that you aren't competing with the big cities in the south."
Crombie said municipalities across the province need new funding models that respect the "on-the-ground expertise" of elected officials, as well as the staff hired to co-ordinate municipal activities.
"City-building is nation-building, and I want our Ontario Liberals to be builders again, but we can't build without our municipal leaders," she commented.
Crombie accused the Conservatives of failing Ontarians time and again. But she also pointed to the provincial Liberals and said they have not always "gotten it right."
Crombie shared a little of her upbringing with the audience. She said she and her mother moved into a rooming house (owned by her grandparents) when she was just three, after her mom left her father. He was an alcoholic and struggled with his mental health.
The lack of funding for mental health care, which is currently trending in Ontario, is troubling and deeply personal for Crombie.
"The failures we see today in our health care system are deeply concerning to me on a personal level. We need to do so much better," she told the audience. "It's these values of caring for one another, of the importance of community, that were so important to me and to my family."
If elected premier in 2026, Crombie said she plans to establish an education policy targeting colleges and universities that actually benefits the province.
"We need to invest in our future," she said. "Education is the foundation of a 21st century economic plan. We can't compete without it and without a strong economy, we can't provide the public services - the scaffolding of society, if you will - that will allow all of us to work together to build a better and stronger Ontario."
Crombie acknowledged the cost of living has spiked and she said for many, their dreams feel out of reach these days.
She was critical of the Conservatives and accused them of "spending more. It's the highest spending budget on record - $214 billion. Higher than any other budget of any other government in history."
But she said there have been few improvements.
"It spent more, but did less," Crombie quipped. "Never has there been a budget so high that achieved so little."
Schools are in crisis; the crises in hospitals and emergency rooms are deepening; food bank usage is up; and roads and highways are in bad shape.
"With all the money the Conservatives are spending, where is it going and who is really benefiting? I think it's benefiting friends and donors," Crombie said. "It certainly isn't going to northern ERs or your roads - I know you've had commitments for dozens of years to extend the last 68 km of Highway 69. It's not going to colleges or universities, who are crying out for help and relying on foreign students to meet their budgets. It's not going to a new northern economy, that we can build right here."
The picture Crombie painted was bleak and depressing. While the American economy is "surging ahead," growth seems to have stalled in Canada. Crombie said it will barely "hit 0.3 per cent this year." She also pointed out debt is skyrocketing and the deficit has reached $9 billion.
Crombie said the productivity of the average worker in Ontario is on par with a worker in Alabama.
"Unemployment is up. Business confidence, productivity and growth are all down. Everything is trending in the wrong direction," she said. "We just aren't competing."
Crombie said there is much work to do, no matter your political stripe. She said we need to come together to build a sustainable and comfortable future.
"We have work to do. Ontario is counting on us," she said. "Let's not let it down." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown

Subject: Mental health; Educational leadership; Budgets; Colleges & universities; Funding; Snow removal; Public officials; Roads & highways

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Colleges & universities

Document 227 of 281

Time for NDP to force departure of Trudeau; Letters To The Editor

Author: Richard Eberhardt Lively

Publication info: Richard Eberhardt Lively. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-ndp-force-departure-trudeau-letters-editor/docview/3053001016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+for+NDP+to+force+departure+of+Trudeau%3B+Letters+To+The+Editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Richard+Eberhardt+Lively&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Leader of Canada's NDP must use the balance of power to force Justin Trudeau to quit.
For Jagmeet Singh to defend his party's moral victories and valuable new programs, he must make what may be the boldest political play in recent memory. It is the only way to disrupt the growing certainty of a massive Conservative majority.
The upside? Singh would be getting out in front of the nation's largest parade. Canadians have overwhelmingly rejected this prime minister and four in every five want him gone.
No party strategist, no trusted adviser, not even Justin's own bloated self-interest could withstand the fever pitch of pressure for him to go. Faced with the real threat of mutually assured destruction (a fall election), the Liberal caucus and millions of Canadians will get what they want most: an immediate end to the Trudeau era.
In exchange for blowing up the Liberal Party as we know it, Singh could graciously promise to give them one year to put a new leader in place. That also means guaranteed time to solidify the gains of pharmacare and dental care and more.
More importantly, this dramatic end to the productive but painful confidence and supply agreement may turn devastating defeat into the possibility for progressive renewal.
It's clearer every day - there is nothing more the NDP can squeeze out of the Liberals which will move the political needle any direction but right. Simply: nobody is listening. This government and its supporters can't sell voters on anything if Trudeau remains in charge. Look no further than the big post-budget bump they worked so hard for - it boosted the Conservatives five points.
With Justin gone, the certainty of Tory victory suddenly softens. Cut the anchor loose and the fortunes of the orange and red teams will lift. The departure would shake Pierre Pollievre's core identity as the anti-Trudeau, throwing his careful strategy into chaos. Without his arch-rival, will Pollievre ever be the same? And what will they do with all those flags and hats? Mr. Singh, it's time to drag Justin Trudeau on a walk through the spring tulips. This is more than your moment. It may be our last chance.

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: leader; balance; power; force; justin; trudeau

Document 228 of 281

Trudeau's bullying helped crush his popularity

Author: Staples, David

Publication info: Staples, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-bullying-helped-crush-his-popularity/docview/3051787819/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+bullying+helped+crush+his+popularity&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Staples%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reputation is in tatters. David Coletto, chief executive of Abacus polling, reports the Conservatives have more support, 44 per cent, than the coalition of the Liberals at 23 per cent and Jagmeet Singh's NDP at 17 per cent, combined, and the polls are unlikely to change with Trudeau as leader.
"What I've learned from 20 years of public opinion research into Canadian politics: You cannot persuade people or lead them when they don't trust you and think your time has passed," Coletto said on social media. "Any fight the Liberal government has is bound to fail as long as this is how people feel about it and its leader ... Reputation is everything."
Coletto's point got me thinking not only about Trudeau, but about recent allegations against Alberta Premier Danielle Smith for uncaring and authoritarian policies.
What is the issue that has sunk Trudeau's reputation? His failed economic policy tops the list, but second is his propensity for bullying and authoritarian governing. We've seen it in the way he demonizes opponents, hammers outcasts from his own party, and scoffs at our constitution and courts.
Of course, Trudeau's authoritarian streak was there from the beginning. Remember Trudeau's 2014 dictate that no longer would anti-abortion candidates be tolerated in Liberal ranks, not to mention Trudeau's now infamous pro-Chinese statement from 2013. Asked which country he most admired, Trudeau said, "There's a level of admiration I actually have for China, because their basic dictatorship is allowing them to actually turn their economy around on a dime and say we need to go green, we need to start, you know, investing in solar."
An authoritarian line runs to Trudeau's unrelenting agenda to tax and thwart Alberta's oil and gas economy. Even our federal courts have become alarmed, ruling against Trudeau's plastics ban and industrial assessment policy.
Far from being humbled by the court decisions, Trudeau rushes from issue to issue, from a new, discriminatory cap on Alberta's oil and gas emissions to his federal housing policy, stomping on provincial rights as if they were a mosquito on his arm rather than Canada's foundational agreements. He was last heard threatening on the housing file: "I'd always rather work with provinces, but if we have to, I will go around them and be there for Canadians."
My own wake-up call on Trudeau's character came in 2017 when his government brought in a rule that no group could get government funding for student summer job grants unless it signed an attestation confirming it respected abortion rights. I'm pro-choice, but I respect the rights of conscience of others. Trudeau evidently did not, then went down the same path forcing discriminatory race and sex quotas into government business contracts and research grant agreements.
We might also remember Trudeau's ugly blowups with former Liberal Party insiders like Jody Wilson-Raybould, who complained she finally quit his cabinet because: "I just wanted it to end. The condescension. The belittlement. The lack of seriousness. The lack of commitment to the truth."
Finally, there's Trudeau's demonizing of opponents as racists or misogynists over basic policy or personal disagreements.
Trudeau's most unforgivable authoritarian act was his support of banks freezing the personal accounts of anti-lockdown protesters.
As for Smith, she's done well on several hot-button issues to avoid acting the bully. On two tricky files, drug addiction and the medical gender transition of children, she's brought in far less permissive policies than the progressive left pushes, but she's kept public support by focusing her rhetoric on what's best for homeless drug addicts and for vulnerable gender-dysphoric children facing monumental adult decisions.
Last week, Smith crossed a line, coming down too aggressively on the authority of elected municipal councillors, then cutting funding for our poorest transit users. At once, an outcry exploded over her being uncaring and a bully.
On the transit issue, Smith reversed herself at once. It now looks like her government is open to walking back some disagreeable measures in its municipal governance reform.
We are all, quite rightly, on high alert for bullies, for the authoritarian streak of powerful people. Only arrogant politicians destined to go down in infamy fail to tone down their own bullying tendencies. dstaples@postmedia.com

Subject: Authoritarianism; Bullying; Court decisions; Abortion; Reputations

Identifier / keyword: prime; minister; justin; reputation; tatters; david

Document 229 of 281

China 'tainted' riding races; Meddling did not alter election outcomes: foreign interference report

Author: Nardi, Christopher; Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher; Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/china-tainted-riding-races-meddling-did-not-alter/docview/3050644034/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=China+%27tainted%27+riding+races%3B+Meddling+did+not+alter+election+outcomes%3A+foreign+interference+report&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher%3BL%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chinese international students may have received "veiled threats" from the People's Republic of China's consulate and been provided with falsified documents to allow them to vote for Liberal candidate Han Dong's nomination ahead of the 2019 election, according to a new report.
That's one example of riding-level foreign interference that "tainted" the 2019 and 2021 federal elections but ultimately did not affect the overall results or the integrity of Canada's electoral system, according to a first report by the Public Inquiry into Foreign Interference published Friday.
The Liberals undoubtedly won the last two elections, Hogue said, and the country's voting system has been strong and secure.
But the commissioner had "no difficulty" concluding that there was foreign interference in certain ridings that likely impacted at least one nomination race and potentially undermined certain candidates viewed unfavourably by the Chinese government.
"Although the election result at a national level was not impacted, and only a few races were potentially impacted at a riding level, I nevertheless conclude that foreign interference impacted the overall election ecosystem in 2019 and 2021," Hogue wrote.
"The acts of interference that occurred are a stain on our electoral process and impacted the process leading up to the actual vote," she said in a statement.
Hogue's 200-page report covers the content of tens of thousands of documents - many classified for national security reasons - and weeks of public and confidential testimony by over 40 witnesses ranging from the heads of Canada's spy agencies and federal departments to top PMO officials, a coterie of ministers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
It concludes that the "greatest harm" of foreign interference in Canada to date has been to undermine public confidence in Canada's democracy and electoral system.
The report provides some detail into alleged interference in a handful of federal ridings in the last two federal elections that targeted Liberal, Conservative and NDP politicians. It focused mainly on the Chinese government's attempts to influence the elections, but also noted efforts by India, Pakistan and to a lesser extent Russia and Iran.
Among the most shocking revelations in the report were the circumstances around Liberal candidate Han Dong's hotly contested nomination contest.
Hogue's report said some intelligence, which was not "firmly substantiated," suggests Chinese students were provided with "falsified documents" and then bused in to the Liberal Don Valley North nomination election so they could vote for Dong despite not being residents of the riding.
Those documents were provided by individuals associated with a well-known Chinese proxy agent, the report read.
Intelligence agencies however reported after the fact that those students were in fact coerced into voting for Dong.
Dong, who resigned from the Liberal caucus to sit as independent last year because of media reports alleging he had close ties to the PRC consulate, denied any involvement in the matter.
"Some intelligence reported after the election indicated that veiled threats were issued by the PRC Consulate to the Chinese international students, implying their student visas would be in jeopardy and that there could be consequences for their families back in the PRC if they did not support Han Dong," read the report.
At the time, the Security and Intelligence Threats to Elections Task Force stated in its report on the 2019 federal election that "PRC officials likely manipulated one of the nomination contests in the Toronto riding of Don Valley North," but said that some of the allegations remained "unconfirmed."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was briefed during the election about those allegations but decided not to overturn the results of the nomination contest because he felt at the time where was no "sufficient or sufficiently credible information" to justify removing his new MP. "Mr. Trudeau noted that un-endorsing Mr. Dong would have direct electoral consequences as the LPC (Liberal Party of Canada) expected to win DVN (Don Valley North). It would also have a devastating impact on Mr. Dong personally," read the report.
Still in 2019, Hogue added that it was "likely" there were at least two transfers of funds for an approximate total of $250,000 originating from PRC officials in Canada, possibly for foreign-interference purposes.
But she could not conclude that this money went to any of the 11 candidates. Instead, it was transferred via "multiple individuals to obfuscate their origins, including an Ontario MPP, between late 2018 and early 2019.
The report also mentions intelligence indicating that PRC officials also co-ordinated the exclusion of some candidates perceived as "anti-China" in the Greater Vancouver area from attending local community events related to the 2019 election.
NDP MP Jenny Kwan testified at the commission that she had observed a "seismic shift" in her relationship with Chinese community organizations starting in 2019, as she started to become critical of PRC policies with respect to Taiwan, Hong Kong and the Uyghur minority.
The most significant case of potential foreign interference in the 2021 federal election was in the B.C. riding of Steveston-Richmond East targeting Conservative candidate Kenny Chiu.
She found that during the election, media outlets linked to the People's Republic of China and Chinese state media helped push a "false narrative" that Chiu was "anti-China" because he had tabled a private member's bill that would establish a foreign agent registry.
For the first time, the report identifies two Markham, Ont.-based media organizations - 105.9 Yes My Radio and Global Chinese Convergence Media (CGCTV) - which intelligence indicates have "close links" to the Chinese government or state media.
The outlets'stories were written to dissuade Chinese Canadians from voting for Chiu and Erin O'Toole's Conservatives and circulated widely on Chinese social media platform WeChat, the report says.
But Hogue stopped short of saying those efforts directly impacted the riding election result, instead concluding that was only a "reasonable possibility."
But she disagreed with the government's view that the fact the disinformation about Chiu stopped spreading weeks before election day was a good sign.
"I am concerned by this reliance on the idea of a self-cleansing media ecosystem. By the time that disinformation fades away, it may be too late. I am also concerned by the absence of clear guidelines for when the government will act short of a public announcement," she wrote.
Though the Conservatives and NDP MP Jenny Kwan suspected there was further Chinese interference in other ridings in 2021, Hogue said she could not conclude the same.
Hogue's report also revealed that CSIS, Canada's spy agency, had managed to reduce a foreign interference threat originating from Pakistan ahead of the 2019 campaign.
Hogue also highlighted communication issues between government intelligence agencies and departments as well as between election integrity watchdogs and political parties.
But it stopped short of providing any recommendations.

Subject: Nominations; Foreign students; False information; Candidates; Prime ministers; Diplomatic & consular services; Intelligence services; Elections

Location: China; Pakistan; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin
