Document 230 of 281

Trudeau turns labour vote right

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-turns-labour-vote-right/docview/3050016570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+turns+labour+vote+right&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Workers of the world, unite! This is your moment. Across the globe, parties are courting the labour vote as life gets more unaffordable, technology takes over more jobs and everything seems more uncertain. Traditionally enamoured with the left, unionized workers are turning to the right as progressive parties dive down policy rabbit holes that have little to do with improving their members'working conditions, but much to do with identity politics and social engineering.
Here in Canada, it is no different. Federal and provincial conservative parties are gaining support among blue-collar voters. In the most recent Ontario election, several private sector unions, including the Laborers'International Union of North America (LIUNA), endorsed Doug Ford's Conservatives, a first for the party. Federally, Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are gaining traction in traditional NDP territory, from British Columbia to Ontario, as workers flip their votes from orange to blue.
But it's not just the NDP that is feeling the pain. The federal Liberals are losing support too, due to growing working-class opposition to the carbon tax. In what could be the biggest policy reversal of the decade, even the NDP says the tax is too expensive. Why? Because polls show the worse off a voter is, the more likely they are to vote Conservative. Even 36 per cent of public sector union workers would back the Tories, compared with 26 per cent each for the Liberals and NDP.
So the counter-campaign has started. Two weeks ago, the head of the Canadian Labour Congress, Bea Bruske, warned Poilievre is "a fraud" and not to be trusted due to his voting records on workers' rights.
Then last week, a second salvo: the "milestone" announcement of a new Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, set to create "over 1,000 well-paying manufacturing jobs" and boost the Canadian auto industry. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was all smiles, and no wonder: the plant is right in the Liberals'sweet spot, producing green energy for cars and good jobs for unionized workers.
Except there's a fly in the ointment: foreign workers. And it's turning a good news announcement into yet another boondoggle for the Liberals.
On April 10, Sean Strickland, executive director of Canada's Building Trades Unions, wrote Trudeau a letter over concerns about foreign workers taking Canadian jobs at another EV project, the NextStar plant in Windsor, a venture between Chrysler's Stellantis and Korean firm LG. Strickland said foreign workers are building the plant while Canadian workers, including 180 local millwrights and ironworkers, "are being sidelined without consequence." In response, the government says only 70 of the 2,000 workers currently at the site are not Canadian.
But the union says 900 South Korean workers are set to come to Windsor during the installation phase of the battery plant's development. Asked by Strickland about the new Honda deal on Monday, Trudeau said "it's part and parcel of it that we expect that the construction, the installation, the maintenance be done by Canadians as much as is humanly possible."
That answer didn't satisfy either the NDP or the Conservatives. The same day, the Tories tabled a motion in the House of Commons to get the details of the Honda deal and see just how many Canadian jobs have been guaranteed. The Liberals have said they cannot release the contracts because they contain sensitive business information that could jeopardize future EV plant deals.
But with $5 billion of taxpayers'money on the line, surely the government could demand that Honda hire local workers? Alas, you would be mistaken. Canada's history is littered with similar auto deals gone bad, where workers ended up sidelined after public funds piled in. In 2018, General Motors shuttered its Oshawa plant and put 2,500 workers out of work, despite promising in 2016 to keep things running until 2020. At the time, GM still owed the government an undisclosed amount of its $10.5-billion taxpayer bailout received in 2009.
The Liberals should be careful not to oversell the Honda deal and be fully transparent on the terms. Unless Trudeau can satisfy concerns that the plant isn't just an election-friendly photo op, and will actually employ Canadians, workers may well be asking: who's the fraud now? National Post

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: workers; world; unite; moment; globe; parties

Document 231 of 281

UCP bends Alberta authorities to its will

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ucp-bends-alberta-authorities-will/docview/3049152312/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UCP+bends+Alberta+authorities+to+its+will&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New rules for Alberta towns and cities make it clear who's the boss. It's the United Conservative Party government, not local councils or the voters who elect them.
Under proposed laws unveiled Thursday, the province will be able to step into a local council and fire individual members. he province can also can overturn a local bylaw on any subject, widely expanding its existing power to block only land-use laws.
In another echo of post-pandemic grudges, the UCP appears to take control over any municipal orders on public health measures.
These are the latest moves in a widespread consolidation of provincial power.
Step by step, local authorities are being nudged into line as the UCP lays down the building blocks of sovereignty.
Premier Danielle Smith has stepped into university research funding, adding a requirement for more conservative ideology.
Annoyed by Ottawa striking deals with towns and cities, a UCP bill would make any agreement invalid if the province isn't directly involved.
That seems to be aimed mainly at Calgary and Edmonton, which constantly annoy the UCP with bilateral federal deals.
But the bill reaches into the smallest municipality, education boards, universities, health authorities, and Crown and public agencies.
No public authority in Alberta dares to deal with the federal government unless provincial watchers are on hand to stamp approval.
That's a heavy load of bureaucracy from a government that loves to boast about its department of red tape reduction.
Thursday's announcements relate to local elections, councils and political funding, all based on the premise that the province is supreme.
That's true in law, of course; but the province usually has been a benevolent parent, tolerant of local diversity and even a certain amount of opposition.
That era is fading fast, despite Municipal Affairs Minister Ric McIver's promise that the province will rule with a gentle hand.
The most striking measure - drawing up rules for civic political parties - is driven by UCP dreams of sweeping bigcity progressives from power.
That will only happen in Edmonton and Calgary. McIver says smaller centres are free to allow parties if they like, but there's little interest.
Another reason, I'd suggest, is that most councils in rural Alberta are already comfortably loaded with UCP supporters.
But Conservative governments, from Progressive Conservative to United Conservative, always have failed to wedge their own loyalists into the mayors'offices in Calgary and Edmonton. This they find deeply irritating.
The ultimate slap was Edmonton's election of Amarjeet Sohi, a former Trudeau Liberal cabinet minister.
In Calgary, they're stuck with progressive Jyoti Gondek, who in 2021 handily defeated conservative challenger Jeromy Farkas.
In 2001, Dave Bronconnier became mayor after running federally for the Liberals in 1997 and getting soundly thumped.
Next came Naheed Nenshi.
In 2010, he defeated conservative McIver, who moved on to provincial politics and introduced the measures Thursday.
In 2017, conservative backers put up local businessman Bill Smith, a former president of the provincial PC party, who finished 30,000 votes behind Nenshi.
It's a sorry history. But now, big-city parties might work for the UCP.
Gondek and the current council majority brought in measures, including blanket zoning and the bag bylaw, that were never mentioned in the 2021 campaign.
Parties would make the stakes much clearer, the theory goes, by releasing platforms saying what they would and would not do with a majority. And that would draw out conservative-minded voters who previously had no idea for what their ward candidates stood.
But there's no missing what goes on here. The UCP is massaging the whole public sector into fit shape for loyal service. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Local elections; Councils; Voters

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

Identifier / keyword: rules; alberta; towns; cities; clear; united

Universities warn student activists encampments will not be tolerated; Warnings comes after protests set up camp at McGill University in Montreal

Author: Baig, Fakiha

Publication info: Baig, Fakiha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/universities-warn-student-activists-encampments/docview/3049151427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Universities+warn+student+activists+encampments+will+not+be+tolerated%3B+Warnings+comes+after+protests+set+up+camp+at+McGill+University+in+Montreal&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Baig%2C+Fakiha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two Ontario universities are warning anyone planning to follow the lead of pro-Palestinian student activists in the U.S. and elsewhere in Canada that setting up encampments on campus will not be tolerated.
The University of Toronto and the University of Ottawa say they understand tensions are high, many people are feeling anxious due to the scale of suffering in the Middle East and peaceful protests are central to campus and democratic life. But, the universities say they have clear policies that state the use of their facilities without proper authorization can carry serious consequences and protests need to be organized within the limits of university policies and the law.
"U of T's lands and buildings are private property, though the university allows wide public access to them for authorized activities," Sandy Welsh, vice-provost of students, wrote in an email sent out to the student community on Sunday.
"Unauthorized activities such as encampments or the occupation of university buildings are considered trespassing. Specifically, our Code of Student Conduct prohibits intentional damage to university property, unauthorized entry and use of university property contrary to instructions, disruptions of university activities, and other offences to property and persons."
Eric Bercier, the associate vice-president of student affairs at the University of Ottawa, said the university had heard of a protest planned for Monday and warned that the setup of any encampment would carry serious consequences.
"While peaceful protest is permitted in appropriate public spaces on campus according to our policies and regulations, encampments and occupations will not be tolerated," Bercier wrote in a statement posted on the university's website.
"We ask everyone to thoughtfully consider their responsibilities and the wellbeing of our entire community. We continue to call on all members of our community to treat each other with respect and empathy as they interact in our classrooms, common spaces, and online."
The warnings from the University of Ottawa and the University of Toronto come after an encampment was set up by pro-Palestinian student activists on the grounds of McGill University in Montreal, where student activists from Concordia University are also participating.
Student activists at McGill have said they are demanding the school divest from Israeli companies that are "complicit in the occupation of Palestine." They also expect the school to cut academic ties with Israeli institutions. McGill has said the camp violates both school policies and the law.
Campuses in the United States have seen a wave of protests in recent weeks linked to the Israel-Hamas war.
On Monday, Columbia University escalated its stance against protesters on its New York campus by sending an ultimatum for students to sign a form and leave the encampment by the afternoon or face suspension.
Early protests at Columbia, where demonstrators set up tents in the centre of the campus, sparked pro-Palestinian demonstrations across the U.S. Students and others have been sparring over the Israel-Hamas war and its mounting death toll. Many students are demanding their universities cut financial ties with Israel.
When asked Monday about the planned sit-in at the University of Ottawa, Premier Doug Ford said at an unrelated news conference that Ontario is known for its inclusivity.
"You know what I wish? I wish everyone gets along," Ford said. "Sure, there's conflicts around the world. They want to voice their opinion. And that's their democratic right. Do it peacefully. That's all I ask." The Canadian Press, with files from The Associated Press

Document 235 of 281

Baranyai: Ford's reversals show citizens can effect change

Author: Baranyai, Robin; to, Special

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin; to, Special. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/baranyai-fords-reversals-show-citizens-can-effect/docview/3042430628/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Document 236 of 281

Ford's PCs hold commanding lead, Liberals steal NDP support

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-pcs-hold-commanding-lead-liberals-steal-ndp/docview/3039698327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+PCs+hold+commanding+lead%2C+Liberals+steal+NDP+support&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If an election were held today, Doug Ford's PC Party would win another big majority government. The latest poll from Liaison Strategies also suggests Bonnie Crombie's Liberals would form the Opposition and regain official party status at Queen's Park.
Oh, and the NDP, they'd be relegated to third place once again.
The telephone poll of 1,280 eligible Ontario voters finds that 40 per cent would back Ford's PCs, 30 per cent would back Crombie's Liberals but just 18 per cent would back the NDP under Marit Stiles. Ford's support is pretty much where it was in the last election, when his party won 83 seats, but Crombie has been able to move the Liberals up since taking over as leader in December, but only at the NDP's expense.
In the last two elections, the Liberals haven't been able to even win enough seats to be considered a recognized party in the legislature. Under the official rules, the nine Liberal MPPs are considered independents and given limited resources and time in question period compared to the NDP.
But like they did under former leader Andrea Horwath and continue to do under Stiles, the NDP has squandered their opportunity to cement themselves as the left-of-centre choice in Ontario. In every province west of Ontario, there is essentially no Liberal Party to speak of and elections are between the NDP and parties representing what is often called a free market coalition.
Had the Ontario NDP played their cards right starting in 2018, they could have ensured the same in Ontario. Instead, the Ontario Liberal Party is showing signs of coming back to life.
Not that the Crombie's Liberals should be popping champagne corks yet.
When Steven Del Duca took over as Liberal leader in March 2020, they saw a bounce in the polls. There were periods in 2020, 2021 and even early 2022 where the Del Duca Liberals were leading Ford and his PC Party and then the campaign happened and the Liberals ended up at 24 per cent, 16 points behind Ford.
It's always important for political types to remember voters are fickle, polls can change and campaigns matter. Those are three rules that apply to every party, including Ford, who appears to be sitting pretty at the moment.
Beyond the 40 per cent voter support there are other good news numbers for Ford in this poll.
Just shy of half of voters, 49 per cent, say the federal carbon tax should be scrapped. The environment isn't the issue it was before for the Liberals or NDP with 62 per cent saying pollution has decreased in the province compared to five years ago.
In a head-to-head question on which issue is more important, 54 per cent said affordability while just 25 per cent said the environment and 21 per cent said they didn't know.
Which makes Bonnie Crombie's recent comments to an Empire Club luncheon in Toronto problematic.
During a fireside chat after her speech last week, Crombie was asked about her promises to put more money into health care and education and how she'd make sure the government, if she were running it, could pay for them.
"Well, it's very interesting, because I've been thinking about what exactly the government is spending money on other than gimmicks and freezing the gas tax and giving you a rebate for your stickers and making them so you don't have to pay for them," Crombie said.
"That's really reducing the fiscal capacity and I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?" Calling tax cuts and fee cuts a reduction in the fiscal capacity is a tell, Crombie thinks government should have that money instead of voters. Given that her party is trying to win a byelection in the suburban seat of Milton, these comments will hurt the party.
Crombie has done well taking support from the NDP, but she's likely taken as much as she can there. To win, she'll need to peel voters away from Ford's PCs and she won't do it by promising to increase taxes and fees on commuters.
Such promises are music to Doug Ford's ears.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Elections

Identifier / keyword: election; party; another; majority; government; latest

Ford's policies destroying health care in North: Gelinas; In Brief

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-policies-destroying-health-care-north/docview/3038014231/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+policies+destroying+health+care+in+North%3A+Gelinas%3B+In+Brief&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP health critic and Nickel Belt MPP France Gelinas is decrying the state of health care in rural and Northern Ontario under the leadership of Premier Doug Ford.
"After six years, Premier Ford's neglect has plunged Northern Ontario's health care into a crisis state, with no signs of improvement," said Gelinas in a statement.
The MPP pointed to a situation in Sault Ste. Marie, where 10,000 people will lose access to health care next month due to doctors leaving or retiring and not being replaced.
Meanwhile, with "280 emergency room closures and 1,200 hospital service shutdowns, rural and Northern Ontario are bearing the brunt of Ford's underfunding and neglect," said Gelinas.
In Elmvale, a rural town near Barrie, "the community was on the brink of gaining access to much-needed health care, with a doctor ready to open a shop," said the MPP. "However, the health minister denied their proposal, dashing their hopes and leaving elderly seniors without timely access to care."
Gelinas said access to care shouldbeafundamentalrightbut"hasbecomealuxury.It'sashamefullegacyforthisgovernment, which has done so little to address this crisis, setting our health care system back decades."
It doesn't have to be this way, however, the Nickel Belt member stressed.
"The NDP and Ontarians know we have solutions," she said. "It's time for Ford's Conservatives to step up today and fulfill their duty to the people of rural and Northern Ontario by investing in our health-care system."

More $10-per-day child care spaces coming to Sudbury

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-10-per-day-child-care-spaces-coming-sudbury/docview/3038014224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+%2410-per-day+child+care+spaces+coming+to+Sudbury&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As part of the roll-out of the federal budget, the Liberals announced this week new measures to create more affordable child-care spaces. The Liberals say this will save more families thousands of dollars and help more parents return to their careers.
"Every child deserves the best start in life, but for too long access to affordable and available childcare services was not possible for many families in Sudbury," Sudbury MP Viviane Lapointe said in a release. "Some families I met with told me their childcare costs were higher than their monthly rent or mortgage payments.
"With our government's childcare agreement, Sudbury families are now saving thousands of dollars every month. But we need more spaces. I've heard from providers, like YMCA Northeastern Ontario, and know the challenges in having access to spaces and educators.
"By building more childcare spaces, and attracting and training more childcare workers, we're making sure even more young families can access affordable childcare and save money."
The measures the Liberal government announced include student loan forgiveness for rural and remote early childhood educators. This will encourage educators to work in smaller communities and help families get the childcare they need, the Liberals said.
With a $48-million investment over four years, student loan forgiveness will increase the longer an educator works in a rural or remote area.
The Liberals also said they are launching a new child-care expansion loan program.
"With $1 billion in low-cost loans and $60 million in non-repayable grants, public and not-for-profit childcare providers will be able to build new spaces and renovate their existing child care centres," the federal government explained. "This means more resources for child care providers and more affordable child care options for families."
Finally, the Liberals announced they are increasing training opportunities for early childhood educators.
"We're investing $10 million over two years to train more early childhood educators, building up the talent needed for the expansion of affordable, high-quality child care," the government noted.
The Liberals said there has been progress in building a nation-wide early learning and childcare system Across the country, more than 750,000 children are already benefiting from affordable, high-quality childcare that costs $10 a day. Some families have saved up to $14,000 per child, per year.
Alongside their provincial and territorial counterparts, the Liberals said more than 100,000 new spaces have been created over three years, "well on our way to reaching our goal of creating 250,000 new spaces by March 2026." Star Staff sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 240 of 281

Advocates for supervised consumption site in Sudbury keep fighting; The Spot closed at the end of March due to a lack of provincial funding

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/advocates-supervised-consumption-site-sudbury/docview/3037252330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Advocates+for+supervised+consumption+site+in+Sudbury+keep+fighting%3B+The+Spot+closed+at+the+end+of+March+due+to+a+lack+of+provincial+funding&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Between the closures of the Sudbury Action Centre for Youth and The Spot, the outreach community in Greater Sudbury has taken a hard hit in recent weeks.
But Heidi Eisenhauer, executive director of Reseau ACCESS Network, said her staff remains undaunted.
"Réseau ACCESS Network will continue to advocate for the supervised consumption site to receive provincial consumption and treatment services funding," she said. "Not having a safe consumption site is an irresponsible use of public funds. SCSs are a proven life-saving intervention, and they reduce health costs."
Eisenhauer said it will cost nearly $1.5 million annually to operate The Spot for 12 hours per day.
The Spot, located in Energy Court behind the Beer Store on Lorne Street, closed at the end of March, but it had been on life support for a few months before that.
It first opened its doors in September 2022, funded entirely by the City of Greater Sudbury. Council at that time believed provincial funding was imminent. But Queen's Park remained silent and refrained from committing funds to The Spot (and other safe consumption sites).
At the end of December, city council decided they could no longer afford to finance Sudbury's only safe consumption site. The program was omitted from the 2024-25 budget. Private donors stepped in to keep the site open for three months, but on March 29, The Spot served its last client.
"Réseau is working with the City of Greater Sudbury, and Public Health Sudbury and Districts, along with community partners, to look at gaps in service. As with most charities, Réseau will seek funding to fill gaps in service - as indicated by and for people who use drugs, and people with lived and living experience," Eisenhauer said.
"The agency facilitates multiple focus groups with members to ascertain community needs and wants as a part of the public health response to this toxic drug crisis."
Eisenhauer said ACCESS lost 18 staff "as we awaited word from the pause in funding."
Sudbury MPP Jamie West has joined the call for funding. In a recent column published in The Sudbury Star, the New Democrat blamed the Conservatives at Queen's Park for the closure.
"I want to be clear: The Spot is closing because the Conservative government has continually chosen not to provide the provincial funding it requires while sitting on billions of taxpayer dollars. That decision will result in losing lives and overwhelm our EMS services," West wrote. "We need wrap-around support, including addiction support, mental health support and housing. Supervised consumption sites are a keystone in those services. Without overdose prevention, recovery would never be possible."
West said 40 years of evidence has proven safe consumption sites work.
"There has never been a single death at one of these sites. They reduce police and ambulance calls; keep people out of busy hospitals; and offer vital support," he argued.
"Despite all the research, proof and community support for these sites, Premier Doug Ford arbitrarily capped funding to only 21 supervised consumption sites. I'm not sure why, several years later, only 17 sites have received provincial funding. I do know the Conservative government has chosen not to fund our city's supervised consumption site for two years and seven months. This massive misstep and failure of judgment has cost us lives and continues to affect the mental health of our friends, family and neighbours."
As West noted, health care is a provincial responsibility. It should not fall to municipalities and private donors to fund harm reduction services, he said.
"It should never have been this way. It's not the responsibility of our city to pay for this service, nor is it the responsibility of our neighbours and community businesses," he argued. "This funding is a provincial responsibility. The decision to fund only one site in Northern Ontario - in Thunder Bay - was a decision made by Doug Ford's Conservative government."
Reseau ACCESS Network remains on the frontlines of the toxic drug crisis in Greater Sudbury. Eisenhauer said the agency has been delivering outreach services, seven days per week, for more than a decade.
Eisenhauer said after the Sudbury Action Centre for Youth closed suddenly on March 18 - after 35 years - her agency "received SACY supplies and the community awaits funder news on next steps for the services they provided."
Eisenhauer believes in outreach and harm reduction services. She said we are in a public health emergency and delays cause death, but safe consumption sites save lives.
"The Spot and other safe consumption sites are places of safety, dignity and connection. They allow people who use drugs to connect to other services in a non-judgemental environment, connecting them to health and social services they may not have otherwise due to stigma and discrimination," she said.
"The Spot built trust with the drug-using community, which helped to facilitate knowledge exchange between those who accessed the service and staff, which allowed for real-time information regarding particularly toxic substances.
The exchange of this information can save lives."
As she noted, The Spot was the only SCS in this area.
"Those who relied on The Spot are now left with nowhere to go. When you don't have safe consumption sites, you have unsafe consumption sites in alleys, parks, and washrooms," she explained. "SCSs are a part of the health care continuum. The wrap-around services provided at SCSs allow for individuals, often on the margins, to access information, health services and social services in an environment that is trauma-informed and non-judgemental."
Eisenhauer said safe consumption sites also monitor for HIV, hepatitis C and some sexually transmissible illnesses.
They are more than a safe place to inject drugs. SCSs can be important community gathering spots.
"Not everyone who can benefit from SCSs has substance use disorder, this isn't just an addictions issue," Eisenhauer said. "Many members of the community accessed The Spot on a daily basis, seven days a week. We know these members - they are our friends, our family, the community. Lives will be lost. Lives have been lost." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Public health; Mental health; Funding; Community support; Harm reduction

Document 242 of 281

Doug Ford wants federal workers back in the office. What happens if they don't return?

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-federal-workers-back-office-what/docview/3034035954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Document 244 of 281

How the Liberals blew their credibility on the carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-liberals-blew-their-credibility-on-carbon-tax/docview/3030976417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+Liberals+blew+their+credibility+on+the+carbon+tax&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once a government loses its credibility on an issue, it's hard to recover it.
That's what has happened to the Trudeau Liberals on their carbon tax.
Their loss of credibility began when they claimed they'd come up with a tax that, due to rebates, would leave 80 per cent of Canadian households paying it better off, which never passed the smell test.
That's because what they didn't say is this was only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax - dollars in, dollars out - not its negative impact on the Canadian economy.
That only happened when independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux examined the carbon tax.
He said the government was right to say 80 per cent of households end up better off when only its fiscal impact is considered.
But when you factor in its negative impact on the economy, Giroux estimated 60 per cent of households paying the federal carbon tax are already worse off today, rising to 80 per cent in Nova Scotia in 2025, in Ontario in 2026, in Manitoba in 2029 and in Alberta and P.E.I. in 2030.
Giroux reported that the carbon tax would lower Canada's GDP and reduce business investment and labour income for skilled and unskilled workers. He also noted the government doesn't remit the GST paid on top of the carbon tax back to those paying it.
Ever since, the Liberals and their supporters, frantic to discredit Giroux, have been throwing up red herrings and irrelevant arguments, not to discredit his analysis of the fiscal impact of their carbon tax - because Giroux agrees with the Trudeau Liberals on that - but his added analysis of its economic impact.
They complained Giroux didn't factor in the cost of other things the Trudeau government might have done to lower emissions in place of a carbon tax, ignoring that the PBO's role is to assess government policies that actually exist.
They said the PBO didn't factor in future savings due to new technologies to fight climate change, nor the cost of doing nothing because of increasingly severe weather.
This ignored that what the PBO was assessing was the economic impact of Trudeau's carbon tax that began at $20 a tonne of industrial greenhouse gas emissions in 2019, is now $65 a tonne and will rise on April 1 to $80 a tonne, on its way to $170 a tonne in 2030.
We don't know what the Liberal plan is after 2030, and as Giroux has noted, claiming the carbon tax will save money because of technological innovation or less severe weather in Canada by 2030 is unrealistic.
It's also absurd. Contrary to his critics'claims, Giroux has examined the long-term impact of climate change on the Canadian economy, estimating that, "recent increases in temperature and precipitation combined with future changes in weather patterns will reduce Canada's real GDP by 5.8 per cent in 2100."
The issue, Giroux said, is that even if we were able to reduce our emissions to net zero by tomorrow, it would have no impact on the weather in Canada because our emissions, at 1.5 per cent of the global total, aren't enough to materially impact climate change.
Reducing our emissions will give us the moral high ground in urging major emitters like China to do the same, but the idea that anything Canada does alone through Trudeau's carbon tax is going to mean fewer wildfires, floods and storms in Canada, is absurd. lgoldstein@postmedia.com

Subject: Economic impact; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Cost control; Credibility; Emissions; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic impact Gross Domestic Product--GDP Cost control Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: government; loses; credibility; issue; recover; happened

Document 245 of 281

Is the carbon tax costing you or making you money?; Both sides have been extensively citing the PBO's reports to justify their stances

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-carbon-tax-costing-you-making-money-both-sides/docview/3030976361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+the+carbon+tax+costing+you+or+making+you+money%3F%3B+Both+sides+have+been+extensively+citing+the+PBO%27s+reports+to+justify+their+stances&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As one MP put it this week, Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux has been in the news "almost as much" as Taylor Swift these days, as the parties debate his studies of the federal carbon tax's impact on Canadians' wallets.
Both Liberals and Conservatives have been extensively citing the PBO's reports in attempting to justify their respective stances on the carbon tax, which went up April 1, and the governments tax rebates.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau claims the reports say eight out of 10 families get more from the rebate than they pay in carbon taxes. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre cites the PBO in his claim that "a majority of households will see a negative impact."
The truth is, Liberals and Conservatives are both telling a different version of the truth - with some important caveats.
Giroux's appearance at the House of Commons Government Operations Committee last Wednesday may have been overshadowed by the Saskatchewan premier's testimony, but MPs had pointed questions for the budget watchdog.
Conservative MP Phil Lawrence asked Giroux whether there is ultimately "more money coming into Canadians'pockets or leaving their pockets" for those where the backstop applies.
"If one looks at the fiscal impact, that is the amount of the carbon tax paid directly, indirectly and the GST that applies on these embedded direct carbon taxes paid minus the carbon rebate, most families are better off - we estimate around 80 per cent," said Giroux.
Last year's analysis from the PBO indeed stated that "most households will see a net gain" in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador by the fiscal year 2030-31 - once the tax reaches $170 per tonne of carbon.
The only exception was in Nova Scotia, where households in the third, fourth and fifth highest income quintiles would pay more than they receive in carbon rebates.
The PBO noted that the tax minus the rebates is "broadly progressive," meaning that lower-income households would benefit more than higher income households.
However, the numbers changed once the PBO included the economic impacts of the introduction of the carbon tax. The assumption is that the tax will have had an effect on some sectors of the economy, and therefore family incomes.
"Then, we find that most Canadian families, in provinces where the federal backstop regime is in place, will see a small negative impact of the carbon tax," Giroux told MPs.
The same analysis mentioned above indeed mentioned that "most households will see a net loss" by the fiscal year 2030-2031 since they will be paying more in the federal fuel charge and GST, and it is assumed that they will be receiving lower incomes.
On average, households in Alberta would be paying $2,773 in net losses and those in Ontario would be paying $1,820 by 2030-31, according to the PBO's estimates. Lower-income households would continue to benefit from a net gain, while higher-income households would pay more.
But the PBO's conclusions on the economic impacts of the carbon tax have been challenged by Liberals and by climate think tanks, who claim that he failed to account for the costs of climate change.
The Canadian Climate Institute, for example, accused the PBO of being "misleading" for failing to consider "economic benefits of carbon pricing and the costs of climate inaction, both in terms of stabilizing the climate and competing in a global economy racing to net zero."
And this week, hundreds of economists signed an open letter saying that a national carbon price is the cheapest way to cut the most emissions, while disputing arguments put forward by Poilievre that the carbon price is driving up inflation and the cost of living.
Poilievre dismissed them as "so-called experts" and pledged to listen to the "common people."
However, the PBO did attempt to account for the cost of climate change in a report back in 2022. The analysis noted that extreme weather had already reduced Canada's GDP by 0.8 per cent between 1981 and 2021 and that the GDP could be further reduced by five per cent by 2100. But the report noted that "accounting for the cost of climate change is not straightforward."
Liberal MP Irek Kusmierczyk challenged Giroux this week and said it "boggles the mind" that the PBO did not account for the costs of climate change in its carbon tax analysis.
Giroux said that his mandate is to estimate the cost of government proposals, and encouraged other entities like think tanks or the government itself to disclose their numbers if they want to venture in a cost-benefit analysis of the carbon tax.
"To my knowledge, there hasn't been that much work undertaken on the cost-benefit analysis, because it's a complicated field that requires lots of data spanning several years," he said.
"So that's why even though it would be ideal to have the cost-benefit analysis, the benefits are not always very tangible, and not easily measurable." calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Rebates; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Think tanks; Cost benefit analysis; Costs; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Gross Domestic Product--GDP Cost benefit analysis Costs Environmental tax

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: parliamentary; budget; officer; giroux; taylor; swift

Document 246 of 281

Sudbury won't take no for an answer on The Spot; Letter To The Editor

Author: West, Jamie

Publication info: West, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-wont-take-no-answer-on-spot-letter-editor/docview/3030976331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+won%27t+take+no+for+an+answer+on+The+Spot%3B+Letter+To+The+Editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-04-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=West%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Supervised consumption site closing because the Ford government refuses to fund it Sadly, this weekend marks the closing of 'The Spot', Sudbury's only supervised consumption site. As we count our blessings, we must think of our friends, neighbours and loved ones who have been affected and may continue to be affected by the opioid crisis.
I want to be clear: 'The Spot'is closing because the Conservative government has continually chosen not to provide the provincial funding it requires while sitting on billions of taxpayer dollars. That decision will result in losing lives and overwhelm our EMS services.
We need wrap-around support, including addiction support, mental health support and housing. However, supervised consumption sites are a keystone in those services. Without overdose prevention, recovery would never be possible.
Supervised consumption services are grounded in nearly 40 years of evidence. There has never been a single death at one of these sites. They reduce police and ambulance calls, keep people out of busy hospitals, and offer vital support.
Despite all the research, proof and community support for these sites, Premier Doug Ford arbitrarily capped funding to only 21 supervised consumption sites. I'm not sure why, several years later, only 17 sites have received provincial funding. I do know that the Conservative government has chosen not to fund our city's supervised consumption site for two years and seven months.
This massive misstep and failure of judgment has cost us lives and continues to affect the mental health of our friends, family, and neighbours.
Healthcare is a provincial responsibility, but no funding for The Spot has ever come from the province. Instead, our supervised consumption site ran for 16 months without a single penny from the Conservative government. Our city council boldly decided to fund it (at a cost of $1 million) while waiting for Doug Ford to keep his promises.
When they couldn't afford to do it any longer, members of our community stepped forward for three months with generous donations. It should never have been this way. It's not the responsibility of our city to pay for this service, nor is it the responsibility of our neighbours and community businesses. This funding is a provincial responsibility. The decision to fund only one site in Northern Ontario (Thunder Bay) was a decision made by Doug Ford's Conservative government.
In Sudbury, we don't take no for an answer. From our cancer centre to our PET scanner, we continually prove to Ontario's governments that we are willing to fight for the provincial services we deserve! My fellow MPP France Gélinas (Nickel Belt) and I will continue to stand with you as we demand funding for our supervised consumption site.
NDP MPP Sudbury

Subject: Drug overdose; Mental health; Funding; Community support

Document 247 of 281

Health Sciences North leads the $100,000 club in Sudbury; Province releases its so-called Sunshine List; the top earner in Sudbury is the dean of the medical school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-sciences-north-leads-100-000-club-sudbury/docview/3020311749/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+Sciences+North+leads+the+%24100%2C000+club+in+Sudbury%3B+Province+releases+its+so-called+Sunshine+List%3B+the+top+earner+in+Sudbury+is+the+dean+of+the+medical+school&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 1,000 employees of Health Sciences North and the Health Sciences North Research Institute made $100,000 or more in 2023, according to Ontario's so-called Sunshine List that was released on Thursday.
The annual disclosure of public sector workers in Ontario who were paid more than $100,000 for 2023 had more than 300,000 names, including at least 4,500 who work in Sudbury.
The five top-paid public employees in the province are all at Ontario Power Generation, with the CEO earning $1.9 million last year.
Treasury Board President Caroline Mulroney said the largest year-over-year increases were in the hospitals, municipalities and post-secondary sectors.
OPG CEO Ken Hartwick is again in the top spot, and the Crown corporation's chief strategy officer, chief projects officer, chief operations officer and an executive vice-president are in the subsequent spots.
The presidents and CEOs of the Hospital for Sick Children and the University Health Network are next on the list, earning around $850,000 and the next slot is occupied by Phil Verster, the president and CEO of provincial transit agency Metrolinx.
Premier Doug Ford earned about $208,000 last year, less than several of his top staffers.
In Sudbury, some of the big earners included Dr. Robert Ohle, the vice-president of Academic and Research Impact at the Health Sciences North Research Institute. He made $318,130.75 last year.
Mark Hartman, Health Science North's senior vice-president of Patient Experience and Digital Transformation, made $303,000.
Dominic Giroux, Health Science North's former president chief executive officer, and took home $256,488 plus more than $6,000 in benefits, even though he worked only part of the year before leaving to take up a similar position with Hôpital Montfort in Ottawa on July 31, 2023.
Dr. Sarita Verma had the biggest salary among Northern Ontario School Of Medicine employees in 2023. Verma, the school's president, vice chancellor, dean and chief executive officer was paid $382,825.90. In all, 77 employees at NOSM were on the list. The medical school is based in Sudbury and Thunder Bay.
A total of 27 employees with Public Health Sudbury and Districts made the $100,000 club, led by Dr. Penny Sutcliffe, who took home $370,388.04 plus more than $8,000 in benefits.
As for the City of Greater Sudbury, 688 employees earned three figures in 2023. That includes members of the Greater Sudbury Police Service, whose budget falls under the purview of the municipality.
Topping the 2023 Sunshine List was CAO Ed Archer, who took home about $297,000, as well as a benefits package worth a little more than $11,000.
Next was Paul Pedersen, the police chief. He earned $281,000 and had a benefits package of more than $17,600.
Tony Cecutti, general manager of growth and infrastructure, rounded out the top three at about $247,000. His benefits package was worth about $9,680.
Steve Jacques, the GM of community development, earned about $246,500; while Kevin Fowke, the city's GM of corporate services, took home a little more than $245,800. Both had benefits packages worth more than $10,000.
Fourteen employees at Tom Davies Square earned more than $200,000 in 2023. Another 84 earned at least $150,000, including David Shelsted, director of engineering services. He took home about $171,000 last year.
Of the top 14, only two were women. Sharon Baiden, CAO of police services, earned about $236,000 in 2023, as well as benefits of $17,500. Her colleague, Sara Cunningham, deputy chief of police, took home a little more than $233,800. Cunningham had a benefits package valued at more than $18,000.
Mayor Paul Lefebvre earned $149,788 in 2023 and had a benefits package of about $10,700. Councillors are considered part-time and the highest earners usually make about $50,000, due to committee and board participation. The mayoral position, however, is considered full-time.
Laurentian University had 257 names on the list. The top earner there was Melissa Wiman, a sessional lecturer who made $226,892.47.
Cambrian College had 205 people who made more than $100,000 last year. Topping the list is Kristine Morrissey, the school's president, who earned $253,314.93 plus more than $7,000 in benefits.
College Boreal, which is based in Sudbury but has operations located all over Ontario, had 268 employees on the list. President Daniel Giroux made $274,042.72 in 2023.
The Rainbow District School Board recorded 670 names on the list, led by education director Bruce Bourget, who made $247,959.90 and more than $6,000 in benefits.
The Sudbury Catholic District School Board had 323 who made the Sunshine list. Its education director, Joanne Benard, made $234,845.27 a year ago.
At Conseil scolaire catholique Nouvelon, 376 of its employees are making $100,000 or more a year. Education director Paul Henry has the biggest salary, at $264,574.
At the Conseil scolaire public du Grand Nord de l'Ontario, education director Sébastien Fontaine earned $225,682.16 in 2023. A total of 200 employees were on the list.
Nine people from Science North made the list and its CEO, Ashley, Larose, was the top earner at $184,062.28.
The City Of Lakes Family Health Team has six on the list, led by executive director David Courtemanche at $124,606. Courtemanche is retiring.
Hundreds more provincial employees based in Sudbury are on the list, including those who work for different ministries, the Crown's office and as judges, such as Karen Lische, Regional Senior Judge with the Ontario Court Of Justice in Sudbury. She made $354,612.83. - with files from Canadian Press sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 248 of 281

Steelheads owner eager for return to Sudbury roots; Wolves facing Mississauga to open OHL playoffs for first time since 2019

Author: Leeson, Ben

Publication info: Leeson, Ben. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/steelheads-owner-eager-return-sudbury-roots/docview/3020311602/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Steelheads+owner+eager+for+return+to+Sudbury+roots%3B+Wolves+facing+Mississauga+to+open+OHL+playoffs+for+first+time+since+2019&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=Leeson%2C+Ben&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While the Sudbury Wolves'clash with the Mississauga Steelheads in the 2019 OHL playoffs certainly stirred up fond memories for Elliott Kerr, a rematch between the Central Division rivals this year may leave him feeling even more nostalgic.
Kerr is president of Landmark Sports Group, a company that provides athlete management, sports marketing and event management services, and he has owned the Steelheads since 2012.
The son of Bruce and Joyce Kerr is also a former Sudbury resident, having lived in the city from Grade 3 to 9 before his father took a job in southern Ontario.
While he didn't make the trip north for the last Sudbury-Mississauga series, which saw a Wolves team that featured the likes of Quinton Byfield and Ukko-Pekka Luukkonen win the best-of-seven set in four games, Kerr plans to be in the Nickel City on Wednesday, along with son Jamie and daughter Kerrin, 18-year-old twins.
"I'm pumped about my kids coming with me for Game 4," said Kerr, now 71, reached by The Sudbury Star earlier this week. "I'm going to show them around the homes I used to live in as a kid, so it's going to be a blast."
Game 1 of the Eastern Conference quarter-final was played at Paramount Fine Foods Centre in Mississauga on Thursday. Game 2, also at PFFC, will be Saturday at 4 p.m., before the series switches to Sudbury for Game 3 on Monday and Game 4 on Wednesday, both at 7:05 p.m.
While the Wolves, who were seeded fourth in the conference five years ago, and the Steelheads, who were fifth, featured no small amount of star power, both teams may now be even more promising in their own ways. Sudbury, despite finishing behind Mississauga this time by a single point, had the most offensively-potent squad in the league in 2023-24, with 328 goals in 68 games, and head coach Ken MacKenzie and company hovered near the top of the conference all season, before finding more mixed results down the stretch.
Kerr's club, meanwhile, was one of the league's more defensively sound entries during the regular season, with a goals-against total of 212 that was bettered only by the conference champions from Oshawa and London.
"I'll tell you, Sudbury's team is looking pretty strong," said Kerr, who complimented Wolves general manager Rob Papineau and owner Dario Zulich for their work in building the team.
"They're both great guys and it's going to be a fun series."
The Steelheads have the second-youngest team in the OHL on average, with only one of the 2004-born skaters who make up the core of most contenders, as head coach and GM James Richmond has assembled an impressive young group whose best days may well be in the future.
Make no mistake, however - Kerr and his staff are hoping to come out with a victory.
"This is a development year, but it's certainly more than that now," he said. "We're very confident about what we can do this year, not to mention the next two.
"Rob has done a great job of loading up with some pretty impressive talent for the run they want to go on, but as impressive as it is, we still want to knock them off."
Good as it was in 2019, Kerr believes the latest edition of the Steelheads sextet is deeper, more balanced and better equipped to match up with the Wolves.
The oldest of four children, Kerr moved to Sudbury with his family when his father, who worked for Canada Creosote, was transferred to northeastern Ontario.
Bruce Kerr later left the company for a job at Falconbridge Nickel Mines, where he stayed for several years. He also ran for the Progressive Conservatives in the 1965 federal election.
The family lived on Copper Cliff Road, now Lorne Street, and Kerr attended Gatchell Public School. When they moved to Boland Avenue, he attended Wembley Public School for grades 6, 7 and 8. He went to Lockerby Composite Secondary School for the ninth grade, before his father was hired by a construction company in St. Catharines.
Sports were an important part of Kerr's life from a young age. He was a member of the Idylwylde Golf and Country Club when it was still a nine-hole course and also played baseball. When the mercury dipped, he eagerly strapped on skates, grabbed a stick and made for the outdoor ice pad.
"I tell my kids and they don't believe me, but I played for Spiegel Playground and for Hillsdale Playground and those were all outdoor rinks," Kerr said with a chuckle. "There was a league."
He and his family also attended games at the Sudbury Community Arena, where the Sudbury Wolves senior team competed as a member of the Eastern Professional Hockey League and the Sudbury Cub Wolves vied for supremacy in the Northern Ontario junior league.
It was his own children's hockey careers, particularly his daughter's, who took precedence five years ago over a trip to Sudbury come playoff time. While Kerrin still plays at an elite level as a right-winger for Toronto Metropolitan University, there's a little more flexibility in the family's schedule this time around.
"From a family perspective, I'm really pumped about taking my kids and showing them around Sudbury," Kerr said. "It will be fun - very nostalgic." bleeson@postmedia.com twitter.com/ben_leeson

Document 250 of 281

Support for son does not meet his needs, Val Caron family says; Little Alex needs around-the-clock care and more support from the provincial government, his parents say

Author: Kruzel, Hugh

Publication info: Kruzel, Hugh. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/support-son-does-not-meet-his-needs-val-caron/docview/2986724322/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Support+for+son+does+not+meet+his+needs%2C+Val+Caron+family+says%3B+Little+Alex+needs+around-the-clock+care+and+more+support+from+the+provincial+government%2C+his+parents+say&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kruzel%2C+Hugh&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Val Caron family says they are overwhelmed by the pressures of setting up and monitoring private health - or self-directed - care for their son who needs around-theclock attention.
"People don't realize how little that funding is and how many hoops you have to jump through to qualify and receive support," said Tina Senior. "Instead of date nights, we spend Friday evenings doing paperwork or there is no money to cover for the hours we have already paid workers for."
Overages or submitting paperwork late becomes a family budget burden with no recourse: "Don't miss a deadline. There is no forgiveness," she said.
Often, respite workers don't show up or quit after a short time. This leaves this family shattered.
Because of administrative and system changes, Ontario families with health needs can become, in effect, employers. Direct funding, though, comes at a cost. It means they have to navigate hours of paperwork and recruit, train and schedule their respite workers.
Making an error in form-filling and the bureaucratic hammer comes down, she said.
Alex is the son of Senior and Daniel Brousseau. "He is almost seven years old," said Senior as she tenderly brushed his hair.
The crisis is not Alex's mobility - he uses a special wheelchair because he cannot walk - or that Alex's two brain hemispheres do not communicate.
Alex has Agenesis of the Corpus Callosum, meaning he needs a high level of care. Alex will never be independent.
Alex receives food, hydration and medication via a pump and gastrostomy tube, or G-tube.
It was an emotional visit to Val Caron where this family shared their story. "It has become impossible to balance all the needs," said Senior.
By speaking out, Senior and Brousseau worry they will face repercussions. They say the for-profit health-care system leaves them and families like them unable to get the care they need.
Their concerns, they say, fall on deaf ears. Going public with the frustrations of trying to get suitable care has come only after exhausting all other routes.
Adding to the burden has been finding daycare and then, as Alex became school-aged, before-and after-care. Even finding a school has been a battle.
All this meant many extra hours of driving for Tina Senior. When a day went sideways for Alex, his mum had to leave work and race to wherever Alex was.
"I had to drop everything and go to him. It throws your life into a tailspin."
Senior spent more than 20 years as a registered nurse and manager at Health Sciences North, which included looking after intensive care patients. Her days were stressful already and further impacted by surprise calls to Alex's side. She had to step down.
"I tried to be resilient ... I thought I could make it work. I had to quit. It was necessary for us to survive. It is a huge financial hit for the family. We will feel the consequences of that decision for the rest of our lives.
"All this comes at a cost to your personal well-being. He is my son and I will do anything for him. The love we have for our son is immeasurable. I will keep fighting for him."
She now has no benefits or pension for the future. Her full-time job is Alex. Together, Alex's mum and dad are his voice and advocates.
"Alex is vocal but non-verbal." It is the feeding tube procedure where the family hopes to see change. When Alex is at school, a qualified homecare person is required to hook up the nutrient bag, complete the feeding process and then dismantle the tube and pump.
A 1984 policy means school staff are not regarded as qualified to do this, so a nurse from Bayshore Home Health is supposed to carry out the tube-feeding procedures.
However, this personnel initiate the feeding and then leave, which concerns Alex's parents.
"The issue is that they would never stay for the duration of the visit," Senior said. "So they would set up the feed and even though they were getting paid to stay there and watch the treatment, or you know, supervise the treatment, they would go render care somewhere else, where they were also being paid, and then return back to the school."
Sometimes the pump would start and then stop. The implications for Alex meant he was neither nourished nor hydrated.
Nickel Belt MPP France Gélinas, the NDP's health critic, has been in Alex's corner, too. "This family came to me for help. They are courageous ... They worry if they complain about anything, they won't have any care at all. Today's provincial programs are failing more families than helping them.
"We are the only province that has privatized their homecare system, where big companies are often putting profit ahead of quality care. So in this particular case, it's Bayshore that has the contract to make sure that Alex gets fed and hydrated through a G-tube and they get paid an hour and a half to provide that care."
However, its staff don't stay to monitor the tube. Gélinas confirmed staff visit other patients during the 90 minutes of Alex's feeding.
Senior and Brousseau said they want to raise awareness of the hardships families like theirs live through. "We are just one small family, without much financial means," Senior said They believe others want to raise their voices, too. They want to see changes in how services are delivered and support improved so they aren't overwhelmed by paperwork, policies and lack of responses on top of the challenges they already face daily. GOVERNMENT DEFENDS PROGRAM In an emailed statement, a Bayshore official said that, "Unfortunately, we cannot comment due to patient confidentially. Please rest assured our local team in Sudbury is doing everything they can to support Alex and his family."
As for the provincial government, a spokesperson defended its record, saying it has "invested a historic $1 billion to expand the delivery of home and community care services to help more people connect to the care they need in the comfort of their own home. This includes accelerating investments as part of the 2023 Budget to bring funding up to $569 million.
"Our investments in home care are estimated to increase service volumes by 10 per cent this year alone. This investment in home and community care supports a range of programs, including Family-Managed Home Care programs, also known as Self-Directed Care."
In addition, the government said it has improved the wages of personal support workers and supported programs to train PSWs.
"This fall, our government also passed legislation to make it easier to find and navigate home care services and move seamlessly between providers throughout their health care journey and we will continue to work with our partners to build a more connected home care sector for Ontarians at every stage of life."
When it comes to Alex's feeding, the ministry noted the College of Nurses of Ontario's guidelines state "that a nurse may decide to assign responsibility for a particular aspect of care to another individual. Depending on the responsibilities of their positions, registered nurses, registered practical nurses or nurse practitioners with the required knowledge, skill and judgment may assign care to other nurses, unregulated care providers, or a person wanting to treat a member of their household. This might and can include assigning the administration of G-tube feeds." The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Document 251 of 281

$42M Spent On Firearms Confiscation Program; Four years since Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun law

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/42m-spent-on-firearms-confiscation-program-four/docview/2986722757/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%2442M+Spent+On+Firearms+Confiscation+Program%3B+Four+years+since+Liberals+announced+sweeping+changes+to+Canada%27s+gun+law&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four years after the Trudeau Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun laws, the government has so far spent $42 million on a federal firearms confiscation program that doesn't yet exist.
In a response to an order paper question filed by Sen. Don Plett in September, Public Safety Canada revealed that $41,904,556 has been spent so far on the government's "firearms buyback program," and that 60 department employees are working on the project.
"This is a boondoggle, and it hasn't even begun," Plett said Friday during the Senate question period, directing his question at government representative Sen. Marc Gold.
"How can your government have spent $42 million on this, when not a single firearm has been bought back?" As well, the documents reveal that IBM has been awarded a $2.27-million contract to develop, design and implement the program.
That figure is nearly double what that contract was worth when Public Safety Canada announced the firm's involvement in the program in 2020.
In the documents, the RCMP say it is managing a team of 15 full-time employees devoted to the gun grab.
As well, Service Canada has assigned two employees to the program, and a response from Public Services and Procurement Canada said it has devoted "the equivalent of 5.825 full-time employees" to the project.
Last April, the federal government commenced the first phase of the program by entering into a $700,000 agreement with the Canadian Sporting Arms and Ammunition Association to confiscate so-called "assault rifles" from retailers.
That program is expected to start later this year.
Work on Phase 2, which involves confiscating legally purchased firearms from their licensed owners, began earlier this year.
The government issued an invitation to qualify earlier this year, seeking vendors interested in collecting and destroying the over 1,500 gun models summarily banned by the federal government under the May 1, 2020 order-in-council.
That order-in-council instantly reclassified around 1,500 popular firearm models to prohibited status, including so-called "military-style" or "assault" firearms.
Initially meant to happen by April 2022, amnesty for licensed owners was extended until Oct. 23, 2023, and again until 2025.
The government estimates that between 10,000 and 15,000 newly prohibited firearms are in the hands of Canadian businesses, and anywhere between 125,000 to 200,000 are owned by licensed individuals.
"These estimates could vary as the number of affected non-restricted firearms is unknown," the invitation remarked.
As many crime guns are committed with prohibited firearms smuggled into Canada from the United States - and therefore unlawful for Canadian licence-holders to own - critics say the government is preoccupying itself with an expensive program whose affect on Canada's crime epidemic will be negligible.
National Post contacted Public Safety Canada for comment.
Firearms policy expert Tim Thurley said that with such an unprecedented program, there's no question it will be expensive.
"Consider, for example, the logistics of confiscating firearms in rural and remote areas, where some of these firearms - such as the Ruger Mini-14 - were widely used by hunters, farmers and recreational or competitive sport shooters," he said.
"Expect the costs to climb significantly further as the business and personal stages of the program are implemented and as more firearms are prohibited."
He said he expects the cost to stretch into the billions.
Simon Fraser University Prof.
Gary Mauser mused that nearly $42 million is a lot of money to spend for what he said amounts to "grandstanding" by the federal government.
"Ottawa is not having much success with their efforts on this file," he said.
"Ontario has refused to co-operate with future confiscations. Saskatchewan has asked to join the appeal. Requests for co-operating organizations have fallen flat."
The fact that the now-banned guns sat safely with their owners without incident for the past four years suggests the 2020 bans - enacted after the 2020 mass shooting in Nova Scotia - were introduced for reasons other than just public safety, Mauser said.
Last October, National Post learned Quebec-based anti-gun group PolySeSouvient was so angry that mandatory confiscation wasn't part of the government's gun control scheme it disinvited Prime Minister Justin Trudeau from future commemorations of the 1989 École Polytechnique shooting.
The government added a confiscation program later that year.
Questions are still swirling around Parliament Hill over how much the confiscation will ultimately cost taxpayers.
Government documents obtained by National Post say costs could reach as high as $2 billion, far greater than election promises suggesting the program would only cost between $400 million and $600 million.
Tracey Wilson, spokesperson for the Canadian Coalition for Firearm Rights (CFFR), told National Post that skyrocketing costs so far don't bode well for the program's future.
"Not only has not a single firearm been confiscated, the program itself doesn't even exist - not even for willing retailers anxious to off-load unsellable inventory," she said.
"The whole premise of the 2020 gun ban was that these firearms, previously approved for sporting and hunting purposes by the RCMP lab, were simply too dangerous to own. Yet four years later, they are all exactly where they've been for decades, if not generations - in the gun safes of licensed, RCMP-vetted gun owners." National Post

Subject: Bans; Licenses; Mass murders; Public safety; Costs; Employees; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Employees
