Document 252 of 281

N.B. premier shows the way; Editorial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/n-b-premier-shows-way-editorial/docview/2973019658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=N.B.+premier+shows+the+way%3B+Editorial&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's provincial government tabled its sixth consecutive balanced budget on Tuesday, which is virtually unheard of in Canada. This is a fiscal approach residents shouldn't take for granted, and policymakers across the country should adopt.
Since taking office in 2018, Premier Blaine Higgs has been a light of responsible, centre-right governance. He is not a flashy leader; rather, he's measured, determined and disciplined. His Progressive Conservatives have focused on stabilizing the province's precarious finances - turning New Brunswick from a warning sign to a success story.
These are real accomplishments. Perhapsa the biggest indicator is the parliamentary budget officer's judgment that New Brunswick's finances are now sustainable over the long term - one of only five provinces to get this nod.
Change did not happen overnight. Instead, Higgs gradually improved the balance sheet and paid down debt - creating about $100 million of new fiscal room and reducing the impact of punishing debt-servicing charges. More than $2.3 billion in surpluses have accumulated since the PCs took power, according to Public Accounts. (For context, New Brunswick's entire budget comes in at about $13 billion.) This is a stark contrast to the irresponsibility we've seen in Ottawa, where the federal government has moved its goalposts over and over again to justify ever-higher spending. This year's deficit is expected to reach nearly $50 billion.
It's the kind of policy-making that will saddle future generations with the effects of today's bad decisions. New Brunswick seems to get this point in a way few other provinces do.
Higgs is not without his critics on the left, who point out that surpluses could support higher social spending and that revenue increases owe to higher federal transfers.
Yet these ideas are exactly what got New Brunswick into trouble in the first place. The Liberals'"see a dollar, spend a dollar" routine sent the province careening toward a fiscal cliff, until cooler heads decided to break that cycle.
There have also been right-leaning critics who want Higgs to make substantive reforms. While the premier has delivered modest income tax relief, he hasn't slashed tax rates for a simple reason: it would be premature to do so with so much economic uncertainty, including low growth, high interest rates and inflation still running relatively high.
To be sure, deeper cuts will eventually be needed to make New Brunswick competitive. But the premier is putting his province on a fiscal footing to be able to afford them, without plunging back into deficit.
Others have criticized Higgs's social policy agenda, specifically around gender policies in public schools. But here, too, we find his approach to be the opposite of extreme.
His government's reforms have observed a common-sense distinction between older children aged 16 and up - who can choose to use a different name and pronoun in school - and those who are younger. The latter group needs parental consent to go ahead with such changes.
Far from being a revolution, this is a good balance between individual students'freedoms and parental rights. It's a dose of maturity and reason in a highly emotional, sometimes hysterical culture war.
This is the kind of leadership we need: not only in Ottawa, but in all our provincial capitals. New Brunswick's example shows that even small, less-advantaged provinces can get ahead through good policy. It's an approach that deserves emulation to get Canada back on track. Postmedia News

Subject: Provinces; Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: provincial; government; tabled; sixth; consecutive; balanced

Title: N.B. premier shows the way; Editorial

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: B.5

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 21, 2024

Section: Canada

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08392544

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Doug Ford on Ottawa's carbon tax: 'Don't know what the guy is smoking up there'

Author: Williams, Brian; Initiative reporter

Document 255 of 281

Patients without a family doctor to double in Sudbury, northeast by 2026; 'We know we can reverse this trend, but the province needs to see this as a major health care crisis'

Author: Stradiotto, Laura

Publication info: Stradiotto, Laura. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/patients-without-family-doctor-double-sudbury/docview/2955977018/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Patients+without+a+family+doctor+to+double+in+Sudbury%2C+northeast+by+2026%3B+%27We+know+we+can+reverse+this+trend%2C+but+the+province+needs+to+see+this+as+a+major+health+care+crisis%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Stradiotto%2C+Laura&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New data from the Ontario College of Family Physicians shows that the number of residents without a family doctor in Sudbury, Manitoulin Island and Parry Sound will nearly double in the next two years.
According to the association, there are currently 32,000 people without a family physician in the jurisdiction encompassing the three above-mentioned communities and without additional support from the provincial government, that number is expected to grow to 62,900 by 2026.
"Ontario is facing a major healthcare crisis right now," said Dave Courtemanche, executive director of the City of Lakes Family Health Team and board member of the Ontario College of Family Physicians. "There's an exodus of physicians leaving family medicine right across Ontario."
Many physicians have reported an overwhelming administrative burden - spending 19 hours a week on administrative work - along with a lack of team supports and compensation that has not kept up with inflation.
Courtemanche called the numbers "staggering" and the situation "dire" as 2.3 million Ontarians are currently without a family doctor and that number will grow to 4.4 million by 2026.
He said the situation in Northern Ontario is far worse than southern Ontario. For example, Sault Ste. Marie's Group Health Centre announced earlier this year that 10,000 patients will lose access to their family doctor as of May 31, while another 6,000 are at risk of losing access later this year.
Courtemanche said the situation in Hearst is no better, with 55 per cent of residents in that community without a family physician.
The Ontario College of Family Physicians has outlined areas that need to be immediately addressed to reverse the trend.
"We need to retain the doctors we have and recruit the doctors of the future as well," said Courtemanche. "When you talk to family physicians across Ontario, what you hear is that they care deeply for their patients and want to be there for them but there are factors that are contributing to their practice."
Those 19 hours spent a week on administrative work means doctors are looking at "paper not patients," he said.
Courtemanche said the province can offer simple solutions here, such as eliminating employer-mandated sick notes and modernizing outdated referral systems.
Additionally, he said family physicians are essentially running a business and are struggling to keep up with operating costs, as well as not being compensated for their work. New family physicians want to practice with the support of teams, with registered and practical nurses and social workers, and he said the province needs to better support such team-based primary care. Courtemanche said that only last week did the City of Lakes Family Health Team in Greater Sudbury learn it would receive an operational funding boost after years of no increase. Still, he said it doesn't properly address compensating health care providers, such as registered nurses, who are paid less in primary care than, for example, in a hospital-based setting or public health.
Courtemanche said another important player in solving the province's health care crisis is better support for medical schools like the Northern Ontario School of Medicine University, which is based in Sudbury and Thunder Bay.
"The province of Ontario needs to partner with NOSM and they need to invest more in family medicine training for Northern Ontario," he said.
Ultimately, it must become more attractive for medical students to pursue family medicine, he said.
"Here in Northern Ontario, the situation is dire," said Courtemanche "The province needs to work closely with NOSM to address the issues facing communities across Northern Ontario."
NOSM understands the health care needs of rural and Northern Ontario and "they have the solutions in making this more attractive for medical students."
Despite the grim forecast, Courtemanche is optimistic the trend can be reversed.
"We know we can reverse this trend, but the province needs to see this as a major health-care crisis, understand there's an exodus of family physicians and take urgent action," he said.
"But the province has to get moving now. They have to take substantial action to address the issues that family physicians have voiced in terms of what is hurting them in their practices and preventing them from providing the kind of care they want to provide to their patients."
Hannah Jensen, spokesperson for the Minister of Health Sylvia Jones, outlined a number of initiatives the province is pursuing to address the family physician situation, as well to improve access to primary care in Ontario.
Jensen said that under Premier Doug Ford, the government has added more than 10,400 new physicians since 2018, which includes a 10 per cent increase in family doctors, by adding hundreds of undergraduate and medical seats across the province, with 60 per cent of seats specifically for family medicine.
She notes that at NOSM, more than half of residency students are studying family medicine. The province is also looking to add another 50 new physicians in the province this year through the Practice Ready Ontario Program, which helps internationally and interprovincially educated healthcare workers transition to work in Ontario.
She said Ontario is also "expanding interprofessional primary care teams by investing $110 million, triple the original amount set out in our 23-24 budget to create and expand 78 primary care teams, that will ensure 98 per cent of Ontarians are connected to a primary care provider over the next several years."
This includes an expansion at Centre de Santé Univi Health Centre, located in Alban, in the community of French River.
When it comes to the administrative burden noted by family physicians, she said the province established the Bilateral Burnout Task Force by improving OMA-endorsed priority government forms.
"Our work has significantly accelerated work to simplify forms and we look forward to sharing the improvements that have been made in the near future," Jensen said. "As of Oct. 12, the Ministry of Health is in negotiations with the Ontario Medical Association to determine the physician services agreement. We look forward to further discussions at the table with the OMA."
In addition, the province has launched the initiative called Patients Before Paper Work (PB4P) to "further tackle the administrative burden on physicians while reducing the risk of delays of delays in diagnosis and treatment."
The program includes initiatives like eReferral, which was designed "to tackle referrals, consults, prescriptions and central intake while beginning the process to Axe the Fax, replacing fax machines with a digital communications alternative at all Ontario health care providers," she said. The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar

Subject: Health care access; Primary care; Medicine; Medical students; Teams; Physicians

Document 256 of 281

Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-at-queens-park-show-ignorance-on-guns/docview/2955976443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+at+Queen%27s+Park+show+ignorance+on+guns+and+justice&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you ever needed proof that Liberals don't understand gun crime, gun control or gun policy beyond using it to scare people, Stephen Blais is walking proof.
The MPP for Orleans in Ottawa stood up to try and make a point this week on guns and judges getting tough on crime and utterly failed.
The Liberals at Queen's Park have been trying to make hay out of the fact that Premier Doug Ford appointed two people who used to work for him to the panel that vets lawyers seeking judicial appointments. Imagine that, the party that won the election appointing people who philosophically agree with them to advisory roles in the government.
This is a complete non-scandal because not only does every party do this, but they should do this. You advance the agenda you were elected on by relying on those who agree with you to get things done.
No matter that the previous Ontario Liberal government did this or that the federal Liberal government that Blais supports does this, he and his party want to make this an issue.
On Wednesday though, Blais decided to go a step further and point out that Matt Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff who is now chair of the judicial appointments advisory committee, is also registered to lobby for a gun manufacturer.
"How can we believe the premier's tough-oncrime stance when he's asked the guy who sells the guns to appoint the judges?" Blais asked during question period.
And with that, he showed everyone that he knows absolutely nothing about guns.
Bondy is indeed a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer, a company out of Kitchener called Colt Canada that only makes and sells weapons to the Canadian military and law enforcement. In fact, Colt's only reason to exist with their facility in Kitchener is so that Canada has a secure domestic supply of rifles and other small arms for the Canadian military.
If Blais walked into his local gun store, he wouldn't be able to buy anything from Colt Canada. They make some fantastic firearms, but they do not sell into the retail market.
Nor are they guns found in the hands of criminals on the streets of Ottawa. No one is doing a drug deal with a C7 rifle, a C20 sniper rifle or Colt's EAGLE single-shot grenade launcher.
These are products designed for and sold to the Canadian military and police services across the country.
Colt produces the guns used by the guys who arrest the bad guys with guns. But why let the truth get in the way of scaring people? Doug Ford has expressed his frustration with judges and justices of the peace giving light sentences or letting repeat violent gun offenders out on bail for years now. This isn't a new thing for him, he's been saying this since before he was elected premier.
But now that he's appointed two people who used to work with him, and who agree with his judicial philosophy, to the panel that screens judges and the Liberals, the NDP and much of the media are losing their collectivist little minds.
Why? It's not because Liberals don't appoint like-minded people when they are in office, it's that they do it and claim they are all being non-partisan and neutral when in reality, they aren't. Ford is being upfront in what he wants.
"I'm proud to go out there and tell the people who are seeing violence in their homes, violence on the streets, violence in our subways, that we're going to get judges that are actually going to keep these criminals in jail," Ford said in response to Blais'bizarre question.
Ask the public what they care about, it isn't lobbyists, it's keeping criminals behind bars. That's something Blais'Liberal Party has been against for years. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Violence; Judicial appointments; Firearms

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: needed; proof; liberals; understand; crime; control

Tough times at Sudbury's hospital; Letters to the editor

I believe the only choice is byelections in both wards

Author: Peach, Arthur

Publication info: Peach, Arthur.

Document 259 of 281

Pharmacare a scheme to keep power

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-scheme-keep-power/docview/2933296439/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+a+scheme+to+keep+power&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is pharmacare doomed to fail? Last week, Quebec announced it would opt out of the Liberal-NDP plan for a national pharmacare program. This week, it was Alberta's turn. Health Minister Adriana LaGrange blasted the lack of provincial consultation and accused the federal government of overstepping its jurisdiction.
"We're not going to allow Ottawa to pick and choose what coverage should be available to Albertans based on issues they find politically palatable."
Instead of taking orders from Ottawa, Alberta will take the cash.
"Give us the dollars," LaGrange said. "Allow us to enhance the programs we actually have now, rather than create more bureaucracy."
In response, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked Quebec Premier François Legault and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
"I'm not surprised at the Conservative governments that have cut investments in health care. These are the same premiers that, when you look at their province, they made massive cuts that hurt workers and patients."
Ah, the C-word. Never mind that Legault's government doesn't go by that name: it's a convenient excuse to attack the ideology, and by extension the federal Conservatives as well. Singh is eager to savour his pharmacare "victory" and position the NDP as the true alternative to the Tories. And since he has as much a chance of gaining seats in Quebec as he does in Alberta, bashing those two governments is a low-risk strategy.
But the NDP aren't the only ones playing politics with this deal. The Liberals are as well, starting with the choice of contraceptive and diabetes medication as the first drugs to be covered.
Contraception is a relatively low-cost item for the feds: it's already fully covered in B.C. and Manitoba, and partly covered in Ontario. Most private health plans - which four in five Canadians have - pay for it as well. More importantly, the coverage appeals to a key voter demographic - women - the Liberals need to get back. The latest polls show that 36 per cent would vote Conservative, compared to 26 per cent who would vote Liberal.
As for diabetes, that is also a numbers game.
Nearly 10 per cent of Canadians - six million people - suffer from the disease, with an additional six million classified as "pre-diabetic." Rates are even higher among seniors (20 per cent) and First Nations populations on reserve (17 per cent). Almost everyone knows someone with diabetes and knows that insulin is a lifesaver.
But that doesn't mean you need universal pharmacare to cover people who can't afford it. It's like bringing in a $27-billion dollar bazooka to kill a fly. A study released last week by the Montreal Economic Institute found only 2.8 per cent of Canadians currently have no drug coverage whatsoever. Twenty-five million people have private insurance, and existing provincial plans subsidize or cover a host of medications for persons who do not, including low-income patients and seniors.
The study also confirmed that imposing a single-payer universal drug insurance plan would jeopardize the quality of coverage for at least 21.5 million people. That's because a single-payer model would not fund many medications currently covered by private plans, including innovative medicines.
Based on the experiences of New Zealand, cutting-edge drugs won't be introduced into the Canadian market at all, because a universal plan would deem them too costly. A 2019 report commissioned by Medicines New Zealand ranked the country last for access to medicines and pharmaceutical investment among 20 OECD nations. Of 304 medicines introduced between 2011 and 2017, New Zealand funded only 17. Life-saving cancer therapies and other innovative medicines were rendered unavailable.
In other words, national single-payer pharmacare will consign Canadians to the same problems as national single-payer medicare: lack of choice, rationing of care and worse outcomes. Instead of keeping what works and fixing what's broken, the NDP chose to score political points, and the Liberals bought themselves another year in power.
So kudos to Alberta and Quebec for standing up and opting out. Let's hope other provinces join them and expose this deal for what it is: a political game that leaves patients in the cold. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Diabetes; Canadians; Indigent care

Location: New Zealand; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: pharmacare; doomed; quebec; announced; national; program

Title: Pharmacare a scheme to keep power

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 29, 2024

Document 260 of 281

Universities, colleges face hard choices

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/universities-colleges-face-hard-choices/docview/2933295693/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Universities%2C+colleges+face+hard+choices&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The good news for parents and students: Tuition at Ontario's colleges and universities won't be going up for the next three years. The bad news for the province's universities and colleges is they won't be getting as much money as they hoped for.
Minister Jill Dunlop announced this week the government of Doug Ford was putting $1.3 billion in new funding into the post-secondary education sector.
Questioned time and again about other issues, Dunlop repeated the government was investing $1.3 billion. It was as if she thought the journalists present hadn't heard her many pronouncements on the matter previously.
The news of the investment comes after a blue-ribbon panel appointed by the Ford government suggested last November tuition should go up, and urged the province to increase funding by $2.5 billion during three years. Instead, the province is offering no tuition increase and just $1.3 billion.
Opposition parties were quick to denounce the move as not enough.
"What has been promised today by the government comes nowhere close to what the blue-ribbon panel recommended was needed," NDP critic Peggy Sattler said.
Yet, while denouncing the government for not following the blue-ribbon panel, Sattler was quick to endorse the tuition freeze.
"We absolutely support a tuition freeze. Students are suffering from the cost-ofliving crisis the same way that everyone else in Ontario is," she said.
Liberal critic Adil Shamji made similar complaints, that the government didn't listen to its own panel on increased funding, while also saying he supports the tuition freeze the blue-ribbon panel opposed. That panel had called for a five per cent tuition hike starting in September, followed by automatic tuition hikes of two per cent a year afterward.
That wouldn't help anyone deal with affordability issues.
"I will not apologize for continuing to freeze tuition in this province," Dunlop said. "Let me take you back to 2019, when Ontario had the highest tuition in all of Canada."
Now, three provinces have higher university tuition than Ontario, while seven provinces have higher college tuition.
Governing is about choices, and Ford has decided raising tuition rates right now isn't the best option. Could he have given the universities and colleges more money? Obviously, he could have, and Sattler made a solid argument that if the province is going to freeze tuition, they need to offer more subsidies.
But here we are, back to choices, and is giving colleges and universities ever-increasing amounts of money a good thing? It is worth asking whether we need to keep every single university and college in this province. In 2022-23, seven of Ontario's 24 colleges had more international students, paying higher fees, than they had domestic students.
That included Sarnia's Lambton College with 82 per cent international students, Northern College in Timmins with about 80 per cent, North Bay's Canadore College at 72 per cent and Conestoga College in Kitchener-Waterloo at 71 per cent. This is part of a pattern, that despite NDP and Liberal claims, isn't all Doug Ford's fault; it started about a decade ago and really took off in around 2016.
There is no doubt the Ford government has let this get out of control. Not that Dunlop would admit that Monday; in fact, she dodged question after question, putting all the blame on Ottawa.
The reality is they are both to blame, but so are the schools which became dependent on the higher tuition paid by foreign students and now want the government at Queen's Park to bail them out.
That shouldn't happen. Governing is about choices; so, too, is running a major college or university.
Ford has given families what they need. He's given the colleges and universities some of what they asked for, but not all of it. It's time for those in charge of these institutions to start making hard choices.
It won't be pretty, but it is necessary. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 262 of 281

Supreme Court should have decided Bill 124 case

Author: Lille y, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supreme-court-should-have-decided-bill-124-case/docview/2927051938/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+should+have+decided+Bill+124+case&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is little doubt the fate of Bill 124 should have been decided by the Supreme Court of Canada.
The Ontario Court of Appeal issued a split decision Monday, finding the law legislating wage increases was, in part, unconstitutional.
But rather than appeal to the Supreme Court, the cowards in the government of Doug Ford announced they will repeal the wage-cap legislation imposed on public sector workers.
Justice Lise Favreau, writing the majority opinion in the Court of Appeal's 2-1 decision, twisted herself into bizarre positions to arrive at her final conclusion, one that leaves more questions than answers.
Thanks to the court, Bill 124 is invalid as far as unionized workers of the Ontario public service are concerned, but valid for non-unionized workers. The challenge to the law was brought forward by several unions, which argued Bill 124 violated their charter-protected right to collective bargaining.
"The rights protected by s. 2(d) of the charter do not apply in the same way to non-represented employees and accordingly, the Act is only unconstitutional in so far as it applies to the represented employees covered by the Act," Favreau wrote.
About 82 per cent of the Ontario public service is covered by a union, roughly 530,000 people, according to the Financial Accountability Office, while roughly 120,000 are non-union employees.
The government argued that Bill 124 had been necessary to control wages at a time when Ontario's finances were in a horrible position. The province's net debt-to-GDP ratio was above 40 per cent and more than eight per cent of the province's budget was being spent on debt charges.
Favreau ruled the fiscal restraint created by the bill could have been achieved "through good faith bargaining" and, despite the fiscal situation of the province, there was no evidence that "expediency" was required.
That's a claim Justice William Hourigan, writing a dissenting opinion, found nearly laughable.
"If a government sees an economic cliff on the horizon, courts should not require it to wait till the last moment to act," Hourigan wrote.
He was also highly critical of a statement by Favreau that she was striking the law down because, "many of whom are women, racialized and/or low-income earners, have lost the ability to negotiate for better compensation or even better work conditions."
"There are many issues to unpack in this comment. However, I will restrict myself to reminding my colleagues that 'women, racialized and/or low-income earners'pay taxes in this province, and they, too, have an interest in ensuring the responsible management of the province's finances and the protection of sustainable public services," Hourigan said.
Laws restricting wage settlements for government employees have survived many court challenges in the past. The Manitoba government passed a similar law in 2017 and had it struck down in court, but an Appeals Court overturned that ruling, declaring the law valid in 2021.
When unions tried to appeal to the Supreme Court, the justices declined to hear the case.
When the government of Stephen Harper brought in the Expenditure Restraint Act in response to the 2008 financial crisis, the law was challenged several times on similar grounds and upheld. There was a good chance Favreau's decision would be overturned if Ford's government appealed to the Supreme Court.
Citing the Expenditure Restraint Act cases, Favreau said, in that instance, the law was justified due to the economic crisis but in the case of Bill 124, things weren't bad enough to enact such measures. She was effectively saying the Ford government could have restricted wages, and it would have been constitutional, they just did it wrong.
Given the weak ruling by Favreau and the fact it contradicts existing precedent from the Supreme Court and is at odds with a recent Manitoba decision, the Ford government should have appealed. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nonunion; Expenditures; Collective bargaining; Employees; Low income groups; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Collective bargaining Employees Wages & salaries

Document 263 of 281

Public health officials in Sudbury, Sault areas facing a 'big decision'; Provincial government is encouraging health units to merge and have until Feb. 20 to make a decision

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-health-officials-sudbury-sault-areas/docview/2922712515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+health+officials+in+Sudbury%2C+Sault+areas+facing+a+%27big+decision%27%3B+Provincial+government+is+encouraging+health+units+to+merge+and+have+until+Feb.+20+to+make+a+decision&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Greater Sudbury will know later this month what public health could look like in the coming years.
Algoma Public Health and Public Health Sudbury and Districts are currently in the midst of merger discussions, and their boards are considering all the pros and cons of amalgamation.
While Greater Sudbury's city council has not voted on a merger - the topic has not been broached yet in chambers - city council in Sault Ste. Marie has rejected the idea.
Sault Ste. Marie councillors last week unanimously passed a resolution opposing a merger between Algoma Public Health and Public Health Sudbury and Districts.
Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Minister of Health, indicated more details would be provided in the future.
"Details regarding our government's historic expansion of interdisciplinary primary care teams will be available in the coming weeks," she said in an email. "As stated in the news release, this expansion in addition to the changes our government has made to add more health care workers to our workforce, including new undergraduate and residency seats at NOSM, with 60% of new seats reserved specifically for family medicine, will ensure 98% of Ontarians have a primary care provider over the next several years- the current percentage is 90%, the highest in Canada."
Regarding nurse practitioner clinics broadly, she said the province has invested nearly $50 million each year to connect hundreds of thousands of Ontarians to primary care through 25 nurse practitioner-led clinics across the province. "Ontario is the first jurisdiction in Canada to adopt this model," she said.
Nickel Belt MPP France Gelinas, who is also the NDP's health critic, reiterated her party's stance that the province is funnelling public dollars into private health care.
"At the 40,000-feet level, we have a government that is really, really trying hard to bring private health care into Ontario," said Gelinas. "If enough people are not having the public system meet their needs, then they come to a point and say they are Ok with the privatization of health care, they don't want to wait three years for hip surgery or wait 18 months for their child to see a mental health provider, etc."
Gelinas said there is "untapped opportunity" in Capreol and rural Northern communities.
"I have 40,000 people in Nickel Belt who do not have access to primary care," she said. Yet "we have a nurse-practitioner clinic in Capreol that is able to recruit and has nurse practitioners underemployed right now, who would love to take on another 1,000 patients and give them access to primary care."
Gelinas said she's not suggesting a multi-million-dollar solution. "We are talking hundreds of thousands of dollars (that) would make a huge difference in the lives of thousands of people who would gain access to a primary care provider ..." she said.
"It just breaks my heart that we have a government that is so callously putting people's health and lives at risk." The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca As one Sault city councillor noted, the distance covered by a merged health unit would include 700 km east to west and about 5,000 km from Chapleau to Manitoulin Island.
But ultimately, it may not matter how councils vote.
"A municipality cannot order a board of health in any way because the board is independent," said Rene Lapierre, chair of the board of health in Sudbury. "The Sault Ste. Marie resolution they passed, I understand why they're concerned about it" Lapierre said resolutions approving or rejecting the merger "might be a little premature because not all the facts and details" are known at this time.
But he said there are options available to both boards, should they choose not to proceed with a merger.
"This isn't a decision to merge 100 per cent," Lapierre said. "There are still off-ramps for boards to take, so even if Algoma says yes, let's go to the next steps, and Sudbury says yes, we submit that to the government. There is still another off-ramp. This is one more step, but not the final step."
The boards of both health units will meet in-camera later this month to determine next steps on a potential merger.
"Right now, we're doing our due diligence with regards to exploration, so comparing apples to apples," said Dr. Penny Sutcliffe, the medical officer of health in the Sudbury area. "What we're also trying to do is map out what a future state would look like if we were merged."
While Sutcliffe qualifies the merger as voluntary, the provincial government began discussing the idea in 2019 - in a not-so-voluntary way.
At that time, the Conservatives attempted to squeeze all the health units in northeastern Ontario into a single agency. It was part of a bigger plan to merge 35 health units throughout Ontario into 10 bodies; however, that plan was abandoned when COVID-19 surfaced.
"They would still like to see a reduction in the number of public health units across the province," Lapierre said. "Now they're taking a softer approach."
Lapierre said the Conservatives have even offered funding to health units that choose to merge.
"That wasn't the case in 2019, but since they're offering to pay for some of the (exploration) work, we felt it was prudent to truly have a look, rather than digging in our heels and saying no," he explained.
While there are financial incentives for health units that merge, Lapierre said that is not the impetus for Public Health Sudbury and Districts considering amalgamation. But he said there could be harmonization of some backof-house operations, including IT services.
"I don't think we'll find a million dollars in savings by doing that, but there could be a more streamlined approach to those things," he said.
Sutcliffe said her primary concern is the delivery of care and programs.
She said one of the goals of Queen's Park is to "increase the capacity of public health programs and services in their respective health units."
She said she wants to ensure there is no "erosion" of services, especially where they pertain to rural, Francophone and Indigenous communities.
She is also cognizant of the tight timelines the government has imposed.
"It's too early to tell (how a merged health unit may function), but things are going so fast it won't be that long before the boards will be needing to make a decision," she said.
The future of Algoma Public Health and PHSD should be made clear by the end of this month, as Feb. 20 is the "go/no-go" date, Lapierre added.
At that point, if the boards decide to merge, they will present a proposal to the provincial government.
"If everything happens, the government would like the official merger to occur by name, on paper, by Jan. 1, 2025, and then a slow integration after that," Lapierre said. "There's still lots of time for discussion and other decision points."
Sutcliffe is finishing her career soon - she begins a year-long sabbatical next month that will usher her into retirement. But after steering the ship at Public Health for more than 20 years, she said "this is, far and away, the biggest transformation or potential for change that I've seen in my tenure. Darn right, the boards are taking this seriously and taking their time to consider what is in the best interests of the enterprise of public health. This is a very big decision." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Document 264 of 281

People are 'coming in and begging for us to take them as patients'; Pressure on health-care system could be eased withprovincial funding

Author: Stradiotto, Laura

Publication info: Stradiotto, Laura. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/people-are-coming-begging-us-take-them-as/docview/2922712483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=People+are+%27coming+in+and+begging+for+us+to+take+them+as+patients%27%3B+Pressure+on+health-care+system+could+be+eased+withprovincial+funding&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Stradiotto%2C+Laura&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nurse practitioners could help ease the burden on Greater Sudbury's health care system - if only the province better supported NP clinics, say advocates.
The Capreol Nurse Practitioner-Led Clinic hasn't received any operational funding increase since it opened its doors in 2012, according to the clinic's executive director, Amanda Rainville, who also works as a nurse practitioner there.
"We actually had a budget decrease in 2019 of $44,000 and since 2020, none of our staff have had any increases in their wages," said Rainville.
There are no family physicians or walk-in clinics in Capreol. The closest family physician is located in Hanmer and the doctor there, Dr Nicole Desmarais, is already at full capacity. In fact, Greater Sudbury is in need of 33 family doctors, according to HealthForceOntario, Ontario Health's recruitment agency that posts job openings for physicians across the province.
The Capreol NP-led team, consisting of nurse practitioners, registered and registered practical nurses, a social worker and a dietitian, serves 3,200 patients in the community and surrounding area. Its patients are predominantly seniors living with multiple comorbidities.
Rainville said that even though the clinic cannot accept more patients, there is a waiting list of at least 600 people.
To be able to take in those patients and more, she said the clinic needs about $350,000 to hire four more employees: two more nurse practitioners, one social worker, one registered practical nurse and another administrative staff member.
"We're getting burnt out and it's frustrating for our admin staff because patients are coming in and begging for us to take them as patients, but we don't have the capacity to do so," said Rainville. "It's difficult to see the patients we have right now because they are quite complex; so we can't take on new ones. And it's difficult to retain staff when we haven't had any increases in the last four years and other sectors are paying much higher - at least 15 to 30 per cent higher than what we can pay with our funding."
Rainville said the clinic has applied for funding as part of the province's effort to better connect patients to primary care but has not received a response.
"We are hopeful but not optimistic that we will be one of the clinics that will be funded," said Rainville.
Last week, Health Ontario announced it was investing $110 million to help connect Ontarians to primary care teams. Included in the announcement was a promise to add more than 400 primary care providers as part of 78 new and expanded interprofessional primary care teams, as well as an additional $20 million to provide a "boost" to all existing primary care teams to help them meet increased operational costs.
The Capreol clinic was not included in the initial allotment of funds.
The Sudbury Star contacted Ontario Health to ask what the recent funding announcement meant for Greater Sudbury residents without access to primary care. Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for the Minister of Health, indicated more details would be provided in the future. "Details regarding our government's historic expansion of interdisciplinary primary care teams will be available in the coming weeks," she said in an email.
"As stated in the news release, this expansion in addition to the changes our government has made to add more health care workers to our workforce, including new undergraduate and residency seats at NOSM, with 60% of new seats reserved specifically for family medicine, will ensure 98% of Ontarians have a primary care provider over the next several years- the current percentage is 90%, the highest in Canada."
Regarding nurse practitioner clinics broadly, she said the province has invested nearly $50 million each year to connect hundreds of thousands of Ontarians to primary care through 25 nurse practitioner- led clinics across the province. "Ontario is the first jurisdiction in Canada to adopt this model," she said. Nickel Belt MPP France Gelinas, who is also the NDP's health critic, reiterated her party's stance that the province is funnelling public dollars into private health care.
"At the 40,000-feet level, we have a government that is really, really trying hard to bring private health care into Ontario," said Gelinas. "If enough people are not having the public system meet their needs, then they come to a point and say they are Ok with the privatization of health care, they don't want to wait three years for hip surgery or wait 18 months for their child to see a mental health provider, etc." Gelinas said there is "untapped opportunity" in Capreol and rural Northern communities.
"I have 40,000 people in Nickel Belt who do not have access to primary care," she said. Yet "we have a nurse-practitioner clinic in Capreol that is able to recruit and has nurse practitioners underemployed right now, who would love to take on another 1,000 patients and give them access to primary care."
Gelinas said she's not suggesting a multi-million-dollar solution. "We are talking hundreds of thousands of dollars (that) would make a huge difference in the lives of thousands of people who would gain access to a primary care provider ..." she said. "It just breaks my heart that we have a government that is so callously putting people's health and lives at risk." The Local Journalism Initiative is made possible through funding from the federal government. sud.editorial@sunmedia

Subject: Health care access; Nurse practitioners; Primary care; Clinics; Social workers; Funding; Northern communities

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: nurse; practitioners; burden; greater; health; system

Title: People are 'coming in and begging for us to take them as patients'; Pressure on health-care system could be eased withprovincial funding

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.1

column: Local Journalism Initiative

Document 265 of 281

NDP continues to support French university in Sudbury; Ford government 'buried' report showing such a school would succeed, NDP leader Marit Stiles says

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-continues-support-french-university-sudbury/docview/2921619526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+continues+to+support+French+university+in+Sudbury%3B+Ford+government+%27buried%27+report+showing+such+a+school+would+succeed%2C+NDP+leader+Marit+Stiles+says&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The New Democrats say northerners deserve to be educated near home in either official language.
NDP leader Marit Stiles was in Greater Sudbury on Thursday to demand the Ford government fund a stand-alone French university in the city.
Joined by Sudbury MPP Jamie West, Nickel Belt MPP France Gelinas, Guy Bourgouin, MPP for Mushkegowuk-James Bay, and Joanne Gervais, executive director of l'Association canadienne française de l'Ontario du grand Sudbury, Stiles said the Conservatives are ignoring Northern Ontario.
"The Conservatives continue to ignore the realities of Franco-Ontarians and the North by denying the University of Sudbury funding for a stand-alone Franco-Ontarian university that's by, for and with the French-speaking community," Stiles said. "To add salt to the wound, the government buried a report that shows the University of Sudbury meets - and in some cases, exceeds - the criteria to create a stand-alone French university."
The New Democrats said the community has spent decades campaigning for a French-language post-secondary institution.
"We're here for our Francophone community - period," West said. "They want their voices heard, they want this university to be successful and they want to be able to study in their chosen fields in Sudbury. It is their right to do so, and I will always stand with them."
The opposition leader said recent media reports reveal "the Conservatives denied the University of Sudbury funding for a stand-alone French university despite the Post-secondary Education Quality Assessment Board's recommendation that the project go forward, and after the province approved an initial proposal for the project."
While the Conservatives have said there is not enough demand for a French-language university in Sudbury, Stiles said they have been dishonest with the public. She said they commissioned the Post-secondary Education Quality Assessment Board report, which found the University of Sudbury was ideally suited to become a French-language university.
Then "they hid the report. They buried it; they stone-walled. They lost it behind a filing cabinet - you tell me," Stiles commented. "And then they turned their backs on Franco-Ontarians here in Northern Ontario. They refused them their fundamental right to education in their own language."
Stiles said this latest refusal is in keeping with the way the Conservatives address Northern Ontario. She said there is a history of disrespect and poor service between Queen's Park and the north.
Bourgouin called it a betrayal. "Once again, the Ford government has betrayed us," he said. "The ministry blatantly ignored the (Post-secondary Education Quality Assessment Board) report and led us to believe the University of Sudbury was not viable and did not meet the needs or the demands of the Franco-Ontarian population. We are constantly taking steps back with the Francophone community."
Gervais said the local Francophone community has been calling for the creation of a French-language university "run by, for and with the Franco-Ontarian community" for 50 years. She cited a list of reports and research documents that support the creation of the university.
"As we can see, there is no lack of supporting evidence; no lack of research; no lack of reports," she said. "What is lacking, in reality, is the good will on the part of the provincial government to serve the Franco-Ontarian community in the near north and to meet their needs, which they have been demanding for more than 50 years."
Gervais said educating young people in French close to home encourages them to grow roots in the north. The lack of a French-language university, on the other hand, allows the "hemorrhage of knowledge, of our thinkers and of our young Francophones."
Gervais said the Northern Ontario Francophone community will not back down and are ready to fight for a university.
"We are a tenacious group and we don't take no for an answer. I can assure you your underhanded refusal, made late in the afternoon on a Friday before a long weekend, changes nothing about our demands; changes nothing about our desire for this long-sought institution; and changes nothing about our expectations," she said. "If you think your refusal will make us give up and abandon our demands, it simply shows how little you know about the Franco-Ontarian community and its history."
Gelinas said her riding of Nickel Belt includes a lot of small communities that rely on post-secondary in the north and she noted 40 per cent of the populace is Francophone. Many Francophone students were studying at Laurentian University when it declared bankruptcy and cut many French programs. She said students were robbed - many were left with thousands in student loans and no way to finish their degrees.
"A lot of them lost their opportunity to finish in the programs they were in because 60 per cent of the French programs got cut," she commented. "Those young people are back in their communities, with $25,000 or $30,000 in debt and they will never finish their university education."
She said the community has spoken and it wants the University of Sudbury to host the French-language institution.
"The Francophone community is united," Gelinas said. "We want the University of Sudbury to be a university for, by and with Francophones in the north. We have waited far too long. It is time for this government to take responsibility and show us some respect. Enough is enough." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Francophones; French language; Education

Company / organization: Name: University of Sudbury; NAICS: 611310

Document 267 of 281

NDP leadership race needs to find the ring road out of Edmonton

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-leadership-race-needs-find-ring-road-out/docview/2921034854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leadership+race+needs+to+find+the+ring+road+out+of+Edmonton&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP won't win government again unless it breaks out of its comfy northern fortress.
But the leadership race to replace Rachel Notley already is looking like loads of Edmonton, not much Calgary or anywhere else.
There could be four Edmonton MLAs in the race: Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi, David Shepherd and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse.
Only one Calgarian, Kathleen Ganley, seems set to run.
Nobody has formally announced yet.
Application packages were sent out Monday. The official campaign starts Feb. 5.
Last May showed the NDP won't win another election without sweeping Calgary, or having a break through in rural Alberta.
During the campaign, the party fell over itself making pitches to the city.
The central campaign office was in Calgary.
Notley made dozens of local appearances.
It almost worked. The NDP captured 14 of 26 seats in the city.
But the fatal mistake - promising to raise the business tax - showed a deep disconnect with a crucial Calgary voting bloc at that pivotal moment.
And Notley carried every single seat in Edmonton.
The question now is whether the Edmonton wing is so dominant, and the local voting members so abundant, that a geographic outsider simply can't win the leadership.
Several Edmonton candidates might split that city's vote, and a sole Calgary candidate - Ganley - could win.
There have been eight Alberta NDP leaders since 1962. The only leader from outside the capital was Rachel Notley's father, Grant, from northern Spirit River-Fairview.
With a leadership record like that, it's hard to convince people you're a real provincial party.
Since the 1960s - and even before - Alberta's conservative governments have been led by an array of pan-Alberta premiers.
Ernest Manning (Edmonton) was followed by Harry Strom (Cypress) as the final Socred leaders. Then came PC premiers Peter Lougheed (Calgary); Don Getty (Edmonton, and then Stettler); Ralph Klein (Calgary); Ed Stelmach (Fort Saskatchewan), Alison Redford (Calgary), Dave Hancock (Edmonton) and Jim Prentice (Calgary).
The UCP premiers have been Jason Kenney (Calgary) and Danielle Smith (Brooks- Medicine Hat).
The NDP needs to develop this kind of reach and variety. Falling back on another comfortable Edmontonian won't get the job done.
In the 2015 election campaign, Prentice offended many Albertans when he said: "Alberta is not an NDP province."
He lost, but he wasn't entirely wrong. Notley won with 40 per cent of the provincial vote because of the conservative split between PC and Wildrose.
Last year's strong results in Calgary have convinced many New Democrats the party is permanently on the edge of victory.
It's not. Without serious signs they're reaching out to the whole province, the NDP is doomed to stagnate.
A couple of Edmonton hopefuls told me Monday they can fully champion Calgary (and by implication, rural Alberta, too).
They try. The NDP launched the new Calgary Cancer Centre that was stalled by PC dithering. But moves like that, while welcome, often seemed like foreign aid.
The party did take two essential steps at its weekend meeting in Red Deer.
First, they abandoned the rule that gave up to 25 per cent of leadership votes to "official organizations," meaning unions.
In this race, the standard will be one member, one vote.
The party also will ignore its longtime rule saying members can't also hold cards in other parties.
The party's chief returning officer, Amanda Freistadt, says they will keep an eye out for hostiles who try to flood the party, but will assume others genuinely support the NDP.
Most of all, though, this party needs a leadership divorce from Edmonton. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Leadership; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Notley, Rachel

Document 268 of 281

Community groups tell budget hearing they need more money

Author: Carmichael, Harold

Publication info: Carmichael, Harold. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/community-groups-tell-budget-hearing-they-need/docview/2921034241/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Community+groups+tell+budget+hearing+they+need+more+money&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Carmichael%2C+Harold&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Community groups told members of the province's Standing Committee on Finance and Economic Affairs meeting in Sudbury they need more provincial funding if they are going to keep their doors open.
Representatives from organizations such as the Reseau Access Network, the Capreol Nurse Practitioner Clinic and Better Beginnings Better Futures and others made the pitch to MPPs.
Sudbury New Democrat MPP Jamie West, who attended the consultation at the Radisson Hotel in Elm Place, said The Spot, the city's safe injection site that Reseau Access Network manages, is a good example of the province not properly funding badly needed healthcare services.
"Since 2021, they (Reseau Access Network) have been looking for (provincial) approval on this," he said in an interview following the end of the consultation's morning session.
West said with no provincial funding and city council deciding to no longer fund the safe injection site, The Spot faces an uncertain future. Temporary funding from Vale and anonymous donors means The Spot will remain open until the end of February. If no new funding is found or provided, it will likely close.
"When they first started out, people were dying on the street," West said. "There are three (safe injection) sites in the North, but only one has full funding. Sudbury was never fully funded."
A local nurse practitioner clinic, noted Nickel Belt New Democrat MPP France Gelinas, has been dealing with a provincial funding freeze since 2007, yet "everything has gone up since that point in time," including electricity, heating, internet and telephone costs.
Gelinas said the province is choosing to not boost funding to health-care organizations to adequate levels, yet is sitting on a huge $5.4-billiion fund of taxpayers'money that could help address the underfunding problem.
London North Centre New Democrat MPP Terence Kernaghan, who is a member of the Standing Committee, said consultations across the province held to date are hearing the same message.
"Ontario is the richest province but spends the least (on health care)," he said. "We are hearing health care is tragically underfunded. It's affecting everyone. There are some organizations which have had to fundraise simply to pay their staff."
To date, the Standing Committee has held 16 consultations across the province, with three more to go. The Standing Committee will then prepare a report for the province to consider when preparing the next budget, which is expected in the spring.
"It is pouring rain for the people of Ontario, but they (the government) ignore it," West said.
"We have all the evidence that shows the best way to spend health-care money is on the people that need them (services)," continued Kernaghan. "Yet, they are committed to the (private) agencies that skim the profit at the top ... They should be trying to get the best bang for their (health-care) buck." NDP LEADER IN SUDBURY Official Opposition leader Marit Stiles, who is also the leader of the NDP, is in Sudbury.
She met and toured the Afro Women and Youth Foundation in Sudbury on Tuesday evening.
On Wednesday, she toured Vale's Copper Cliff South Mine and Glencore's Nickel Rim South Mine before holding a social with NDP members at Crosscut Distillery, 1347 Kelly Lake Rd.
On Thursday, she will tour the Centre for Research in Occupational Safety and Health at Laurentian University and Soucie Salo at 1300 Lorne St. before lunching with the United Steel Workers, Local 6500 at the USW Local 6500 Hall, 66 Brady St.
Before leaving, Stiles will hold a press conference to call on the Ford government to fund a standalone French language university at the University of Sudbury.
Northern NDP MPPs and local Franco-Ontarian stakeholders will join her. hcarmichael@postmedia.com Twitter: @HaroldCarmichae

Document 269 of 281

We need Ontario's leadership to reform the tobacco industry; Letters to the editor

Author: Seale, Andrea

Publication info: Seale, Andrea. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-ontarios-leadership-reform-tobacco/docview/2918911734/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+Ontario%27s+leadership+to+reform+the+tobacco+industry%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Seale%2C+Andrea&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's National Non-Smoking Week, and Ontario has a historic opportunity to reduce healthcare costs and reform the tobacco industry.
Over many decades, Big Tobacco has engaged in deception and targeted marketing that has addicted youth and resulted in immense suffering, disease and premature death for so many people. The devastating impacts of tobacco use in Ontario are extensive and undeniable -1.4 million (11 per cent) still smoke tobacco, with 16,000 tobacco-related deaths and $2.2 billion in healthcare costs every year.
All 10 provinces have filed lawsuits against the tobacco industry collectively seeking more than $500 billion in damages, and right now the Ontario government is negotiating a settlement with Big Tobacco.
To prevent disease and save lives, it's vital that the settlement implement strict new controls on the tobacco industry, and establish substantial long-term funding for tobacco control through an independent fund. We must protect future generations. As such, the Canadian Cancer Society (CCS) urges Premier Doug Ford to use this monumental opportunity to hold Big Tobacco accountable and prevent future harm to Ontarians.

Document 270 of 281

Carbon price best policy to fight climate change

Author: Serré, Marc

Publication info: Serré, Marc. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-price-best-policy-fight-climate-change/docview/2918910965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+price+best+policy+to+fight+climate+change&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Serr%C3%A9%2C+Marc&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Putting a price on carbon pollution is the most cost-effective way to fight climate change and to hold big polluters accountable. In provinces where the federal pollution pricing system applies, Climate Action Incentive rebates are issued quarterly throughout the year and put money back into Canadians' pockets.
On Jan. 15, people in Ontario received their first Climate Action Incentive payment and they will receive their next payment on April 15. Payment amounts are $244 for a family of four, $183 for a couple and $122 for an adult living alone. Recently, the supplement for eligible residents living in rural areas was increased by 20 per cent.
Since 2019, every jurisdiction in Canada has implemented a price on carbon pollution. As 2024 kicks off, the Government of Canada reiterates its commitment to pollution pricing and its crucial role in meeting targets to cut emissions by 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030 and achieve net-zero emissions by 2050.
Estimates show that pollution pricing will contribute about a third of the total reductions in emissions that will occur from now to 2030. Putting a price on pollution and returning the proceeds to eligible Canadians through rebates provides support for individuals and families while also maintaining an incentive to reduce emissions.
Despite it being a tax, the federal price on pollution is revenue neutral, meaning the majority of people receive more back than they pay. The price on pollution is a high-profile policy that sparks conflicting reactions. However, it is undeniable that we are facing unprecedented consequences from climate change, which includes more devastating fires, floods, hurricanes, and other natural disasters around the globe.
The cost of rebuilding communities after these extreme weather events is significant. In 2023, insured losses in Canada reached $3.1 billion, the fourth-highest amount ever reported in the country's history. These numbers under-represent the full cost of damage, with much going unreported and borne by the taxpayer.
Actions to evacuate and relocate people often pose severe psychological and emotional trauma and can devastate communities. Thus, the fight against climate change must include adaptation and strong action to build community resilience in anticipation of these continued changes.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party uses flashy slogans like axe the tax to talk about carbon pricing; what they're failing to tell you is that the cost of inaction on climate change is significantly greater. More than 50 countries and experts agree carbon pricing works. The opposition has consistently voted against every environmental and climate change measure and it appears their climate plan is to watch the planet burn.
As Parliamentary Secretary to the minister of Energy and Natural Resources, I support the minister on many issues such as the implementation of National Firefighters to address forest risk management and to address the country's never-before-seen forest fire season of 2023. Canada's response to climate action must remain a priority.
I'm proud to be part of a government that continually supports green investments, which, in turn, creates jobs and leverages our economy. In 2022, we doubled down to attract investments and jobs in manufacturing zero emissions vehicles in Canada through the $8 billion Net Zero Accelerator. We launched the Canada Greener Home Grants and have recently paused the collection of the fuel charge on home heating oil across the country to further encourage the switch to heat pumps To be eligible for the climate action incentive payment (the rebate on the price on pollution, a person must simply file their income tax. The payment is not income tested.
The price on pollution is only one of the pillars of the Federal government's plan to ensure a cleaner tomorrow for future generations. Ultimately, protecting the environment while also tackling national affordability challenges is possible and necessary - which is why we have made great strides in both. I encourage Nickel Belt constituents and people across the country to be weary of misinformation and to get their facts from credible sources. We can all play a role in creating a more inclusive and strong democracy for all. Marc Serre is the Liberal MP for Nickel Belt.

Subject: Rebates; Forest & brush fires; Climate action; Emissions; Carbon; Climate change; Pollution; Insured losses

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Insured losses

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: putting; price; carbon; pollution; fight; climate

Title: Carbon price best policy to fight climate change

Publication title: Sudbury Star; Sudbury, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 27, 2024

column: Nickel Belt Mp

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Sudbury, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 08392544

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 271 of 281

Student cap will hit schools'finances hard

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/student-cap-will-hit-schoolsfinances-hard/docview/2918434299/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Student+cap+will+hit+schools%27finances+hard&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals'cap on international students could hit some universities and colleges in Ontario particularly hard, as schools there have become reliant on the higher tuition fees those students pay to keep their books balanced.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced the cap on Monday, saying his department will issue no more than 360,000 new visas this year, 35 per cent lower than last year, and keep them at that number for two years.
The visas will be handed out proportionally to provinces based on their population, which will force even larger cuts at some schools in Ontario. Schools in the province have been aggressive about bringing in international students and the new cap could mean a 50 per cent reduction in the number of new students schools can admit. Miller was still working with provinces on specific allocations, but Ontario, British Columbia and Nova Scotia were expected to have to make the biggest reductions.
Other provinces that were not as aggressive about recruiting could actually be able to increase the number of international students they bring in.
The federal government specifically exempted Masters and PhD students from the change, but otherwise will leave the decision on how to use allocations to provincial governments, forcing premiers to pick whether to send more international students or dollars to schools.
Mikal Skuterud, an economics professor at the University of Waterloo, said provinces that have to reduce their international student numbers should see which schools produce the best long-term financial outcomes for graduates, which is data the government already has.
"Avoid the politics, look at the data, let the data do the talking. I really would encourage them to do that."
Skuterud said the international student program became a way to bring in low-wage labour in many cases, which it never should have been. "The student program is not about plugging holes in low skilled labour markets, but that's kind of what it's become in a lot of ways."
He said the federal decision to lower the number of hours an international student can work, brought in last fall, should also help.
Alongside the labour market implications, provincial governments will have to decide how to handle the impact on universities and colleges, which, especially in Ontario, have become reliant on international student money.
Ontario's auditor general Bonnie Lysyk warned in 2022 that the provinces'institutions were becoming too reliant on international students to keep their finances in order.
"A high reliance on international student enrolment by universities poses risks outside of the Ministry's and the universities'control," she said Lysyk examined the system as a whole with detailed audits of some schools.
She found many Ontario schools relied heavily on international students to make their finances work. Algoma University in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for example has a satellite campus in Brampton where 90 per cent of students were international students. She found that the Brampton campus was responsible for half the school's enrolment and 65 per cent of its revenue.
After Laurentian University sought creditor protection, Lysyk examined that school's books and found they took a significant hit when Saudi Arabia pulled students from the school during a diplomatic dispute with Ottawa.
"Laurentian lost 130 students from Saudi Arabia. These students would have paid an estimated $3 million in tuition revenues and ancillary fees over the full length of their degrees," she said.
Steve Orsini, President and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities said the cut to international students is coming at a time when universities are struggling with a tuition freeze and no new money coming from the provincial government.
"Before (Monday's) announcement, there were at least 10 universities facing sizable operating deficits, totalling $175 million this year and growing to $273 million next year," he said.
Orsini said universities have been more responsible about bringing in international students and called on the government to give them more time to adapt.
Ontario's Advanced Education Minister Jill Dunlop wasn't available for an interview, but in a statement following Miller's decision she said the province would focus on bad actors in the system.
In 2021, Lysyk raised the same concerns she had with the province's universities with the province's colleges, noting they were growing primarily to serve international students.
In addition to the cap, Miller announced people taking part in these programs would no longer be able to claim a postgraduate work permit, making them much less desirable for international students.

Subject: Provinces; Tuition; Foreign students; Schools; Colleges & universities; College campuses; Labor market
