Document 272 of 281

Here's why Canada needs to step up military spending

Author: Humphrey, Charles

Publication info: Humphrey, Charles. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/heres-why-canada-needs-step-up-military-spending/docview/2918434284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+Canada+needs+to+step+up+military+spending&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Humphrey%2C+Charles&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With so many domestic challenges facing this country - homelessness, addiction, lack of housing affordability, inflation and violent protests to name a few - it is natural to suggest the government focus on such domestic pocket-book issues.
When people are struggling to pay their mortgages, put gas in their cars and food on their table, nobody wants to hear about spending government money on tanks and jetfighters.
For much of my life and those Millennials who are now in or entering the pressures of growing families and careers, the ethics of military spending have always been presented disfavourably. When there are people living in the streets, children receiving substandard education and Indigenous reserves without clean drinking water, how can one justify the purchase of multimilliondollar killing machines? From the 1990s until the last decade, I would have tended to agree. Before the last five years, I might have disagreed with the appraisal, but nodded along and politely kept my mouth shut, knowing I was in an unpopular minority. But we are well past that point and to continue to allow our national discussion around defence to be framed as a kind of perverse reactionary luxury that our progressive vanity cannot afford is downright irresponsible.
There is a well-trodden but not-quite-cliché quote attributed to Trotsky that "you may not be interested in war, but war is interested in you."
It is time for Canadians to wake up to the fact that war is very interested in us these days and we are already extremely late to the game.
We have become aware in recent years of the potential for conflict with China over Taiwan, and then in February 2022, we had to face up to the risks of Russian aggression. This has been disturbing enough, particularly given the poorly recognized northern proximity that means that we are, quite literally, a likely front in any great-power conflict. Our far north is poorly monitored and even more poorly defended and we are contiguous with the nation that would be the #1 target of our adversaries in any global conflict: the US.
Add in a US military that looks increasingly likely to become heavily committed to the Middle East in the aftermath of the Hamas attacks on Israel, Houthi attacks on shipping lanes and potential escalation with Iran coupled with the possibility of isolationist Trump's return to the White House in 2024, and the risks to Canada are clear.
For the first time for many Canadians, we must contemplate the potential for an existential military threat to our nation. There is a significantly non-zero possibility that Canada could experience armed conflict on our territory in the coming decades. Although this seems impossible, it is only the weight of the past 70-odd years of history weighed against the realities of the past two years that makes us see it that way. We are too conditioned by the circumstances of Canada from 1945 to 2022 to recognize the realities we face in Canada in 2022 and beyond.
We can no longer rely on international organizations increasingly targeted for irrelevance by authoritarian regimes and seen with suspicion and mistrust by large pluralities of the community of nations. We can no longer rely on the guaranteed, essentially free protection of our southern neighbour as their military preeminence is no longer guaranteed and is being eroded by trying to do everything, everywhere at once.
We can no longer rely on the predictable trust and cooperation that has defined our relationship with the US in light of the political division, congressional deadlock and growing antipathy towards the outside world that increasingly defines US politics.
We can no longer guarantee our national security on the terms that we have since the 1990s and it is time that our government recognizes this and acts with a sense of haste and duty.
Although the Russians, Chinese or Americans are not at our door, time is not on our side. Military capabilities are not created overnight. We'd be lucky if we could build up a credible deterrent force in five years. More likely, it would take 10, along with significant financial and cultural investments in redefining our attitude towards national defence and diplomacy.
Let there be no mistake, though, defence and security are no longer mere issues of international politics and prestige; we don't need to beef up our military for peacekeeping or to meaningfully participate in operations with our allies. These are nice side benefits, but no, defence is now a domestic policy concern. The fundamental assumptions of Canadian defence policy are shifting under our feet, the alarm bells are ringing and our government and our people remain asleep.
Wake up Canada, hard times are coming; it is time to redefine our relationship to military force to ensure that we can protect our land, our families and our loved ones from an increasingly unstable and unpredictable world. Charles Humphrey is the candidate of record for the Conservatives in Nickel Belt.

Subject: International organizations; Politics; Defense spending

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: domestic; challenges; facing; country; homelessness; addiction

Document 273 of 281

Falling into the 'population trap'

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/falling-into-population-trap/docview/2916297612/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Falling+into+the+%27population+trap%27&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's immigration policies are damaging the economy. That's the finding of Canada's top bank economists, who issued a dire warning to the federal government. By admitting 455,000 new permanent residents and more than 800,000 non-permanent residents last year, Canada is on the brink of recession, or worse.
"I'll put it bluntly: We've fallen into the population trap," said Stéfane Marion, chief economist at National Bank of Canada. An increase in the standard of living is no longer possible because "you don't have enough savings to stabilize your capital-to-labour ratio."
We also learned the government was warned two years ago by its own officials about high immigration causing a housing shortage - and ignored those warnings.
Asked about this, Housing Minister Sean Fraser replied, "I don't think anybody needs a briefing note to understand that having more people in the housing market impacts the housing market, but having gone through in consecutive years the process of building Canada's immigration levels plan, I can tell you that there are an enormous number of factors that go into them."
OK then, so what are those factors? Why did the government decide immigration levels should increase at this pace? Why does it continue to allow hundreds of thousands of foreign students to work unlimited hours? Why did it admit so many people into the country in such a short time? The classic answer is we need to increase the labour supply. Businesses are short of workers. Canadians are not having enough children to replace them. That's the argument of the Century Initiative, the business community and immigration advocates.
But simply pulling in adult immigrants and temporary workers is not the same as boosting fertility in the existing population. Those workers have needs that are very different from those of children.
They won't live under their parents'roof; they need their own housing. They won't be ferried about by their parents; they need independent transportation. They will be buying adult levels of food, clothing and other necessities. In short, they will be competing with other adults for basic needs, and that increased demand means two things: price inflation and resource scarcity. Which is exactly what Canada is now experiencing.
Fraser is right. You don't need a study to figure this out. It's simple common sense. So again, why did the government do this - especially after it was warned not to by its own bureaucrats? The cynic in me sees one reason: votes.
Immigrants tend to settle in cities. City dwellers tend to vote for "progressive" parties. In Canada, since the 1960s, that has translated to increased Liberal support. According to research done at Western University, a oneunit shift in the "urbanity score" for a riding produces an increase of eight percentage points in the Liberal party's vote share in that riding. No surprise then, that in the 2021 election, the Liberals won 109 of the 150 most urban ridings, while the Conservatives took only 23.
Immigrants also are grateful to the party that brings them in. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau learned this from his father. Pierre Elliott Trudeau took credit for the citizenship of millions of Canadians, even though many had come to Canada under previous governments. Why? Because they took their oath under his watch. And when it came to elections, the immigrant communities of the day - Italians, Greeks, Haitians, Vietnamese - tended to vote Liberal.
The implications of Justin Trudeau's replication of this policy are dire. Studies show they are turning Canadians against immigration: even 62 per cent of current immigrants think we're letting in too many people. They are impoverishing Canadians, both current and newcomer, according to the bank study. And they are also helping fuel Quebec separatism, as francophones look with alarm at rising immigrant populations in the rest of Canada. By the end of the century, Quebec risks becoming a bit player in Confederation, with only 15 per cent of its population.
This cannot continue. Instead of flooding the country with newcomers, the government needs to boost domestic productivity. That's a harder fix - and one that won't give them more votes. But then again, if Canadians can't afford a decent life, the Liberals may not get their votes, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Canadians; Immigrants; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: immigration; policies; damaging; economy; finding; economists

Document 274 of 281

Conservative leader promises to speed approvals for new mines; Pierre Poilievre also says Sudbury and its minerals have a big role to play in clean energy

Author: Moodie, Jim

Publication info: Moodie, Jim. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/conservative-leader-promises-speed-approvals-new/docview/2915457233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservative+leader+promises+to+speed+approvals+for+new+mines%3B+Pierre+Poilievre+also+says+Sudbury+and+its+minerals+have+a+big+role+to+play+in+clean+energy&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Moodie%2C+Jim&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre took advantage of the bracing conditions in Sudbury on Saturday to stress his plan for cheaper heating bills, as well as take some shots at his main political rival, who recently returned from a vacation in Jamaica.
"While Justin Trudeau flies around in a taxpayer-funded private jet, burning fuel and passing greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, he punishes people for heating their homes," said the Conservative leader, against the backdrop of an Axe the Carbon Tax billboard on Lorne Street. "He is a high-tax, high-carbon hypocrite."
Poilievre said 60 per cent of Canadians are paying more in carbon tax than they get back in rebates, while the government is still not meeting its climate targets.
A Conservative government would "lower the cost of carbon-free energy, rather than raising the cost of traditional energy that Canadians still need," he said. "How? Well, we're going to greenlight green projects."
The Tories plan to "streamline and speed up approval of hydroelectric dams," said Poilievre, while also promoting "nuclear power, carbon capture and storage technology, and offshore tidal power in the Atlantic provinces."
Sudbury also has a big role to play in providing clean energy.
"We're going to mine the minerals of electrification," he said. "Justin Trudeau wants to give millions of dollars to subsidize multinational corporations that will assemble electric car batteries - using foreign-made and heavily polluting minerals from China when we have those minerals right here."
The problem, he said, is that it currently takes far too long for a new mining project to get off the ground.
"It shouldn't take 25 years to approve a mine in Canada," he said. "It should take 18 months."
Should his party form the next government, Poilievre said it will repeal the Impact Assessment Act, formerly known as Bill C-69, "so we can approve mines in a way that protects our environment and consults First Nations, but gets decisions made and brings home clean, green mining and energy production to our country and our people."
Eight years under the Trudeau administration have not made Canadians happier or more prosperous, according to Poilievre.
"Life costs more, work doesn't pay, housing costs have doubled," he said. "Crime, chaos, drugs and disorder are common in our streets. And now he wants to punish you for heating your home, driving your car, feeding your family."
A second carbon tax introduced by the Liberals means the cost will rise to 61 cents/litre, said Poilievre, "forcing seniors to choose between eating and heating, and taxing our farmers who make the food, the truckers who ship the food, and therefore all who buy the food - all while we have record food bank use because people can't afford to feed themselves."
Apart from scrapping the carbon tax, a Conservative government would balance the budget, bring down inflation and cut income tax, pledged Poilievre.
It would also spur more housing construction "so our youth can afford homes again," he said, and tackle crime and opioid abuse through stricter bail rules for serial offenders and better rehab options for those struggling with substances.
"We're going to bring safety by ending Justin Trudeau's catchand-release policy, and bringing jail and not bail for repeat violent offenders," he said. "By giving treatment, not more decriminalized and taxpayer-subsidized drugs, so we can bring our loved ones home drug-free."
At the same time, Poilievre promised to protect Canadians'hunting rights, arguing gun-control legislation brought forward by the Trudeau government is unfairly impacting law-abiding citizens.
"Hunters are not the problem," he said. "Justin Trudeau wants to protect turkeys from hunters; I want to protect Canadians from criminals."
The would-be prime minister also echoed the language of an earlier Ontario premier - Mike Harris, with his Common Sense Revolution - in describing how his plan for the country differs from that of the current leader.
"Canadians will have a choice in the next election between a costly coalition of Trudeau and the NDP - the tax your heat, punish your work, take your money, double your housing costs, and unleash crime and chaos in your community - or common-sense Conservatives, who axe the tax, reward your work, lower your costs, and allow you to earn powerful paycheques that buy affordable food, gas and homes. That is the choice based on common sense." jmoodie@postmedia.com

Document 275 of 281

Sudburians to receive carbon tax rebate Monday, Liberal government says; That's $244 for a family of four in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudburians-receive-carbon-tax-rebate-monday/docview/2915455469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudburians+to+receive+carbon+tax+rebate+Monday%2C+Liberal+government+says%3B+That%27s+%24244+for+a+family+of+four+in+Ontario&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just in time for Pierre Poilievre's visit to Sudbury and northeastern Ontario, the Liberal government is reminding Sudburians -and all Canadians -that they will receive their first carbon tax rebate of the year on Monday.
"Canada's approach to pollution pricing is not only one of the best ways to fight climate change, it also puts money back into the pockets of individuals and families," the government said in a release. "The direct proceeds from the federal pollution price are returned to the jurisdiction from which they were collected to help with cost-of-living challenges while keeping the incentive to pollute less."
Poilievre was in Sudbury, Timmins and North Bay on Saturday, and Sault Ste. Marie on Sunday. As part of his Axe the Tax tour, the federal Conservative leader promises to kill the carbon tax if elected prime minister.
The Conservatives argue the Liberals'carbon tax is inflationary and a tax plan driving up the price of gas, groceries and home heating.
The Liberals have defended the carbon tax as a price on pollution that helps address the worsening climate crisis. They also argue the carbon tax is returned to Canadians.
On Monday, for example, the government noted people living in provinces where the federal fuel charge applies will receive their first pollution pricing rebate of the year through direct bank deposit or by cheque. This means a family of four in Ontario will receive a pollution price rebate, called the Climate Action Incentive payment, of $244.
"Eight out of 10 households receive more money back through the pollution price rebate than they pay as a result of the federal pollution pricing system, with low-and middle-income households benefitting the most," the government said.
Residents of small and rural communities receive an extra 10 per cent supplement beyond the base rebate amount. As of this April, the government said it will double the rural supplement to 20 per cent in recognition of the increased energy needs of rural residents and their reduced access to cleaner transportation options.
In its release, the Liberal government said it remains committed to pollution pricing "and its crucial role in meeting targets to cut emissions by 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030 and achieve net-zero emissions by 2050.
"Estimates show that pollution pricing will contribute about a third of the total reductions in emissions that will occur between now and 2030. Putting a price on pollution and returning the proceeds to eligible Canadians through rebates provides support for individuals and families while also maintaining an incentive to reduce emissions."
A carbon tax, also known as a price on carbon, is levied on emissions from fossil fuel sources, such as coal, oil, natural gas or gasoline.
The carbon tax is a standard price per tonne of CO2-equivalent emissions generated. In 2024, that's $65 per tonne but it will increase over time to encourage Canadians to transition away from fossil fuels.
The amount you're taxed depends on the fuel you use - the more emissions it produces, the more you'll pay. The carbon tax on home heating oil, as an example, works out to $0.1738 per litre, regardless of the cost of oil, or about nine per cent of your heating bill, experts say.
The government calls the carbon tax program revenue-neutral because all proceeds are returned to the province where they were collected.
It said 90 per cent of the revenues are returned to households through a rebate program. The other 10 per cent is directed to programs to help businesses, schools, municipalities and other grant recipients reduce their fossil fuel consumption.
However, it's not revenue-neutral for individual Canadians. Whether you come out with more cash depends on how you heat your home and how much you drive your car.
Someone with a big house heated with fossil fuels and who drives a gas-powered vehicle a lot will pay more money than they get back. It's that dynamic the government hopes will create an incentive for Canadians to lower their carbon footprint. sud.editorial@sunmedia.ca X: @SudburyStar Star Staff

Subject: Canadians; Rural areas; Fossil fuels; Emissions; Energy consumption; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Pollution; Heating; Environmental tax

Document 276 of 281

Amid declining rights, youth provide hope

Author: Mulcair, Tom

Publication info: Mulcair, Tom. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/amid-declining-rights-youth-provide-hope/docview/2913343397/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Amid+declining+rights%2C+youth+provide+hope&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Mulcair%2C+Tom&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The decline of rights in Quebec is alarming and concerns us all.
With Bill 21, the Coalition Avenir Québec government brazenly attacked religious minorities.
The justification for this overt discrimination is swaddled in high-sounding claptrap about "laïcité," the separation of church and state.
It's hard to see it as anything other than the crass politics of exclusion, playing to the intolerance of the lowest common denominator. Laïcité has been spray-painted on to give this discrimination a superficial sheen.
Elsewhere, Premier François Legault has attacked the constitutionally guaranteed rights of the English-speaking minority to control and manage its school boards.
Legault also unilaterally changed the founding 1867 Constitution, the BNA Act, and removed the equality of English and French before the courts. Shamefully, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did nothing to challenge him.
Bill 96 contains outrageous provisions allowing for warrant less search and seizure of computers and cellphones by the vocabulary constabulary.
Like Bill 21, it has an ostensibly virtuous purpose: it's about protecting and preserving the French language in Quebec. Except the numbers show the French language is doing just fine, thank you. But it's become an undebatable truth that French is in such desperate need of discriminatory legislation that we'll become another Louisiana without it.
Like Bill 21 before it, Bill 96 has, for now, been peremptorily shielded by using the notwithstanding clause to remove charter rights.
We can presume it's precisely because those limits on rights are not demonstrably justifiable in a free and democratic society that Legault uses the notwithstanding clause.
When Ontario Premier Doug Ford considered using the notwithstanding clause, Trudeau flew into a state of high dudgeon and Ford backed down. Removing rights in Quebec is apparently in a different category. Trudeau actually made things worse with Bill C-13 that reduced the rights of the English linguistic minority, leading to Marc Garneau's resignation as a Liberal MP.
Back in Quebec, during the study of his mammoth reform, Health Minister Christian Dubé brought in a clause that could eviscerate language guarantees for bilingual health and social service institutions. Thanks in part to the great work of the Opposition Liberals, Dubé beat a hasty retreat, but it did nothing to assuage fears.
In Bill 96, the ability to hire bilingual staff to ensure services in English in those institutions has been curtailed. Bureaucratic interpretations only have made things worse and the McGill University Health Centre has caved and made board presentations in French only.
Legault also has betrayed a 47-year-old deal with anglophone parents who choose to send their kids to French school. The law says their kids are deemed to have studied in English for purposes of admission in the future, but Legault has broken that long-standing rule by diktat and refused to issue eligibility certificates to many.
The recent attacks on McGill and Concordia universities supposedly were necessary because too much English was being heard on the streets in Montreal. Think about that for a second. Can you imagine how that will be perceived by tourists? Bienvenue au Grand Prix de Montréal, please don't speak English in the streets. We can't stand it.
Since leaving politics, I've had the good fortune to teach at the Université de Montréal. The next generation of Quebec leaders I've met there gives me the greatest hope. While the likes of Legault, French Language Minister Jean-François Roberge and Higher Education Minister Pascale Déry appear to be in a race to the bottom against the Parti Québécois, young Quebecers are overwhelmingly open, tolerant and respectful of differences. They are the greatest hope for our future together. Tom Mulcair, a former leader of the federal NDP, served as minister of the environment in the Quebec Liberal government of Jean Charest.

Subject: French language; Bilingualism

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: decline; rights; quebec; alarming; concerns; coalition

Document 277 of 281

Federal parties haunted by elections past

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-parties-haunted-elections-past/docview/2913341533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+parties+haunted+by+elections+past&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Settle in, voters of Canada: the next federal election likely won't be until 2025. That's an eon away in political terms, and anything - literally anything - can happen in that time. If you had said in January 2023 that one year later, the Middle East would be a war zone, the federal government would call a foreign interference inquiry and the Conservatives would be trouncing the Liberals at the polls, most people would have called you crazy - or at least hoped you were wrong about the first one.
That said, there are a few certainties all three major federal parties will face in the lead-up to the next vote. And we got a taste of them in the past two weeks, a reminder that ghosts of elections past are always hanging around, ready to haunt you in elections future if you're not careful.
Let's start with the Liberals. Their Achilles heel is elitism, making them easy to brand as out of touch with the average Canadian. That elitism was on full display over Christmas when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau jetted off to Jamaica for a family vacation. And not just any vacation: this wasn't a Beaches or Sandals-level trip, but a $9,300-a-night epic getaway at a posh resort, gifted by owner and family friend Peter Green. Total tab: $84,000.
To put that into perspective, that same $84,000, if you had it lying around, would qualify you to buy a $660,000 Toronto condo at current mortgage rates - if your household income was $150,000 or more. The median Toronto household earns just over half that, according to the last census. In other words, for the price of Trudeau's holiday, they can't even afford to buy a home - a fact the Conservatives will likely point out at every opportunity.
But the Tories need to be careful, too.
Their Achilles heel is Uncle Marvin. As in your crazy Uncle Marvin, who trots out his collection of tin foil hats every time the family gets together for Thanksgiving. Uncle Marvin says a lot of nutty things, and a lot of not-so-nice things, too. He rants about gay people burning in lakes of fire and Jews running the world. He has surfaced in various political campaigns throughout the ages, and it has never ended well.
Uncle Marvin came out of the woodwork this week, in the form of a petition sponsored by Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis. The petition asks for Canada to quit the United Nations, and is the brainchild of a man named Doug Porter, who told the left-leaning website Press Progress that Lewis's staff helped him with the wording. Porter also said that if the UN gets what it wants, "It would be the elimination of a majority of the population and those that would remain would be slaves, and they would be repeatedly injected with mRNA injections to change their DNA so they lose their humanity and they are different creatures." Liberal attack ads, start your engines.
As for the NDP, their biggest issue is trust.
By 2025, they will have been propping up this government for a full four years. So when Leader Jagmeet Singh recently said, "that's off the table" next time around, it's kind of hard to believe him.
Of course, Singh's pension will have vested by then, so perhaps there is less risk in having the government fall, especially when his own party only gave him an 81 per cent approval rating at its last national convention.
Cynical? Yes. But this is politics, and cynicism is par for the course. The NDP will also need to keep its radical wing in check if it is to stop the labour vote from migrating to the Conservatives. This is where issues like the Hamas-Israel war come into play - if union voters feel the NDP has become too "progressive" for their tastes on issues like Palestinian statehood, they may tune out, and tune into the Tories'strong economic message instead.
Ultimately, the next election will be decided in the pocketbook. If the economy turns around, the Liberals could see a boost - but they will still need other issues to stay in power. So watch for those ghosts to keep haunting us throughout 2024 - and into the next new year. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Elitism; Negative campaigning; Voters; Politics; Elections

Location: Canada

Document 278 of 281

Sudbury doctor recognized with Order of Ontario for work in geriatric care; While she alone was nominated, she wants to be clear her successes are the result of committed teamwork

Author: Keown, Mary Karine

Publication info: Keown, Mary Karine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sudbury-doctor-recognized-with-order-ontario-work/docview/2912167577/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sudbury+doctor+recognized+with+Order+of+Ontario+for+work+in+geriatric+care%3B+While+she+alone+was+nominated%2C+she+wants+to+be+clear+her+successes+are+the+result+of+committed+teamwork&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Keown%2C+Mary+Karine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dr. Jo-Anne Clarke is mostly physician, with a dash of sleuth tossed in for good measure.
Older patients sometimes present with a collection of symptoms that may seem unrelated - and can be confusing - but Clarke, a geriatrician, said she likes putting together the puzzle pieces of geriatric care.
"You look at all the pieces that might have contributed and you adjust," she explained. "We have to look at all the factors that might be contributing. That's the sort of thing I always enjoyed."
Clarke is a pioneer in the northern health-care landscape. She has been working in Greater Sudbury for about 14 years and during that time, she has built a framework on how to deliver geriatric care with compassion, finesse and expertise.
Clarke said growing up she had "some very positive aging role models," including her grandparents, with whom she was close. When she was younger, she also did some volunteer work with older adults and she learned to appreciate their contributions.
During medical school, "I was really lucky or maybe the universe had its own ideas for me. Many of my undergraduate preceptors were geriatricians; when I did my internal medicine rotations I had geriatricians. I just really was highly influenced by geriatric care."
Clarke has also enjoyed learning from her patients.
"You learn what life was like when they were born, how the world has changed. We also learn clinically from them every day," she said. "Medicine is life-long learning. What I've come to appreciate from developing geriatric programs over the last 14 years is that you really do need to work with so many individuals and organizations to care for older adults."
Most older adults aging well-Clarke said the elderly have wisdom and knowledge to share. Those who are perceptive enough to listen will benefit.
"I've always thought of aging as success. If you're aging, it's success," she commented. "I think we have a fairly negative lens when it comes to aging - I don't think our society looks at aging as success. We correlate it with weakness and frailty and impairment, but by and large, aging is a success story. We need to recognize the wisdom and breadth of knowledge that comes from our older adults."
These days, Clarke said most seniors are aging well. Only 15 per cent of seniors over the age of 75 are considered frail. That means 85 per cent of older adults "are not. You want to be in that 85 per cent," she said.
It takes a village to create an age-friendly community, which could include meal delivery services, snow shovelling, home and health care, as well as access to recreational opportunities.
Locally, Clarke said Greater Sudbury has "come a long way." When she first stepped into the Nickel City in 2009, there were very few options for geriatric care. But more than a decade later, the landscape has changed and has been enriched by community efforts.
Recognized for her effortsClarke said medicine "is a calling, it's what I love to do."
And she does it very well. Clarke was one of 25 Ontarians honoured with the Order of Ontario.
"A transformational trailblazer and true visionary, Dr. Jo-Anne Clarke became the first geriatrician in northeastern Ontario, filling a glaring need throughout the region," Ajithaa Thillainathan, senior issues and media adviser with the Ministry of Citizenship and Multiculturalism, said Friday. "She initiated a Care of the Elderly training program and opened clinics in Sault Ste. Marie, Sudbury, Timmins, North Bay, Parry Sound and James Bay, brightening the future for thousands of seniors. An exceptional physician, her courageous and relentless efforts have led to the evolution of geriatric care."
The Order of Ontario - the highest honour proffered by the provincial government - recognizes "outstanding people who have made exceptional contributions to help build a stronger province, nation and world," the government stated on its website.
Appointees to the Order of Ontario come from all walks of life and represent various sectors of society.
"These Ontarians have demonstrated the highest levels of merit, excellence and dedication in their respective disciplines, and they have made significant impacts here at home and around the world," said Lieutenant Governor Edith Dumont. "Our province gratefully acknowledges their remarkable contributions, which inspire us all to be leaders and change-makers."
An independent advisory council to the Order of Ontario reviewed the nominations and made recommendations to the lieutenant governor. The advisory council included the chief justice of Ontario, the speaker of the Legislative Assembly, the secretary of the cabinet, as well as additional members appointed by Premier Doug Ford.
A great start to 2024Clarke has been steadfastly building a more age-friendly Northern Ontario since her arrival in Greater Sudbury.
She was once the only doctor in northeastern Ontario specializing in older adult care. She worked tirelessly to launch health care clinics throughout this part of the province, but these days, Clarke said there are a number of geriatricians working throughout the region. The Northeast Specialized Geriatric Centre (nesgc.ca), located at Pioneer Manor, lists clinics throughout the north, including Cochrane, New Liskeard and Elliot Lake.
Clarke said throughout her career in Greater Sudbury, she has worked with many invaluable colleagues, including physiotherapists, nurses, nutritionists, pharmacists and social workers.
"That is something I always enjoyed - being able to learn from other clinicians," she offered.
Clarke also credits the City of Greater Sudbury. She said in 2009, city hall worked with the Local Health Integration Network (LHIN) to secure funding for an aging-at-home strategy. The city also paid her salary during those early days.
While the collective effort put her on centre stage, the accolades must be distributed equally. Clarke said she was "still shocked" by the honour. While she alone was nominated for the Order of Ontario, she wants to be clear her successes are the result of committed teamwork.
"I really feel all the work I've done has been a team effort. I can list about 100 names since I came here in 2009 that have made the growth of geriatrics possible," she said. "It could not have happened without these people. I'm one person, so from that point of view, I'm quite floored. I am, however, so pleased to see geriatrics celebrated and recognized as important. That's a great way to start 2024." mkkeown@postmedia.com X: @marykkeown Facebook: @mkkeown

Subject: Older people; Success; Geriatrics; Aging

Document 279 of 281

Scott Moe tops in scaring, upsetting other Canadians

Author: Staples, David

Publication info: Staples, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scott-moe-tops-scaring-upsetting-other-canadians/docview/2912167291/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scott+Moe+tops+in+scaring%2C+upsetting+other+Canadians&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Staples%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith no longer dominates the list of craziest conservatives in the imagination of other Canadians. There's a new contender, Premier Scott Moe of Saskatchewan, who is now scaring and upsetting many residents of Ontario, British Columbia, Quebec and the Maritimes with his refusal to collect Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax on home heating and his recent use of the notwithstanding clause to ensure parents know if their child is taking on a new sexual identity at school.
I put this matter to you for two reasons.
First, I'm curious about how it all will play out politically and, second, because it's so unusual. I can't recall a time when it came to pushing a conservative agenda that Alberta and its leaders weren't front and centre.
Did anyone expect Smith - the raucous Wildroser, the fearless, even reckless radio talk show host, the vaccine-hesitant, Truckers Convoy-boosting, Sovereignty Act-thumping, Canada Pension Plan-bashing leader of Alberta's conservative movement - would shy from being Canada's most outspoken conservative leader? But that's what we're seeing. In October, Moe charged into the trans issue, bringing in legislation and invoking the notwithstanding clause to prevent children under 16 from changing their names or pronouns at school without parental consent.
Polls show support in the 60 to 70 per cent range from Canadians for parents knowing if their child has changed his or her pronouns at school. Trudeau seemingly conflated such support with hatred of trans people and other gender minorities. It blew up into an ugly wedge issue, but Smith kept out of it.
When cornered and asked if Alberta would follow Moe's lead, Smith said her party was monitoring the issue but had no plans to bring in legislation. Smith expressed compassion for the adults involved and also for children grappling with puberty and gender identity. "It's been my hope from the beginning that we wouldn't politicize this," she said.
Thus ended any major political uproar in Alberta on this issue.
On the carbon tax, when the Trudeau Liberals exempted home heating oil commonly used in the Maritimes, Smith and Moe asked Trudeau to do the same with natural gas home heating. When he refused, however, Moe had his province no longer collect the tax on home heating.
Globe and Mail columnist Andrew Coyne, a stalwart voice in the Eastern Canada establishment, now alleges Moe's action is pushing Canada to the brink. "We have reached, in short, a dangerous turn in the current epidemic of provincial lawlessness."
I'll suggest we reached a dangerous turn in the epidemic of federal lawlessness long ago, with the Trudeau Liberal power grab on assessing industrial projects and their bizarre law declaring all plastic toxic, two matters our courts have now deemed examples of illegal federal overreach.
Smith has spoken out in harsh terms against the feds'ongoing unlawful aggression, but she's not going so far as Moe in acting.
Is there some method to her lack of madness? Smith's only concern should be Alberta's success. That happens when Alberta wins major court cases against the Trudeau Liberals, but with a lot less heartache, disruption and bother when there's a Conservative government with heavy Alberta representation in Ottawa, one that doesn't bring in dark measures destructive to Alberta's success.
The less out-of-step Alberta is with most Canadians, the more likely we'll see such a government in Ottawa. That will mean other Canadians recognizing the wisdom of our agenda, but also that we take care to pay some small respect to their values and policies.
The more other Canadians are persuaded that a strong Alberta, free to produce oil and gas wealth to share with all regions, is in their best interest, the freer Alberta will be to build Canada's collective prosperity.
Essentially, if the Trudeau Liberals can paint Smith as crazy and Alberta as dangerous, they win.
Alberta can't bear such a defeat. The stakes are too high. Another Trudeau-Singh government will mean the full enactment of Trudeau's chaos-inducing, job-killing energy policies. Perhaps it's best if Smith folds now, presents Alberta's case as reasonably as possible, and backs away from fighting every small battle to win the greater war. dstaples@postmedia.com

Subject: Canadians; Heating; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Ford ignoring alarm bell on delays: Gelinas; Local briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ignoring-alarm-bell-on-delays-gelinas-local/docview/2912166526/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ignoring+alarm+bell+on+delays%3A+Gelinas%3B+Local+briefs&amp;title=Sudbury+Star&amp;issn=08392544&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Sudbury+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP France Gelinas is demanding the government deliver funding to ensure cancer patients receive timely surgeries.
The Nickel Belt member, who also serves as health critic to the NDP, pointed to recent stats showing nearly half of the most aggressive cancers are not being treated in the recommended time frame.
"This troubling report from Ontario Health makes clear that the staffing crisis in our health care system is having a direct impact on patient outcomes, on matters of life and death," said Gelinas in a release. "Early detection and timely access to surgery are vital for those diagnosed with cancer, especially aggressive forms."
Longer wait times for diagnosis and surgery can be attributed to a system "stretched by chronic government underfunding and the exodus of overworked, underpaid workers," said Gelinas. "Yet the (Doug) Ford Conservatives continue to act like the crisis doesn't exist."
The MPP said Queen's Park has the resources to address this pressing need and must act immediately.
"We have the money to fix the problem," she said. "The government is sitting on billions of dollars-$5.4 billion, to be precise-of unspent funds that should be going to our hospitals. Ford must treat the crisis with the urgency it warrants and stop withholding billions from health care."

Document 281 of 281

New year, same inept leadership

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha.

Abstract: None available.

Full text: The year is only a few days old, yet it feels like we're stuck in 2023. Russia remains at war with Ukraine. Hamas is still at war with Israel. China is meddling in Taiwan's election. Artificial intelligence is eating our jobs. Social media are filled with rage about inflation, Palestine, trans rights and Donald Trump. It's enough to make you hide under the covers, shut off your news feeds and watch cat videos until you fall into a fretful sleep.
Here in Canada, it also feels as though, despite the new year, we're grappling with the same problems, and no idea how to tackle them. In fact, our federal government's contradictory policies are making things worse.
Inflation continues to erode our standard of living, yet carbon tax increases will roll ahead on April 1. Housing remains in short supply, yet immigration levels are at historic highs. Geopolitical strife has increased, yet our military is short 16,000 personnel and if Canada were at war, we'd only have three days worth of ammunition to defend ourselves. Cue the cat videos, please.
In his year-end interviews, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau acknowledged Canada's military is woefully short on supplies, but blamed the situation on our contributions to Ukraine. On the carbon tax, he confirmed he will make no further exemptions beyond those announced for home heating oil.
As for immigration, while Trudeau acknowledged that, "We all bear some responsibility; this is a challenge that we have to work on all together," he added that it isn't the 500,000 new immigrants who are the problem: "It's the temporary immigrants that have spiked massively over the past couple of years that is putting so much pressure on the whole system."
Fair enough, but all these phenomena have happened under his watch. Eight years and three mandates have given the Liberals plenty of opportunity to assess the effects of their policies, and change course if things get worse instead of better. So given the mess we're in today, does Trudeau plan to change his tune? Apparently not. "This is exactly not the time to be slowing down," Trudeau told Global News. "The context we're in right now - where progress has become so fragile because of global and large macro events - is the time to be doubling down and rolling up our sleeves, and that's what I'm here for."
Actually, you're not here for that. As Albert Einstein purportedly quipped, "The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results." Canadians'mental health is bad enough; we don't need more nuttiness from Ottawa.
The government needs to start making the hard choices - the ones it doesn't want to make because they are politically unpalatable to its key voter bases, such as the progressive left and immigrant communities.
It should start by repealing the carbon tax to ease the pressure on Canadians' pocketbooks. It should not merely threaten to cap student visas to dubious diploma mills, but actually do so, and stop allowing these students to be used as cheap labour by working more than 20 hours a week off campus. It should increase military spending on not just hardware, but also on incentives for recruitment and support for military personnel, to make a career in the Armed Forces an attractive option for young people.
The irony is these policies may help the Liberals at the polls, not hurt them. Polls show a majority of voters in Liberal strongholds like Atlantic Canada want more carbon tax carveouts. Sixty-two per cent of Canadians born outside of the country say immigration levels are too high. And two-thirds of Canadians - including 68 per cent in vote-rich Ontario - support increasing military spending to meet NATO's two per cent target.
This feels like a watershed year for many issues in Canada and around the world. Instead of doubling down, Trudeau should wise up. If these problems continue to fester into 2025, Canadians will roll up their sleeves and show him the door. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.
